PC-A → R1 frame: SRC MAC = PC-A, DST MAC = R1 ingress; SRC IP = PC-A, DST IP = PC-B
R1 looks up 10.2.2.20 in RIB/FIB, finds egress interface toward PC-B
R1 ARPs for PC-B (if not cached) on the egress segment
R1 rewrites: SRC MAC = R1 egress MAC, DST MAC = PC-B MAC
R1 decrements TTL by 1 and recomputes IP checksum
Source IP and destination IP remain PC-A and PC-B end-to-end

back.text: Key takeaway: IP addresses do NOT change as a packet is routed; MAC addresses are rewritten at every Layer 3 hop.

front.text: What happens to data as it moves down the OSI stack on a sending host?

items: Layer 7 — application generates data
Layer 4 — TCP/UDP header added (segment)
Layer 3 — IP header added with source and destination IP (packet)
Layer 2 — Ethernet header added with source/destination MAC (frame)
Layer 1 — frame converted to bits and transmitted on the media

back.text: Each layer adds its own header (encapsulation). On the receiving side the process is reversed (de-encapsulation) until the data reaches the application at Layer 7.

front.text: What is an OUI in a MAC address?

back.text: The Organizationally Unique Identifier (OUI) is the first 3 octets (24 bits) of a MAC address, assigned by the IEEE to a specific device manufacturer. The remaining 3 octets are assigned by the manufacturer and must be unique. Together, the 6 octets form a globally unique 48-bit MAC address.
OUIs can be useful in troubleshooting — looking up an OUI can identify the vendor of an unknown MAC address seen on the network.

front.text: What does a host do with frames whose destination MAC does not match its own?

back.text: A device only processes a frame up the stack to Layer 3 if the destination MAC address matches its own MAC, is the broadcast MAC FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF, or is a multicast MAC the host has joined. Other unicast frames are silently discarded by the NIC and never consume CPU at higher layers.

front.text: Compare half-duplex and full-duplex Ethernet operation.

rows: No — one direction at a time
Effectively double — both directions at full rate

front.text: When you create VLAN 99 on a Catalyst switch with 'vlan 99' / 'name Guest', when is the VLAN actually committed?

back.text: The VLAN is not created until you exit VLAN configuration mode (return to global config or move to a different VLAN ID). Until that happens, the VLAN definition is pending and won't appear in 'show vlan' output.

SW1(config)# vlan 99
SW1(config-vlan)# name Guest
SW1(config-vlan)# exit  ! VLAN is now committed

front.text: Compare access ports and trunk ports.
What is the default trunk encapsulation on modern Catalyst switches, and what does it look like in 'show interfaces trunk'?

back.text: The default and effectively only trunk encapsulation on modern Catalyst switches is IEEE 802.1Q (sometimes shown as 'dot1q' or '802.1q'). The legacy Cisco proprietary ISL is no longer supported on most current platforms.

Port        Mode   Encapsulation  Status     Native vlan
Gi1/0/2     on     802.1q         trunking   1

front.text: What is the PCP field in an 802.1Q tag used for?

back.text: The Priority Code Point (PCP) is a 3-bit field in the 802.1Q tag that carries the Layer 2 Class of Service (CoS) value, ranging from 0 to 7. It is used by switches to apply Layer 2 QoS treatment, such as queuing and scheduling, between trunk-connected devices.
PCP is meaningful only on tagged frames — untagged traffic on access ports has no PCP value and must rely on Layer 3 markings (DSCP) for QoS.

front.text: What happens if two trunked switches have different native VLANs configured?

items: CDP/spanning-tree generates 'native VLAN mismatch' messages on syslog
Untagged traffic from one side is placed in the wrong VLAN on the other side
Spanning-tree behavior may become inconsistent
Potential security risk — frames effectively 'leak' between VLANs

back.text: Resolution: ensure both ends of the trunk use the same 'switchport trunk native vlan <id>' and ideally an unused VLAN (not VLAN 1).

front.text: How do you remove VLAN 50 from the allowed VLAN list of a trunk without affecting the other VLANs?

back.text:
interface Gi1/0/2
 switchport trunk allowed vlan remove 50

Avoid using 'switchport trunk allowed vlan 1,10,20' to 'just keep these VLANs' — that REPLACES the entire allowed list and silently removes any VLANs not listed. Use 'add' and 'remove' for safe incremental changes.

front.text: A virtualization team asks for a switch port that can carry traffic for 6 different VLANs to their hypervisor. What kind of port should you configure and why?

back.text: Configure the port as an 802.1Q trunk. Hypervisors run a virtual switch internally and can map VMs to different VLANs. The trunk allows multiple tagged VLANs to reach the hypervisor over a single physical port. Restrict the allowed VLAN list to only what the hypervisor needs.

interface Gi1/0/24
 description ESXi-host-1 trunk
 switchport mode trunk
 switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20,30,40,99,200

front.text: What optional filters can you use with 'show mac address-table'?

rows: Find one specific MAC across all ports
Show entries for a specific VLAN

back.text:
SW1# show mac address-table address aabb.cc00.0001
SW1# show mac address-table dynamic
SW1# show mac address-table vlan 10

front.text: Why do MAC and ARP entries 'age out', and what happens after they expire?

back.text: MAC and ARP tables have aging timers so entries are removed after a period of inactivity. This:

items: Frees memory for entries that are still active
Removes stale info if a host moves or changes its MAC/IP
Forces re-learning, which keeps tables accurate

back.text: After expiration, the next packet to that destination triggers re-learning: ARP for IP→MAC, or unknown unicast flooding for MAC table population.

front.text: In 'show interfaces status', what does the 'err-disabled' state mean and how do you recover?

back.text: 'err-disabled' means the switch detected a problem on the port (e.g., port-security violation, BPDU guard, DTP violation, link-flap, UDLD, storm control) and administratively disabled it to protect the network.

! Manual recovery
SW1(config)# interface Gi1/0/5
SW1(config-if)# shutdown
SW1(config-if)# no shutdown

! Automatic recovery
SW1(config)# errdisable recovery cause psecure-violation
SW1(config)# errdisable recovery interval 300

front.text: Trace the steps a host takes to forward a packet to another host on the same subnet.

items: Compare destination IP to local IP/mask → same subnet
Look in local ARP table for the destination IP
If no entry, send broadcast ARP request: 'Who has X.X.X.X?'
Destination host replies with unicast ARP reply containing its MAC
Source caches the IP-to-MAC mapping
Source builds frame: SRC MAC = own, DST MAC = destination host MAC
Frame is sent down to L1 and out the egress interface

front.text: Trace the steps a host takes to forward a packet to a host on a different subnet.

items: Compare destination IP to local IP/mask → different subnet
Determine next hop = default gateway IP
Look up default gateway IP in local ARP table (ARP if needed)
Build frame: SRC MAC = own, DST MAC = default gateway MAC
DST IP remains the original destination host's IP
Send frame to default gateway — gateway routes packet onward

front.text: Which command displays the ARP table, and what filters can be applied?

back.text: show ip arp [<mac-address> | <ip-address> | vlan <vlan-id> | <interface-id>]

items: By IP address — locate a single host's ARP entry
By MAC address — find which IP a MAC belongs to
By VLAN — see all ARP entries for a specific SVI/VLAN
By interface — see ARP entries learned via a specific port

front.text: When is configuring a secondary IPv4 address on an interface useful, and what is one risk?

back.text: Secondary IPv4 addresses let you put two IP networks onto the same physical broadcast domain. Common uses include:

items: Migrating from one IP subnet to another without an immediate cutover
Supporting overlapping legacy networks during integration
Adding capacity when a single subnet is exhausted

back.text: Risk: hosts in the secondary subnet must still ARP through the same Layer 2 segment. Misconfiguration can cause asymmetric routing, suboptimal forwarding, and broadcast/multicast complications.

front.text: Compare a routed switch port and a Switched Virtual Interface (SVI).

rows: Logical L3 interface tied to a VLAN
At least one port in that VLAN must be up

front.text: An SVI for VLAN 50 with an IP address shows status 'down/down' even though the VLAN exists. What are the likely causes?

items: No active access or trunk port carrying VLAN 50 is up
VLAN 50 not actually defined in the VLAN database
All ports in VLAN 50 are err-disabled or shut down
VLAN 50 is not in the allowed list on relevant trunks
Spanning tree has blocked all paths in VLAN 50

back.text: An SVI requires at least one VLAN member port in an up/up state to come up — this is the 'SVI autostate' behavior.

front.text: What does 'encapsulation dot1Q <vlan-id>' do on a router subinterface?

back.text: It tells the router that this subinterface processes traffic tagged with the specified 802.1Q VLAN ID on the parent physical interface (which must be connected to a trunk). Frames received with that tag are passed up to this subinterface; frames sent out are tagged with that VLAN ID.

interface Gi0/0/1
 no ip address
!
interface Gi0/0/1.10
 encapsulation dot1Q 10
 ip address 10.10.10.1 255.255.255.0

front.text: How do you handle native (untagged) VLAN traffic on a router-on-a-stick subinterface?

back.text:
interface Gi0/0/1.1
 encapsulation dot1Q 1 native
 ip address 192.168.1.1 255.255.255.0

The 'native' keyword on the encapsulation command tells the router that traffic on this VLAN arrives untagged. The native VLAN configured on the router subinterface must match the native VLAN configured on the connected switch trunk.

front.text: Why does 'show ipv6 interface brief' show a link-local FE80::/10 address even when only a global IPv6 address was configured?

back.text: Every IPv6-enabled interface automatically generates a link-local address in the FE80::/10 range. Link-local addresses are mandatory in IPv6 and are used for:

items: Neighbor Discovery (NDP) — equivalent of ARP
Router Advertisements and Solicitations
IPv6 routing protocol adjacencies (e.g., OSPFv3, EIGRPv6)
Next-hop addresses on point-to-point links

front.text: A router's CPU is at 80% during what should be a routine forwarding load. List configurations or features that can force traffic into the slow path (process switching).

items: Heavy use of IP options (record-route, source-route, timestamp)
ACLs with unsupported or complex matches not offloadable to hardware
Features like NBAR or some deep-packet inspection on older platforms
Disabled CEF (e.g., 'no ip cef')
Excessive ICMP-redirect or TTL-exceeded generation
Punted control-plane traffic (BGP/OSPF/SSH/SNMP) under attack
Glean adjacencies from a directly connected scan/sweep

back.text: Use 'show processes cpu sorted' and 'show platform' style commands to identify which process or feature is driving the load.

front.text: What does it mean for a FIB entry to be 'recursive', and why does this matter for static routes?

back.text: A recursive FIB entry has a next-hop IP that is not directly connected; CEF must perform another lookup to resolve which directly connected interface and adjacency to use.

items: Static routes pointing to a next-hop IP rather than an outgoing interface are recursive
Recursion uses FIB lookups efficiently in hardware on modern platforms
If the recursive lookup fails (next-hop unreachable), the route is removed from the FIB
Best practice on multi-access links: combine next-hop IP and outgoing interface, e.g., 'ip route 10.1.0.0 255.255.0.0 Gi0/0/0 10.99.99.1'

front.text: How do you verify and toggle CEF on a Cisco IOS router (when supported)?

back.text:
! Verify CEF state
R1# show ip cef summary
R1# show ip cef

! Globally disable / enable CEF (use with caution)
R1(config)# no ip cef
R1(config)# ip cef

! Per-interface
R1(config-if)# no ip route-cache cef
Disabling CEF is generally for troubleshooting only — almost all modern platforms rely on CEF for hardware forwarding. On many newer platforms CEF cannot actually be disabled.

front.text: A multilayer switch's CPU spikes whenever you add a long ACL. What is likely happening, and how do you confirm it?

back.text: If TCAM space for ACLs is exhausted, ACEs that don't fit in hardware are processed in software by the CPU, dramatically reducing performance. This is sometimes called ACL 'unloading' or 'punt'.

items: Check TCAM utilization with platform-specific commands (e.g., 'show platform hardware fed switch active fwd-asic resource tcam utilization')
Review SDM template with 'show sdm prefer'
Consider switching to an SDM template with more security ACEs
Simplify ACLs (combine, summarize, eliminate redundant entries)
Use object-groups to reduce ACE count where supported

front.text: A Catalyst 9300 is deployed as a pure access-layer switch with mostly Layer 2 and a few SVIs. Which SDM template is generally most appropriate?

back.text: The 'vlan' SDM template is typically appropriate. It optimizes TCAM allocation for a Layer 2-heavy access role:

items: More entries available for MAC tables and Layer 2 features
Fewer entries for advanced features like policy-based routing or microflow policers
Reasonable allocation for security and QoS ACEs

back.text:
SW1(config)# sdm prefer vlan
SW1(config)# end
SW1# reload

front.text: On a hardware-based platform, what is the relationship between software CEF and hardware CEF?

back.text: Software CEF runs on the route processor's CPU and maintains the master FIB and adjacency tables based on the RIB and ARP/ND. On hardware-based platforms, software CEF is NOT used to forward data-plane packets; instead, it programs the hardware FIB and adjacency tables in TCAM/ASICs/NPUs on the line cards. Actual packet forwarding happens entirely in hardware.
If a route or adjacency exists in software CEF but cannot be programmed in hardware (e.g., TCAM full), traffic for that destination is punted to the CPU and process-switched.

front.text: A user reports they cannot reach a server in another VLAN. Outline a structured troubleshooting approach using Layer 2 and Layer 3 forwarding concepts from this chapter.

items: Verify physical/data link: 'show interfaces status' on access port and uplinks
Confirm VLAN assignment: 'show vlan brief' and 'show interfaces switchport'
Verify trunk allowed VLANs and native VLAN: 'show interfaces trunk'
Check MAC learning of user/server: 'show mac address-table'
Confirm IP/mask/default gateway match the SVI/router (host side)
Verify ARP entries: 'show ip arp' on host and gateway
Verify routing: 'show ip route' for source and destination subnets
Verify CEF entries: 'show ip cef <dest>' and adjacency
Check ACLs/firewall rules in path
Use ping/traceroute end-to-end and from each hop

back.text: Always work bottom-up (L1 → L2 → L3) to avoid chasing higher-layer issues caused by a lower-layer problem.

front.text: What problem does Spanning Tree Protocol (STP) solve in a Layer 2 network?

back.text: STP prevents Layer 2 forwarding loops in topologies with redundant links by temporarily blocking redundant ports, while still allowing failover. Without STP, broadcasts and unknown unicast floods would loop endlessly because Ethernet frames have no TTL, causing MAC table instability, high CPU/memory usage, and possible switch crash.

front.text: List the major STP iterations and indicate which are IEEE standards vs Cisco proprietary.

items: 802.1D — original IEEE STP (one tree for all VLANs)
PVST — Cisco proprietary, per-VLAN STP
PVST+ — Cisco proprietary, per-VLAN STP with 802.1Q support
802.1w RSTP — IEEE Rapid Spanning Tree Protocol
802.1s MST — IEEE Multiple Spanning Tree Protocol

back.text: Catalyst switches today operate in PVST+, RSTP, or MST mode, all backward compatible with 802.1D.

front.text: List the 802.1D STP port states in the order a port transitions through them after coming up.

items: Disabled — administratively shut down
Blocking — receives BPDUs only, no forwarding
Listening — sends/receives BPDUs (15s, forward delay)
Learning — populates MAC table, no data forwarding (15s, forward delay)
Forwarding — forwards traffic and learns MACs

back.text: Total 802.1D convergence from blocking to forwarding ≈ 30 seconds with default timers. The Broken state indicates a configuration/operational problem.

front.text: Define the three 802.1D STP port types: Root Port (RP), Designated Port (DP), and Blocking Port.

items: Root Port (RP) — port on a non-root switch closest to the root bridge; one per VLAN per switch
Designated Port (DP) — port that forwards BPDUs/traffic on a segment; one DP per link/segment; all root bridge ports are DPs
Blocking Port — does not forward data; receives BPDUs only to prevent loops

front.text: What three components make up the STP Bridge ID, and how do system priority and sys-id-ext interact?

items: System priority — 4-bit value, default 32768
System ID extension (sys-id-ext) — 12-bit VLAN ID
System MAC address — switch base MAC

back.text: The advertised priority is system priority + sys-id-ext (VLAN). Example: VLAN 10 with default priority shows 32768 + 10 = 32778. This allows per-VLAN root election while keeping a single configurable priority field.

front.text: How does STP elect the root bridge, and what tiebreaker is used when priorities match?

items: Each switch initially assumes it is the root and advertises configuration BPDUs with itself as root.
Switches compare bridge IDs in received BPDUs to their own.
Lowest bridge priority wins.
If priorities tie, the lowest system MAC address wins.

back.text: Older switches often have lower MACs and may unintentionally become root. Tune priorities (e.g., spanning-tree vlan X root primary) to control placement.

front.text: Compare STP short-mode and long-mode default port costs for common interface speeds.

back.text: Short mode is 16-bit (ref 20 Gbps); long mode is 32-bit (ref 20 Tbps). Enable long mode network-wide with: spanning-tree pathcost method long

front.text: What are the two types of BPDUs in 802.1D, and what destination MAC do they use?

items: Configuration BPDU — identifies root bridge, root/designated/blocking ports; carries STP type, root path cost, root bridge ID, local bridge ID, max age, hello time, forward delay
Topology Change Notification (TCN) BPDU — sent by a switch toward the root to signal a topology change

back.text: Both BPDUs use the multicast destination MAC 01:80:C2:00:00:00.

front.text: What are the default values and purposes of the Hello, Forward Delay, and Max Age STP timers?

rows: Time spent in listening and learning states each
How long a bridge stores BPDU info if no updates received

back.text:
spanning-tree vlan 10 hello-time 2
spanning-tree vlan 10 forward-time 15
spanning-tree vlan 10 max-age 20

front.text: How is the root path cost calculated and updated as a configuration BPDU travels through the network?

items: Root bridge advertises BPDUs with root path cost = 0.
Outgoing BPDUs do NOT include the cost of the egress port.
Receiving switch adds the ingress port's STP cost to the cost in the received BPDU.
That sum becomes the switch's root path cost and is then advertised onward.

back.text: Cost is added on receive, not on transmit. The root bridge always reports cost 0 for itself.

front.text: List, in order, the tiebreakers used to select a switch's Root Port (RP).

items: Lowest cumulative root path cost
Lowest sender (advertising) switch system priority
Lowest sender (advertising) switch system MAC address
Lowest sender port priority (for parallel links to same switch)
Lowest sender port number (final tiebreaker)

front.text: Between two non-root switches sharing a link, how does STP decide which side becomes the Designated Port and which side blocks?

items: Port must be a designated port candidate (not the RP).
Switch with the lower root path cost forwards; the other blocks.
If costs tie: lower system priority wins.
If still tied: lower system MAC wins; the higher MAC switch's port goes to blocking.

front.text: Which command quickly shows the root bridge ID, root path cost, and root port for each VLAN?

back.text: show spanning-tree root
Output includes VLAN, Root ID (priority + MAC), Cost, Hello/Max Age/Forward Delay, and the local Root Port. If the Root Port column is empty, the local switch is the root for that VLAN.

front.text: Which command shows per-interface STP role, state, cost, and port type for a specific VLAN?

back.text: show spanning-tree vlan 10
Displays root bridge info, local bridge info, and a per-interface table with Role (Root/Desg/Altn), Sts (FWD/BLK/LRN), Cost, Prio.Nbr, and Type (P2p, P2p Edge).

front.text: Which command shows topology change counts and the last interface that triggered a TCN?

back.text: show spanning-tree vlan 10 detail

Look for: 'Number of topology changes X last change occurred HH:MM:SS ago from <interface>'. A rapidly increasing TCN count usually indicates a flapping access port — consider enabling PortFast on host-facing ports.

front.text: Which command narrows STP output to a single interface and shows BPDU sent/received counts per VLAN?

back.text: show spanning-tree interface gi1/0/1 detail

Useful for trunk troubleshooting: shows port path cost, port priority, designated root/bridge, link type, transitions to forwarding, and BPDU counters per VLAN. If a VLAN is missing, check the trunk's allowed VLAN list.

front.text: What sequence of events occurs when a non-root switch detects a topology change?

items: Detecting switch sends a TCN BPDU out its Root Port toward the root bridge.
Each upstream switch acknowledges and forwards the TCN toward the root.
Root bridge sets the Topology Change flag in its next configuration BPDU and floods it.
All switches reduce the MAC aging timer to forward delay (15s) to flush stale entries.
After the second BPDU, MAC aging returns to its normal value (default 300s).

back.text: Side effect: temporary increase in unknown unicast flooding while the MAC table rebuilds.

front.text: A non-root switch loses its Root Port link, but it has an alternate (currently blocking) port to a peer that still reaches the root. What is the convergence time using default 802.1D timers, and why?

back.text: ≈ 30 seconds.

items: Direct link failure is detected immediately, so Max Age (20s) is skipped.
Previously blocking port transitions to Listening (15s).
Then transitions to Learning (15s).
Then to Forwarding — total 30s.

front.text: A link stays physically up but BPDUs stop arriving (indirect failure). How long does 802.1D take to converge, and why is it slower than a direct failure?

back.text: ≈ 50 seconds.

items: Max Age (20s) must expire before cached BPDU info is flushed (link is still 'up').
Alternate port transitions to Listening (15s).
Then to Learning (15s).
Then to Forwarding — total 50s.

back.text: This slowness is a major reason RSTP and features like UDLD/loop guard exist.

front.text: Compare 802.1D port states to RSTP (802.1w) port states.

back.text: RSTP collapses Disabled/Blocking/Listening into a single Discarding state — three RSTP states vs five in 802.1D.

front.text: Define the four RSTP port roles: Root, Designated, Alternate, and Backup.

items: Root Port — best path toward the root bridge (one per non-root switch per VLAN)
Designated Port — forwards traffic on a segment (one per segment)
Alternate Port — backup path to the root via a different switch; replaces RP if RP fails
Backup Port — backup of a DP on the same shared segment (typical only with hubs)

front.text: What are the three RSTP port types, and what determines each?

items: Edge port — host-facing port with PortFast enabled; cannot form a loop
Non-Edge port — any port that has received a BPDU
Point-to-point port — full-duplex link to another RSTP switch (enables rapid handshake)

back.text: Half-duplex ports default to legacy 802.1D behavior because they suggest a shared (multi-access) segment.

front.text: Describe the RSTP proposal/agreement (sync) handshake between two newly connected switches.

items: Switches confirm the link is point-to-point (full-duplex).
Each sends a proposal claiming to be the DP for the segment.
The inferior switch (higher priority/MAC) accepts the superior as root.
Inferior switch marks its local port as RP and moves all non-edge ports to Discarding (sync).
Inferior switch sends an agreement BPDU back to the superior.
Both sides immediately move the RP/DP to Forwarding.
The inferior switch then repeats the proposal/agreement with its downstream neighbors.

front.text: Why does RSTP converge much faster than 802.1D?

items: Uses proposal/agreement handshake instead of timer-based listening/learning waits.
Ages out neighbor info after 3 missed hellos (~6s) instead of waiting for Max Age (20s).
Alternate ports are pre-computed and can take over almost immediately.
Edge (PortFast) ports skip the discarding phase entirely.

back.text: If a neighbor doesn't respond to the proposal, RSTP falls back to legacy 802.1D timers to remain safe.

front.text: What happens on an RSTP port if the neighbor does not respond to the RSTP handshake?

back.text: The port falls back to legacy 802.1D behavior on that link to prevent loops. End hosts (PCs/printers) without PortFast still experience the ~30-second listening + learning delay before traffic forwards. This is why edge/PortFast configuration on host ports is critical even in RSTP environments.

front.text: On a Catalyst switch, 'show spanning-tree interface gi1/0/1' shows 'Type *TYPE_Inc' next to a VLAN. What does this indicate, and what should you check?

back.text: It indicates a port-type or mode mismatch between this switch and its neighbor on that link.

items: Verify both sides agree on access vs trunk mode
Verify trunk encapsulation matches (dot1q on both sides)
Verify allowed VLAN list and native VLAN consistency
Verify P2P/edge port-type config is consistent on both ends

front.text: Users complain about intermittent slowness and 'show spanning-tree vlan 20 detail' shows TCN counts increasing rapidly. How do you locate the source?

items: Run 'show spanning-tree vlan 20 detail' and note the interface listed in 'last change occurred ... from <interface>'.
If it's a host-facing port, the access port is likely flapping — enable PortFast (or BPDU Guard) so its up/down events don't trigger TCNs.
If it's an inter-switch link, use CDP/LLDP to follow the link to the next switch.
Repeat 'show spanning-tree vlan 20 detail' on each upstream switch until you reach the originating port.
Check that port for physical issues (cable, SFP, CRC errors, duplex mismatch).

front.text: After installing an old switch in a closet, traffic patterns change unexpectedly. 'show spanning-tree root' shows the new switch as the root bridge for all VLANs. Why, and how do you fix it?

back.text: All switches use default priority 32768, so the lowest system MAC wins root election. Older switches frequently have lower MACs and become root by accident.

! On the intended root (e.g., a core/distribution switch):
spanning-tree vlan 1-4094 root primary
! Or set an explicit low priority:
spanning-tree vlan 1-4094 priority 4096

! On the intended secondary root:
spanning-tree vlan 1-4094 root secondary

front.text: In a network mixing 1G, 10G, and 100G links, RPs seem to choose suboptimal paths. What STP configuration change can help, and what is the deployment caution?

back.text: Switch from short-mode (default) to long-mode path costs so high-speed links are properly differentiated. In short mode, 10G/100G/1Tbps all collapse to cost 1–2, masking real bandwidth differences.
spanning-tree pathcost method long
Caution: every switch in the same Layer 2 domain must use the same path cost method, or root path calculations will be inconsistent and topology may become unstable. Audit and roll out network-wide.

front.text: Quick STP/RSTP recall facts for the ENCOR exam.

items: BPDU destination MAC: 01:80:C2:00:00:00
Default bridge priority: 32768 (advertised as 32768 + VLAN ID)
Default timers: Hello 2s, Forward Delay 15s, Max Age 20s
802.1D port states: Disabled, Blocking, Listening, Learning, Forwarding (5)
RSTP port states: Discarding, Learning, Forwarding (3)
Direct link failure 802.1D convergence: ~30s; Indirect failure: ~50s
RSTP ages BPDUs after 3 missed hellos (~6s)
Root bridge: lowest priority, then lowest MAC
Root Port: lowest cost to root, then lowest sender priority/MAC/port-priority/port-number
All ports on the root bridge are Designated Ports

front.text: Why is a Layer 2 forwarding loop more dangerous than a Layer 3 routing loop?

back.text: Ethernet frames have no TTL field, so a looping frame is never aged out. Broadcasts and unknown unicast floods circulate indefinitely, saturating links, destabilizing the MAC address table (constant port relearning), and driving switch CPU/memory toward exhaustion. Layer 3 packets, by contrast, are dropped when TTL reaches 0.

front.text: If STP blocks redundant ports, how does redundancy still provide value in a switched network?

back.text: Blocking ports are not down — they are administratively up but not forwarding data. STP keeps them in a discarding/blocking state ready for instant promotion to a forwarding role if the active path fails. Redundancy provides failover, not load sharing (in classic STP). Per-VLAN STP and MST are used to load-share across VLANs.

front.text: If a switch's configured priority is 24576 and it is advertising for VLAN 100, what value will appear in 'show spanning-tree vlan 100' as the bridge priority?

back.text: 24576 + 100 = 24676. The displayed priority is always system priority + sys-id-ext (VLAN). Bridge priorities must be configured in increments of 4096 because the lower 12 bits are reserved for the VLAN ID.

front.text: What command sets a switch as the root bridge for VLANs 1–10 without manually choosing a priority value?

back.text: spanning-tree vlan 1-10 root primary

This macro lowers the local priority below the current root's priority (typically to 24576, or lower if needed). Use 'root secondary' on the backup switch to set priority 28672. These values are NOT dynamically updated if the topology later changes.

front.text: What fields are carried inside a configuration BPDU?

items: STP type (protocol/version)
Root bridge identifier (priority + sys-id-ext + MAC)
Root path cost
Local (sending) bridge identifier
Port identifier (port priority + port number)
Message age
Max age
Hello time
Forward delay
Flags (including Topology Change and TC Acknowledgment)

front.text: Compare the Listening and Learning states in 802.1D — what does the port do or not do in each?

back.text: Both phases protect against loops while STP elects roles. Only after Learning does the port move to Forwarding.

front.text: SW1 (root) connects to SW2 via a 1G link. SW2 connects to SW3 via a 10G link. Using short-mode costs, what is SW3's root path cost on its RP toward SW1?

back.text: Short-mode cost: 1G = 4, 10G = 2.

items: SW1 advertises BPDU with cost 0.
SW2 receives on its 1G port and adds 4 → SW2 root path cost = 4.
SW2 advertises BPDU to SW3 with cost 4.
SW3 receives on its 10G port and adds 2 → SW3 root path cost = 6.

back.text: Remember: cost is added on the receiving interface, not the sending one.

front.text: SW2 has two parallel 1G links to the same upstream switch SW1. Both have equal cost. How does SW2 select the Root Port, and how can you influence it?

items: Cost ties → compare sender (SW1) system priority (same switch, ties).
Then sender system MAC (same switch, ties).
Then sender port priority — lower wins.
Then sender port number — lower wins.

back.text:
! On SW1, prefer Gi1/0/2 as the link SW2 uses as RP:
interface gi1/0/2
  spanning-tree port-priority 64
Port priority is configured on the upstream (advertising) switch, not on the local switch making the RP decision.

front.text: Which command shows a quick summary of the STP mode and per-VLAN root status across all VLANs on a switch?

back.text: show spanning-tree summary
Displays the running STP mode (PVST/Rapid-PVST/MST), portfast/BPDU guard defaults, and a count of VLANs in each STP role state. Useful first-glance health check.

front.text: Which command lists only the ports currently blocked by STP?

back.text: show spanning-tree blockedports
Quickly identifies redundant paths and confirms STP is doing its job. If unexpected ports are blocking — or none are — investigate the topology.

front.text: Which command shows STP ports that are in an inconsistent (error-disabled by STP guards) state?

back.text: show spanning-tree inconsistentports
Lists ports affected by Root Guard ('root inconsistent'), Loop Guard ('loop inconsistent'), or PVST/dispute conditions. These ports do not forward traffic until the inconsistency clears.

front.text: When a TCN-flagged BPDU is received, why does the MAC aging timer drop to forward delay (15s) instead of flushing the entire MAC table immediately?

back.text: Flushing the entire MAC table would cause massive unknown-unicast flooding for every host. Instead, lowering aging to 15s causes only stale entries (those not actively forwarding) to age out, while currently active hosts refresh their entries normally. After the topology stabilizes (second BPDU), aging returns to the default 300 seconds.

front.text: Why are TCNs scoped per-VLAN, and what operational impact does this have in networks with many VLANs and many hosts?

back.text: Each VLAN runs its own STP instance (in PVST+/RSTP), so TCNs and MAC table flushes occur per VLAN. The more hosts in a VLAN, the more devices affected by each TCN-driven aging change and resulting unicast flooding. Large flat VLANs amplify TCN impact — a key reason to keep broadcast domains small and enable PortFast on host ports.

front.text: What does PortFast do, and how does it relate to TCN generation?

items: Skips listening/learning and moves the port directly to forwarding on link up.
Marks the port as an RSTP edge port.
Suppresses TCN generation when the port goes up/down (host link flaps don't trigger network-wide MAC flushes).

back.text: Use PortFast only on access ports connected to end hosts — never on switch-to-switch links.

front.text: Why does a direct link failure where the failed link was already in a blocking state cause minimal data-plane impact?

back.text: Because the blocked port wasn't carrying traffic, no flows are interrupted. Both adjacent switches still send TCNs upstream so the topology updates and MAC tables reage, but the active forwarding path is unchanged. Convergence still occurs, but users don't notice it.

front.text: Why is STP slow to detect indirect failures (one-way link / unidirectional fiber), and what feature is commonly used to help?

back.text: STP relies on physical link state and BPDU timeouts. If the link stays 'up' but BPDUs are silently dropped in one direction, STP must wait the full Max Age (20s) before it considers the cached BPDU stale. UDLD (Unidirectional Link Detection) actively probes the fiber pair and err-disables the port when it detects a one-way failure, allowing STP to fail over much faster. Loop Guard is a complementary feature that prevents the alternate port from incorrectly transitioning to forwarding when BPDUs go missing.

front.text: In RSTP, what does the Discarding state replace from 802.1D, and what does the port do in this state?

back.text: Discarding replaces three 802.1D states: Disabled, Blocking, and Listening. In Discarding, the port may receive BPDUs (if not administratively down) but does not forward data frames and does not learn MAC addresses. Collapsing these three states simplifies the state machine and speeds convergence.

front.text: Compare an RSTP Alternate port and a Backup port — when does each appear?
What happens to an RSTP edge port if it ever receives a BPDU?

back.text: It immediately loses its edge status and is treated as a normal non-edge port (subject to RSTP synchronization, possibly transitioning through discarding/learning). To proactively protect edge ports against rogue switches, enable BPDU Guard so the port is err-disabled instead of just losing edge status.

front.text: How do you enable Rapid PVST+ globally on a Catalyst switch?

back.text: spanning-tree mode rapid-pvst
This runs RSTP per VLAN. Other valid modes: 'pvst' (legacy 802.1D per VLAN, default on many older platforms) and 'mst' (Multiple Spanning Tree, 802.1s).

front.text: Why does RSTP require a point-to-point (full-duplex) link to perform its rapid proposal/agreement handshake?

back.text: The handshake assumes only two endpoints on the segment so an agreement from one neighbor is sufficient to safely transition to forwarding. On half-duplex/shared media, more than two devices could share the segment, so a quick handshake is unsafe and RSTP falls back to legacy 802.1D timer-based behavior. You can manually force link type with: 'spanning-tree link-type point-to-point' on an interface.

front.text: How does BPDU generation differ between 802.1D and RSTP?

items: 802.1D: Only the root bridge originates BPDUs; non-root switches relay them. If hellos stop, downstream switches go silent.
RSTP: Every switch generates its own BPDUs every hello interval (2s by default), independent of receiving from the root.
RSTP ages out a neighbor after 3 missed hellos (~6s) instead of waiting for Max Age (20s).

back.text: This independent BPDU generation is what enables RSTP's much faster failure detection.

front.text: Compare topology change handling in 802.1D and RSTP.

rows: Switch that detects change → root bridge
Any non-edge switch that detects change
TCN sent up to root, root re-floods with TC flag
Switch directly floods TC BPDUs to all neighbors
Do NOT generate TCs (edge ports are excluded)
Immediately flush MACs on all non-edge ports except the receiving port

front.text: You enable Rapid PVST+ on a new distribution switch, but several access switches in the same VLAN still run legacy PVST+. Will the network work, and what is the convergence trade-off?

back.text: Yes — RSTP is backward compatible with 802.1D. On any link where RSTP detects an 802.1D neighbor (no agreement to its proposal), that specific port reverts to legacy timer-based behavior. RSTP-to-RSTP links keep their fast convergence; legacy links suffer the ~30–50s 802.1D delays. Migrate the rest of the switches to Rapid PVST+ to gain full benefit.

front.text: Traffic between two access switches is taking an unexpectedly long path through the core. Both switches have direct fiber between them. What STP-related cause should you investigate?

items: Run 'show spanning-tree vlan X' on both switches and check the Role of the inter-switch port. It may be Alternate/Blocking on one side.

Compare root path costs — the lower-cost switch keeps the DP, the other blocks. The 'short' direct link may have a higher cost than the multi-hop path through the core (e.g., 1G direct vs 10G via core).
Check whether 'spanning-tree pathcost method long' is configured consistently network-wide. Inconsistent path-cost mode causes asymmetric topologies.
Consider tuning port priority or path cost so the direct link is preferred.

front.text: CPU is at 99%, links are saturated with broadcasts, and MAC addresses keep moving between ports. What STP failure is occurring and what immediate steps stabilize the network?

back.text: Symptoms describe an active Layer 2 forwarding loop — STP has failed to block a redundant path.

items: Identify ports with extremely high broadcast/unicast rates: 'show interfaces counters' / 'show interfaces | include rate'.
Look for MAC flapping syslogs (HOST_MOVING / MAC flap).
Manually shut down the suspected redundant link to break the loop.
Verify STP is enabled on all switches and BPDUs aren't being filtered ('show spanning-tree summary', 'show spanning-tree interface ... detail').
Investigate root cause: BPDU filter misuse, unmanaged switch in path, broken EtherChannel, or unidirectional link.
Long term: enable BPDU Guard on edge ports, Loop Guard on uplinks, and UDLD on fiber.

front.text: Users in VLAN 30 lose connectivity intermittently while VLAN 10 works fine across the same trunks. STP shows VLAN 30 missing on one trunk. What is the likely cause?

items: Run 'show interfaces trunk' on both ends — VLAN 30 is probably not in the allowed VLAN list.
Run 'show spanning-tree interface <trunk> detail' — VLAN 30 won't appear if not pruned/allowed.
Verify VLAN 30 exists in the VLAN database on both switches.
Check VTP pruning behavior if VTP is in use.
Fix with: 'switchport trunk allowed vlan add 30' on both ends.

front.text: In a typical 3-tier campus design, where should the STP root bridge be placed and why?

back.text: The root bridge should be placed on a distribution-layer switch (or core, in a collapsed-core design), not on an access switch. Reasons:

items: Distribution switches have higher capacity and stable uplinks.
Access ports (with PortFast) shouldn't influence the spanning tree.
Predictable, deterministic forwarding paths align with traffic flows toward the core.
Use 'root primary' on the active distribution switch and 'root secondary' on its HSRP/VRRP peer for symmetry between L3 and L2 redundancy.

front.text: You have two distribution switches and many VLANs. How can you achieve uplink load balancing using only PVST+/Rapid PVST+ (no MST)?

back.text: Configure each distribution switch to be root for a different set of VLANs so that traffic for half the VLANs prefers Dist-A's uplink and the other half prefers Dist-B's uplink.

! Dist-A
spanning-tree vlan 1-100 root primary
spanning-tree vlan 101-200 root secondary

! Dist-B
spanning-tree vlan 101-200 root primary
spanning-tree vlan 1-100 root secondary
Pair this with HSRP/VRRP active gateways aligned to the STP root for symmetric forwarding and to avoid asymmetric paths.

front.text: Quick STP decision-tree recall for ENCOR exam questions.

items: Root bridge: lowest priority → lowest MAC.
Root Port (per non-root switch): lowest root path cost → lowest sender priority → lowest sender MAC → lowest sender port priority → lowest sender port number.
Designated Port (per segment): lowest root path cost on the segment → lowest bridge priority → lowest MAC.
All other ports between non-root switches: blocking/alternate.
Path cost is added when a BPDU is RECEIVED (on the ingress port), not when sent.
Bridge priority is configured in increments of 4096; advertised priority = priority + VLAN ID.
PortFast = edge port = no TCN on flap (host ports only).
RSTP = 3 states (Discarding/Learning/Forwarding), 4 roles (Root/Designated/Alternate/Backup).

front.text: What IEEE standard originally defined Spanning Tree Protocol, and what is its primary scope?

back.text: IEEE 802.1D defined the original STP. Its scope is a single spanning-tree instance for one VLAN/broadcast domain, building a loop-free Layer 2 topology by electing a root bridge and selectively blocking redundant ports. It is the foundation that RSTP (802.1w) and MST (802.1s) build upon.

front.text: Why does STP terminology use 'bridge' instead of 'switch', and are the two terms interchangeable?

back.text: STP was standardized when transparent bridges (slow, software-based, few ports) were the dominant Layer 2 device. Modern switches perform the same Layer 2 forwarding role at much higher speed and port density. In STP context, 'bridge' and 'switch' are interchangeable — you'll see both in commands, output (Bridge ID, root bridge), and documentation.

front.text: Where does a BPDU live in the Ethernet frame, and what does that imply for VLAN tagging?

back.text: BPDUs are sent as 802.3 LLC frames (LSAP 0x42) using destination MAC 01:80:C2:00:00:00 — they are NOT IP packets. In PVST+/Rapid PVST+, Cisco sends one BPDU per VLAN; on trunks, BPDUs for non-native VLANs are encapsulated in dot1Q tags using a Cisco SSTP (Shared Spanning Tree) format. The native VLAN's BPDUs are sent untagged. This is why native VLAN mismatches across a trunk are flagged by STP.

front.text: What is the default STP mode on modern Catalyst switches?

back.text: Rapid PVST+ (rapid-pvst) is the default on most modern Catalyst platforms (e.g., Catalyst 9000 series). It runs an RSTP instance per VLAN, providing both fast convergence (802.1w) and per-VLAN flexibility. Older platforms may default to legacy PVST+. Always verify with 'show spanning-tree summary'.

front.text: What makes one BPDU 'superior' to another during STP comparisons?

items: Lower Root Bridge ID (priority + sys-id-ext + MAC).
If equal, lower Root Path Cost.
If equal, lower Sender Bridge ID.
If equal, lower Sender Port ID (port priority + port number).

back.text: A 'superior' BPDU causes the receiving switch to update its information; an 'inferior' BPDU is ignored (in 802.1D) — though some features like Loop Guard treat the absence of expected BPDUs as significant.

front.text: Why must the STP bridge priority be configured in multiples of 4096?

back.text: The Bridge ID has a 16-bit priority field. The lower 12 bits were repurposed as the sys-id-ext (VLAN ID), leaving only the upper 4 bits (16 possible values) for the configurable priority. Each value of those 4 bits represents 4096, so valid priorities are 0, 4096, 8192, 12288, ... 61440. Default is 32768.
spanning-tree vlan 10 priority 4096

front.text: When you run 'spanning-tree vlan 10 root primary', what value does the switch actually configure?

items: If the current root's priority is greater than 24576: local priority is set to 24576.
If the current root's priority is 24576 or less: local priority is set to (current root priority − 4096) to ensure superiority.

back.text: This is a one-time calculation, not dynamic. If a new lower-priority switch joins later, the macro will not re-adjust — explicit priority values are more predictable for long-term designs.

front.text: Why does PVST+/Rapid PVST+ create scalability concerns in environments with many VLANs?

items: One STP instance per VLAN — each has its own BPDUs, timers, and state machine.
CPU and memory consumption scales linearly with VLAN count.
BPDU traffic on trunks scales with VLAN count (one BPDU per VLAN every 2s).
Convergence events affect all instances simultaneously.

back.text: MST (802.1s) solves this by mapping many VLANs to a small number of spanning-tree instances.

front.text: What is the default STP port priority on a Cisco interface, and what range can it be configured in?

back.text: Default port priority is 128. Valid range is 0–224 in increments of 16 (0, 16, 32, ..., 224). Lower is preferred.

interface gi1/0/1
  spanning-tree port-priority 64
  spanning-tree vlan 10 port-priority 64

front.text: How do you manually set the STP cost on a specific interface, either globally or per-VLAN?

back.text:
! Set cost for all VLANs on this interface
interface gi1/0/1
  spanning-tree cost 10

! Set cost only for VLAN 10
interface gi1/0/1
  spanning-tree vlan 10 cost 10
Useful when you want to influence root port selection without changing link speeds. Lower cost is preferred. Always verify with 'show spanning-tree interface <intf> detail'.

front.text: How does STP treat an EtherChannel bundle, and why is this beneficial?

back.text: STP sees the entire EtherChannel as a single logical port, not as multiple physical links. This means:

items: All member links forward simultaneously — no member is blocked by STP.
Member link failures don't trigger STP convergence (the bundle stays up).
Path cost is calculated based on the aggregate bandwidth of the bundle.
BPDUs are sent only on the bundle, not per member link.

front.text: Which command shows the local switch's bridge ID for each VLAN?

back.text: show spanning-tree bridge
Displays the local Bridge ID (priority + MAC) and timers per VLAN. Pair with 'show spanning-tree root' to compare local vs root identity — if they match, this switch is the root for that VLAN.

front.text: How can you display only active (forwarding/learning) STP ports and skip blocked ones in 'show spanning-tree' output?

back.text:
show spanning-tree active
show spanning-tree vlan 10 active
The 'active' keyword filters to ports where STP is operational. Helpful in switches with many shut/blocked ports to focus on the live topology.

front.text: Which debug command can help observe STP state changes and BPDU exchanges in real time, and what caution applies?

back.text:
debug spanning-tree events
debug spanning-tree bpdu
debug spanning-tree pvst+

Caution: BPDU debugs on production switches with many VLANs can flood the console and impact CPU. Use conditional debugs (e.g., 'debug condition vlan 10' or 'debug condition interface gi1/0/1') and 'logging buffered' to scope the impact.

front.text: What is the difference between the Topology Change Notification (TCN) BPDU and the Topology Change (TC) flag in a configuration BPDU?

rows: Notify root that a change occurred
Tell all switches to age MACs faster
Root bridge in its config BPDU
TCA flag set in upstream config BPDU

front.text: Why is reducing the 802.1D forward delay timer dangerous, and what is the recommended modern alternative?

back.text: Forward delay (default 15s) gives BPDU information time to propagate fully across a diameter-7 network before a port forwards traffic. Reducing it risks a port transitioning to forwarding before the topology has fully reconciled, which can produce a temporary loop.
Recommended alternative: deploy Rapid PVST+ or MST. RSTP achieves sub-second convergence through proposal/agreement handshake without changing safety-margin timers — far safer than tuning 802.1D.

front.text: What is STP diameter, and how is it related to the default timers?

back.text: STP diameter is the maximum number of switches a BPDU may traverse from the root to the most distant switch. Default 802.1D timers (Hello 2s, Forward Delay 15s, Max Age 20s) are designed for a diameter of 7. If your topology is larger, BPDUs may not propagate before timers expire, causing instability.

! Tune timers automatically by setting the diameter on the root
spanning-tree vlan 10 root primary diameter 4
Modern best practice: keep diameter ≤ 7, use RSTP/MST, and rely on hierarchical design rather than timer tuning.

front.text: If you change the hello time on a non-root switch in 802.1D, does it take effect?

back.text: No. In 802.1D, only the root bridge originates BPDUs and dictates the hello, forward delay, and max age values for the entire spanning tree. Non-root switches relay the root's timer values from received BPDUs. To change effective timers network-wide, configure them on the root bridge.
RSTP differs — every switch generates its own BPDUs at its local hello interval — but timers carried in the BPDU still come from the root for consistency.

front.text: During RSTP synchronization, why must the inferior switch place all its non-edge ports into discarding before sending the agreement?

back.text: Sending the agreement causes the new RP and the upstream DP to immediately begin forwarding. If any of the inferior switch's non-edge downstream ports were still forwarding old data, a transient loop could form between the new path and the old path. Discarding all non-edge ports first guarantees the switch is loop-safe before the rapid transition occurs. After sending agreement, sync propagates outward as each downstream link completes its own proposal/agreement exchange.

front.text: How does RSTP's independent BPDU generation help detect link failures faster than 802.1D?

back.text: Each RSTP switch sends its own BPDUs every hello interval (default 2s) on every active port. A neighbor expects to receive BPDUs at this rate. If 3 consecutive hellos are missed (~6s), the BPDU info is aged out and the port reacts immediately. 802.1D, by contrast, only ages out after the full Max Age (20s) because it's waiting for the relayed root BPDU to expire.

front.text: How does RSTP's MAC table flush behavior on a topology change differ from 802.1D's, and why is it more aggressive?

back.text: RSTP immediately flushes all dynamic MAC entries on all non-edge ports except the port that received the topology change BPDU. 802.1D instead lowers the MAC aging timer to 15s and lets entries naturally age out.

RSTP is more aggressive because rapid forwarding-state transitions could send traffic toward stale destinations within a few seconds. Aggressive flushing trades a brief unicast flood for guaranteed correctness at sub-second timescales — the tradeoff is acceptable because edge ports (PortFast) are excluded, sparing TCs from host link flaps.

front.text: How do you force an interface's RSTP link type, and when would you do this?

back.text:
interface gi1/0/1
  spanning-tree link-type point-to-point
! or
  spanning-tree link-type shared

Use point-to-point on full-duplex links (default for full-duplex) to enable rapid handshake. Force shared on hub-connected ports. Manually setting point-to-point on a half-duplex link is rare but possible — use only when you can guarantee only two endpoints exist on the segment.

front.text: How does an RSTP switch detect that its neighbor is running legacy 802.1D, and what is the consequence on that link?

back.text: When the RSTP switch sends an RSTP BPDU (version 2) and gets back an 802.1D-style BPDU (version 0), or doesn't receive an agreement to its proposal within the hello interval, it concludes the neighbor is legacy. The local port reverts to 802.1D timer-based behavior (listening/learning, 15s each) on that specific link only. Other RSTP-to-RSTP links keep rapid convergence. Note: even if the legacy switch is later replaced, the port stays in 802.1D mode until manually cleared with 'clear spanning-tree detected-protocols'.

front.text: After upgrading the last 802.1D switch to RSTP, your RSTP switches still use legacy 802.1D timers on those links. How do you fix this without bouncing ports?

back.text:
clear spanning-tree detected-protocols
clear spanning-tree detected-protocols interface gi1/0/1
This forces an immediate protocol re-negotiation on all (or specified) ports. Each port retries the RSTP handshake; if the neighbor now responds with RSTP, the link runs in RSTP mode going forward.

front.text: An RSTP link between two switches is unexpectedly slow to converge. 'show spanning-tree interface' shows Link type 'shared'. What's the cause and fix?

items: Shared link type means the port is operating half-duplex — RSTP cannot run its rapid handshake.
Run 'show interfaces gi1/0/1' to confirm duplex state.
Likely cause: duplex mismatch (one side hard-coded, other auto), bad cable, or speed mismatch forcing half-duplex.
Fix: ensure both ends auto-negotiate or both are hard-coded to the same speed/duplex (full).
After correcting, run 'clear spanning-tree detected-protocols' to re-evaluate.

front.text: After a network event, 'show spanning-tree root' on different switches shows different root bridge IDs for the same VLAN. What does this indicate?

back.text: The Layer 2 domain is partitioned — switches no longer share a single STP topology. Possible causes:

items: Trunk link down or VLAN removed from allowed list — the VLAN is split into two islands, each electing its own root.
BPDU Filter misconfigured on an inter-switch link, suppressing BPDUs.
Unidirectional link allowing data but blocking BPDUs in one direction.
VTP/VLAN inconsistency causing the VLAN to not exist on intermediate switches.

back.text: Verify trunks ('show interfaces trunk'), allowed VLAN lists, BPDU filter config, and physical link health. When the partition heals, the higher-priority island will yield root role to the lower-priority root.

front.text: An RSTP network sometimes takes 30+ seconds to converge after a link failure when it should take <1s. What three causes should you investigate?

items: Mixed STP modes: One or more switches running 802.1D/PVST forces RSTP-to-legacy fallback on those links.
Half-duplex / shared link type: RSTP can't perform rapid handshake on shared media.
Detected-protocols cache: A previously legacy switch was upgraded but ports didn't re-evaluate. Run 'clear spanning-tree detected-protocols'.
Bonus: PortFast not configured on host ports, causing TCNs and aggressive MAC flushing on every host flap.
Bonus: UDLD not enabled on fiber, allowing indirect failures to delay detection.

front.text: Why is it considered best practice to align the STP root bridge with the HSRP/VRRP active gateway on the same distribution switch?

items: STP determines the Layer 2 forwarding path; HSRP determines the Layer 3 default gateway.
If the STP root and active HSRP gateway are on different switches, traffic from access switches takes the L2 path to the STP root, then crosses an inter-distribution link to reach the active gateway.
This causes asymmetric traffic flow, wastes inter-distribution bandwidth, and makes troubleshooting harder.
Aligning both roles on the same distribution switch creates a single, deterministic forwarding path. The peer holds STP secondary + HSRP standby for failover.

front.text: Why does using switch stacking, VSS, or StackWise Virtual reduce reliance on STP convergence in a campus design?

back.text: These technologies present multiple physical switches as a single logical control plane and Bridge ID. This means:

items: Inter-switch redundant uplinks become Multi-chassis EtherChannels (MEC), seen as one logical link by STP — no blocking.
There are no STP-blocked redundant paths between distribution and access; all uplinks forward simultaneously.
Failover happens at the EtherChannel/stacking layer (sub-second) rather than via STP convergence.
STP remains as a safety net to prevent loops if cabling errors occur, but is not the primary convergence mechanism.

front.text: Memorize key STP convergence times for ENCOR exam questions.
Why is root bridge placement important in a properly designed Layer 2 network?

items: Minimizes hops between any switch and the root bridge
Aligns active forwarding paths with high-bandwidth core links
Reduces blocked links in undesirable locations
Provides predictable, deterministic convergence behavior
Aligns Layer 2 root with Layer 3 boundary (typically core/distribution)

back.text: Best practice: place the root on a core/distribution switch — never let access switches accidentally become root.

front.text: STP bridge priority can only be configured in increments of what value, and what is the valid range?

back.text: Increments of 4096. Valid range: 0 to 61,440 (0, 4096, 8192, 12288, ..., 61440). Default is 32,768. The lower 12 bits of the priority field are reserved for the sys-id-ext (VLAN ID), leaving only the upper 4 bits configurable.
spanning-tree vlan 1 priority 4096

front.text: Compare the priority values set by 'root primary' and 'root secondary' macros.

rows: Make this switch the root bridge

back.text: If another switch already has priority ≤ 24576, 'root primary' lowers itself further (current root priority − 4096) to ensure it wins. The macro is a one-time calculation, not dynamic.

front.text: What priority values are recommended to firmly lock down primary and secondary root bridges, and what additional feature should accompany them?

items: Primary root: priority 0 (lowest possible)
Secondary root: priority 4096
All other switches: leave at default 32768 (or higher)
Pair with Root Guard on designated ports facing downstream/access switches

back.text:
! Primary root
spanning-tree vlan 1-4094 priority 0

! Secondary root
spanning-tree vlan 1-4094 priority 4096

front.text: What does the optional 'diameter' keyword do in 'spanning-tree vlan X root primary diameter Y', and where does it need to be configured?

back.text: It tunes STP timers (hello, forward delay, max age) based on the maximum number of Layer 2 hops between the root and the most distant switch. Only configure it on the root bridge — timers propagate to all other switches via the root's BPDUs.
spanning-tree vlan 1 root primary diameter 4
Default diameter is 7. Reducing it shortens timers for faster convergence in smaller topologies.

front.text: True or false: The advertised path cost in a configuration BPDU includes the cost of the egress (advertising) port. Explain.

back.text: False.

The BPDU carries only the sender's accumulated root path cost. The receiving switch adds the cost of the ingress port to the value in the BPDU to compute its own root path cost. The root bridge always advertises a root path cost of 0.

front.text: How do you modify the STP port cost on a specific interface, and what's the effect of using the 'vlan' keyword?

back.text:
! All VLANs on this interface
interface gi1/0/1
  spanning-tree cost 1

! Per-VLAN cost
interface gi1/0/1
  spanning-tree vlan 10 cost 1

Without 'vlan', the cost applies to all VLANs traversing the interface. Lowering cost makes a path more preferred (turn alternate → designated/root); raising cost makes a path less preferred (turn designated → alternate/blocking).

front.text: When a switch has two equal-cost links to the same upstream switch, which side controls port priority and port number tiebreakers, and why?

back.text: The upstream (advertising) switch — the one closer to the root — controls both. The local switch making the RP decision uses the port priority/port number values it sees in the inbound BPDUs. To influence the decision, configure 'spanning-tree port-priority' on the upstream switch's interfaces, not on the local switch.
Lower port priority is preferred. Default is 128; valid range 0–224 in increments of 16.

front.text: How do you change the STP port priority on an interface, and what's the default value?

back.text:
interface gi1/0/6
  spanning-tree port-priority 64
  spanning-tree vlan 10 port-priority 64
Default is 128. Valid values 0–224 in increments of 16. Lower wins. Configure on the switch closer to the root (upstream) so that the downstream switch sees the preferred value.

front.text: What are the most common operational symptoms of an active Layer 2 forwarding loop?

items: CPU usage at 99% on switches
Memory exhaustion
MAC addresses constantly flapping between interfaces (syslog: %SW_MATM-4-MACFLAP_NOTIF)
Saturated links from broadcast/multicast amplification
Severe network slowdown reported by users
Switches potentially crashing

back.text: Investigate any MAC flap syslog immediately — it's an early indicator of a loop or a misbehaving NIC/load balancer.

front.text: List four common scenarios that can introduce a Layer 2 forwarding loop into a network.

items: STP disabled on a switch (intentional or accidental)
Misconfigured load balancer transmitting traffic out multiple ports with the same MAC
Misconfigured virtual switch bridging two physical NICs (vSwitches typically don't run STP)
End user plugging in an unmanaged switch or hub that creates a back-door path

front.text: What does Root Guard do, and what state does a port enter when it triggers?

back.text: Root Guard prevents a configured port from ever becoming a Root Port. If a superior BPDU is received on a Root Guard–enabled port, that port is placed into a 'root inconsistent' state, behaving like listening — it does not forward traffic. When the superior BPDUs stop, the port automatically recovers.

interface gi1/0/4
  spanning-tree guard root

front.text: Where in the network should Root Guard be deployed, and where should it NOT be deployed?

items: DEPLOY on designated ports facing switches that should never be root (e.g., distribution-to-access ports, ports facing partner/tenant networks)
DO NOT deploy on a switch's root port or on links facing the legitimate root bridge
DO NOT deploy on ports that may need to become root ports during a failover scenario

back.text: Root Guard protects the root bridge's identity downstream — it keeps rogue or misconfigured switches from hijacking the root role.

front.text: What two main benefits does STP PortFast provide on access ports?

items: Bypasses 802.1D listening and learning states — port goes directly to forwarding (helps DHCP, PXE, and slow-booting hosts)
Suppresses TCN generation when the port goes up/down (host link flaps don't trigger network-wide MAC table aging)

back.text: PortFast also marks the port as an RSTP edge port. If a BPDU is received, PortFast status is removed and the port behaves normally.

front.text: Show the commands to enable PortFast per-interface, globally for all access ports, and on a trunk port.

back.text:
! Per access interface
interface gi1/0/13
  switchport mode access
  spanning-tree portfast

! Globally for all access ports
spanning-tree portfast default

! Disable on a specific port (when global is enabled)
interface gi1/0/8
  spanning-tree portfast disable

! On a trunk port (use ONLY if connected to a single host, e.g., hypervisor)
interface gi1/0/20
  spanning-tree portfast trunk

front.text: When is 'spanning-tree portfast trunk' acceptable to use, and when is it dangerous?

back.text: Acceptable: trunk ports connecting to a single end host that needs multiple VLANs, such as a hypervisor with one NIC carrying VM traffic on different VLANs, or a server with 802.1Q-tagged virtual interfaces.
Dangerous: any trunk to another switch — the receiving switch could forward traffic before STP converges, creating a loop. Always pair with BPDU Guard on host-facing trunks for safety.

front.text: What does BPDU Guard do when a BPDU is received on a protected port?

back.text: It immediately places the port into the err-disabled state and generates a syslog message. The port stops processing all traffic. Recovery requires manual 'shut/no shut' or the err-disable recovery service for BPDU Guard.

%SPANTREE-2-BLOCK_BPDUGUARD: Received BPDU on port Gi1/0/2 with BPDU Guard enabled. Disabling port.
%PM-4-ERR_DISABLE: bpduguard error detected on Gi1/0/2, putting Gi1/0/2 in err-disable state

front.text: Show the commands to enable BPDU Guard globally on all PortFast ports and to override it per-interface.

back.text:
! Global — applies to all PortFast-enabled ports
spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default

! Disable on a specific interface
interface gi1/0/8
  spanning-tree bpduguard disable

! Enable explicitly on a specific interface
interface gi1/0/9
  spanning-tree bpduguard enable
Best practice: enable BPDU Guard on every host-facing access port that has PortFast.

front.text: How do you enable automatic recovery for ports err-disabled by BPDU Guard, and how do you change the recovery interval?

back.text:
errdisable recovery cause bpduguard
errdisable recovery interval 60   ! seconds (range 30–86400)

! Verify
show errdisable recovery
Default recovery interval is 300 seconds (5 minutes). Recovery only succeeds if the original cause has been resolved — if BPDUs are still arriving, the port will be err-disabled again immediately.

front.text: Compare Root Guard and BPDU Guard — trigger, action, and typical placement.

rows: Prevent unauthorized switches on access ports

front.text: How does BPDU Filter behave differently when enabled globally vs on a specific interface?

rows: Sends 10–12 BPDUs at port-up. If BPDUs received, port loses PortFast and behaves normally
Never sends or processes BPDUs on that port — STP is effectively disabled on the link

back.text: Caution: interface-mode BPDU Filter can silently create loops. Most designs do not need BPDU Filter — use BPDU Guard instead.

front.text: Why is BPDU Guard generally preferred over BPDU Filter on host-facing ports?

items: BPDU Guard treats any received BPDU as a security event and shuts the port (safe default)
BPDU Filter (interface mode) silently ignores BPDUs — a rogue switch could form a loop undetected
BPDU Filter adds complexity and risk without significant benefit in most designs
BPDU Guard preserves the loop-prevention guarantee STP is supposed to deliver

front.text: Why are unidirectional fiber links especially dangerous to STP, and what symptom do they produce?

back.text: Fiber uses separate strands for TX and RX. If one strand breaks, the link reports line-protocol up but BPDUs cannot traverse in one direction. The downstream switch eventually times out its cached root info (Max Age, 20s) and may transition a previously blocking port to forwarding — creating a Layer 2 loop because traffic now flows in a circle that STP can't see.
Two Cisco features address this: STP Loop Guard and UDLD.

front.text: What does STP Loop Guard do, what state does a port enter when triggered, and where should it NOT be enabled?

back.text: Loop Guard prevents a Root Port or Alternate Port from incorrectly transitioning to Designated/Forwarding when BPDUs stop arriving. The port is placed into 'loop-inconsistent' state until BPDUs resume, after which it automatically recovers.

! Globally
spanning-tree loopguard default

! Per-interface
interface gi1/0/1
  spanning-tree guard loop
Do NOT enable Loop Guard on PortFast (edge) ports — it conflicts with edge port logic.

front.text: Compare UDLD normal mode and aggressive mode.

rows: Marks the link as 'undetermined' but keeps the port active
Sends 8 more packets at 1-second intervals; if still unanswered, places port in err-disabled state

back.text: Aggressive mode is recommended on inter-switch fiber links where one-way failure must trigger a clean shutdown for fast STP failover.

front.text: Show the commands to enable UDLD globally (aggressive), disable it on one port, and verify status.

back.text:
! Globally enable UDLD aggressive on all SFP ports
udld enable aggressive

! Disable on a specific port
interface gi1/0/24
  udld port disable

! Per-port enable (overrides global default)
interface gi1/0/25
  udld port aggressive

! Recovery
udld recovery interval 30

! Verify
show udld neighbors
show udld gi1/0/1
Global UDLD only applies to SFP-based fiber ports. Both ends of the link must run UDLD for it to function.

front.text: Compare STP Loop Guard and UDLD — what does each protect against, and how do they detect a problem?

back.text: Best practice: deploy BOTH on inter-switch fiber for defense in depth.

front.text: Which command shows all STP ports currently in an inconsistent state (root, loop, etc.) and why each is inconsistent?

back.text: show spanning-tree inconsistentports

Output lists each VLAN and interface in 'Root Inconsistent' (Root Guard triggered) or 'Loop Inconsistent' (Loop Guard triggered) state. These ports are not forwarding traffic. Use this command to quickly diagnose protective-feature trips.

front.text: An access port keeps cycling between up and err-disabled. Syslog shows '%SPANTREE-2-BLOCK_BPDUGUARD'. What is happening, and how do you investigate?

items: BPDU Guard is err-disabling the port because BPDUs are arriving on a PortFast access port — something on the other end is sending BPDUs.
Likely causes: an unauthorized switch/hub plugged in, a misconfigured virtual switch bridging NICs, or a hypervisor with STP-emulation enabled.
Identify the device: 'show mac address-table interface gi1/0/X' to see what was learned before err-disable; check CDP/LLDP cache.
Physically locate and remove/reconfigure the offending device.
If err-disable recovery for BPDU Guard is enabled, the port will keep cycling every recovery interval until the source is removed.

front.text: You're hardening STP on a campus switching design. List the recommended baseline of STP-related features to enable, and where to apply each.

rows: Inter-switch trunks (RP/alternate ports) — NOT edge ports
Detect and shut one-way fiber failures
Auto-recover ports once cause is cleared

front.text: What design criteria should guide where you place the STP root bridge in a campus topology?

items: Place at the Layer 2/Layer 3 boundary (typically core or distribution)
Choose a switch with sufficient backplane/CPU to handle cross-switch traffic
Minimize maximum hop count to the furthest switch in the topology
Align with high-bandwidth uplinks so blocked links are predictable
Choose a stable, redundant switch — not access-layer or temporary equipment
Align with HSRP/VRRP active gateway for symmetric traffic flow

front.text: What three-step strategy ensures consistent root bridge placement in an enterprise design?

items: Lower the priority on the intended primary root to the lowest reasonable value (e.g., 0 or 4096).
Set the secondary root slightly higher (e.g., 4096 or 8192) so it takes over if primary fails.
Optionally raise priority on access/edge switches above default (e.g., 61440) to ensure they cannot accidentally win the election.

back.text: Pair with Root Guard on designated downstream ports to enforce the design at runtime.

front.text: When the diameter macro adjusts STP timers, why does it only need to be configured on the root bridge?

back.text: Hello, forward delay, and max age values are carried inside every configuration BPDU originated by the root. Non-root switches simply use the timer values they receive in the root's BPDUs — they don't honor locally configured timer values when they conflict with the root's. Configuring once on the root propagates timers consistently to every switch in the spanning tree.

front.text: SW3's Gi1/0/2 is currently an alternate (blocking) port. You want to convert it to a designated/forwarding port without changing physical cabling. What is the simplest tuning option?

items: Lower the STP cost on the link you want preferred (the new forwarding path) so the downstream switch sees a lower root path cost via that path.
Alternatively, raise the cost on the competing link so it loses the comparison.
Verify with 'show spanning-tree vlan X' — confirm the role changed from Altn/BLK to Desg/FWD on the intended port.

back.text:
interface gi1/0/1
  spanning-tree cost 1

front.text: How can per-VLAN STP cost manipulation be used to load-balance traffic across two parallel uplinks in a PVST+/Rapid PVST+ environment?

back.text: On the access switch, lower the cost on Uplink-A for half the VLANs and lower the cost on Uplink-B for the other half. Each VLAN's STP picks a different RP, so traffic for VLAN groups uses different physical uplinks while still providing failover.

! Uplink-A preferred for VLANs 1-100
interface gi1/0/1
  spanning-tree vlan 1-100 cost 1

! Uplink-B preferred for VLANs 101-200
interface gi1/0/2
  spanning-tree vlan 101-200 cost 1
Coordinate with root placement: distribution switches should be root for the VLANs that prefer their uplink — otherwise traffic crosses the inter-distribution link unnecessarily.

front.text: You see this syslog: '%SW_MATM-4-MACFLAP_NOTIF: Host 70df.2f22.b8c7 in vlan 1 is flapping between port Gi1/0/3 and port Gi1/0/2'. What does this indicate and what should you check first?

back.text: The same MAC address is being learned on two different ports rapidly — a classic indicator of a Layer 2 forwarding loop or a misbehaving end device.

items: Confirm STP is running on all switches in the VLAN: 'show spanning-tree summary' / 'show spanning-tree vlan 1'.
Look for blocked ports — if none exist where you'd expect, STP may be missing a redundant path.
Check the two ports for unauthorized switches, hubs, or virtual switches bridging NICs.
Verify no BPDU Filter is silently disabling STP on inter-switch links.
Check for an active load balancer or NIC teaming config that mistakenly sends from multiple ports with the same MAC.

front.text: What happens when superior BPDUs stop arriving on a Root Guard–protected port that is currently in root-inconsistent state?

back.text: The port automatically transitions out of root-inconsistent and rejoins normal STP operation — typically returning to its previous Designated Forwarding state. No manual intervention is required.
This is a key advantage over BPDU Guard, which leaves the port err-disabled until manually recovered or an err-disable recovery service intervenes.

front.text: A partner organization will connect a switch to your network for a temporary lab. You don't trust their STP configuration. How do you protect your root bridge?

items: Identify the port(s) connecting to the partner switch on your distribution/edge switch.
Apply Root Guard on those designated ports.
If the partner switch has a lower priority and tries to claim the root role, your local port goes root-inconsistent and stops forwarding.
Your existing topology and root bridge remain unaffected.
When the partner removes their misconfiguration (or the connection is torn down), the port automatically recovers.

back.text:
interface gi1/0/24
  description Partner uplink
  spanning-tree guard root

front.text: Why is suppressing TCN generation on host ports especially important in environments with many user devices?

back.text: Every link up/down on a non-PortFast port triggers a TCN, which propagates to the root and causes all switches in the VLAN to reduce their MAC aging timer to 15 seconds. With hundreds of users powering on/off PCs, IP phones rebooting, or laptops docking/undocking, TCN storms cause continuous unicast flooding and severely degrade network performance. PortFast eliminates these unnecessary TCNs by treating host ports as edge ports.

front.text: What happens to a PortFast-enabled port when it receives a BPDU (assuming BPDU Guard is NOT enabled)?

back.text: The port loses its PortFast/edge-port status and transitions through normal STP states (listening → learning → forwarding in 802.1D, or sync handshake in RSTP). It still functions in the topology but no longer skips state transitions. The port also resumes generating TCNs on subsequent flaps. Without BPDU Guard, this is a silent change — the only indication is in 'show spanning-tree' output where the port type changes from 'P2p Edge' to 'P2p'.

front.text: Which command shows which err-disable causes have automatic recovery enabled and lists ports currently waiting to recover?

back.text: show errdisable recovery

Output displays each err-disable reason (bpduguard, udld, link-flap, etc.), whether recovery is Enabled/Disabled, the current timer interval, and a list of interfaces currently waiting in err-disable with their remaining countdown.

front.text: How do you manually bring an err-disabled port back online without waiting for the recovery timer?

back.text:
interface gi1/0/2
  shutdown
  no shutdown
Always remediate the root cause first (e.g., remove the rogue switch). Otherwise the port will be err-disabled again immediately upon reactivation.

front.text: Why is interface-mode BPDU Filter considered dangerous, and what type of failure can it silently introduce?

back.text: Interface-mode BPDU Filter completely disables BPDU send and receive on that link — STP becomes blind to the segment. If a redundant path exists through that link (intentionally or accidentally), no switch will block any port on the segment, allowing a Layer 2 loop to form silently. Unlike BPDU Guard, there is no syslog or err-disable to alert the operator. Most modern designs avoid interface-mode BPDU Filter entirely and rely on BPDU Guard with PortFast for edge protection.

front.text: Loop Guard is enabled on SW2's Gi1/0/1 (its Root Port). BPDUs stop arriving on that interface. What does Loop Guard do, and how does this differ from default STP behavior?

rows: After Max Age, port may transition through listening/learning to forwarding (potential loop on unidirectional links)
Port enters Loop-Inconsistent state and stays blocking

back.text: Loop Guard treats the absence of expected BPDUs as suspicious rather than a reason to converge — protecting against unidirectional fiber failures.
