front.text: How can you identify ports placed in Loop-Inconsistent state by Loop Guard, and what does the output look like?

back.text: show spanning-tree inconsistentports

Name              Interface                Inconsistency
----------------- ------------------------ ------------------
VLAN0001          GigabitEthernet1/0/1     Loop Inconsistent
VLAN0010          GigabitEthernet1/0/1     Loop Inconsistent
Also visible in 'show spanning-tree' as 'BKN*' with '*LOOP_Inc' in the Type field. The port automatically recovers when BPDUs resume.

front.text: Describe how UDLD detects a unidirectional link using its echo handshake mechanism.

items: Local switch sends a UDLD packet containing its system ID and port ID.
Neighbor receives the packet and echoes back the local info plus its own system ID and port ID.
Local switch verifies its own info appears in the echo — confirming bidirectional communication.
If echoes stop arriving (one-way fiber), Normal mode marks the link 'undetermined'; Aggressive mode sends 8 more packets at 1-sec intervals.
If still no echo in Aggressive mode, the port is err-disabled.

front.text: Which UDLD command quickly shows neighbor state across all UDLD-enabled ports, and what state indicates a healthy fiber link?

back.text: show udld neighbors

Healthy state: 'Bidirectional'. Other states include 'Unknown' (no neighbor yet), 'Undetermined' (Normal mode missed echo), and 'Shutdown' (Aggressive mode err-disabled). For per-port detail use 'show udld <interface>'.

front.text: On a fiber inter-switch link, you have two competing options: UDLD aggressive vs STP Loop Guard. Why is deploying both recommended?

items: UDLD detects physical-layer one-way failures even when STP isn't running on the segment, and acts at the protocol layer (err-disable) regardless of STP state.
Loop Guard protects against the STP-specific scenario where BPDUs stop but link stays up — works even if UDLD is disabled or fails.
Defense in depth: a UDLD bug or misconfig won't leave the network exposed if Loop Guard is also active.
They have different recovery behaviors (UDLD = err-disable, Loop Guard = inconsistent state) — together they cover both fast shutdown and graceful auto-recovery.

front.text: How do you configure PortFast on a trunk port connecting to a hypervisor, and what additional protection should accompany it?

back.text:
interface gi1/0/20
  description ESXi host trunk
  switchport mode trunk
  switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20,30
  spanning-tree portfast trunk
  spanning-tree bpduguard enable
Always pair PortFast Trunk with BPDU Guard — if the hypervisor is later replaced with another switch, BPDU Guard prevents an immediate loop by err-disabling the port.

front.text: Which single command shows the global STP mode and which protective features (PortFast, BPDU Guard, BPDU Filter, Loop Guard, UDLD) are enabled by default?

back.text: show spanning-tree summary

Output shows the running STP mode (e.g., 'Switch is in rapid-pvst mode'), and global toggles: 'PortFast Default: edge', 'BPDU Guard Default: enabled', 'BPDU Filter Default: disabled', 'Loopguard Default: disabled', 'UplinkFast: disabled', 'BackboneFast: disabled'. Per-VLAN summary follows.

front.text: How do BPDU Guard and BPDU Filter (global mode) compare in their reaction when a BPDU is received on an edge port?

rows: Port loses PortFast status, behaves as a normal STP port (silent)

back.text: BPDU Guard is the safer default — it makes unauthorized switch insertion immediately visible, while BPDU Filter silently absorbs it.

front.text: Which command verifies BPDU Guard, PortFast, and BPDU Filter status on a specific interface?

back.text: show spanning-tree interface gi1/0/13 detail
Look for lines like 'The port is in the portfast mode', 'Bpdu guard is enabled', 'Bpdu filter is enabled'. Also displays BPDU sent/received counters useful for confirming filter/guard behavior.

front.text: After a power event, an entire access-layer closet has poor performance. 'show spanning-tree' shows the access switches now consider one of THEMSELVES the root for several VLANs. What happened?

items: The Layer 2 path back to the real root is broken (uplink failure, fiber issue, or distribution switch down).
With no superior BPDUs arriving, each access switch elects itself root for the affected VLANs.
Sub-optimal traffic now traverses the access layer instead of the high-bandwidth core.
Investigate uplinks: 'show interfaces status', 'show cdp neighbors', UDLD/Loop Guard state, and physical fiber.
If the distribution switch is up but its uplink port is in Loop-Inconsistent or Root-Inconsistent state, check Loop Guard / Root Guard triggers.
Restore the path; root election will return to the configured primary root automatically.

front.text: A user complains their PC takes ~30 seconds before getting a DHCP address after plugging in. The access port is configured correctly. What STP-related setting is likely missing?

back.text: PortFast is not enabled on the access port. Without PortFast, the port spends 15 seconds in listening + 15 seconds in learning before forwarding — the PC sends DHCP discovers during this window but they're dropped because the port isn't forwarding yet.

interface gi1/0/13
  switchport mode access
  switchport access vlan 10
  spanning-tree portfast
  spanning-tree bpduguard enable

front.text: Why must Loop Guard NOT be enabled on PortFast/edge ports?

back.text: Edge ports are not expected to receive BPDUs at all — they connect to end hosts. Loop Guard's purpose is to act when expected BPDUs go missing on RP/Alternate ports. Enabling Loop Guard on an edge port would cause it to enter Loop-Inconsistent state because no BPDUs would ever arrive, breaking host connectivity entirely. The two features serve opposite use cases and are mutually exclusive in practice.

front.text: Why is UDLD primarily intended for fiber links rather than copper?

back.text: Copper Ethernet uses auto-negotiation and pulse signaling that detects most one-way failures at the physical layer — the link comes down on either side. Fiber, in contrast, has independent TX and RX strands that can fail individually while the line protocol stays up. UDLD's echo handshake is essential for fiber to detect this hidden one-way state. The 'udld enable' global command only activates UDLD on SFP/fiber ports by default; copper requires explicit per-port enabling.

front.text: What's the tradeoff between enabling automatic err-disable recovery vs leaving ports manually recovered?

rows: Self-healing after transient issues; less helpdesk load; ports come back if user removes rogue device
Hides repeating issues; may oscillate up/down; can mask security events
Forces operator awareness; clearer audit trail; no oscillation
User downtime until ticket is processed; high admin overhead at scale

back.text: Common compromise: enable auto recovery with a reasonable interval (e.g., 60–300s) and ensure syslog/SNMP traps notify operators of every err-disable event.

front.text: Design exercise: in a 3-tier campus (core, distribution, access), where would you apply each major STP tuning feature?

rows: Priority 0 / 4096; Loop Guard on inter-switch trunks; UDLD aggressive on fiber
Root Guard on designated ports facing access; Loop Guard on RP/alternate; UDLD on fiber
PortFast + BPDU Guard on host ports; high priority (e.g., 61440) so access never becomes root

front.text: Summarize the key 'do not combine' rules among STP tuning features.

items: Loop Guard ⛔ PortFast/Edge ports — Loop Guard expects BPDUs; edge ports won't receive them
Root Guard ⛔ on the actual Root Port — would block legitimate root path
BPDU Filter (interface) + Root/Loop Guard — Filter suppresses the BPDUs that Guards rely on
BPDU Guard ⚠ trunks to other switches — would err-disable normal trunk operation
PortFast ⚠ inter-switch links — bypasses listening/learning, can cause transient loops

back.text: Generally: Edge port = PortFast + BPDU Guard. Inter-switch port = Loop Guard + UDLD (and Root Guard where appropriate).

front.text: Quick recall: which STP feature triggers on which condition, and what state does the port enter?

rows: Any BPDU received on PortFast port

front.text: What does 'root-inconsistent' mean operationally for a port, and how is it different from err-disabled?
When Loop Guard places a port in loop-inconsistent state, does the port still send BPDUs, and does it still receive them?

back.text: The port stops forwarding data and behaves as if blocked, but the switch still listens for incoming BPDUs on it. As soon as a valid BPDU arrives again, the port automatically transitions out of loop-inconsistent and rejoins the spanning tree in its proper role (Root or Alternate). This auto-recovery is what makes Loop Guard safer than relying on Max Age expiry.

front.text: You enable Root Guard on a distribution-to-access trunk. The access switch is later swapped for a switch with a much lower priority. What happens?

items: The new access switch advertises a superior BPDU toward the distribution switch.
Root Guard on the distribution port detects the superior BPDU.
The distribution port is placed in root-inconsistent state and stops forwarding.
The access switch loses uplink connectivity to that distribution switch via this link.
Operator should investigate and either re-prioritize the access switch (raise its priority) or remove the rogue/misconfigured device.
Once the superior BPDUs stop, the port automatically returns to designated/forwarding.

front.text: Summarize which STP protection features have global commands vs per-interface commands and the typical default deployment pattern.
Why does deploying StackWise / VSS / StackWise Virtual reduce — but not eliminate — the need for STP tuning features?

items: Multi-chassis EtherChannels remove most STP-blocked uplinks because both physical switches act as one logical switch.
Failover happens at the EtherChannel/stack layer, not via STP convergence.
However, STP must remain enabled as a safety net for accidental loops (mis-cabling, rogue switches).
PortFast + BPDU Guard on host ports is still required to suppress TCNs and protect edges.
Root Guard, Loop Guard, and UDLD still useful where non-stacked switches or external links exist.
Bottom line: stacking simplifies the data path but does not replace STP hardening.

front.text: Two access switches share a direct 1G link AND each has a 10G uplink to the same distribution switch. STP blocks the direct 1G link. You want to use it for a specific low-priority VLAN to offload traffic from the core. How do you tune?

items: Identify the VLAN you want to ride the 1G direct link (e.g., guest VLAN 200).
On the distribution switch's port toward one access switch, raise the per-VLAN cost for VLAN 200 so the access switch prefers the 1G direct path:

interface gi1/0/1
      spanning-tree vlan 200 cost 200
Verify on the access switch with 'show spanning-tree vlan 200' that the direct 1G link is now Root/Forwarding for VLAN 200, while other VLANs still use the 10G uplink.
Caveat: this trades core bandwidth for direct path; ensure 1G is sufficient for VLAN 200 traffic.

front.text: List the recommended order to apply STP hardening features in a brownfield network without causing outages.

items: Audit current STP mode and root placement: 'show spanning-tree summary', 'show spanning-tree root'.
Set explicit priority on intended primary/secondary roots BEFORE other changes.
Migrate to Rapid PVST+ (or MST) if still on legacy PVST+ — confirm convergence improves.
Add Root Guard on designated ports facing access/partner switches.
Add PortFast + BPDU Guard on access ports (start with one closet to validate).
Add Loop Guard on inter-switch trunks (NOT edge ports).
Add UDLD aggressive on fiber inter-switch links.
Configure errdisable recovery for safe transient causes (link-flap, bpduguard).
Document and monitor: enable syslog and SNMP traps for STP state changes.

front.text: Which sequence of show commands validates an STP-hardened design after configuration?

items: show spanning-tree summary — confirm mode, defaults, feature enablement
show spanning-tree root — confirm correct root for each VLAN
show spanning-tree vlan X — confirm RPs, designated ports, blocked ports match design
show spanning-tree inconsistentports — confirm no unexpected guard trips
show spanning-tree interface <intf> detail — confirm portfast/bpduguard/root-guard on key ports
show udld neighbors — confirm bidirectional state on all fiber
show errdisable recovery — confirm recovery causes/intervals as expected

front.text: On modern Catalyst IOS XE, what's the difference between 'spanning-tree portfast edge' and 'spanning-tree portfast network'?

rows: Skip listening/learning; mark as RSTP edge port; no TCN on flap
Marks port as 'network' type; enables Bridge Assurance to detect missing BPDUs

back.text: 'edge' is the modern keyword equivalent to legacy plain 'portfast'. 'network' is paired with Bridge Assurance for inter-switch links — a separate hardening feature.

front.text: What does Bridge Assurance do, and how does it complement Loop Guard / UDLD on inter-switch links?

back.text: Bridge Assurance causes BPDUs to be sent on ALL operational ports configured as 'network' type — including designated AND blocked ports. If a port stops receiving BPDUs from its neighbor, it is moved to BA-inconsistent (blocking). This protects against scenarios where a neighbor's STP process has stopped (e.g., software bug) but the link is still up and forwarding. Both ends must support and enable Bridge Assurance for it to function.

interface gi1/0/1
  spanning-tree portfast network

front.text: An engineer enables 'spanning-tree portfast trunk' on an inter-switch trunk by mistake. What risk does this introduce, and how would you detect/fix it?

items: Risk: the trunk skips listening/learning at startup, potentially creating a transient loop before STP converges.
Risk: if the link flaps, no TCN is generated, so MAC tables don't refresh and traffic may blackhole briefly.
Detect: 'show spanning-tree interface <trunk> detail' shows 'The port is in the portfast mode' on a trunk to a switch.
Detect: 'show spanning-tree' shows port type 'P2p Edge' on an inter-switch trunk — should be plain 'P2p'.
Fix: 'no spanning-tree portfast' on the interface; pair inter-switch trunks with Loop Guard / UDLD instead.

front.text: Which command shows MAC address movements (flapping) and helps confirm a Layer 2 loop or NIC issue?

back.text:
show mac address-table notification mac-move
show logging | include MACFLAP
Look for repeated %SW_MATM-4-MACFLAP_NOTIF entries with the same MAC oscillating between two ports. Investigate immediately — this is one of the strongest indicators of an active forwarding loop.

front.text: How does storm-control complement STP protection features, and what does it actually do?

back.text: Storm-control monitors broadcast, multicast, and/or unknown-unicast traffic rates on a port and drops or shuts the port when a configured threshold is exceeded. It limits the damage during a Layer 2 loop or broadcast storm BEFORE STP can converge — buying time and preventing a single misbehaving device from saturating the network.

interface gi1/0/13
  storm-control broadcast level 1.00
  storm-control multicast level 5.00
  storm-control action shutdown
STP prevents loops; storm-control limits the blast radius if one occurs. Use both.

front.text: What does the bottom of 'show spanning-tree summary' show beyond global feature defaults?

back.text: It lists per-VLAN totals: Blocking, Listening, Learning, Forwarding, and STP Active port counts, plus a grand total at the bottom. A sudden change in these counts (e.g., many more Forwarding ports than expected) is a quick indicator that STP topology has changed unexpectedly.

front.text: Which command shows which conditions can err-disable a port, regardless of whether recovery is enabled?

back.text: show errdisable detect

Output lists every cause (bpduguard, udld, link-flap, dhcp-rate-limit, security-violation, etc.) and shows whether detection is enabled and the action mode (port shutdown vs port per-vlan disable). Pair with 'show errdisable recovery' to see which causes auto-recover.

front.text: Why is PortFast typically required on access ports configured with a voice VLAN, and how does BPDU Guard interact with the IP phone?

back.text: IP phones boot via DHCP/TFTP and need the port forwarding immediately — without PortFast, the 30-second listening/learning delay would prevent the phone from registering. The IP phone is a passthrough device and does NOT generate BPDUs, so BPDU Guard remains safe. If a user plugs a switch into the phone's PC port, BPDU Guard will err-disable the access port — exactly the desired protection.

interface gi1/0/13
  switchport mode access
  switchport access vlan 10
  switchport voice vlan 110
  spanning-tree portfast
  spanning-tree bpduguard enable

front.text: Roughly how does the 'diameter' macro adjust default STP timers when reduced from 7 to 4?

back.text: Reducing diameter speeds up convergence in smaller topologies but is risky in 802.1D — RSTP achieves faster convergence safely without timer manipulation. Most modern designs leave diameter at default and use RSTP/MST instead.

front.text: Why is global Loop Guard ('spanning-tree loopguard default') generally safe to enable network-wide?

back.text: The global command applies Loop Guard ONLY to point-to-point ports that are running STP — not to PortFast/edge ports, which are already excluded by definition. So a typical campus deployment with PortFast on access and trunks elsewhere can safely enable Loop Guard globally and gain protection on every inter-switch link without any per-interface configuration.
Verify with 'show spanning-tree' — only RP/alternate ports on inter-switch trunks should show loop-guard active.

front.text: What is the default UDLD message interval, and how do you tune it for faster detection?

back.text: Default UDLD message interval is 15 seconds. Detection of a unidirectional link in normal mode therefore takes around 45 seconds (3 missed messages). Aggressive mode uses additional 1-second probes after the first miss, detecting a one-way failure in approximately 8–10 seconds.
udld message time 7   ! seconds (range 7–90)
On critical fiber, UDLD aggressive + lowered message time provides sub-30s detection. Always pair with Loop Guard for defense in depth.

front.text: After a tuning exercise, you want to confirm that no leftover STP cost or priority overrides remain on a switch. How can you verify quickly?

back.text:
show running-config | include spanning-tree
show running-config | section interface

Look for stale per-interface 'spanning-tree cost', 'spanning-tree port-priority', and per-VLAN overrides. To revert: 'no spanning-tree cost', 'no spanning-tree port-priority', or 'default interface <intf>' to clean a port back to baseline. Always 'show spanning-tree' after to confirm the topology re-converges as expected.

front.text: How does an RSTP switch automatically detect edge port status, and why is explicit PortFast configuration still recommended?

back.text: RSTP infers edge status if a port doesn't receive any BPDUs within a short timeout after coming up. However, this auto-detection delays the first traffic flow until the timeout expires. Explicitly configuring PortFast (edge) on access ports skips even that delay, providing immediate forwarding from link up. It also makes intent visible in configuration and ensures BPDU Guard semantics behave predictably.

front.text: If PortFast is enabled on every host port in a VLAN, what is the operational benefit during many simultaneous host flaps (e.g., overnight PC shutdowns)?

items: No TCNs generated by host port flaps → no MAC aging timer reduction across the VLAN
MAC table entries age normally (300s default) for active hosts
No unicast-flood spike from prematurely aged-out entries
Inter-switch trunk BPDU traffic remains stable
CPU and memory usage on switches stays predictable through high-flap windows

back.text: This is the single biggest operational win of PortFast in large user-access environments.

front.text: Do STP tuning features (Root Guard, BPDU Guard, Loop Guard, UDLD) still apply when running MST instead of Rapid PVST+?

back.text: Yes — these features are mode-independent and operate identically under MST, Rapid PVST+, or PVST+. Configuration syntax is the same. The only difference is that MST groups VLANs into instances, so root selection (priority/macros) is per-instance rather than per-VLAN. Protective features still trigger on superior BPDUs, missing BPDUs, or rogue BPDUs regardless of mode.

front.text: An inter-switch fiber link sporadically goes err-disabled and recovers a few minutes later. UDLD aggressive is enabled. What likely causes this pattern, and what should you investigate?

items: Pattern suggests intermittent unidirectional behavior — UDLD detects loss of echo, err-disables, then errdisable recovery brings it back, then it fails again.
Investigate the physical fiber: clean connectors (most common cause), check for fiber bend radius, replace patch cables.
Check optical levels: 'show interfaces transceiver detail' — look for low/marginal RX power on one strand.
Inspect SFPs on both ends — a failing transceiver may produce intermittent one-way RX.
Check for environmental issues: temperature, vibration in the cable path.
Temporarily switch UDLD to 'normal' mode to confirm — if the link works without err-disable, the unidirectional condition is real.

front.text: You've inherited a network. What three STP audit checks reveal the most about its hardening posture?

items: Where is the root for each VLAN? ('show spanning-tree root' across switches) — is it where it should be? Is it consistent? Any access switches as root unintentionally?

Are protective features deployed? ('show spanning-tree summary' globally; 'show spanning-tree interface ... detail' on edge/trunk samples) — PortFast + BPDU Guard on access? Loop Guard + UDLD on trunks? Root Guard on relevant ports?
Are there any inconsistent or err-disabled ports? ('show spanning-tree inconsistentports', 'show interfaces status err-disabled') — these are unresolved alarms hiding in plain sight.

front.text: Provide a complete sample interface configuration for a hardened user access port and a hardened inter-switch trunk port.

back.text:
! Hardened access port (host-facing)
interface gi1/0/13
  description USER ACCESS
  switchport mode access
  switchport access vlan 10
  switchport voice vlan 110
  spanning-tree portfast
  spanning-tree bpduguard enable
  storm-control broadcast level 1.00
  storm-control action shutdown
  no shutdown

! Hardened inter-switch trunk
interface gi1/0/24
  description UPLINK to DIST-SW1
  switchport mode trunk
  switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20,30
  spanning-tree guard loop
  udld port aggressive
  no shutdown

front.text: What key fields should you check in 'show udld <interface>' output to confirm a healthy fiber link?

items: Port enable operational state: Enabled
Current bidirectional state: Bidirectional
Current operational state: Advertisement - Single neighbor detected
Neighbor entry shows: Device ID, Port ID, and Neighbor echo device/port (your own info echoed back)
Message interval and Time out interval consistent with config

back.text: Any state other than Bidirectional needs investigation. 'Unknown' = no neighbor; 'Undetermined' = missed echoes (Normal mode); 'Shutdown' = Aggressive mode err-disabled the port.

front.text: Beyond PortFast, what other operational practices reduce the rate of TCN events and their impact in a switched network?

items: Keep VLAN broadcast domains small — fewer hosts per VLAN = less unicast flooding when MAC aging is reduced
Use Rapid PVST+ or MST — RSTP excludes edge ports from TC generation entirely
Disable unnecessary debug events; monitor 'show spanning-tree vlan X detail' for TCN spikes
Use stable hardware — flapping cables/SFPs are a primary TCN source
Leverage stack/MEC to eliminate STP-blocked redundant uplinks (no STP convergence needed for failover)
Apply errdisable detection on link-flap to quarantine chronically flapping ports

front.text: Quick decision: which STP feature do you reach for in each scenario?

rows: Force a specific switch to be root
Prevent any other switch from becoming root
Prevent end users from plugging in switches
Speed up access port forwarding for hosts
Prevent loops when BPDUs unexpectedly stop
Pick which redundant link is RP

front.text: Master recall list: command syntax for every major STP tuning feature on the ENCOR exam.

rows: spanning-tree vlan X root {primary | secondary} [diameter Y]

errdisable recovery cause bpduguard
errdisable recovery interval <30..86400>

front.text: What two primary problems does Multiple Spanning Tree Protocol (MST/802.1s) solve?

items: Enables traffic load balancing across redundant links by allowing different VLANs to use different STP topologies (similar to PVST+).
Reduces CPU and memory burden in environments with many VLANs by mapping multiple VLANs to a small number of STP instances rather than one instance per VLAN.

back.text: MST gives PVST+'s flexibility with CST's efficiency — best of both worlds.

front.text: Compare CST, PVST+, and MST in terms of STP instances and load balancing.

rows: Configurable; many VLANs map to few instances

front.text: What is an MST Instance (MSTI), and what are VLANs directly associated with in MST?

back.text: An MSTI is a single spanning-tree instance that one or more VLANs map to. In MST, VLANs are directly associated with INSTANCES (not regions). Each MSTI computes its own independent STP topology, allowing different VLAN groups to take different paths through the network.
Adding more VLANs to an existing MSTI adds zero additional STP processing overhead — the VLANs simply ride the existing tree.

front.text: What three configuration attributes must match exactly for switches to belong to the same MST region?

items: MST region name (configured with 'name <region-name>')
MST configuration revision number (configured with 'revision <num>')
VLAN-to-instance mapping table (must be identical on every switch)

back.text: If ANY of these three differ between switches, they treat each other as in different regions and the link becomes a region boundary.

front.text: What is the Internal Spanning Tree (IST), and which MST instance number is it always assigned?

back.text: The IST is a special MST instance that runs on ALL switch ports inside an MST region, regardless of which VLANs are on those ports. The IST is always Instance 0. Information about all other MSTIs is nested inside the IST BPDU, so MST sends only one BPDU set per port instead of one per VLAN — minimizing BPDU traffic regardless of the number of MSTIs.

front.text: Why is MST more efficient than PVST+ in terms of BPDU traffic on a trunk carrying many VLANs?

items: PVST+ sends one BPDU per VLAN every hello interval — 100 VLANs = 100 BPDUs every 2 seconds
MST sends ONE IST BPDU per port that nests information for all configured MSTIs
Whether you have 1 or 64 MSTIs, MST still sends only one BPDU per port per hello
CPU/memory consumption stays nearly flat as VLAN count grows

front.text: How many MST instances are typically supported, and how are they numbered?

back.text: Most Cisco platforms support at least 16 MST instances. Instance 0 is always the IST. Instances 1 through 15 (or higher on some platforms) are user-configurable MSTIs. Any VLAN not explicitly mapped to a higher MSTI remains in instance 0 (the IST) by default.

front.text: List the basic configuration steps to enable MST on a Catalyst switch.

items: Enable MST mode globally: 'spanning-tree mode mst'
(Optional) Set MSTI priority or root: 'spanning-tree mst <instance> priority <val>' or 'spanning-tree mst <instance> root primary'
Enter MST configuration submode: 'spanning-tree mst configuration'
Set the region name: 'name <region-name>'
Set the revision number: 'revision <number>'
Map VLANs to instances: 'instance <num> vlan <vlan-list>'
Exit and verify: 'show spanning-tree mst configuration'

front.text: Show a complete example MST configuration that places VLANs 10 and 20 in instance 1, VLAN 99 in instance 2, makes the switch the root for all instances, and uses region 'ENTERPRISE_CORE' revision 2.

back.text:
spanning-tree mode mst
spanning-tree mst 0 root primary
spanning-tree mst 1 root primary
spanning-tree mst 2 root primary
!
spanning-tree mst configuration
  name ENTERPRISE_CORE
  revision 2
  instance 1 vlan 10,20
  instance 2 vlan 99
  exit

front.text: True or false: The MST root bridge dynamically advertises VLAN-to-instance mappings to all other switches in the region. Explain.

back.text: False. VLAN-to-instance mappings are NOT advertised in BPDUs. Each switch must be MANUALLY configured with the same name, revision, and VLAN-to-instance mapping table. MST only advertises a digest (hash) of the configuration in BPDUs so peers can detect mismatches. If any switch's mapping differs, that link becomes a region boundary instead of an internal MST link.

front.text: True or false: The MST configuration revision number is locally significant. Explain.

back.text: False. The revision number must MATCH on every switch in the same region. It's an operator-managed integer (NOT auto-incremented like VTP) used as part of the region identity. Two switches with different revision numbers — even with identical name and VLAN mappings — will be in different regions.
Best practice: bump the revision number whenever you change the region's VLAN-to-instance mapping, and update all switches together.

front.text: Which command quickly verifies MST region name, revision, and VLAN-to-instance mappings on a switch?

back.text: show spanning-tree mst configuration

Name      [ENTERPRISE_CORE]
Revision  2     Instances configured 3

Instance  Vlans mapped
--------  -----------------------------------------
0         1-9,11-19,21-98,100-4094
1         10,20
2         99

front.text: Which command shows a consolidated view of all MST instances with their VLANs and per-port roles, and how do you scope it to one instance?

back.text:
! All instances
show spanning-tree mst

! Specific instance
show spanning-tree mst 1
Output groups output by '##### MST<n> vlans mapped: ...' and shows interfaces, roles (Root/Desg/Altn), states (FWD/BLK), costs, and types — much more readable than 'show spanning-tree' for MST.

front.text: Which command shows MST status for a single interface across all instances, including BPDU Guard / Filter / boundary status?

back.text: show spanning-tree mst interface gi1/0/1

Useful fields: Edge port (yes/no), Link type (point-to-point), Boundary (internal/PVST/etc.), BPDU guard/filter state, BPDUs sent/received, and a per-instance role/state table mapping each MSTI to its VLANs.

front.text: How does MST display priority differ from PVST+? If the configured priority is 24576 and you're looking at MST instance 2, what bridge priority will appear?

back.text: MST adds the INSTANCE NUMBER (not the VLAN ID) to the configured priority for the sys-id-ext field. So priority 24576 + instance 2 = displayed priority 24578.
PVST+ uses sys-id-ext = VLAN ID. MST uses sys-id-ext = MST instance number. Same 4096-multiple rule applies to the configurable portion.

front.text: How do you modify port cost and port priority for a specific MST instance on an interface?

back.text:
interface gi1/0/1
  spanning-tree mst 0 cost 1
  spanning-tree mst 1 port-priority 64
Tuning is per-MSTI, allowing different cost/priority decisions for different VLAN groups on the same physical interface — this is how MST achieves per-instance load balancing.

front.text: You have two distribution switches and 100 VLANs. How do you use MST to load-balance across two uplinks while minimizing STP overhead?

items: Map half the VLANs to MST instance 1 and the other half to MST instance 2 (e.g., odd VLANs → MSTI 1, even VLANs → MSTI 2).
Make Dist-A root primary for MSTI 1 and root secondary for MSTI 2.
Make Dist-B root primary for MSTI 2 and root secondary for MSTI 1.
Each uplink forwards for one MSTI and blocks for the other — true active/active load balancing.
Total STP processing: only 3 instances (IST + MSTI 1 + MSTI 2), regardless of VLAN count.

back.text: Compare to PVST+ where 100 VLANs would require 100 STP instances — MST achieves the same load-balancing benefit with ~30× less STP overhead.

front.text: From the perspective of a switch OUTSIDE an MST region, how does the MST region appear in the spanning tree?

back.text: The entire MST region appears as a single virtual bridge — one logical hop. External switches see the region as a 'black box' with one entry/exit point per boundary link. This means an external switch counts ONE hop to traverse the entire region, regardless of how many physical MST switches are inside.
Consequence: external STP calculations may produce different blocking decisions than expected, because the region's internal complexity is hidden.

front.text: What constitutes an MST region boundary, and what are the two main scenarios?

items: A port connecting to a switch in a DIFFERENT MST region (different name/revision/mapping)
A port connecting to a switch running 802.1D (CST) or 802.1W (RSTP) / PVST+ / RPVST

back.text: MSTIs never extend outside the region. Only the IST (instance 0) is propagated externally, mapped to the legacy STP topology via the PVST simulation mechanism.

front.text: What is the PVST simulation mechanism, and why is it needed at the MST region boundary?

back.text: PVST simulation is a feature on MST boundary switches that converts IST BPDUs into per-VLAN PVST+/RPVST BPDUs (one BPDU per VLAN sent toward the PVST side). It's needed because PVST+ topologies expect a separate BPDU per VLAN and don't understand the IST BPDU format. The boundary switch performs a one-to-many mapping: one IST BPDU → one BPDU per VLAN going to the PVST+ neighbor.
Conversely, when PVST+ BPDUs arrive at the boundary, only the VLAN 1 PVST+ BPDU is mapped back to the IST — all other VLAN BPDUs are essentially ignored for IST purposes.

front.text: Compare the two valid scenarios for interoperating an MST region with PVST+/RPVST switches.

rows: MST region is root for ALL VLANs
MST advertises superior BPDUs for every VLAN to PVST+ side; PVST+ switches pick blocking ports
Yes — PVST+ side can adjust per-VLAN port cost
MST region is root for NO VLANs (PVST+ side is root)
MST boundary ports either forward or block for ALL VLANs together (must stay consistent with IST)
No — IST cannot diverge per VLAN

back.text: Mixed scenarios (MST root for some VLANs but not others) trigger PVST simulation check and place the port in root-inconsistent state.

front.text: What is the PVST simulation check, and what triggers a port to enter root-inconsistent state at an MST boundary?

back.text: If an MST boundary port receives a SUPERIOR BPDU for one or more VLANs (but not all), it means the PVST+ side claims to be root for some VLANs while the MST region is root for others. Since the IST cannot diverge per-VLAN, this would create a logical inconsistency. To prevent loops, MST blocks the port via root-inconsistent state. This safety mechanism is the PVST simulation check.
Fix: ensure either MST is root for ALL VLANs at the boundary or PVST+ is root for ALL — never mix.

front.text: VLAN 10 traffic between SW1 and SW2 takes an unexpected path. VLAN 10 is mapped to the IST (instance 0). Why might this happen, and what are the two solutions?

back.text: Cause: the IST runs on ALL ports in the region — not just ports carrying VLAN 10. So the IST topology may block a port that carries VLAN 10, even if a different physical link would seem 'closer' for VLAN 10's access ports.

items: Move VLAN 10 to a non-IST MSTI (e.g., MSTI 1). The MSTI builds its topology only over links that actually carry that instance, producing the expected forwarding path.
Allow IST-mapped VLANs on ALL inter-switch trunk links so the IST topology aligns with the physical paths VLAN 10 traffic must take.

front.text: An engineer prunes VLANs on trunks for 'load balancing' in an MST region. Users in some VLANs lose connectivity. What rule was violated?

back.text: Rule: All VLANs in the same MSTI must share identical trunk membership across the region. If you prune VLAN 10 from one trunk but keep VLAN 20 (both in MSTI 1), the MSTI's calculated topology may forward through a link where one of those VLANs has been removed, blackholing traffic.
Simple rule: if you prune VLANs on a trunk in an MST region, prune ALL VLANs in the same MSTI together — or use MSTI-based load balancing instead of manual pruning.

front.text: What underlying STP behavior does MST use within a region — 802.1D timers or RSTP-style rapid convergence?

back.text: MST uses RSTP (802.1w) mechanics internally. Each MSTI converges using the proposal/agreement handshake, edge-port detection, and rapid transitions just like Rapid PVST+. Default timers (Hello 2s, Forward Delay 15s, Max Age 20s) exist as fallback values but are rarely used in practice when topology is healthy.
MST = RSTP convergence + multiple instances + region scoping.

front.text: When you first enable MST mode without any 'instance' commands, where do all VLANs end up?

back.text: All 4094 VLANs are mapped to MST instance 0 (the IST) by default. As you add 'instance N vlan X' commands, those VLANs move from instance 0 into the specified MSTI. Any VLAN you don't explicitly map remains in instance 0.

! Verify: instance 0 holds everything not explicitly remapped
show spanning-tree mst configuration

front.text: True or false: MST supports the same root bridge tuning (priority, root primary/secondary, diameter) as PVST+ and RSTP.

back.text: True. MST supports per-INSTANCE tuning using equivalent commands:

spanning-tree mst 1 priority 4096
spanning-tree mst 1 root primary [diameter 4]
spanning-tree mst 1 root secondary
Just like PVST+, 'root primary' sets priority to 24576 and 'root secondary' sets it to 28672 for that instance.

front.text: Do STP protection features (PortFast, BPDU Guard, BPDU Filter, Root Guard, Loop Guard, UDLD) work the same way under MST as under PVST+/RSTP?

back.text: Yes — all the protection features are mode-independent. Configuration syntax and behavior are identical. The only difference is that root-related features (e.g., Root Guard) operate per MSTI rather than per VLAN, since the root is elected per instance.
Best practice baseline doesn't change when migrating to MST: PortFast + BPDU Guard on access, Loop Guard + UDLD on inter-switch links, Root Guard on appropriate boundaries.

front.text: You're migrating a Rapid PVST+ network with 200 VLANs to MST. Outline a safe migration approach.

items: Document current root placement and per-VLAN load-balancing scheme.
Decide on instance design (e.g., 2 instances for 50/50 load balance, or more for finer control).
Pre-stage MST configuration on every switch BEFORE enabling MST mode: set name, revision, and instance mappings — but stay in 'mode rapid-pvst' for now.
Verify mappings match exactly across the planned region: 'show spanning-tree mst configuration' on every switch.
During a maintenance window, change 'spanning-tree mode mst' on all switches simultaneously (or core-out so PVST simulation handles transitional states).
Verify root election per instance: 'show spanning-tree mst' on each switch.
Confirm boundary behavior with any remaining PVST+ devices using 'show spanning-tree mst interface' (look for Boundary type).

front.text: Quick MST recall facts for the ENCOR exam.

items: MST = IEEE 802.1s; runs on RSTP mechanics internally
VLANs map to MSTIs (not regions); regions group switches with identical config
Region identity = name + revision + VLAN-to-instance mapping (all three must match)
Region config is NOT advertised — must be manually configured identically on every switch
Revision number is operator-managed; not auto-incremented
IST = instance 0, runs on all ports, carries nested info for all other MSTIs in one BPDU
Sys-id-ext field carries MST instance number (not VLAN ID as in PVST+)
External switches see entire MST region as one virtual bridge / one hop
PVST simulation maps IST↔per-VLAN BPDUs at region boundaries
PVST simulation check = root-inconsistent state if MST is root for some VLANs but not all
Default: all 4094 VLANs map to instance 0 until explicitly remapped
All STP protection features (PortFast, BPDU Guard, Root/Loop Guard, UDLD) work identically under MST

front.text: What is Common Spanning Tree (CST), and what is its main limitation in modern multi-VLAN networks?

back.text: CST is the original 802.1D model where ONE STP instance covers the entire switched network — all VLANs share the same topology. Limitation: you cannot load-balance traffic by blocking different ports for different VLANs. Every VLAN must use exactly the same forwarding tree, leaving redundant links idle even when capacity exists.
CST and 802.1D have one thing in common — both support only ONE topology for all VLANs.

front.text: In a network with 1000 VLANs, what specific operational problems does PVST+ create?

items: 1000 STP instances per switch — significant CPU and memory consumption
1000 BPDUs flooding every 2 seconds across every trunk — bandwidth and CPU drain
1000 separate convergence calculations on a topology change
Each instance maintains its own state machine, timers, and port roles
Operational visibility becomes painful — 'show spanning-tree' produces enormous output
Risk of hitting platform-specific STP instance limits

back.text: MST solves this by collapsing many VLANs into a small handful of instances.

front.text: What is the CIST (Common and Internal Spanning Tree), and how does it relate to the IST?

back.text: The CIST is the unified spanning tree that spans BOTH the inside of MST regions and the outside (legacy STP/PVST world). It is the combination of:

items: IST (Internal Spanning Tree) — the active topology inside each MST region
CST (Common Spanning Tree) — the topology connecting MST regions and legacy switches

back.text: From outside an MST region, the region appears as a single virtual bridge participating in the CIST. The IST is just the CIST's portion inside the local region.

front.text: Compare the CIST root with an MSTI root in MST.

rows: Within a single MST region only
Lowest CIST priority across all switches in the network
Lowest MSTI priority within the region
Visible to legacy STP / PVST+ neighbors

front.text: What is an MST region's IST master, and how is it different from the CIST root?

back.text: If the CIST root is OUTSIDE the local MST region, the switch within the region that has the best path to the CIST root becomes the IST master. The IST master is the region's 'gateway' to the external CIST root — its boundary port is the region's RP toward the CIST root. If the CIST root is INSIDE the region, then the CIST root and IST master are the same switch.
Think of the IST master as the region's locally elected leader for talking to the outside world.

front.text: What are the possible 'Boundary' values shown in 'show spanning-tree mst interface' output, and what does each mean?

rows: Connected to another switch in the same MST region (no boundary)
Connected to a PVST+/RPVST switch — PVST simulation engaged
Connected to a switch in a different MST region or running plain RSTP

front.text: Two switches both run MST with identical region name and revision number, yet 'show spanning-tree mst interface' shows the link between them as a Boundary port. What's the most likely cause?

items: VLAN-to-instance mapping differs between the two switches.
Even one missing VLAN in one switch's instance list breaks the digest match.
Verify with 'show spanning-tree mst configuration' on both switches and compare the Instance/Vlans-mapped table line by line.
Sync the configurations exactly, including any VLANs that should be in instance 0 (defaults can hide subtle differences).
After fixing, the boundary should change to 'internal' on both ends.

front.text: How does an MST switch detect whether a neighbor belongs to the same MST region without exchanging the full VLAN-to-instance table?

back.text: MST BPDUs carry a 16-byte MD5 digest computed over the configured VLAN-to-instance mapping table, plus the region name and revision number. A neighbor compares the received digest, name, and revision to its own. If ALL three match, the link is internal to the same region. If any field differs, the link is treated as a region boundary. The digest avoids transmitting the full 4094-VLAN mapping in every BPDU.

front.text: What are the default MST region name, revision number, and VLAN-to-instance mapping when MST mode is first enabled?

items: Region name: empty string ("")
Revision number: 0
VLAN-to-instance mapping: all 4094 VLANs in instance 0 (the IST)

back.text: Two freshly enabled MST switches with no further config will be in the same default region — but it's still best practice to explicitly set name, revision, and mappings.

front.text: When you enter MST configuration submode and make changes, when do they actually take effect?

back.text: Changes inside the 'spanning-tree mst configuration' submode are STAGED in a pending configuration. They only become active when you exit the submode (typing 'exit' or 'end'). This allows you to make multiple instance/name/revision changes atomically — preventing transient mismatches that would briefly turn internal links into boundary ports.

! Use 'show' commands to compare staged vs active
show current   ! active config (within submode)
show pending   ! staged changes (within submode)
abort          ! discard pending changes

front.text: While in MST configuration submode, how do you preview your staged changes before committing them?

back.text:
switch(config)# spanning-tree mst configuration
switch(config-mst)# show pending
switch(config-mst)# show current

'show pending' displays your staged config (about to apply). 'show current' displays the active config. Use 'abort' to discard pending changes, or 'exit' to commit. This is critical when modifying production MST regions to avoid accidental boundary creation.

front.text: What is the MST 'max hops' parameter, what's its default, and how does it differ from Max Age?

back.text: Max hops limits how far an MST BPDU can propagate within a region — the BPDU's hop count is decremented at each switch (similar to IP TTL). When it reaches 0, the BPDU is discarded. This replaces the timer-based Max Age inside the region. Default is 20 hops.
spanning-tree mst max-hops 30
Max Age still applies at region boundaries when interacting with legacy STP. Inside the region, max-hops governs BPDU lifetime.

front.text: In an MST region with three switches in a triangle topology, MSTI 1 has SW1 as root and MSTI 2 has SW2 as root. Where does the blocking port appear for each MSTI?

items: MSTI 1 (SW1 root): SW2-SW3 link has the blocking port (likely on SW3 toward SW2). All traffic for MSTI 1 VLANs goes through SW1.
MSTI 2 (SW2 root): SW1-SW3 link has the blocking port (likely on SW3 toward SW1). All traffic for MSTI 2 VLANs goes through SW2.
Result: traffic load-balances by VLAN group across the three links — different links forward for different MSTIs.

back.text: Each MSTI computes its own independent topology — the blocking port location can differ per instance even on the same physical topology.

front.text: Which command shows only the MST instances that are operational on a switch, filtering out those with no active ports?

back.text:
show spanning-tree mst | include MST
show spanning-tree mst active
'show spanning-tree mst active' filters to instances and ports that are currently in operational forwarding/learning state — useful for quickly verifying the active topology in a busy switch.

front.text: List best practices for designing an MST region in a campus environment.

items: Use a meaningful, documented region name (e.g., CAMPUS_CORE)
Use a non-zero revision number; bump it whenever VLAN mappings change
Keep the region as large as practical — encompass all switches that share VLANs
Use 2-4 MSTIs typically; more provides finer load balancing but adds operational complexity
Avoid mixing MST and PVST+ at the boundary if possible — use MST throughout
Plan root placement per MSTI on distribution/core switches and align with HSRP/VRRP
Document VLAN-to-instance mapping centrally and version-control it
Use the same MST mode/version on every switch in the region

front.text: On a boundary port between an MST region and a PVST+ switch, do MSTIs other than the IST forward any traffic over the boundary?

back.text: All MSTIs INHERIT the IST's port state at the boundary. If the IST is forwarding on the boundary port, all MSTIs forward; if the IST is blocking, all MSTIs block. This is because MSTI BPDUs do not exist outside the region — only the CIST/IST does. The boundary port acts as a single forwarding/blocking decision for every VLAN, regardless of MSTI mapping.
Implication: you cannot load-balance per-MSTI across boundary links — load balancing must be done by the PVST+ side (per-VLAN cost).

front.text: If a link fails in an MST region, do all MSTIs converge simultaneously, or one at a time?

back.text: All MSTIs that traverse the failed link converge in parallel using RSTP's proposal/agreement handshake. Because all MSTI information is carried in a single IST BPDU, a single BPDU exchange triggers convergence across every affected instance simultaneously. This is significantly faster than PVST+, where 100 VLANs would require 100 separate convergence cycles.

front.text: What are the design tradeoffs of having one large MST region vs multiple smaller MST regions?

rows: Limited by max VLANs / instances per region
Each region independent — better for very large networks
All switches affected by region issues

back.text: Most enterprise networks use one region per administrative domain (e.g., per campus) — keep it simple unless scale demands segmentation.

front.text: After adding a new VLAN to your MST region, traffic for that VLAN starts looping. What likely went wrong?

items: The VLAN was not added to an MSTI on every switch consistently — different switches see the VLAN in different instances.
OR the VLAN was added to an instance on some switches but left in instance 0 (default) on others.
OR the revision number was bumped on some switches but not others, splitting them into different regions.
Verify with 'show spanning-tree mst configuration' on every switch — all must show identical name, revision, and VLAN-to-instance mappings.
Always make MST mapping changes in a coordinated maintenance window across all region switches.

front.text: In a two-instance MST design, configure Dist-A and Dist-B for active/active load balancing where Dist-A is primary for MSTI 1 and Dist-B is primary for MSTI 2.

back.text:
! Dist-A
spanning-tree mst 0 root primary
spanning-tree mst 1 root primary
spanning-tree mst 2 root secondary

! Dist-B
spanning-tree mst 0 root secondary
spanning-tree mst 1 root secondary
spanning-tree mst 2 root primary
Also align HSRP/VRRP active gateway with the MST root for each VLAN group to keep L2 and L3 paths symmetric.

front.text: What VLAN list formats are accepted by the 'instance N vlan ...' command in MST configuration submode?

back.text:
! Single VLAN
instance 1 vlan 10

! Multiple individual VLANs
instance 1 vlan 10,20,30

! VLAN range
instance 1 vlan 10-19

! Combination
instance 1 vlan 10-19,25,30-39

! Add to existing instance (new entries appended)
instance 1 vlan 40

! Remove a VLAN from an instance (returns it to instance 0)
no instance 1 vlan 30

front.text: What happens if you configure an MSTI with no VLANs mapped to it?

back.text: An MSTI with zero mapped VLANs is technically valid but operationally meaningless — it consumes an instance slot but carries no traffic. STP still computes a topology for the empty instance, but no data ever flows through it. 'show spanning-tree mst N' will display the instance but show 'vlans mapped: <none>'. Best practice: don't create empty instances; remove them with 'no instance N'.

front.text: How do you remove an entire MST instance and return its VLANs to the IST (instance 0)?

back.text:
spanning-tree mst configuration
  no instance 2
  exit

! Or remove specific VLANs only
spanning-tree mst configuration
  no instance 2 vlan 99
All VLANs previously mapped to instance 2 return to instance 0 (the IST). Remember to update revision number and apply on every switch in the region.

front.text: Should voice VLANs and their associated data VLANs be mapped to the same MSTI or different MSTIs?

back.text: Same MSTI. A voice VLAN and its paired data VLAN share the same physical access ports and trunks — splitting them across MSTIs would force one to take a suboptimal path or cause asymmetric forwarding. Group all VLANs that ride the same physical paths into the same MSTI.
General rule: VLANs that always traverse the same trunk links belong in the same MSTI. Use multiple MSTIs only to deliberately steer different VLAN groups across different physical paths.

front.text: How does MST treat an EtherChannel (Port-channel) bundle?

back.text: MST sees the entire EtherChannel as a single logical port for STP purposes — same as PVST+/RSTP. All member links forward simultaneously; member failures don't trigger STP convergence as long as the bundle stays up. STP cost is calculated on the bundle's aggregate bandwidth. Per-MSTI tuning (cost, priority) is applied to the Port-channel interface, not the individual members.

interface port-channel 1
  spanning-tree mst 1 cost 5

front.text: Which command provides detailed MST information including topology change history and BPDU counters per instance?

back.text:
show spanning-tree mst detail
show spanning-tree mst 1 detail

Detail output includes per-instance: TC count, last TC time and source interface, BPDUs sent/received, hello/forward-delay timers, and full per-port detail with edge/p2p/boundary state. Critical for troubleshooting MST stability issues.

front.text: Why does MST use a hop count inside the region instead of relying on the Max Age timer like 802.1D?

back.text: Max Age is a timer-based lifetime — it depends on accurate timer values everywhere and degrades convergence speed. Hop count is decremented per switch (like IP TTL), giving deterministic BPDU lifetime regardless of timing. This makes the IST behavior predictable and aligns with how RSTP-based protocols handle BPDU propagation. The CIST root sets the initial hop count; each downstream switch decrements it by 1. When it reaches 0, BPDUs are discarded.

front.text: An MST region connects to a PVST+ network via two boundary links. The MST region is root for all VLANs. If the active boundary link fails, how does the PVST+ side react?

items: Failure detected on the active boundary link.
PVST+ switches stop receiving the simulated PVST BPDUs they were using as superior root info.
After Max Age (or via direct link failure detection), PVST+ switches transition the alternate boundary link out of blocking.
Alternate link goes through listening/learning (or rapid handshake if RPVST) and starts forwarding.
MST region's PVST simulation kicks in on the new active boundary, sending per-VLAN BPDUs.
Convergence time depends on PVST+ side's STP mode — RPVST converges in seconds, legacy PVST+ in 30–50 seconds.

front.text: List the top common mistakes when first deploying MST.

items: Forgetting that VLAN-to-instance mappings must be configured manually on every switch (not advertised)
Mismatched revision numbers across switches in the same intended region
Region name typo (case-sensitive) breaking region membership
Pruning VLANs on trunks asymmetrically across MSTI member VLANs
Mapping VLANs only to the IST, expecting per-VLAN load balancing (won't work)
Trying to load-balance per-VLAN across an MST/PVST boundary (boundary forwards/blocks for ALL VLANs together)
Not coordinating MST changes across switches → boundary ports appearing where internal expected
Forgetting to enable 'spanning-tree mode mst' globally before configuring instances

front.text: Master MST quick-recall summary for ENCOR exam.

rows: Name + Revision + VLAN-to-instance mapping (all 3 must match)
Always instance 0; runs on every port in region
NO — manual on every switch (digest verifies match)
ONE IST BPDU carries info for ALL MSTIs
MST instance number (PVST+ uses VLAN ID)
Region appears as ONE virtual bridge / one hop
Region's switch closest to CIST root
Max-hops (default 20) inside region; Max Age externally
Per-VLAN tuning required on PVST+ side
Root-inconsistent if MST root for SOME VLANs
Per-MSTI: priority, cost, port-priority, root primary/secondary

front.text: What was the original IEEE standard that defined Multiple Spanning Tree Protocol, and when was it merged into 802.1Q?

back.text: MST was originally defined in IEEE 802.1s (2002). It was later folded into the IEEE 802.1Q-2005 standard, where it now lives alongside VLAN tagging specifications. While Cisco documentation still commonly refers to '802.1s', the current authoritative reference is the consolidated 802.1Q standard.

front.text: What are the three primary design goals MST was created to achieve?

items: Provide multiple STP topologies for VLAN-based load balancing (like PVST+)
Reduce the per-VLAN STP overhead found in PVST+ (CPU, memory, BPDU traffic)
Be standards-based and interoperable with non-Cisco switches (unlike Cisco-proprietary PVST+)

front.text: Compare MSTP (802.1s) directly with RSTP (802.1w).
What format does an MST BPDU use, and how does it carry information for multiple MSTIs in a single packet?

back.text: MST BPDUs use the RSTP BPDU format with version 3 and append a series of M-records (MSTI configuration messages) — one M-record per active MSTI in the region. The base RSTP BPDU carries the IST (instance 0) info; M-records add per-instance root, cost, and port role data. A single physical BPDU can therefore carry state for instance 0 plus all configured MSTIs, scaling efficiently.

front.text: What does an M-record contain inside an MST BPDU?

items: MSTI Regional Root Identifier
Internal root path cost for that instance
Bridge priority (with sys-id-ext = MSTI number)
Port priority and port role for that instance
Remaining hops (for max-hops decrement inside region)

back.text: M-records do NOT cross MST region boundaries — they are scoped within the region only. Outside, only the CIST/IST info propagates.

front.text: Which command provides MST-specific bridge ID and timer information for the local switch across all instances?

back.text:
show spanning-tree mst | begin Bridge
show spanning-tree summary
Both show per-instance bridge priority (with sysid added), local bridge MAC, and timer values. 'show spanning-tree summary' also confirms 'Switch is in mst mode' at the top.

front.text: When you change a switch from 'spanning-tree mode rapid-pvst' to 'spanning-tree mode mst', what happens to the existing forwarding state?

items: All STP instances are torn down and rebuilt under the new mode.
Ports may briefly enter discarding/listening states during reconvergence.
Brief data plane disruption (typically 1–30 seconds depending on topology and timers).
All VLANs initially map to MST instance 0 (IST) until you configure custom instance mappings.
BPDU format changes immediately — neighbors not yet in MST mode see boundary behavior.

back.text: Always perform mode changes during a maintenance window, ideally network-wide simultaneously to minimize transitional boundary states.

front.text: How do you verify that all switches you intend to be in the same MST region are actually treating each other as 'internal' rather than 'boundary'?

back.text: show spanning-tree mst interface <inter-switch-port>

Look for 'Boundary : internal' on every inter-switch link inside your intended region. Any port showing 'boundary (PVST)', 'boundary (STP)', or 'boundary (RSTP)' indicates the neighbor isn't recognized as part of the same region — investigate name, revision, and mapping consistency.

front.text: What does the 'spanning-tree mst <instance> force-version' family of commands do, and when might it be used?

back.text: On some Cisco platforms, you can force the MST BPDU version sent on specific links — useful for backward compatibility testing with older switches that may not handle the modern MSTP BPDU format. Default behavior is automatic version negotiation, so manual forcing is rarely needed in modern networks. Check the platform-specific configuration guide before using these commands in production.

front.text: An MST boundary port keeps entering root-inconsistent state. The PVST+ neighbor is intentionally set as root for VLAN 50 only. How do you fix this?

items: Cause: PVST simulation check detects that the PVST+ side is root for SOME VLANs (50) but not others — incompatible with MST's all-or-nothing IST behavior.
Solution 1: Make the MST region root for ALL VLANs (lower MST priority below PVST+ for every VLAN, including VLAN 50).
Solution 2: Make the PVST+ side root for ALL VLANs, including those currently rooted in the MST region. Reconfigure MST priorities to be higher than PVST+.
Verify after change: 'show spanning-tree mst interface <port>' should show normal designated/root state, not root-inconsistent.

back.text: Mixed root-of-some-VLANs designs are not supported across MST/PVST boundaries — pick one side.

front.text: How do you globally or per-port disable PVST simulation, and what's the consequence?

back.text:
! Globally disable PVST simulation
no spanning-tree mst simulate pvst global

! Per-port disable
interface gi1/0/1
  spanning-tree mst simulate pvst disable

Disabling PVST simulation means an MST boundary port will go into 'PVST Peer Inconsistent' (blocking) if any PVST BPDUs are received. Useful when you've migrated everything to MST and want to detect/quarantine accidental PVST+ devices reconnecting to the network.

front.text: What path cost mode does MST use by default, and what is the default cost for a 1G link?

back.text: MST uses the long path cost method by default, regardless of the global 'spanning-tree pathcost method' setting (which only affects PVST+/Rapid PVST+). 1 Gbps = cost 20,000 in MST. This explains why MST output frequently shows costs like 20000 instead of the 4 you'd see in short-mode PVST+.

front.text: Compare 'external root path cost' and 'internal root path cost' in MST.

rows: Calculating path to CIST root from outside the region

back.text: External cost increments only when crossing a region boundary; it stays the same inside a region. Internal cost increments at each switch within the region. This separation is what makes the region appear as a single hop from outside while still preserving accurate path selection inside.

front.text: When an MST region is in the path between two external switches, how many hops does the external CIST count for traversing the region?

back.text: One. The entire region counts as a single CIST hop, regardless of how many physical switches are inside. The external cost increases only by the cost of the boundary links, not by the internal traversal. This 'collapsed view' is what allows MST to integrate cleanly with legacy STP/PVST+ networks while masking internal complexity.

front.text: Can two MSTIs have completely different root bridges within the same region?

back.text: Yes — and this is exactly how MST achieves load balancing. Each MSTI runs its own independent root election, so SW1 can be root for MSTI 1 while SW2 is root for MSTI 2. The IST (instance 0) has its own separate root election as well. Tuning per-instance priorities (e.g., 'spanning-tree mst 1 root primary' on SW1) creates this topology divergence.

front.text: Why is the IST (instance 0) special compared to other MSTIs, and why can't you delete it?

items: The IST is the only instance that interacts with the outside world via the CIST
It always exists by default — it cannot be created or deleted, only its VLAN mappings adjusted
It runs on every port in the region, regardless of VLAN membership
It is the carrier for all M-records describing other MSTIs
Boundary ports inherit their forwarding decision from the IST
It has the same priority/root election mechanics as any other instance, but its role is foundational

front.text: Does PortFast / edge port behavior work the same way under MST as under Rapid PVST+?

back.text: Yes — PortFast/edge ports under MST behave identically. Configuration syntax is unchanged. Edge ports skip listening/learning, suppress TCN generation, and remain edges across all MSTIs simultaneously. BPDU Guard pairs with PortFast just as it does with Rapid PVST+.

interface gi1/0/13
  switchport mode access
  switchport access vlan 10
  spanning-tree portfast
  spanning-tree bpduguard enable

front.text: If a port is configured as PortFast/edge in MST, does that edge status apply to all MSTIs or only the IST?

back.text: Edge port status applies to ALL MSTIs simultaneously. There is no concept of per-MSTI edge port — a port is either an edge for the entire MST configuration or not at all. This makes sense because edge ports connect to end hosts that don't run STP regardless of VLAN.

front.text: Compare what 'show spanning-tree' displays for PVST+ vs MST mode.

rows: Use 'show spanning-tree mst' for VLAN-to-MSTI mapping

front.text: Why can topology changes inside one MST region NOT propagate as TCs into a neighboring MST region?

back.text: MSTI-specific BPDUs (M-records) are scoped to within their region only. When an MSTI's topology changes inside Region A, neighbors in Region B never see that change because M-records don't cross boundaries — only IST/CIST information does. This makes regions natural fault-isolation boundaries: an unstable MSTI inside one region cannot trigger reconvergence in another region.
TCs in the IST/CIST DO propagate across regions, since the CIST spans the entire bridged network.

front.text: What is 'pre-standard' MST, and why might you encounter it in older Cisco environments?

back.text: Before IEEE 802.1s was finalized, Cisco shipped a pre-standard implementation of MST. Some legacy IOS versions still run this older format. Pre-standard and standard MST switches don't natively interoperate — Cisco provides the 'spanning-tree mst pre-standard' interface command on modern platforms to force pre-standard BPDU format on specific links facing legacy devices.

interface gi1/0/24
  spanning-tree mst pre-standard

front.text: Can you set different hello/forward-delay/max-age timers per MSTI?

back.text: No. Timer values are GLOBAL per region — they live on the CIST/IST and apply to all MSTIs within the region. There is no per-MSTI timer tuning. Timers can be set with 'spanning-tree mst hello-time / forward-time / max-age', and propagate from the CIST root throughout the region.

spanning-tree mst hello-time 2
spanning-tree mst forward-time 15
spanning-tree mst max-age 20
spanning-tree mst max-hops 20

front.text: You inherited an MST network with no documentation. What is your audit checklist to understand its design?

items: On each switch run 'show spanning-tree mst configuration' — record name, revision, VLAN-to-instance mapping.
Confirm all switches show identical mapping tables (any difference = boundary).
Run 'show spanning-tree mst' on every switch — identify root for each MSTI and the IST.
Walk inter-switch ports with 'show spanning-tree mst interface' — confirm Boundary type is 'internal'.
Identify region boundary links (if any) and document the neighbor protocol (PVST+/RPVST/another MST region).
Map physical topology to MSTI logical topology (which links forward/block per MSTI).
Document which VLANs ride which MSTI for change-management going forward.

front.text: Does Cisco Catalyst Center (DNAC) automate MST configuration deployment?

back.text: Catalyst Center can push consistent STP mode and protective-feature configurations as part of its templates and design intent. While it doesn't have a dedicated MST region wizard, you can use templates and CLI configurations to ensure region name, revision, and VLAN-to-instance mappings are deployed identically across all switches in a fabric — solving the manual-sync problem inherent to MST. This is especially valuable in greenfield deployments and large-scale brownfield migrations.

front.text: What happens if you have three switches: SW1 in MST, SW2 in Rapid PVST+, SW3 in MST. SW1 and SW3 are connected only through SW2. Can SW1 and SW3 form an MST region?

back.text: No. MST regions require contiguous MST switches. Since SW2 is running Rapid PVST+, it acts as a region boundary on both sides. SW1 sees SW2 as a PVST+ neighbor and engages PVST simulation. SW3 does the same independently. SW1 and SW3 are in two SEPARATE MST regions (each containing one switch), with PVST simulation handling the connection through SW2. To merge them, you must convert SW2 to MST with the same name/revision/mappings.

front.text: Does the 'diameter' keyword in 'spanning-tree mst <instance> root primary diameter <num>' work the same as in PVST+?

back.text: Mostly yes. The diameter macro tunes timers (hello, forward delay, max age) based on the maximum number of hops between the root and the most distant switch. In MST, this also influences max-hops behavior. Default diameter is 7, default max-hops is 20. Reducing diameter on a smaller topology can speed certain edge cases, though MST's RSTP-based proposal/agreement convergence makes timer tuning rarely necessary in modern designs.

front.text: How does an MST boundary port behave during topology changes received from the legacy/PVST+ side?

back.text: When the boundary receives a TC from the PVST+ side, the MST switch translates it into an IST topology change and floods it through the region. Similarly, IST topology changes get translated into per-VLAN TCs sent toward the PVST+ side via PVST simulation. This bidirectional translation keeps both sides synchronized but means a single TC event can cause MAC table churn across both the region and the legacy side.

front.text: Does Loop Guard work per-MSTI, or globally across all instances on a port?

back.text: Loop Guard operates per-MSTI. If BPDUs stop arriving for a specific MSTI on an RP/alternate port, only that MSTI's port state moves to loop-inconsistent — other MSTIs on the same physical port continue forwarding/blocking based on their own state. This makes Loop Guard granular and powerful in MST: you can experience an instance-specific loop-inconsistent condition without affecting unrelated VLAN traffic.

spanning-tree loopguard default
! or per-port:
interface gi1/0/1
  spanning-tree guard loop

front.text: Final MST gotcha list — common exam pitfalls to memorize.

items: VLAN-to-instance mapping is NOT advertised — must match manually on every switch
All three (name, revision, mapping) must match for region membership
Default: all VLANs are in instance 0 (IST)
MSTI BPDU information NEVER crosses region boundaries — only IST does
External switches see entire region as ONE virtual bridge / one hop
MST root primary sets priority to 24576; root secondary sets 28672 — same as PVST+
MST sys-id-ext = MSTI number (NOT VLAN ID)
Inside region: hop-count limits BPDU lifetime (not Max Age)
Default MST path cost is LONG mode regardless of global pathcost setting
MST is root for ALL VLANs at boundary OR for NONE — mixed = root-inconsistent
Trunk pruning must be consistent across VLANs within the same MSTI
All STP protections (PortFast, BPDU Guard, Root/Loop Guard, UDLD) work identically

front.text: Master MST command reference — every command you should be ready to recall on the ENCOR exam.

rows: show current (in MST submode) / show spanning-tree mst configuration

front.text: What is VLAN Trunking Protocol (VTP), and what problem does it solve?

back.text: VTP is a Cisco-proprietary protocol that automates VLAN provisioning across multiple switches in the same VTP domain. Instead of manually creating a VLAN on every switch (e.g., 100 switches = 100 logins), VTP allows you to create the VLAN once on a VTP server and have it propagate automatically to all VTP clients in the domain.
Predates modern API/automation. Still widely deployed but used carefully due to historical 'VTP bomb' incidents.

front.text: Compare the four VTP modes: Server, Client, Transparent, and Off.
What are the key differences between VTP versions 1, 2, and 3?

rows: Token Ring support, version-dependent transparent forwarding
Primary server concept, MST/private VLAN support, hidden password, better protection from accidental override

back.text: VTPv3 is the modern recommendation when VTP is used at all.

front.text: What are the three types of VTP advertisements?

items: Summary — sent every 300 seconds or after a VLAN change. Contains VTP version, domain, configuration revision, and timestamp (NOT the VLAN data itself)
Subset — sent after a VLAN change. Contains the actual VLAN configuration details that other switches need to update their database
Client request — sent by a client when its revision is lower than what's heard from the network; requests a subset advertisement

front.text: Show the steps and commands to configure a switch as a VTPv3 primary server in domain 'CISCO' with a password.

back.text:
! Step 1: Set version
vtp version 3

! Step 2: Set domain (resets revision to 0)
vtp domain CISCO

! Step 3: Set mode
vtp mode server

! Step 4: Set password (recommended)
vtp password PASSWORD

! Step 5: VTPv3 — promote to primary (privileged exec)
end
vtp primary
In VTPv3, only the primary server can add/remove VLANs. Other servers in the domain process updates like clients until promoted.

front.text: Which command shows VTP version, mode, domain, configuration revision, and VLAN counts?

back.text: show vtp status

Key fields: VTP version running, VTP Domain Name, VTP Operating Mode (Server/Client/Transparent/Off/Primary Server), Configuration Revision, Number of VLANs, MD5 digest. Compare revision number across switches in the domain — they should match if synchronized.

front.text: A switch is moved between sites and connected to the production VTP domain. All VLANs disappear from the network. What happened, and how do you prevent this?

back.text: Classic VTP bomb. The moved switch had a HIGHER configuration revision number than the production VTP server (e.g., it was previously a server in another domain or had VLANs deleted). When connected, its higher-revision update propagated to the entire domain, deleting any VLANs not in its database.

items: Always reset VTP revision to 0 BEFORE introducing a switch to a domain. Easiest method: change the VTP domain to a temporary value, then back. Or change mode to transparent and back.
Verify with 'show vtp status' that revision = 0 before connecting.
Use VTPv3 (primary server requirement) or VTP off mode to eliminate this risk entirely.
Use a strong VTP password — switches without the password cannot influence the domain.

front.text: What is Dynamic Trunking Protocol (DTP), and how often does it advertise?

back.text: DTP is a Cisco-proprietary protocol that allows two switch ports to negotiate trunk formation automatically. It advertises every 30 seconds and requires the VTP domain to match between switches. Both ends communicate their willingness to form a trunk; if compatible, the link becomes a trunk dynamically.

front.text: Describe the three DTP-related switchport modes: trunk, dynamic desirable, dynamic auto.

back.text: auto + auto = NEVER trunks (both sides waiting). All other combinations form a trunk.

front.text: For each combination, will a trunk form? trunk-trunk, trunk-desirable, trunk-auto, desirable-desirable, desirable-auto, auto-auto
Which command disables DTP negotiation on a switchport, and why is this a best practice?

back.text:
interface gi1/0/2
  switchport mode trunk
  switchport nonegotiate

switchport nonegotiate prevents the port from sending DTP frames or negotiating trunk status. Best practice because: (1) eliminates DTP attack vector (rogue device negotiating a trunk), (2) makes port behavior deterministic, (3) reduces unnecessary protocol traffic. Always pair with explicit 'switchport mode access' or 'switchport mode trunk'.

front.text: Which command quickly verifies trunk port mode, encapsulation, status, and allowed VLANs?

back.text:
show interfaces trunk
show interfaces gi1/0/1 trunk

Output Mode column: 'on' = static trunk, 'desirable' = dynamic desirable, 'auto' = dynamic auto. Encapsulation typically 802.1q. Status 'trunking' confirms successful negotiation. Native VLAN and allowed VLAN list are also shown.

front.text: What is an EtherChannel bundle, and what are its two main benefits?

back.text: An EtherChannel (also called port-channel) is a logical interface that aggregates multiple physical links between two switches/devices into a single logical link. STP sees only the logical interface, not the individual member links.

items: Increased bandwidth — aggregate throughput of all active member links
Reduced topology change events — a single member link failure does NOT trigger STP/routing reconvergence as long as one member stays up

back.text: Industry term: Link Aggregation Group (LAG) per IEEE 802.3AD.

front.text: Which switchport types can be aggregated into an EtherChannel?

items: Layer 2 access ports — all members must be access ports in the same VLAN
Layer 2 trunk ports — all members must have identical trunk config (native VLAN, allowed VLANs)
Layer 3 routed ports — all members must use 'no switchport' before joining the bundle

back.text: Loopback interfaces cannot be bundled. The first member's port type determines the bundle's type.

front.text: Compare PAgP and LACP — origin, modes, multicast MAC, and protocol code.

back.text: LACP is preferred for cross-vendor interoperability. PAgP only works between Cisco devices.

front.text: Compare PAgP auto and desirable modes — when does an EtherChannel form?

back.text: auto = passive (responds only). desirable = active (initiates and responds). At least ONE side must be desirable to form the bundle.

front.text: Compare LACP active and passive modes — when does an EtherChannel form?

back.text: passive = responds only. active = initiates and responds. At least ONE side must be active to form the bundle. Same logic as PAgP, just different terminology.

front.text: Why is statically configuring an EtherChannel with 'mode on' (no LACP/PAgP) risky, especially across optical/DWDM/IPS infrastructure?

back.text: Static 'on' mode has NO health-check mechanism. If an intermediary device (DWDM transponder, network tap, IPS, Layer 2 firewall) keeps the local line protocol up despite a downstream link failure, the switch will continue load-balancing traffic out the broken link — causing partial packet loss with no automatic recovery.
LACP/PAgP exchange keepalives end-to-end and detect this scenario, deactivating the broken member from the bundle. Always prefer dynamic protocols over static 'on'.

front.text: Show the commands to create LACP EtherChannel 1 (active mode) on Gi1/0/1-2 as a Layer 2 trunk.

back.text:
interface range gi1/0/1-2
  channel-group 1 mode active
!
interface port-channel 1
  switchport mode trunk
  switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20,30
Always configure trunk/access settings on the port-channel interface, NOT individual members. Members inherit the bundle's L2/L3 configuration.

front.text: What are the possible 'channel-group X mode ?' values, and which protocol does each use?
