back.text: If the peer is stuck in Active, the most common cause is that one side cannot reach the configured neighbor IP (TCP cannot complete).

front.text: What are AFI and SAFI in MP-BGP, and what's their purpose?

back.text: Address Family Identifier (AFI) defines a network protocol — for example, IPv4 (1) or IPv6 (2). Subsequent Address Family Identifier (SAFI) further subclassifies it — for example, unicast (1) or multicast (2). MP-BGP (RFC 4760) tags every NLRI with an AFI/SAFI so a single BGP session can carry multiple route types (IPv4 unicast, IPv6 unicast, VPNv4, VPNv6, etc.) with separate routing policies and tables per AFI/SAFI.

front.text: Configure a basic eBGP session between AS 65100 (R1: 10.12.1.1) and AS 65200 (R2: 10.12.1.2) with explicit IPv4 unicast activation.

back.text:
! R1 (AS 65100)
router bgp 65100
 bgp router-id 192.168.1.1
 no bgp default ipv4-unicast
 neighbor 10.12.1.2 remote-as 65200
 !
 address-family ipv4 unicast
  neighbor 10.12.1.2 activate

! R2 (AS 65200)
router bgp 65200
 bgp router-id 192.168.2.2
 no bgp default ipv4-unicast
 neighbor 10.12.1.1 remote-as 65100
 !
 address-family ipv4 unicast
  neighbor 10.12.1.1 activate
'no bgp default ipv4-unicast' is a best practice — it forces explicit AF activation per neighbor and standardizes behavior when working with IPv6/VPNv4.

front.text: How is the BGP Router ID selected, and why is setting it statically a best practice?

items: Configured 'bgp router-id <id>' takes precedence
Otherwise, highest IP on any UP loopback interface
Otherwise, highest IP on any UP physical interface

back.text: Statically setting the RID prevents disruption: if the dynamic RID changes (e.g., loopback removed), ALL BGP sessions reset and must be re-established. The RID must be a 32-bit dotted-decimal number, unique within the AS, and non-zero.

front.text: List the most common BGP verification commands.

back.text:
show bgp ipv4 unicast summary               ! session/peer state, prefix counts
show bgp ipv4 unicast                       ! Loc-RIB contents
show bgp ipv4 unicast <network>             ! detailed paths and PAs
show bgp ipv4 unicast neighbors <ip>        ! neighbor state, capabilities, timers
show bgp ipv4 unicast neighbors <ip> advertised-routes   ! Adj-RIB-Out
show bgp ipv4 unicast neighbors <ip> received-routes     ! Adj-RIB-In (requires soft-reconfig)
show ip route bgp                            ! BGP routes installed in RIB
show tcp brief                               ! TCP/179 sessions

front.text: Name the three BGP tables and their purpose.

rows: Routes received from a peer BEFORE inbound policy. Cleared after processing to save memory.
All routes (locally originated + accepted from peers) — the BGP table; best-path runs here.
Routes after outbound policy, prepared for advertisement to a specific peer.

front.text: True or false: BGP advertises ALL paths for every prefix so neighbors can build their own topology table.

back.text: False. By default, BGP advertises ONLY the best path for each prefix to peers. To advertise multiple paths (e.g., for ECMP across iBGP) you'd need features like BGP Add-Path or BGP multipath. This default behavior is part of what makes BGP a path vector — not link-state — protocol.

front.text: What does the BGP 'network' statement actually do, and what's required for it to advertise a prefix?

back.text: Unlike OSPF/EIGRP, the BGP 'network' statement does NOT enable BGP on an interface. Instead, it tells BGP to install a SPECIFIC prefix into the Loc-RIB IF an exact-match route already exists in the global RIB (connected, static, or via any IGP). The match must be exact for both the network and mask.

router bgp 65100
 address-family ipv4 unicast
  network 192.168.1.0 mask 255.255.255.0    ! exact match required in the RIB

front.text: Interpret this BGP Loc-RIB row:

items: * = path is valid
> = best path (selected for the RIB and for advertisement)
10.12.1.2 = next-hop attribute
First 0 = MED (metric)
Second column 0 = Weight (Cisco-only, locally significant)
65200 = AS_Path (single AS hop = AS 65200)
i = origin code IGP (network statement). 'e' = EGP, '?' = incomplete (typically redistributed)

front.text: What are the three BGP origin codes and what does each represent?

rows: Redistributed into BGP from another protocol or static route

front.text: What administrative distance does BGP use for eBGP- and iBGP-learned routes?

back.text: The high AD on iBGP routes (200) means OSPF/EIGRP/static routes will preferentially install into the RIB. This is intentional — the underlay IGP should provide reachability to BGP next-hops.

front.text: Why is redistributing IGP routes into BGP generally safe but redistributing BGP into IGP is risky?

items: BGP scales to 940,000+ routes (full Internet table); IGPs can struggle with even 20,000
Link-state IGP SPF cost grows quickly with prefix count → CPU/memory issues
BGP path attributes (AS_Path, communities, MED) cannot be carried in IGPs
Redistributing BGP→IGP can cause routing loops, instability, or full router meltdown
Best practice: keep BGP at the edge; use iBGP across the AS to carry external routes; redistribute IGP→BGP only for local prefixes

front.text: Compare static (network + Null0) vs. dynamic (aggregate-address) BGP summarization.

rows: Summary always advertised, even if components are missing
Summary advertised only when at least one matching component is in the BGP table; auto-installs Null0 discard route on the originator

front.text: What does each option of 'aggregate-address' do?

back.text: aggregate-address 172.16.0.0 255.255.240.0 [summary-only] [as-set]

rows: Advertise summary AND all component routes
Advertise only the summary; component routes are SUPPRESSED on this router
Preserve path attributes from components; AS_Path includes AS_SET listed in {} (loop-prevention safe)

front.text: What is the BGP Atomic Aggregate attribute, and when is it set?

back.text: Atomic Aggregate is a well-known discretionary path attribute set on a summarized route to indicate that PATH INFORMATION HAS BEEN LOST during aggregation. By default, when an ABR-style summarizer creates an aggregate route, the original AS_Path, MED, and community info are dropped — the summary is treated as a brand-new route originating at the summarizer. The atomic-aggregate flag warns downstream routers of this loss. Using the 'as-set' keyword preserves path info and removes the atomic-aggregate marker.

front.text: Why might using 'aggregate-address ... as-set' cause a peer to reject the summary route?

back.text: The 'as-set' option includes ASNs from ALL component routes in the aggregate's AS_Path (in braces, e.g., {65100,65300}). If one of those component routes was originally learned from a downstream AS, that AS will see its own ASN in the aggregate's AS_Path when it receives the summary — and BGP loop prevention will discard the route. This commonly affects rings/triangles of ASs where the summarizer aggregates routes that originated in the same AS that should also receive the summary.

front.text: What does MP-BGP (RFC 4760) add to support IPv6, and how is the same TCP session reused?

back.text: MP-BGP uses the same BGP-4 protocol and the same TCP/179 session, but introduces two new optional non-transitive path attributes: Multiprotocol Reachable NLRI (MP_REACH_NLRI) for advertising IPv6 prefixes, and Multiprotocol Unreachable NLRI (MP_UNREACH_NLRI) for withdrawing them. AFI/SAFI fields tag each NLRI so the same session can carry IPv4, IPv6, VPNv4, VPNv6, etc. simultaneously. Older routers that don't support these attributes simply ignore them.

front.text: Show how to configure a BGP IPv6 unicast session between AS 65100 and AS 65200 using global IPv6 addresses.

back.text:
router bgp 65100
 bgp router-id 192.168.1.1
 no bgp default ipv4-unicast
 neighbor 2001:DB8:0:12::2 remote-as 65200
 !
 address-family ipv6 unicast
  neighbor 2001:DB8:0:12::2 activate
  network 2001:DB8::1/128
  network 2001:DB8:0:1::/64

items: BGP RID is still a 32-bit dotted-decimal value — set it manually if no IPv4 interface exists
Recommended: peer using global unicast (not link-local) addresses for stability
Use 'no bgp default ipv4-unicast' so each AF must be activated explicitly
Same TCP/179 session carries both IPv4 and IPv6 NLRIs if both AFs are activated

front.text: Which commands verify BGP IPv6 unicast neighbors, the BGP table, and routes installed in the RIB?

back.text:
show bgp ipv6 unicast summary
show bgp ipv6 unicast
show bgp ipv6 unicast <prefix>/<len>
show bgp ipv6 unicast neighbors <ipv6-addr>
show ipv6 route bgp
In 'show ipv6 route bgp', the next-hop is typically resolved to the neighbor's link-local address (FE80::/10) via recursive lookup, even if BGP peering uses a global unicast address.

front.text: Quick BGP facts to remember for the ENCOR exam.

items: Path vector protocol — uses AS_Path for loop prevention
Runs over TCP port 179; supports multi-hop sessions
Neighbors must be configured statically by IP and ASN — no auto-discovery
iBGP: same AS, AD 200, default TTL 255, AS_Path/next-hop unchanged
eBGP: different AS, AD 20, default TTL 1, AS_Path prepended, next-hop rewritten
Four message types: OPEN, UPDATE, NOTIFICATION, KEEPALIVE
Default hold = 180s, keepalive = 60s; lower of the two negotiated values is used
Six FSM states: Idle → Connect → Active → OpenSent → OpenConfirm → Established
Three BGP tables: Adj-RIB-In, Loc-RIB, Adj-RIB-Out
BGP advertises only the best path by default
MP-BGP (RFC 4760) carries IPv4, IPv6, VPNv4, VPNv6 etc. via AFI/SAFI
'aggregate-address' supports 'summary-only' (suppress components) and 'as-set' (preserve path attributes)
Atomic Aggregate flag indicates path information was lost during summarization

front.text: Why must iBGP routers in the same AS form a full mesh by default, and what's the formula for the number of sessions?

back.text: iBGP has a split-horizon rule: a route learned from one iBGP peer is NOT readvertised to other iBGP peers. This prevents loops within the AS but means every iBGP speaker must peer directly with every other iBGP speaker so all routers see all routes. The number of sessions in a full mesh of n routers is n(n-1)/2.
Scaling alternatives: BGP Route Reflectors (RFC 4456) and BGP Confederations (RFC 5065) reduce the iBGP full-mesh requirement.

front.text: What is a 'transit AS', and why do enterprises typically NOT operate as one?

back.text: A transit AS provides connectivity BETWEEN other autonomous systems — traffic from one AS passes through it to reach another AS. Service providers operate transit ASs. Enterprises are consumers of connectivity, not providers, so they should configure their BGP policy to advertise only their own prefixes (not propagate routes between two upstream ISPs). Otherwise, the enterprise risks becoming an unintended transit path with major performance and security implications.

front.text: When and how do you configure 'ebgp-multihop'?

back.text: eBGP packets default to TTL 1, so neighbors must be directly connected. If you're peering between loopbacks (or otherwise multiple hops away in eBGP), you must extend the TTL using 'ebgp-multihop'.

router bgp 65100
 neighbor 192.168.2.2 remote-as 65200
 neighbor 192.168.2.2 ebgp-multihop 3       ! TTL up to 3 hops
 neighbor 192.168.2.2 update-source Loopback0
Common pairing: 'ebgp-multihop' + 'update-source Loopback0' on both ends so the eBGP session survives if one of multiple inter-router links fails.

front.text: What does 'neighbor X update-source Loopback0' do, and why is it important for iBGP?

back.text: By default, BGP uses the egress interface IP as the source of the TCP session. 'update-source' overrides this to use the loopback's IP. For iBGP, peering between loopbacks (driven by IGP) means a single physical link failure won't drop the session — IGP just reroutes the underlying TCP/179 traffic. The peer must also be able to reach this loopback in its routing table, and the configured 'neighbor <ip>' on each side must match the source IP each peer is using.

front.text: What is 'next-hop-self', and why is it commonly applied on iBGP sessions?

back.text: On iBGP advertisements, the BGP next-hop is NOT changed by default — it remains the eBGP neighbor's IP from the original external advertisement. Internal routers may not know how to reach that external next-hop unless an IGP advertises it (or the eBGP link is in the IGP). 'neighbor <ip> next-hop-self' tells the iBGP-speaking border router to rewrite the next-hop to its own IP (typically a loopback) when advertising eBGP-learned routes inward, ensuring internal routers always have a reachable next-hop.

router bgp 65200
 neighbor 192.168.3.3 next-hop-self

front.text: What was the BGP synchronization rule, and what is its status today?

back.text: Historically, BGP would not advertise an iBGP-learned route to eBGP peers unless the same prefix existed in the IGP — to prevent black-holing in transit ASs without full iBGP mesh. Modern Cisco IOS XE has BGP synchronization DISABLED by default (since 12.2(8)T), which matches typical operational expectations. The feature is rarely needed today because most networks have full iBGP mesh, route reflectors, or are MPLS-enabled.

front.text: What is BGP soft reconfiguration inbound, and why use it?

back.text:
router bgp 65100
 neighbor 10.12.1.2 soft-reconfiguration inbound
!
! Then to reapply policy without dropping the session:
clear ip bgp 10.12.1.2 soft in

Soft reconfiguration stores the original Adj-RIB-In received from a peer in memory. When you change inbound policy (route-map, prefix-list), you can re-evaluate WITHOUT tearing down the session. The trade-off is memory usage. The modern alternative is route-refresh capability (negotiated automatically), which is non-disruptive and stateless.

front.text: Compare the BGP reset commands and their impact.

rows: Hard reset — tears down TCP session and reopens it; disruptive
Soft reset inbound — re-applies inbound policy without dropping session
Soft reset outbound — re-sends Adj-RIB-Out per outbound policy
Soft reset for all peers, both directions

front.text: How is MD5 authentication configured between BGP neighbors?

back.text:
router bgp 65100
 neighbor 10.12.1.2 password CiscoLab123

Both peers must configure the SAME password. BGP authentication is implemented as a TCP MD5 signature option (RFC 2385). Mismatched passwords cause the session to remain in Active/Idle and you'll see TCP-MD5-related errors in 'show tcp brief' or 'debug ip tcp transactions'.

front.text: What is BGP TTL security (Generalized TTL Security Mechanism, GTSM), and how is it different from ebgp-multihop?

back.text: neighbor 10.12.1.2 ttl-security hops 2

rows: Sets the OUTGOING TTL of BGP packets to a higher value (allows multi-hop sessions)
Sets a MINIMUM expected INCOMING TTL — drops packets that arrive with too low a TTL (DoS protection)

back.text: ttl-security and ebgp-multihop are mutually exclusive on the same neighbor. ttl-security forces packets to come from within 'hops' router hops away — anything farther is dropped at the kernel level, mitigating spoofed BGP attacks from across the Internet.

front.text: A BGP neighbor is stuck in the Active state. What are the most common causes and how do you diagnose?

items: TCP cannot complete to neighbor IP — check 'ping' and routing to neighbor
ACL or firewall blocking TCP/179
Wrong 'neighbor <ip>' configuration — IP must match the source IP the peer uses
Mismatched 'remote-as' value on either side
TTL issues — eBGP without 'ebgp-multihop' when peering loopbacks
Authentication mismatch (MD5 password)
'update-source' configured on one side but not the other (source IP mismatch)

back.text:
show bgp ipv4 unicast summary       ! state and uptime
show tcp brief                       ! TCP/179 status
debug ip bgp <neighbor>              ! detailed session events
debug ip tcp transactions            ! TCP handshake errors

front.text: A BGP neighbor is stuck in Idle, with the session repeatedly retrying. What does this usually indicate?

back.text: Idle with retries (incrementing ConnectRetryTimer) typically means BGP cannot reach the neighbor IP at all. Check that the neighbor's IP exists in the local RIB ('show ip route <neighbor-ip>'), that the neighbor is up, and that there are no routing/forwarding issues. After multiple failures, the ConnectRetryTimer doubles (60s, 120s, 240s, ...) — be patient or 'clear ip bgp <neighbor>' to retry immediately.

front.text: You configured 'network 192.168.1.0 mask 255.255.255.0' but the prefix doesn't appear in the BGP table. Why?

back.text: BGP requires an EXACT match in the local RIB for both network address and mask. If your interface is configured with a /24 but you typed mask 255.255.0.0, BGP won't install it. Verify with 'show ip route 192.168.1.0' and confirm the prefix length matches what 'network ... mask ...' specifies. Also confirm the route is up and reachable (not in 'is subnetted' summary).

front.text: What is the BGP Weight attribute, and how does it differ from other attributes?

items: Cisco-proprietary, NOT carried in BGP updates — locally significant only
Range 0–65,535; higher is preferred
Default = 0 for received routes; 32,768 for locally originated routes
First step in BGP best-path selection — beats every other attribute
Useful for influencing OUTBOUND traffic selection on a single router without affecting peers

front.text: What is BGP Local Preference, and what's its default value?

back.text: Local Preference (LOCAL_PREF) is a well-known discretionary path attribute that influences OUTBOUND traffic policy across an entire AS. Default = 100. Higher value is preferred. It is shared with all iBGP peers but never leaves the AS (not advertised over eBGP). Used to designate a preferred exit point from the AS to a destination prefix.

front.text: What is the BGP MED attribute, and what does it influence?

back.text: Multi-Exit Discriminator (MED) is an optional non-transitive path attribute. Lower value is preferred (opposite of Weight/LocalPref). It's a HINT to a neighboring AS about which entry point to prefer when multiple exist between the same two ASs. By default, MED is compared only between routes from the SAME AS. Default value when unset is 0, but you must configure 'bgp always-compare-med' to compare MED across different neighbor ASs.

front.text: What's the priority order of the most common BGP best-path attributes (just the top tiebreakers)?

items: Highest WEIGHT (Cisco-only, local)
Highest LOCAL_PREF (within the AS)
Locally originated (network, redistribute, aggregate)
Shortest AS_PATH
Lowest ORIGIN code (IGP < EGP < incomplete)
Lowest MED
Prefer eBGP over iBGP
Lowest IGP cost to the BGP next-hop
Oldest route / lowest neighbor RID / lowest neighbor IP (final tiebreakers)

back.text: Mnemonic: 'We Love Oranges As Oranges Mean Pure Refreshment' — Weight, LocalPref, Originated, AS_Path, Origin, MED, eBGP/iBGP (Path type), Reachability/RID.

front.text: What capabilities are typically exchanged in the BGP OPEN message?

items: Multiprotocol extensions (AFI/SAFI list — IPv4 unicast, IPv6 unicast, VPNv4, etc.)
Route Refresh capability (RFC 2918 — soft reset without storing Adj-RIB-In)
Four-octet ASN capability (32-bit ASN support, RFC 4893)
Graceful Restart (RFC 4724)
Add-Path (RFC 7911 — advertise multiple paths)
Enhanced Route Refresh

back.text: Verify with 'show bgp ipv4 unicast neighbors <ip>' under the 'Neighbor capabilities' section.

front.text: Interpret this 'show bgp ipv4 unicast summary' line:

Neighbor   V  AS    MsgRcvd MsgSent TblVer InQ OutQ Up/Down  State/PfxRcd
10.12.1.2  4  65200  342    341     12     0   0    02:14:33  5

items: Neighbor 10.12.1.2 in remote AS 65200; BGP version 4
Sessions has been Established for 2 hours, 14 minutes — Up/Down shows uptime when established
5 prefixes received from this neighbor (State/PfxRcd column)
InQ/OutQ = 0 — no backlog of messages waiting to process or send
342 messages received, 341 sent — healthy, balanced
TblVer 12 — last BGP table version sent to this peer

back.text: If the State/PfxRcd column shows 'Idle', 'Active', 'OpenSent', etc., the session is NOT established and the count column is replaced with the state name.

front.text: What's the difference between 'advertised-routes' and 'received-routes' in BGP show commands?

back.text:
show bgp ipv4 unicast neighbors 10.12.1.2 advertised-routes
show bgp ipv4 unicast neighbors 10.12.1.2 received-routes
show bgp ipv4 unicast neighbors 10.12.1.2 routes      ! routes accepted (after inbound policy)

front.text: What is a BGP Confederation?

back.text: A BGP Confederation (RFC 5065) divides a single AS into multiple sub-autonomous systems (member-AS), each running iBGP internally and a special form of eBGP between member-ASs. To external peers, all member-ASs appear as a single AS. This reduces the iBGP full-mesh requirement by limiting the mesh to within each member-AS, while preserving most BGP path attributes between member-ASs.

front.text: What is a BGP Route Reflector (RR), and what problem does it solve?

back.text: A Route Reflector (RFC 4456) is an iBGP speaker that selectively re-advertises iBGP-learned routes to other iBGP peers (its CLIENTS), bypassing the iBGP split-horizon rule. This eliminates the n(n-1)/2 full-mesh requirement: clients only need to peer with the RR(s) instead of every other iBGP router. RRs use Cluster-ID and Originator-ID attributes to prevent loops introduced by reflection.

router bgp 65100
 neighbor 10.0.0.2 remote-as 65100
 neighbor 10.0.0.2 route-reflector-client

front.text: How is the BGP next-hop attribute set differently for connected, locally originated, and eBGP-advertised routes?

rows: 0.0.0.0 (becomes the BGP session's source IP when advertised)
Set to the advertising router's IP (typically the eBGP link IP)

front.text: What does the 's' code next to a BGP route in 'show bgp' mean?

back.text: 's' = suppressed. The component route is in the local Loc-RIB but is NOT advertised to peers because of an 'aggregate-address ... summary-only' command. The summary route covering it IS advertised instead.
s>  172.16.1.0/24    0.0.0.0    0    32768    ?    ! suppressed by aggregate-address 172.16.0.0/20 summary-only

front.text: Decode common BGP status codes that may appear in 'show bgp ipv4 unicast' output.

rows: Path is valid (next-hop is reachable, AS_Path passes loop check)
RIB-failure (BGP best path could not install in RIB)
History (route was withdrawn but kept for dampening calculations)

front.text: How do you tune BGP keepalive and hold timers for a specific neighbor?

back.text:
router bgp 65100
 neighbor 10.12.1.2 timers <keepalive> <hold> [<min-hold>]
!
! Example: 10s keepalive, 30s hold
 neighbor 10.12.1.2 timers 10 30
After negotiation, the LOWER hold time of the two peers wins. Don't go too aggressive — BGP timers are designed for stability, and very short timers can cause flapping during transient delays.

front.text: Why is BGP slower to converge than IGPs, and what features improve convergence?

items: BGP is event-driven, not periodic — but designed for stability over speed
Long default timers (180s hold) prevent unnecessary route churn at Internet scale
Best-path algorithm runs through many tiebreakers per prefix
Convergence-improving features: BFD for sub-second peer failure detection, BGP fast external fallover (default), 'next-hop-tracking', BGP PIC (Prefix Independent Convergence)
Add-Path / multipath for ECMP and instant failover to backup paths

front.text: Show how to enable BFD for a BGP neighbor for sub-second failure detection.

back.text:
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 bfd interval 50 min_rx 50 multiplier 3
!
router bgp 65100
 neighbor 10.12.1.2 fall-over bfd
BFD detects neighbor failure in tens of milliseconds (50ms × 3 = 150ms above) instead of waiting 180 seconds for the BGP hold timer. Both peers must support and configure BFD on the link.

front.text: How do you protect a router from receiving too many BGP prefixes from a peer (e.g., a misconfigured ISP), and what does the configuration do?

back.text:
router bgp 65100
 neighbor 10.12.1.2 maximum-prefix 1000000 80 restart 5

items: Drops the BGP session if more than 1,000,000 prefixes are received
Logs a warning at 80% (800,000 prefixes)
Auto-restarts the session after 5 minutes (without 'restart', the session stays down until manually cleared)

back.text: Critical safety net — full Internet table is currently ~940,000 prefixes; without limits a peer leak could exhaust router memory and crash the device.

front.text: Final BGP quick-recall list for the ENCOR exam.

items: Path-vector EGP, TCP/179, no auto-discovery, neighbors are static
AS_Path is the loop-prevention mechanism — well-known mandatory
iBGP: same AS, AD 200, TTL 255, no AS_Path/next-hop change, full mesh required
eBGP: different AS, AD 20, TTL 1, AS_Path prepended, next-hop rewritten
Default hold = 180s, keepalive = 60s — lower of two wins
FSM: Idle → Connect → Active → OpenSent → OpenConfirm → Established
Three tables: Adj-RIB-In, Loc-RIB, Adj-RIB-Out
Network statement requires EXACT match in RIB
Best-path order: Weight → LocalPref → Local → AS_Path → Origin → MED → eBGP > iBGP → IGP cost → RID/IP
MP-BGP via AFI/SAFI: same TCP/179 carries IPv4, IPv6, VPNv4, VPNv6, etc.
Aggregate options: 'summary-only' suppresses components, 'as-set' preserves path info
Atomic-aggregate flag indicates path info loss
Soft reconfiguration / route-refresh = re-apply policy without dropping session
Scaling tools: Route Reflectors, Confederations
Safety: maximum-prefix, ttl-security, MD5 authentication, BFD

front.text: R1 is configured with hold time 180, R2 with hold time 60. What hold time will the established BGP session use?

back.text: 60 seconds. During the OPEN message exchange, both routers propose their hold time and the LOWER value is used. Keepalives are sent at 1/3 the negotiated hold time (20 seconds in this case).

front.text: What fields in the BGP OPEN message must match (or be valid) for the session to progress past OpenSent?

items: BGP version (must match — both speak BGP-4)
Source IP of the OPEN must match locally configured 'neighbor <ip>'
AS number in OPEN must match locally configured 'remote-as'
BGP RIDs must be unique and non-zero
Authentication parameters (TCP MD5 password, TTL) must be consistent

front.text: Why does BGP not advertise 'incremental updates' or refresh routes the way OSPF/IS-IS do?

back.text: BGP is designed for STABILITY, not constant churn. With 940,000+ prefixes on the Internet, periodic re-flooding (like OSPF's 30-min LSA refresh) would be devastating. BGP only sends UPDATEs when something actually changes, and downstream routers trust their existing Loc-RIB. KEEPALIVEs are heartbeats only — they don't carry route information. This is why a single misbehaving peer is preferred to be quietly held down rather than disturb the whole table.

front.text: What's the difference between 'show ip bgp summary' and 'show bgp ipv4 unicast summary'?

back.text: Functionally similar in IPv4-only scenarios. 'show ip bgp ...' is a legacy command from before MP-BGP and assumes IPv4 unicast. 'show bgp <afi> <safi> ...' is the modern, AFI/SAFI-aware syntax that scales to IPv6, VPNv4, VPNv6, EVPN, etc. Cisco recommends using the modern AFI/SAFI form for consistency across address families.

front.text: Looking at this output, which router initiated the TCP connection?

R1# show tcp brief
TCB       Local Address       Foreign Address       (state)
F6F84258  10.12.1.1.179      10.12.1.2.59884       ESTAB

back.text: R2 initiated the TCP connection. R2's source port is a random ephemeral port (59884), and the destination port is BGP's well-known port 179 on R1. The router that initiates connects from a random source port TO destination port 179 on the peer; the listener side shows port 179 as its local port.

front.text: What determines whether a BGP session is iBGP or eBGP?

back.text: The 'remote-as' value compared to the local 'router bgp <as>'. If they MATCH, the session is iBGP. If they DIFFER, the session is eBGP. The router automatically applies iBGP or eBGP behavior based on this comparison — TTL, AS_Path prepending, next-hop handling, AD, and split-horizon all change accordingly.

front.text: When AS 100 advertises a prefix to AS 200, who modifies AS_Path and how?

back.text: AS 100's eBGP-speaking router prepends its OWN ASN (100) to the AS_Path BEFORE sending the UPDATE to AS 200. So AS 200 receives the prefix with AS_Path = '100'. If AS 200 then advertises onward to AS 300, the AS 200 router prepends 200 → AS_Path = '200 100'. iBGP-learned routes do NOT modify the AS_Path, only eBGP advertisements do.

front.text: What two indicators in 'show bgp' tell you a route was locally originated by THIS router?

items: Next Hop = 0.0.0.0 (or :: for IPv6)
Weight = 32,768 (default for locally originated routes)
Empty AS_Path (no leading AS hops in the Path column)

back.text: Locally originated routes can come from network statements, redistribution, or aggregate-address commands.

front.text: What are the three modular configuration components of any BGP session?

items: BGP session parameters — peer ASN, authentication, timers, source interface, TTL
Address family initialization — IPv4/IPv6 unicast, VPNv4, multicast, etc.
Activation of the address family on the peer — 'neighbor <ip> activate' inside the AF

back.text: Until at least one address family is activated for a neighbor, BGP will not initiate the session.

front.text: List the five steps of the BGP route-processing pipeline from receiving an UPDATE to advertising it to peers.

items: Store route in Adj-RIB-In; apply inbound policy (route-map, prefix-list, distribute-list)
Update Loc-RIB with the post-policy route; clear Adj-RIB-In
Validity check: NLRI valid and BGP next-hop resolvable in RIB?
Run BGP best-path algorithm to choose best path per prefix
Install best path in RIB; apply outbound policy; place result in Adj-RIB-Out; advertise to peers

front.text: When BGP installs a CONNECTED route into Loc-RIB via a network statement, what BGP path attributes get set?
When BGP installs a STATIC or IGP-learned route into Loc-RIB via a network statement, what BGP path attributes get set?

rows: Next-hop IP from the RIB entry
Set to the IGP metric of the route

back.text: This is why a 'network' statement that matches an OSPF route can carry the OSPF cost as the BGP MED automatically.

front.text: Show how to redistribute connected routes and OSPF routes into BGP, and explain origin codes.

back.text:
router bgp 65100
 address-family ipv4 unicast
  redistribute connected
  redistribute ospf 1

Routes brought in via redistribution carry origin code '?' (incomplete) instead of 'i' (IGP). The metric of the original IGP route becomes the BGP MED. Many designs prefer 'network' statements for greater control over which prefixes enter BGP.

front.text: Why might 'show bgp ipv4 unicast' display two rows for the same prefix?

back.text: BGP can store MULTIPLE paths for a prefix in the Loc-RIB — typically one received from a peer and one originated locally, or paths received from multiple peers. Only the path marked with '>' is the best path (the one used for the RIB and advertised onward). Other paths are kept as alternates and re-evaluated if the best path is withdrawn.

front.text: How do you display the entire BGP table with full path attributes for every prefix?

back.text:
show bgp ipv4 unicast detail
!
! Or for a single prefix
show bgp ipv4 unicast 10.12.1.0
show bgp ipv6 unicast 2001:DB8::1/128

front.text: After 'aggregate-address 172.16.0.0 255.255.240.0 summary-only', the component prefixes appear in the local BGP table with an 's' code. Why?

back.text: 'summary-only' suppresses the components from being advertised to BGP peers — they remain in the Loc-RIB locally (so the router still has them for forwarding) but the 's' status code marks them as suppressed in the Adj-RIB-Out. Only the summary route is sent to peers. To unsuppress specific components, use the 'unsuppress-map' option on the neighbor or 'suppress-map' on the aggregate-address command.

front.text: Compare 'aggregate-address ... summary-only' vs. using a route-map to filter outbound prefixes.

rows: Creates a NEW summary route AND suppresses all matching components
Blocks specific prefixes from being advertised but does NOT create a summary

front.text: What is the AD of the Null0 discard route automatically installed for a BGP aggregate-address?

back.text: 200 (iBGP AD), shown in 'show ip route' as 'B [200/0]' pointing to Null0. This is intentional: the discard route exists for loop prevention but is at low preference, allowing more-specific routes from any source to override it. AD 200 is also why redistributing iBGP into IGPs without filtering can have unexpected results.

front.text: What does the 'atomic-aggregate' marker tell you in this output?

65200, (aggregated by 65200 192.168.2.2)
  10.23.1.2 from 10.23.1.2 (192.168.2.2)
    Origin IGP, metric 0, localpref 100, valid, external,
    atomic-aggregate, best

items: The route was aggregated by RID 192.168.2.2 in AS 65200
atomic-aggregate flag = path attributes from the original component routes were LOST during aggregation
AS_Path no longer reflects pre-aggregation hops
To preserve component path info, the aggregator would need to use the 'as-set' option

front.text: Why does the AS_Path display include curly braces?

back.text: The braces { } indicate an AS_SET — used by 'aggregate-address ... as-set'. ASNs inside the AS_SET are unordered (because they came from multiple component routes that may have had different AS_Paths). The AS_SET counts as a SINGLE hop in AS_Path length computations, regardless of how many ASNs it contains, but it preserves loop-prevention checks across all listed ASNs.

front.text: After enabling 'aggregate-address 192.168.0.0 255.255.0.0 as-set summary-only' on a router, the summary disappears from a downstream peer's table. Why?

back.text: If the AS_SET in the aggregate includes the downstream peer's own ASN (because one of the component routes originated in that AS), the peer will discard the aggregate as an AS_Path loop. Workarounds: don't use 'as-set' when aggregating routes that originated in adjacent ASs, or carefully scope the aggregate range to exclude prefixes from those ASs.

front.text: What are the two MP-BGP-specific path attributes introduced by RFC 4760?

rows: Carry NEW reachable NLRIs (with AFI/SAFI tag) and the next-hop

back.text: Both are optional non-transitive — older BGP routers that don't understand them safely ignore them, allowing graceful interoperability with non-MP-BGP speakers.

front.text: What are the AFI/SAFI numbers for IPv4 unicast and IPv6 unicast?
Why is 'no bgp default ipv4-unicast' considered a best practice?

back.text: By default IOS XE auto-activates the IPv4 unicast AF for every neighbor. This implicit behavior is convenient but can cause confusion when adding IPv6 or VPNv4 neighbors who shouldn't carry IPv4. Disabling the default forces every AF to be activated explicitly per neighbor, making configuration intent clearer and preventing accidental IPv4 advertisements over IPv6-only sessions.

front.text: BGP IPv6 peering is established using global unicast addresses, but 'show ipv6 route bgp' shows next-hops as link-local addresses. Why?

back.text: When a router receives a BGP-IPv6 update, it stores the global unicast next-hop in the BGP table. But to FORWARD packets, the router does a recursive lookup that resolves to the on-link link-local neighbor address (FE80::/10) and the egress interface — because IPv6 forwarding always uses link-local for next-hop resolution on shared media. The BGP table shows the global next-hop; the RIB shows the resolved link-local that the forwarding plane actually uses.

front.text: Why is using GLOBAL UNICAST addresses recommended over LINK-LOCAL addresses for IPv6 BGP peering?

items: Link-local addresses can change on hardware swaps or interface moves (EUI-64 derivation)
Global addresses are stable and routable, so multi-hop peering and loopback peering work cleanly
Operational simplicity — no per-interface qualifier needed in 'neighbor' command
Link-local peering is supported but adds operational risk and complexity
Most enterprise/SP designs use globals for predictability

front.text: Show how to summarize IPv6 prefixes 2001:DB8::/64, 2001:DB8:0:1::/64, 2001:DB8:0:2::/64, 2001:DB8:0:3::/64 into a single advertisement, suppressing the components.

back.text:
router bgp 65200
 address-family ipv6 unicast
  aggregate-address 2001:DB8::/62 summary-only
/62 covers /64 hextet values 0–3 (2 bits = 4 networks). Same syntax and options as IPv4 aggregate-address — 'summary-only' to suppress components, 'as-set' to preserve path information.

front.text: You need to summarize 2001:DB8:0:23::/64 along with 2001:DB8:0:0::/64 through 2001:DB8:0:3::/64. What's the smallest IPv6 summary that covers all of them?

back.text: 2001:DB8:0:0::/58. The fourth hextet 0x23 in decimal is 35, requiring 6 bits to represent (covering values 0 through 63 = 0x3F). A /58 prefix gives you 6 host bits in the fourth hextet (64 - 58 = 6), summarizing 2001:DB8:0:0::/64 through 2001:DB8:0:3F::/64. Always think in HEX, not decimal — '23' in IPv6 means 0x23 = 35 decimal, not twenty-three.

front.text: Interpret this snippet of 'show bgp ipv4 unicast neighbors':

Neighbor capabilities:
  Route refresh: advertised and received(new)
  Four-octets ASN Capability: advertised and received
  Address family IPv4 Unicast: advertised and received
  Enhanced Refresh Capability: advertised

items: Both peers support Route Refresh (RFC 2918) — soft reset is non-disruptive
Both support 32-bit ASNs (RFC 4893)
IPv4 unicast AF is negotiated on both sides — routes can be exchanged
Local side advertised Enhanced Refresh, but the peer didn't acknowledge — only one-way support, so basic refresh is what gets used

front.text: Final BGP fundamentals quick-recall list — anything you should be able to answer instantly.

items: BGP = path-vector EGP, TCP/179, RFC 4271
ASN ranges: 1–64,511 public 16-bit; 64,512–65,534 private 16-bit; 65,536–4,199,999,999 public 32-bit; 4.2B–4.29B private 32-bit
PA categories: Well-known mandatory / discretionary; Optional transitive / non-transitive
Loop prevention: AS_Path (eBGP) and split-horizon (iBGP)
Default timers: hold 180s, keepalive 60s; lower negotiated value wins
FSM: Idle → Connect → Active → OpenSent → OpenConfirm → Established
iBGP AD = 200, eBGP AD = 20, default eBGP TTL = 1, default iBGP TTL = 255
Network statement requires EXACT match in RIB
BGP advertises only best path by default
MP-BGP: AFI/SAFI fields tag NLRIs; same TCP/179 for IPv4/IPv6/VPN
MP_REACH_NLRI / MP_UNREACH_NLRI = optional non-transitive
aggregate-address: summary-only suppresses, as-set preserves PAs
Atomic-aggregate flag = path info lost
Default aggregate Null0 AD = 200
BGP RID is 32-bit dotted-decimal; required (non-zero) for adjacency
Best-path order: Weight → LocalPref → Local → AS_Path → Origin → MED → eBGP/iBGP → IGP cost → RID/IP
Scaling: Route Reflectors and Confederations replace iBGP full-mesh

front.text: Given two switch ports configured with various combinations of `dynamic desirable`, `dynamic auto`, `trunk`, `trunk nonegotiate`, and `access`, can you predict whether DTP will negotiate a trunk between them and why?

rows: Usually yes from trunk side unless nonegotiate configured
Static trunk can trigger the auto side through DTP.
Auto waits for DTP; static side does not send it.

back.text: Key takeaways: (1) `auto` + `auto` never trunks — at least one side must actively initiate. (2) `nonegotiate` disables DTP entirely, so both sides typically must be statically set to `trunk nonegotiate`. (3) For deterministic, secure trunks, prefer `switchport mode trunk` with `switchport nonegotiate` on both ends.

front.text: What is BGP multihoming, and why is it used?

back.text: BGP multihoming is the practice of establishing more than one BGP peering session — typically to multiple service providers or via multiple links — to provide redundancy, load distribution, and traffic engineering. It eliminates single points of failure and enables BGP's best-path algorithm to choose optimal routes when more than one path is available.

front.text: Compare four common BGP multihoming designs and their resilience characteristics.

rows: Single router, single SP, dual links
Single router, single SP, dual PE
Two routers, two SPs (iBGP between CEs)
CE1 → SP1, CE2 → SP2, iBGP CE1↔CE2
Link, PE, SP outage, and CE failure

back.text: The dual-router/dual-SP design with iBGP between CEs is the most resilient enterprise design.

front.text: Why is it dangerous for an enterprise multihomed to two ISPs to inadvertently become a transit AS, and how is it prevented?

back.text: If an enterprise advertises routes learned from one ISP to another ISP, third-party traffic between those ISPs may transit the enterprise's network — saturating WAN links and disrupting legitimate enterprise traffic. Prevention: apply outbound BGP route filtering (route-map, prefix-list, or AS_Path filter) so the enterprise advertises ONLY its own locally originated prefixes to each upstream provider.

front.text: In a multi-SP enterprise WAN design, where is transit routing acceptable and where is it not?

items: Acceptable at data centers or designated hub sites — bandwidth is sized for transit, traffic patterns are predictable
NOT acceptable at branch sites — branch WAN links are sized only for local traffic, leading to oversaturation
NOT acceptable at Internet edge — risk of becoming a transit AS
Prevention: apply outbound filters at branches that only advertise LAN-facing prefixes, never re-advertise WAN-learned routes

front.text: Compare standard and extended ACLs for BGP route filtering.

back.text: For BGP, extended ACLs uniquely use the destination field to match the network MASK — different behavior from how IGPs use extended ACLs.

front.text: What does the extended ACL `permit ip 172.16.0.0 0.0.255.255 255.255.255.128 0.0.0.127` match in BGP?

items: Source 172.16.0.0 wildcard 0.0.255.255 → matches any network starting with 172.16.x.x
Destination 255.255.255.128 wildcard 0.0.0.127 → matches subnet masks 255.255.255.128 (/25) through 255.255.255.255 (/32)
Result: any 172.16.x.x prefix with a prefix length /25 to /32

back.text: BGP uses extended ACLs to encode 'network + prefix-length range' before prefix lists were introduced. Most modern designs prefer prefix lists for clarity.

front.text: What is the full syntax of an IPv4 prefix list, and what's the rule between high-order bit count, ge, and le?

back.text: ip prefix-list NAME [seq <num>] {permit | deny} <network>/<prefix-len> [ge <ge-value>] [le <le-value>]
Rule: high-order bit count < ge-value <= le-value. Example: '10.0.0.0/8 ge 22 le 26' matches any prefix in 10.x.x.x/8 with a length between 22 and 26.

items: 10.168.0.0/13 — NO (length /13 is less than ge 24)
10.168.0.0/24 — YES (high-order 13 bits match, length /24 ≥ 24)
10.173.1.0/28 — YES (high-order 13 bits of 10.168.0.0/13 cover 10.168.x.x – 10.175.x.x; 10.173 is in range, length /28 ≥ 24)
10.104.0.0/24 — NO (10.104 doesn't fall within the /13 range starting at 10.168.0.0)

front.text: What do these BGP regex operators mean, and what AS_Path patterns do they typically match?

rows: Routes received directly FROM AS 200 (next-hop AS)
Routes that pass through AS 200 anywhere in the path
Routes from a directly connected AS (single AS hop)

back.text: show bgp ipv4 unicast regexp _200_

front.text: What are the four components of a route map?

items: Sequence number — controls processing order, top-down
Conditional matching criteria — match prefix, AS_Path, community, metric, tag, etc.
Processing action — permit or deny (default is permit)
Optional action — set BGP attributes (local-preference, weight, MED, AS_Path prepend, community, next-hop, metric, etc.)

back.text:
route-map NAME [permit | deny] [seq]
 match ...
 set ...

front.text: What are the default behaviors of a route map when key elements are omitted?

items: No action specified → default is 'permit'
No sequence number → auto-incremented by 10
No match statement → matches ALL prefixes (implicit match-all)
Implicit deny at the end — any prefix not matched by a permit sequence is dropped
Processing stops after the first matching sequence (unless 'continue' is used)

front.text: What's the difference between multiple VARIABLES on one match command vs multiple match LINES?

rows: match ip address ACL1 ACL2 (one line, multiple variables)
OR — any one ACL match qualifies
match ip address ACL1 + match metric 100 (multiple lines)
AND — every match line must succeed

back.text: If a route is denied by an ACL referenced in 'match ip address', that's treated as 'not matched' for that route-map sequence — the route falls through to the next sequence.

front.text: Name common BGP-related 'set' actions in a route map.

rows: Influence outbound exit point within the AS
Make path appear longer to influence inbound traffic
Set MED for influencing inbound traffic from a peer AS

front.text: Compare the four BGP route filtering methods available on IOS XE.

back.text: Restriction: a single neighbor cannot use a distribute-list AND a prefix-list in the same direction at the same time.

front.text: Show how to filter inbound BGP routes from neighbor 10.12.1.2 to permit only 192.168.x.x/32 prefixes.

back.text:
ip access-list extended ACL-ALLOW
 permit ip 192.168.0.0 0.0.255.255 host 255.255.255.255
!
router bgp 65100
 neighbor 10.12.1.2 remote-as 65200
 address-family ipv4 unicast
  neighbor 10.12.1.2 activate
  neighbor 10.12.1.2 distribute-list ACL-ALLOW in

front.text: Configure an AS_Path ACL that prevents an enterprise (AS 65200) from becoming a transit AS, advertising only locally originated routes outbound to all eBGP neighbors.

back.text:
ip as-path access-list 1 permit ^$
!
router bgp 65200
 neighbor 10.12.1.1 remote-as 65100
 neighbor 10.23.1.3 remote-as 65300
 address-family ipv4 unicast
  neighbor 10.12.1.1 activate
  neighbor 10.23.1.3 activate
  neighbor 10.12.1.1 filter-list 1 out
  neighbor 10.23.1.3 filter-list 1 out
The regex ^$ matches an EMPTY AS_Path — only routes originated locally (network statement, redistribute, or aggregate). Apply outbound to every eBGP peer to fully prevent transit.

front.text: After applying a new inbound route map, what command refreshes routes WITHOUT tearing down the BGP session?

back.text:
clear bgp ipv4 unicast 10.12.1.2 soft in
!
! Or for all peers:
clear bgp ipv4 unicast * soft in

Most modern peers negotiate the Route Refresh capability automatically. The soft reset triggers the peer to re-advertise its prefixes so inbound policy can be re-evaluated. Use 'soft out' for outbound policy changes.

front.text: What are BGP communities, and what type of path attribute are they?

back.text: BGP communities are 32-bit tags that can be attached to a prefix and carried across ASs to signal routing policy. They are an OPTIONAL TRANSITIVE path attribute — recognized when supported, ignored otherwise, and propagated between ASs. Format: full 32-bit number or 'new format' as ASN:value (e.g., 65100:100). Private community convention: first 16 bits = origin ASN, second 16 bits = arbitrary policy code.

front.text: Name the four common WELL-KNOWN BGP communities and what each does.

rows: Do NOT advertise to ANY peer (iBGP or eBGP)
Do not advertise outside the local sub-AS (used in confederations)
Do not advertise to any eBGP peer (iBGP advertisement allowed)

back.text: Well-known communities exist in the range 0xFFFF0000–0xFFFFFFFF and must be supported by every BGP implementation.

front.text: Why do BGP communities sometimes 'disappear' between two routers, and how do you fix it?

back.text: By default, IOS XE does NOT send BGP communities to a neighbor. You must enable community advertisement explicitly per neighbor.

router bgp 65100
 address-family ipv4 unicast
  neighbor 10.12.1.2 send-community           ! standard (default)
  neighbor 10.12.1.2 send-community extended  ! extended
  neighbor 10.12.1.2 send-community both      ! both

front.text: What command displays BGP communities in the readable AS:value format instead of a 32-bit decimal?

back.text: ip bgp-community new-format
Configured globally. Without it, the community 65100:100 would display as the decimal value 4,278,190,180 — much harder to interpret.

front.text: Show how to deny routes carrying community 333:333 and accept all other routes from a peer, setting weight 111.

back.text:
ip community-list 100 permit 333:333
!
route-map COMMUNITY-CHECK deny 10
 match community 100
route-map COMMUNITY-CHECK permit 20
 set weight 111
!
router bgp 65100
 address-family ipv4 unicast
  neighbor 10.12.1.2 route-map COMMUNITY-CHECK in

Standard community lists are 1–99; expanded (regex) community lists are 100–500 — but standard community lists CAN be numbered up to 500 as well. Multiple communities listed on one line require ALL to match.

front.text: What's the difference between `set community 10:23` and `set community 10:23 additive`?

rows: OVERWRITES all existing communities — only 10:23 remains
APPENDS 10:23 to the existing community list — original tags preserved

back.text: Use 'additive' when you want to add policy tags without losing existing ones (e.g., from upstream providers).

front.text: How can advertising both a /16 summary and longer-prefix /24s from different routers steer inbound Internet traffic deterministically?

back.text: Routers always select the LONGEST PREFIX MATCH for forwarding, regardless of BGP attributes. By advertising 100.64.0.0/16 from BOTH border routers but advertising 100.64.1.0/24 from R1 and 100.64.2.0/24 from R2, traffic destined for 100.64.1.x always enters via R1 (longest match wins) and traffic for 100.64.2.x always enters via R2. If either router fails, traffic falls back to the /16 summary on the surviving router.
Most ISPs filter prefixes longer than /24 (IPv4) or /48 (IPv6) on the global Internet, so plan accordingly.

front.text: List the BGP best-path algorithm steps in order.

items: Highest WEIGHT (Cisco-only, local)
Highest LOCAL_PREF
Locally originated (network, redistribute, aggregate) wins
AIGP metric (lower is better)
Shortest AS_Path
Lowest ORIGIN code (IGP < EGP < incomplete)
Lowest MED
Prefer eBGP over iBGP
Lowest IGP cost to BGP next-hop
Oldest established eBGP session
Lowest neighbor RID (or originator ID)
Minimum cluster list length
Lowest neighbor IP address

back.text: Mnemonic: 'We Love Oranges As Oranges Mean Pure Refreshment' — Weight, LocalPref, Originated, AS_Path, Origin, MED, Path type (eBGP/iBGP), Reachability (IGP cost) / RID.

front.text: Compare BGP weight and local preference attributes.
What rule restricts when MED is used in best-path selection, and how can it be overridden?

back.text: By default, MED is ONLY compared between paths from the SAME neighbor AS. If the routes come from different ASs, MED is skipped in best-path. Lower MED is preferred. To force comparison across different ASs, use:

router bgp 65100
 bgp always-compare-med
MED is an optional non-transitive attribute used as a HINT to influence inbound traffic from a neighbor AS. By default, routes without a MED are treated as 0 (most preferred) per RFC 4451.

front.text: Your AS is dual-homed and you want INBOUND traffic to prefer ISP-A over ISP-B. How do you influence this with route maps?

back.text:
route-map PREPEND-OUT permit 10
 set as-path prepend 65100 65100 65100
!
router bgp 65100
 address-family ipv4 unicast
  neighbor <ISP-B-IP> route-map PREPEND-OUT out

Prepend your own ASN multiple times on advertisements to ISP-B. The longer AS_Path makes ISP-B's path look less attractive across the Internet, so external traffic prefers entering via ISP-A. Common practice: prepend 1–3 times (more rarely helps).

front.text: Which command shows ALL paths for a prefix and explains exactly WHY each path won or lost the best-path comparison?

back.text: show bgp ipv4 unicast <prefix>/<len> best-path-reason
Output adds a line like 'Best Path Evaluation: Lower weight' or 'Longer AS path' to each path, making troubleshooting BGP best-path decisions much faster than reading raw attributes manually.

front.text: Final advanced-BGP quick-recall list for the ENCOR exam.

items: Multihoming = redundancy through multiple BGP sessions
Avoid becoming a transit AS — filter outbound to advertise only locally originated routes
Extended ACLs match BGP networks via source (network) and destination (mask) fields
Prefix list rule: high-order bit count < ge < le; 'ge' = minimum prefix length, 'le' = maximum
Common regex: ^$ (local), ^200_ (from AS 200), _200$ (originated AS 200), _200_ (transits AS 200)
Route map: sequence + match + action + optional set; default is permit; implicit deny at end
Multiple match VARIABLES on one line = OR; multiple match LINES = AND
BGP filtering options: distribute-list (ACL), prefix-list, AS_Path filter-list, route-map
Cannot use both distribute-list and prefix-list on same neighbor in same direction
BGP communities = optional transitive 32-bit tag; new-format = AS:value
Send-community must be enabled per neighbor — NOT default in IOS XE
Well-known communities: Internet, No_Advertise, Local-AS, No_Export
Best-path order: Weight → LocalPref → Local → AIGP → AS_Path → Origin → MED → eBGP > iBGP → IGP cost → Oldest eBGP → RID → Cluster list → Lowest IP
MED only compared between routes from same AS by default; use 'bgp always-compare-med' to override
Longest prefix match always wins at the forwarding plane — use it for deterministic ingress traffic engineering
'show bgp ipv4 unicast <prefix> best-path-reason' shows why each path won/lost

front.text: An enterprise uses one CE router with two links to the same PE in the same SP. What types of failures does this design protect against, and what does it NOT protect against?

back.text: Improvement path: dual links to two different PEs (protects against PE failure) → two SPs (protects against SP outage) → two CE routers with iBGP (protects against CE failure).

front.text: In a multihomed BGP design, what is asymmetric routing and why is it problematic?

back.text: Asymmetric routing occurs when traffic from A to B follows a different path than traffic from B to A. In a multihomed enterprise, this can happen because outbound path is influenced by local-pref, weight, or BGP best-path while inbound path is determined by other ASs' policies. Problems include: stateful firewalls/IPS dropping flows, NAT inconsistency, suboptimal latency, harder troubleshooting, and unpredictable bandwidth utilization. Designs aiming for deterministic, symmetric routing simplify operations.

front.text: Why is ACE order critical in any ACL used for BGP filtering?

back.text: ACLs (and similarly prefix lists) process top-down and STOP at the first match. A more specific 'permit' placed AFTER a broader 'deny' is unreachable. Always order ACEs from most specific to most general. There is an implicit 'deny any' at the end of every ACL, which catches everything not explicitly permitted.

front.text: When is a STANDARD ACL appropriate for BGP filtering, and what's its limitation?

back.text: Standard ACLs match only the source (network) field, so they can permit or deny prefixes based on the network address but cannot match a specific prefix length. For BGP this means a standard ACL would match BOTH 10.0.0.0/8 and 10.0.0.0/24 if both fall within the same source pattern. Use extended ACLs or — better — prefix lists when you need to filter by prefix length.

front.text: How does sequence numbering work for IP prefix lists, and why is it important to leave gaps?

items: If 'seq' is omitted, IOS auto-increments by 5 (first entry = 5)
Sequence numbers determine the top-down order of evaluation
Prefix lists can NOT be re-sequenced once configured — leave gaps to insert future entries
To delete a single entry, reference it by sequence number, not just by network
Best practice: 'seq 5, seq 10, seq 15...' makes maintenance much easier

front.text: Compare four common prefix list patterns: exact match, any prefix length, ge only, ge+le.

rows: Any prefix within 10.0.0.0/8 with length 8 to 32
Any prefix within 10.0.0.0/8 with length 16 to 32
Any prefix within 10.0.0.0/8 with length 22 to 26
Any IPv4 prefix of any length (catch-all)

front.text: Show how to create an IPv6 prefix list that permits ALL prefixes under 2001:db8::/32 with any length up to /128.

back.text: ipv6 prefix-list IPV6-DOC seq 5 permit 2001:db8::/32 le 128
IPv6 prefix lists work identically to IPv4 — same ge/le rules. Always think in HEX when calculating ranges, not decimal.

front.text: You want to display all BGP routes that PASS THROUGH AS 200 (regardless of source or destination). What command do you use?

back.text: show bgp ipv4 unicast regexp _200_

rows: All routes that traverse AS 200 anywhere in the path
Routes received DIRECTLY from AS 200 (it's the next hop)

front.text: What is a BGP 'looking glass', and what is it commonly used for?

back.text: A BGP looking glass is a publicly accessible router (or web portal) that lets users run read-only BGP show commands and traceroutes against the public Internet routing table. Common uses: verifying that your prefixes are being advertised globally as intended, debugging Internet reachability issues, learning regex with real-world AS_Paths, and troubleshooting hijack/leak incidents. Examples: bgp4.as, lg.he.net, ripe.net.

front.text: Given this configuration, what happens to a route that DOESN'T match the prefix list?

route-map QUESTION deny 10
 match ip address prefix-list PrefixRFC1918
route-map QUESTION permit 20
 set metric 200

back.text: The route falls through to sequence 20, which has no match (matches everything), and is permitted with metric 200. Sequence 10 is a 'deny' — it would only block routes that DO match the RFC1918 prefix list. Routes not in RFC1918 fall through; routes in RFC1918 are dropped at sequence 10.

front.text: What does it mean when an ACL or prefix list referenced in a route map's 'match' clause has a DENY entry?

back.text: A 'deny' inside the referenced ACL/prefix list means the route does NOT match that condition — the route does not qualify for THIS sequence and falls through to the next. The deny in the ACL is NOT applied to the route itself; it only affects whether the route is considered a match for that sequence. The route map's own permit/deny then determines what happens to matched routes.

front.text: What does the route map 'continue' keyword do, and when is it used?

back.text: Normally a route map stops processing after the first sequence match. The 'continue' command (optionally followed by a target sequence number) lets processing continue past the matched sequence to additional sequences — allowing multiple 'set' actions for the same route.

route-map EXAMPLE permit 10
 match ip address ACL-ONE
 set metric 20
 continue 20
!
route-map EXAMPLE permit 20
 set ip next-hop 10.12.1.1
Use sparingly — 'continue' adds complexity and makes troubleshooting harder. Most designs avoid it.

front.text: Compare BGP 'distribute-list' and 'prefix-list' — when do you use each?

back.text: Restriction: a single neighbor cannot apply BOTH a distribute-list and a prefix-list in the SAME direction.

front.text: When applying an AS_Path filter, what's the difference between applying it 'in' vs 'out'?

rows: Filters routes RECEIVED from this neighbor before they reach the local Loc-RIB
Filters routes SENT to this neighbor — runs against Adj-RIB-Out

back.text: Outbound AS_Path filter '^$' is the classic enterprise control to prevent transit AS behavior — only locally originated routes leave.

front.text: Can you apply a prefix list AND an AS_Path filter on the same BGP neighbor at the same time?

back.text: Yes. Different filter TYPES can coexist on the same neighbor. You can simultaneously apply: prefix-list + AS_Path filter-list + route-map. The only restriction is that you cannot use distribute-list and prefix-list in the SAME direction. Routes must pass ALL configured filters to be accepted.

front.text: Compare BGP hard reset vs soft reset commands and impacts.

rows: clear bgp ipv4 unicast <ip> soft in
Re-evaluate inbound policy via Route Refresh
clear bgp ipv4 unicast <ip> soft out

front.text: What is the BGP Route Refresh capability, and why is it preferred over storing Adj-RIB-In?

back.text: Route Refresh (RFC 2918) is a BGP capability negotiated in the OPEN message that lets one peer ASK the other to re-advertise its full route table for a specific AFI/SAFI on demand. This means inbound policy can be re-applied WITHOUT keeping a memory-heavy copy of all received routes (soft-reconfiguration inbound) and WITHOUT tearing down the session (hard reset). Verify support in 'show bgp neighbors <ip>' under Neighbor capabilities → 'Route refresh: advertised and received'.

front.text: How is the community 65100:200 represented as a 32-bit decimal number, and why does the 'new format' help?

back.text: 65100:200 = (65100 × 65536) + 200 = 4,266,915,400 in decimal. Without 'new format', BGP would display this 32-bit number, which is unreadable. The command 'ip bgp-community new-format' tells the router to display all communities as ASN:value pairs, making them human-readable for matching the 'first 16 bits = AS, second 16 bits = policy code' convention.

front.text: Name typical real-world use cases for BGP communities.

items: Tagging routes by geographic region (e.g., 65100:1=Americas, 65100:2=EMEA)
Signaling traffic engineering preferences to upstream ISPs
Identifying customer-originated vs peer-learned vs internal prefixes
Triggering blackholing/RTBH (e.g., 65100:666 = drop on edge)
Indicating no-export, no-advertise, no-export-subconfed (well-known)
Differentiating routes by service type (e.g., voice vs data) for QoS or path selection
Providing input for downstream route-maps that set local-pref or filter

front.text: What's the difference between these two community list configurations, and how do they match a route carrying communities 100:1 and 100:2?

ip community-list 1 permit 100:1 100:2
!
ip community-list 2 permit 100:1
ip community-list 2 permit 100:2

rows: AND — route must carry BOTH 100:1 AND 100:2

front.text: What does the 'exact' keyword do in 'match community <list> exact'?

back.text: Without 'exact', the route-map matches if the route carries AT LEAST the listed communities (additional ones are OK). With 'exact', the route's community set must EXACTLY equal the list — no more, no less. Useful when policy must be very specific, e.g., matching only routes that carry exactly the communities you tag and no others.

front.text: Show how to display all BGP routes that carry a specific community.

back.text:
show bgp ipv4 unicast community 333:333
!
! Detailed view including communities for any route:
show bgp ipv4 unicast 10.23.1.0/24
Always make sure 'ip bgp-community new-format' is enabled, otherwise the community will display as a 32-bit decimal number that's hard to read.

front.text: Step 3 of BGP best-path is 'locally originated wins'. What counts as locally originated?

items: Routes injected via 'network' statement (highest preference)
Routes injected via 'aggregate-address' (locally summarized)
Routes redistributed into BGP
Routes received from BGP peers (lowest in this step)

back.text: If two paths tie on Weight, LocalPref, and origination type — locally originated still beats peer-learned. This step ensures locally originated routes are preferred when the rest is equal.

front.text: What is AIGP (Accumulated IGP) and when is it useful?

back.text: AIGP is an optional non-transitive BGP path attribute that lets BGP carry an end-to-end IGP-style metric across multiple administrative ASs that all belong to the same operator. Used in large multi-AS service provider networks where consistent metric-based path selection is desired. Behavior: paths with AIGP are preferred over paths without; AIGP increments by IGP cost as routes traverse ASs; lower AIGP wins. AIGP is one of the highest-priority steps in best-path (after weight/local-pref) and is generally seen only in advanced SP designs.

front.text: Why is prepending more than 2–3 ASNs usually ineffective for inbound traffic engineering?

items: AS_Path is just one of many best-path criteria — Weight, Local Preference, and policy decisions UPSTREAM may override path length
Many SPs deploy local routing policies (e.g., 'prefer customer routes regardless of length')
Excessive prepending can be filtered by some ISPs as a policy violation
Real-world testing shows diminishing returns past 2–3 prepends
Better alternative: use BGP communities defined by your ISP to influence local-pref upstream

front.text: What is the BGP origin preference order, and what causes each value to be set?

back.text: If you redistribute connected/static into BGP, those routes get '?'. To make them appear as 'i', use 'network' statements instead OR set origin igp via a route-map.

front.text: Why does BGP prefer the OLDEST eBGP path as a tiebreaker, and what's the downside?

back.text: Stability — preferring the long-established path avoids unnecessary route flaps when newer paths appear and disappear. Downside: this rule is non-deterministic from a design perspective. The 'best' path becomes whichever session came up first, which depends on boot order and operational events. To get deterministic behavior, configure 'bgp bestpath compare-routerid' which forces the lowest router ID to win at this step instead.

front.text: What is the BGP Cluster List attribute and where does it appear in best-path selection?

back.text: Cluster List is a non-transitive BGP attribute used by Route Reflectors to prevent loops within iBGP. Each route reflector appends its Cluster ID when reflecting a route. In best-path selection (after RID comparison), the path with the SHORTEST cluster list wins — meaning the path that traveled through fewer route reflectors is preferred. Cluster ID is locally significant per reflector cluster and is not advertised externally.

front.text: Interpret this output:

BGP routing table entry for 172.16.1.0/24, version 6
Paths: (2 available, best #1, table default)
  300 100
    192.168.6.6 (metric 131072) from 192.168.6.6 (192.168.6.6)
      Origin IGP, metric 0, localpref 333, valid, internal, best
      Best Path Evaluation: Overall best path
  200 100
    100.64.24.2 from 100.64.24.2 (192.168.2.2)
      Origin IGP, localpref 111, valid, external
      Best Path Evaluation: Lower local preference

items: Two paths exist for 172.16.1.0/24
Best path = path #1 (via AS 300 → AS 100), localpref 333
Path #2 (via AS 200 → AS 100) lost because of LOWER LOCAL PREFERENCE (111 vs 333)
Local-pref overrode the fact that path #2 was eBGP (external) and would have won the eBGP-over-iBGP rule lower in the algorithm
Best-path-reason output explicitly explains why each path was rejected — extremely useful for troubleshooting

front.text: Final advanced-BGP fast-recall list — must-know facts for the exam.

items: Multihoming = redundancy; avoid transit AS via outbound filtering (^$)
Standard ACL = source only; Extended ACL for BGP = source matches network, destination matches mask
Prefix list rule: high-order bit count < ge ≤ le; 0.0.0.0/0 le 32 = catch-all
Common regex: ^$ (local), ^200_ (from AS 200), _200$ (originated AS 200), _200_ (transits AS 200)
Route map: sequence + match + set; default permit; implicit deny at end; 'continue' allows multi-sequence processing
Multiple match VARIABLES on one line = OR; multiple match LINES = AND
Filtering tools: distribute-list, prefix-list, AS_Path filter-list, route-map; cannot mix distribute-list + prefix-list same direction
'soft' reset uses Route Refresh (RFC 2918) — non-disruptive policy reapplication
BGP communities = optional transitive 32-bit tags; format ASN:value with 'new-format'
Well-known: Internet, No_Advertise, Local-AS, No_Export
'send-community' is NOT default in IOS XE — must enable per neighbor
Setting communities: default OVERWRITES; use 'additive' to append
'match community' ... 'exact' = full set match required
Best-path order: Weight → LocalPref → Local origin → AIGP → AS_Path → Origin → MED → eBGP > iBGP → IGP cost → oldest eBGP → RID → cluster list → lowest IP
MED only compared between same-AS paths by default; use 'bgp always-compare-med' to override
Longest prefix match always wins at forwarding — use it for ingress traffic engineering
'show bgp ipv4 unicast <prefix> best-path-reason' = the single best command for path-selection troubleshooting

front.text: Why do many enterprises choose two DIFFERENT service providers when multihoming, even though using one SP is simpler?

items: Protects against full SP-wide outages (control plane or transport)
Avoids dependence on a single SP's policy or routing decisions
Provides path optimization through BGP best-path between two SPs
Reduces risk of an SP merger/acquisition affecting service
Enables negotiating better pricing and SLAs through competition

back.text: Trade-offs: more operational complexity, more BGP policy work, and potentially higher cost.

front.text: An enterprise has two ISPs but doesn't want to handle the full Internet table. What's a common simplified approach?

items: Receive only a default route (0.0.0.0/0) from each ISP via BGP
Use local-preference to prefer one ISP for outbound traffic
Advertise enterprise prefixes with AS_Path prepending or communities to influence inbound preference
Optionally accept partial routes (e.g., only customer routes from ISP) for better path selection
Saves enormous amounts of memory and CPU compared to a full Internet feed

back.text:
ip prefix-list DEFAULT-ONLY permit 0.0.0.0/0
!
router bgp 65100
 address-family ipv4 unicast
  neighbor 10.12.1.2 prefix-list DEFAULT-ONLY in

front.text: Compare which BGP tools influence INBOUND vs OUTBOUND traffic in a multihomed AS.

rows: Locally significant or AS-internal — guaranteed effect
AS_Path prepending, MED, communities, more-specific advertisements
Hints only — upstream may override your policy

back.text: It's easier to control your own traffic (outbound) than other people's traffic (inbound). Most BGP traffic engineering complaints stem from trying to control inbound flows that ultimately depend on remote ASs' policies.

front.text: A branch router has multiple WAN circuits to two different MPLS providers. What outbound BGP filtering strategy prevents the branch from becoming an unintended transit?

back.text:
ip prefix-list LAN-ONLY permit 10.20.0.0/16 le 32
!
router bgp 65020
 address-family ipv4 unicast
  neighbor <SP1-IP> prefix-list LAN-ONLY out
  neighbor <SP2-IP> prefix-list LAN-ONLY out

Only LAN-facing prefixes (the branch's own subnets) are advertised outbound. Anything learned from one SP cannot be re-advertised to the other SP. This eliminates accidental transit during failover and protects WAN bandwidth.

front.text: How does the wildcard mask 0.0.0.255 differ from 0.0.255.0 in extended ACL matching for BGP?

rows: Last octet wildcard — matches any value 0–255 in the 4th octet
Third octet wildcard — matches any value in the 3rd octet, but 4th must match exactly
Last 16 bits wildcard — matches any /16 host

back.text: Remember: 0 = must match, 1 = ignore. Wildcards do NOT need to be contiguous in BGP/IGP ACLs (unlike CIDR masks), but non-contiguous wildcards are rare and confusing.

front.text: How do you write a single prefix list entry that matches the default route 0.0.0.0/0 ONLY (not all routes)?

back.text:
ip prefix-list DEFAULT permit 0.0.0.0/0       ! Exactly the default
! NOT:
ip prefix-list DEFAULT permit 0.0.0.0/0 le 32  ! Matches ALL prefixes (catch-all)
Without 'ge'/'le', prefix lists match ONLY the exact prefix length specified. This is a common cause of accidental over-matching when configuring filters.

front.text: Write a BGP AS_Path ACL that PERMITS routes with an AS_Path containing 3 or fewer ASs, and DENIES routes with longer paths.

back.text:
ip as-path access-list 10 permit ^[0-9]+ [0-9]+ [0-9]+?$
ip as-path access-list 10 permit ^$
!
! Or alternatively (cleaner):
ip as-path access-list 10 permit ^([0-9]+ ?){0,3}$
The first regex matches up to 3 AS_Path entries plus the empty path. Useful for filtering out routes with very long paths (often suspicious or suboptimal).

front.text: How do you display all BGP routes that originate in a specific AS using regex on the router?

back.text: show bgp ipv4 unicast regexp _3356$
The pattern '_3356$' matches any AS_Path that ENDS in AS 3356 — meaning the prefix originated in AS 3356. Useful for finding all routes from a specific provider, customer, or geographic origin.

front.text: What's the simplest way to ensure no routes are accidentally dropped by an inbound route map's implicit deny?

back.text:
route-map INBOUND permit 10
 match ip address prefix-list SPECIAL
 set local-preference 200
!
route-map INBOUND permit 999       ! catch-all permit at the end
! (no match clause, no set clause = permit all remaining)

Without the catch-all permit at the end, any route NOT matching prefix-list SPECIAL would hit the implicit deny and be dropped. Always include a final catch-all permit unless intentional filtering is desired.

front.text: Show two ways to use 'set as-path prepend' in a route map.

back.text:
! Method 1: explicit ASNs to prepend
route-map PREPEND permit 10
 set as-path prepend 65100 65100 65100

! Method 2: prepend the LAST AS in the path N times
route-map PREPEND permit 10
 set as-path prepend last-as 3
The 'last-as N' form is dynamic — it prepends the last AS already in the path N times (1–10). Useful when you want consistent prepending behavior regardless of which AS originated the route.

front.text: When would you use 'set ip next-hop self' in a BGP route map?

back.text: It's commonly used on iBGP route reflectors or hub routers to force the BGP next-hop of a re-advertised eBGP-learned route to the LOCAL router's IP. This ensures downstream iBGP peers can resolve the next-hop without needing the eBGP peering subnet in their IGP. Equivalent of the 'neighbor X next-hop-self' command but applied selectively via route-map (e.g., for some prefixes only).

route-map IBGP-OUT permit 10
 match ip address prefix-list EXTERNAL-ROUTES
 set ip next-hop self

front.text: Can you combine a distribute-list AND a route-map on the same BGP neighbor in the same direction?

back.text: Yes. Different filter TYPES can coexist in the same direction. The order of evaluation is: distribute-list/prefix-list (filtering) → AS_Path filter-list → route-map (filtering + manipulation). The route must pass ALL of them. The only forbidden combo is distribute-list + prefix-list in the same direction (both are essentially the same kind of filter).

front.text: You want all routes advertised to ISP-A to have BGP community 65100:100. Where is the route map applied — inbound or outbound?

back.text:
route-map TO-ISPA permit 10
 set community 65100:100
!
router bgp 65100
 address-family ipv4 unicast
  neighbor <ISPA-IP> send-community
  neighbor <ISPA-IP> route-map TO-ISPA out
Outbound. 'out' applies to routes leaving toward the peer. Don't forget 'send-community' — without it, the community attribute won't be transmitted.

front.text: After modifying an OUTBOUND route map applied to neighbor 10.12.1.2, what command applies the changes without dropping the BGP session?

back.text: clear bgp ipv4 unicast 10.12.1.2 soft out
This re-evaluates outbound policy and re-advertises the Adj-RIB-Out to the peer. For inbound changes, use 'soft in' (which leverages Route Refresh capability).

front.text: An ISP wants customers to be able to influence local-preference inside the ISP via communities. How does this work, and what role do communities play?

back.text: The ISP publishes a community pattern, e.g., 'AS:200 = local-pref 200', 'AS:50 = local-pref 50'. The ISP applies an INBOUND route map on customer peerings that matches these communities and SETS local-pref accordingly. The customer tags routes with the appropriate community before advertising — and the ISP's automation gives those routes the requested local-pref. This shifts traffic engineering control to the customer in a controlled, auditable way without manual ISP intervention.

! On ISP side:
ip community-list 1 permit 65000:200
route-map FROM-CUSTOMER permit 10
 match community 1
 set local-preference 200

front.text: What is Remotely Triggered Black Hole (RTBH) filtering, and how does it use BGP communities?

back.text: RTBH is a DDoS mitigation technique. The victim or upstream ISP advertises a /32 (host route) of the attacked destination tagged with a special community (e.g., 65000:666). Edge routers match the community in an inbound route map and SET the next-hop to a discard interface (Null0), causing all traffic to that destination to be silently dropped at the network edge BEFORE reaching the target. This stops the attack from saturating internal links — at the cost of taking the victim address offline.

ip community-list 99 permit 65000:666
route-map BLACKHOLE permit 10
 match community 99
 set ip next-hop 192.0.2.1   ! routed to Null0 globally

front.text: Interpret this output:

BGP routing table entry for 10.23.1.0/24, version 15
  65200
    10.12.1.2 from 10.12.1.2 (192.168.2.2)
      Origin incomplete, metric 333, localpref 100, valid, external, best
      Community: 333:333 65300:333

items: Prefix 10.23.1.0/24 was advertised from AS 65200 (single AS in path)
Origin '?' (incomplete) means it was redistributed somewhere upstream
MED = 333, LocalPref = 100 (default)
Carries TWO communities: 333:333 and 65300:333
Communities follow private convention (AS:value) — 333 = ASN, 65300 = another ASN, both with policy code 333
Best path indicator confirms this is the path installed in RIB

front.text: Two BGP paths to 10.0.0.0/24 exist. Path A: weight 0, local-pref 100, AS_Path '200 100', MED 50, eBGP. Path B: weight 0, local-pref 100, AS_Path '300 100', MED 30, eBGP. Which is best?

items: Weight: tie (both 0)
Local-Pref: tie (both 100)
Locally originated: tie (neither is local)
AS_Path length: tie (both = 2)
Origin: assume tie
MED: ONLY compared between routes from same neighboring AS — Path A from AS 200, Path B from AS 300 — DIFFERENT, so MED is SKIPPED
eBGP vs iBGP: tie (both eBGP)
IGP cost to next-hop: depends on local IGP
Oldest eBGP path or lowest RID typically resolves the tie

back.text: MED would only be used if both paths came from AS 200, OR if 'bgp always-compare-med' is configured.

front.text: Routes redistributed into BGP show as origin '?' (incomplete) and lose to routes with origin 'i' in best-path. How do you fix this?

back.text:
route-map FIX-ORIGIN permit 10
 set origin igp
!
router bgp 65100
 address-family ipv4 unicast
  redistribute ospf 1 route-map FIX-ORIGIN

By setting origin to 'igp' during redistribution, the routes will compete equally with network-statement-injected routes during best-path selection. Alternative: use 'network' statements instead of redistribution (which automatically sets origin 'i').

front.text: An enterprise multihomed to ONE ISP via two links wants inbound traffic to prefer link 1 over link 2. What's the cleanest way to influence this?

back.text: Set a LOWER MED on outbound advertisements through link 1 and a HIGHER MED on link 2. Since both links go to the same ISP (same AS), MED is compared and the lower MED wins inside that ISP's BGP best-path.
