back.text: Use 'set' for clean, simple marking. Use policer-based marking when you want different DSCP values for in-rate vs over-rate traffic (e.g., conform=AF31, exceed=AF32, violate=drop).

front.text: When and why might you tune the Tx-ring size on a router interface?

back.text:
interface Serial0/0/0
 tx-ring-limit 3

Default Tx-ring size varies by platform/interface. SHRINK it on slow WAN interfaces to ensure congestion is detected sooner — so software-based QoS queuing kicks in earlier and protects voice/video. INCREASE it on high-speed interfaces to absorb microbursts. Misconfiguration can cause excessive drops or degraded LLQ behavior. Best practice: use platform defaults unless you have a specific reason.

front.text: What does the 'fair-queue' command do inside a class, and where is it commonly used?

back.text: 'fair-queue' enables flow-based weighted fair queuing INSIDE a class — typically used in class-default. It dynamically creates per-flow queues and services them fairly, preventing any single flow from dominating the class's bandwidth allocation. Common in CBWFQ designs to provide fairness among bulk-data flows competing for the same shared queue.

policy-map QUEUING
 class class-default
   bandwidth percent 25
   fair-queue
   random-detect dscp-based

front.text: How does WRED use minimum threshold, maximum threshold, and Mark Probability Denominator (MPD)?

items: Min threshold: queue depth where WRED STARTS dropping; below this no drops occur
Max threshold: queue depth where WRED drops at full Mark Probability rate
MPD: 1/MPD = drop rate at max threshold (e.g., MPD=10 means drop 1 in 10 at max threshold)
Above max threshold: tail drop kicks in (drop everything)
Drop probability scales LINEARLY between min and max threshold

back.text: Higher-priority DSCP classes get higher min/max thresholds — they survive longer in the queue. Lower-priority classes (e.g., AF13, scavenger) get dropped first.

front.text: When would you use 'priority level 1' AND 'priority level 2' in the same policy?

back.text: When you have TWO real-time traffic types with different priority needs. Voice is most sensitive to delay → 'priority level 1' (always serviced first). Video is delay-sensitive but can tolerate slightly more → 'priority level 2' (serviced after level 1, before all other queues). Each level has its own conditional policer. Allows different policing rates for each (e.g., voice 10%, video 30%).

policy-map QUEUING
 class VOIP
   priority level 1 percent 10
 class VIDEO
   priority level 2 percent 30

front.text: What rule must be followed when summing bandwidth percentages in a CBWFQ policy?

back.text: The total of all 'bandwidth' allocations across all classes (excluding class-default) cannot exceed 75% of the interface bandwidth by default. Cisco reserves 25% for control plane traffic and overhead. To override this, use 'max-reserved-bandwidth <percent>' on the interface — but only do so cautiously. With 'bandwidth percent', sums must total ≤ 100% and respect the max-reserved limit.

front.text: What is a QoS Group, and when would you use one?

back.text: A QoS Group is a LOCALLY SIGNIFICANT internal marker (0-99) that lives only inside the router. It's set on ingress and used for matching on egress. Useful when ingress markings (e.g., MPLS EXP) get stripped or replaced before egress and you need to preserve classification across the device. NOT carried in any packet header — disappears when the packet leaves the router.

policy-map INBOUND
 class VOIP
   set qos-group 5
!
policy-map OUTBOUND
 class match-qos-group-5
   priority level 1 percent 30

front.text: How does QoS apply to sub-interfaces, especially for shaping per-VLAN or per-customer?

back.text: On routers with sub-interfaces (e.g., 802.1Q VLANs or Frame Relay PVCs), shaping is commonly applied PER SUB-INTERFACE so each VLAN/customer gets its own bandwidth allocation. Hierarchical QoS is typical: parent shapes the sub-interface, child queues within that shaped rate. This is critical on multi-tenant or multi-VLAN egress where the sub-interface speed differs from the physical line rate.

interface GigabitEthernet0/0.10
 encapsulation dot1q 10
 service-policy output PARENT-SHAPER

front.text: What's the ONE most important QoS best practice if you only follow one rule?

back.text: CLASSIFY AND MARK AT THE EDGE, ONCE. Once traffic is properly marked (DSCP) at the access layer, every other device in the network can simply trust those markings and apply per-hop behavior accordingly. This eliminates redundant classification, reduces operational complexity, and ensures consistent QoS treatment end-to-end. Re-classification only happens at trust boundaries (e.g., re-marking from a different administrative domain).

front.text: What is a DiffServ Behavior Aggregate (BA)?

back.text: A DiffServ Behavior Aggregate is a collection of packets sharing the same DSCP value crossing a link in a particular direction. The CORE doesn't see individual flows — it sees BAs. For example, all SSH, Telnet, and SNMP traffic might be marked CS2 and treated as a single 'network management' BA in the core. This aggregation is what makes DiffServ scalable: you don't need per-flow state, only per-BA forwarding logic.

front.text: How does a Cisco WLC act as a QoS trust boundary between wireless and wired networks?

items: Wireless QoS policy on each WLAN sets the maximum allowed marking (Platinum/Gold/Silver/Bronze)
WLC ENFORCES the cap — even if a wireless client tries to mark traffic higher (e.g., DSCP EF on a Silver WLAN), the WLC strips it down
On the wired side (CAPWAP tunnel from AP to WLC), traffic is re-marked according to the WLAN's QoS profile
Provides centralized trust boundary for all wireless endpoints

back.text: This prevents misbehaving wireless clients from injecting unauthorized high-priority markings into the wired network.

front.text: Why isn't DiffServ truly 'end-to-end'?

items: Each device makes its own per-hop forwarding decision — no global coordination
Different administrative domains may use different marking schemes (re-marking at boundaries)
SPs may not honor customer DSCP markings
QoS policy at one device doesn't guarantee policy at the next device
The Internet generally treats all traffic as best-effort regardless of DSCP

back.text: DiffServ provides 'consistent treatment' across a domain you control. End-to-end across the public Internet is not realistically possible without something like IntServ (which doesn't scale) or SD-WAN with overlay QoS.

front.text: In a 3-color policer, what's the difference between 'exceed' and 'violate' actions?

rows: Above CIR but within Be (srTCM) or below PIR (trTCM)
Mark down (e.g., AFx2 or AFx3) and transmit

front.text: Why does AF have 4 classes × 3 drop priorities = 12 values, all even decimal numbers?

back.text: AF binary structure is aaadd0 (6 bits): aaa = AF class (1-4), dd = drop priority (1-3), and the trailing 0 ensures all values are EVEN decimal numbers. The unused bit (bit 2) is fixed at 0 in the spec. AF values: AF11=10, AF12=12, AF13=14, AF21=18, AF22=20, AF23=22, AF31=26, AF32=28, AF33=30, AF41=34, AF42=36, AF43=38.

front.text: What happens if Bc is set SMALLER than the largest possible packet?

back.text: There will NEVER be enough tokens in the bucket to send a max-size packet without exceeding the rate. Every large packet will be marked exceed/violate. Result: the policer will appear to drop or mark down ALL packets above a certain size — even if the average rate is well within CIR. Best practice: set Bc ≥ max IP packet size (typically 1500 bytes or jumbo frame size).

front.text: Why should QoS markings be SYMMETRIC across both directions of a flow?

back.text: Real-time traffic (especially voice) requires consistent QoS in both directions for good user experience. If voice from A→B is marked EF but B→A is unmarked, the return path will be subject to congestion drops while the forward path is protected. Result: one-way audio, distortion, or call drops. Always mark in both directions and apply consistent policy at both endpoints/edges.

front.text: Why is tail drop especially harmful for TCP flows?

items: TCP interprets packet loss as a congestion signal and slows down (cwnd halved or reset)
Tail drop hits MANY flows simultaneously when the queue overflows
All affected flows back off at the same time → utilization plummets (TCP global synchronization)
Then all flows ramp up together → utilization spikes again → more tail drops
Result: oscillating throughput, poor link utilization, retransmissions

back.text: WRED prevents this by spreading drops out over time and across flows, so backoff is staggered.

front.text: What is 'qos pre-classify' on a tunnel interface, and when is it needed?

back.text:
interface Tunnel0
 qos pre-classify

When traffic is encapsulated (GRE, IPsec), the outer header replaces the original DSCP and L3/L4 information. 'qos pre-classify' tells the router to use the INNER (original) header for QoS classification on the egress physical interface — even though the packet is now inside a tunnel. Critical for QoS over VPN tunnels where you want to honor the original markings, not the tunnel's outer DSCP.

front.text: Final master QoS quick-recall — facts not yet covered explicitly.

items: QoS only takes effect when the Tx-ring is full (real congestion)
Microbursts cause drops that average rate metrics miss
Don't shape voice — police it or use LLQ
RSVP PATH/RESV creates state in every router along the path (doesn't scale)
NBAR2 protocol packs add app signatures without IOS upgrades
DSCP→CoS mapping uses upper 3 bits of DSCP
MPLS EXP = 3 bits (8 levels); maps from upper 3 bits of DSCP at PE
Edge classifies and marks; core trusts and forwards by PHB
Hard QoS = strict per-flow guarantees (IntServ); Soft QoS = aggregate (DiffServ)
CSn × 8 = DSCP decimal (CS3 = 24)
WRED uses min/max thresholds + Mark Probability to drop randomly before tail drop
Multilevel priority lets voice (level 1) and video (level 2) both bypass the CBWFQ scheduler
Default max-reserved-bandwidth = 75% (Cisco reserves 25%)
QoS groups = router-internal markers, not carried in packets
Sub-interface QoS = parent shapes, child queues
WLC enforces wireless QoS profile cap (Platinum/Gold/Silver/Bronze)
Symmetric marking in both directions is essential for voice/video
Tail drop causes TCP global sync; WRED prevents it
qos pre-classify = honor inner DSCP through tunnels (GRE, IPsec)
DiffServ BA = packets sharing same DSCP across a link
AF = aaadd0 binary structure → all even decimal values

front.text: What is NTP, what transport protocol does it use, and what's the server port?

back.text: Network Time Protocol (NTP), defined in RFC 958, synchronizes clocks across distributed devices. NTP runs over UDP port 123 (server). Clients use ephemeral source ports. NTP follows a client/server model — the client adjusts its clock toward the server's time, never the other way around. NTP is critical for log correlation, certificate validation, encryption key exchange, password timers, and security event tracking.

front.text: What does NTP stratum represent, and how do stratum levels grow as time propagates through the network?

items: Stratum 0 = the authoritative reference (atomic clock, GPS) — never directly accessed by NTP
Stratum 1 = NTP server directly attached to a stratum 0 source
Stratum 2 = client of a stratum 1 server
Stratum 3 = client of a stratum 2 server, etc.
Maximum stratum = 15; stratum 16 = unsynchronized

back.text: Higher stratum = more potential drift from the authoritative source. Always pick the lowest stratum available when configuring multiple NTP servers.

front.text: Show the basic NTP client configuration with two NTP servers, one preferred, and a specific source interface.

back.text:
ntp server 192.168.1.1 prefer source Loopback0
ntp server 192.168.2.2 source Loopback0

The 'prefer' keyword identifies which NTP server is the primary source. The 'source' keyword forces NTP queries to originate from a specific interface (typically a loopback) for stability and ACL/firewall predictability.

front.text: What does the 'ntp master <stratum>' command do, and when is it useful?

back.text: ntp master 1

Configures the device to act as an authoritative NTP server at the specified stratum level when no upstream server is reachable. Common in lab environments or isolated networks where there's no Internet/external NTP source. The reference clock shows up as '127.127.1.1' in 'show ntp associations' — a special internal address indicating the local clock is being used.

front.text: Compare NTP server vs NTP peer configurations.

rows: Bidirectional — both adjust toward each other
Backup peering between two stratum 2 devices using different upstream sources
Both shift max 2 minutes per query

front.text: What two commands verify NTP status and what does each show?

back.text:
show ntp status
show ntp associations

rows: Sync state, current stratum, reference clock, clock offset, drift, polling interval
Configured servers/peers, their stratum, reach count, delay, offset, dispersion

front.text: What is PTP, and how does it differ from NTP?

rows: Compensates with transparent clocks and delay measurements

back.text: PTPv2 is NOT backward compatible with original PTP (IEEE 1588-2002).

front.text: What are the PTP operating modes, and what do Boundary and Transparent modes do?

rows: Multicast PTP packets are forwarded as-is, like regular multicast
Switch acts as a clock — terminates PTP from upstream Grand Master and re-syncs downstream clients
Default — switch measures and corrects for the residence time of each PTP packet to keep accuracy
Delay is measured end-to-end between client and master
Delay is measured between adjacent PTP devices

front.text: What problem do First-Hop Redundancy Protocols (FHRPs) solve?

back.text: Most end-host operating systems support only ONE default gateway. If that gateway router fails, the host loses external connectivity even when redundant routers exist on the segment. FHRPs (HSRP, VRRP, GLBP) create a Virtual IP (VIP) that is shared between two or more physical routers — hosts use the VIP as their default gateway. If one router fails, another takes over the VIP transparently. The host doesn't need to know about the failover.

front.text: Compare HSRP, VRRP, and GLBP.
What is object tracking, and show the two most common configurations.

back.text: Object tracking lets FHRPs (and other features) react to the state of a tracked object — typically an interface line protocol or a route's reachability. When the tracked object goes down, the FHRP can lower its priority to trigger failover.

! Track an interface line protocol
track 1 interface GigabitEthernet0/0 line-protocol

! Track a route's reachability
track 2 ip route 10.1.1.0/24 reachability

show track
show track <object-number>

front.text: Show a typical HSRP configuration for VLAN 10 with priority 110, preemption, and the VIP 172.16.10.1.

back.text:
interface vlan 10
 ip address 172.16.10.2 255.255.255.0
 standby 10 ip 172.16.10.1
 standby 10 priority 110
 standby 10 preempt
Default priority is 100. Higher priority wins. Without 'preempt', a router that comes back up from failure will NOT reclaim its active role even if it has higher priority.

front.text: Compare HSRPv1 and HSRPv2.

back.text: HSRPv2 is required when you need >256 groups (e.g., VLAN per group ID = ID match) or sub-second failover.

front.text: How do you make HSRP failover automatically when an upstream WAN interface goes down?

back.text:
track 1 interface GigabitEthernet0/0 line-protocol
!
interface vlan 10
 standby 10 ip 172.16.10.1
 standby 10 priority 110
 standby 10 preempt
 standby 10 track 1 decrement 20

If the tracked interface goes down, the HSRP priority decreases by 20 (110 → 90). If the other router has priority 100, it now beats us and becomes active. The decrement value MUST be large enough to drop priority below the peer's.

front.text: Show a basic VRRPv2 configuration with VIP 172.16.20.1 and explain key differences from HSRP.

back.text:
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ip address 172.16.20.2 255.255.255.0
 vrrp 20 ip 172.16.20.1
 vrrp 20 priority 110

items: Active = 'master', Standby = 'backup' terminology
Preemption ENABLED by default
VIP MAC: 0000.5e00.01XX (where XX = group ID in hex)
Multicast: 224.0.0.18
IETF standard, multi-vendor compatible

front.text: Show how to configure hierarchical VRRPv3 for IPv4 with the VIP 172.16.22.1 and tracking integration.

back.text:
fhrp version vrrp v3
!
interface vlan 22
 ip address 172.16.22.2 255.255.255.0
 vrrp 22 address-family ipv4
  address 172.16.22.1
  priority 110
  track 1 decrement 20
VRRPv3 supports IPv4 AND IPv6 in a hierarchical address-family submode. Required to enable IPv6 FHRP using VRRP.

front.text: What are the AVG and AVF roles in GLBP, and how does GLBP achieve load balancing?

rows: ONE per group; replies to ARP requests for the VIP with different virtual MAC addresses
Up to FOUR per group; each owns a unique virtual MAC and forwards traffic for hosts assigned to it

back.text: When a host ARPs for the VIP, the AVG hands out a different AVF MAC each time. Hosts using different MACs send traffic to different physical routers — built-in load balancing across all AVFs without requiring separate FHRP groups.

front.text: Show a GLBP configuration with VIP 172.16.30.1, preemption, and weighted load balancing.

back.text:
interface vlan 30
 ip address 172.16.30.2 255.255.255.0
 glbp 30 ip 172.16.30.1
 glbp 30 preempt
 glbp 30 priority 110
 glbp 30 load-balancing weighted
 glbp 30 weighting 80
Other AVFs in the group should also use 'glbp 30 load-balancing weighted'. Set different 'weighting' values to control traffic distribution proportionally (e.g., 80 vs. 20).

front.text: What are the three GLBP load-balancing methods?

rows: AVG cycles through all AVF MACs sequentially in ARP replies
Distributes ARP replies based on the configured 'weighting' value of each AVF
Same host always gets the same AVF MAC — useful for session persistence (assuming AVF count doesn't change)

front.text: Define the four NAT terms: inside local, inside global, outside local, outside global.

rows: The PRIVATE IP address of an internal device (typically RFC 1918)
The PUBLIC IP address that represents an internal device to the outside world
How an external device's IP appears to the inside network (translated)
The actual PUBLIC IP address of an external device on the outside network

back.text: Mnemonic: 'inside' vs 'outside' = the device's actual location. 'Local' vs 'global' = how the address appears from inside vs outside the NAT boundary.

front.text: Compare the three main NAT types: Static, Pooled, and PAT.

rows: Servers that need a stable public address
Larger group of internal hosts sharing a pool of public addresses
Most common — many internal hosts share a single public IP

front.text: Show how to configure inside static NAT mapping 10.78.9.7 to 10.45.1.7.

back.text:
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ip nat outside
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 ip nat inside
!
ip nat inside source static 10.78.9.7 10.45.1.7

Don't forget to mark inside and outside interfaces. The inside global IP doesn't have to be on the outside subnet — but routers in the outside network must have a route TO that global IP pointing back to the NAT router.

front.text: Show how to configure inside pooled NAT for the 10.78.9.0/24 network using a pool of 10.45.1.10–10.45.1.11.

back.text:
ip access-list standard ACL-NAT-CAPABLE
 permit 10.78.9.0 0.0.0.255
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ip nat outside
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 ip nat inside
!
ip nat pool R5-OUTSIDE-POOL 10.45.1.10 10.45.1.11 prefix-length 24
ip nat inside source list ACL-NAT-CAPABLE pool R5-OUTSIDE-POOL
Pooled NAT provides 1-to-1 dynamic mapping. When the pool is exhausted, additional translations FAIL. Default translation timeout is 24 hours.

front.text: Show how to configure PAT (NAT overload) for the 10.78.9.0/24 network using the outside interface's IP address.

back.text:
ip access-list standard ACL-NAT-CAPABLE
 permit 10.78.9.0 0.0.0.255
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ip nat outside
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 ip nat inside
!
ip nat inside source list ACL-NAT-CAPABLE interface GigabitEthernet0/0 overload

The 'overload' keyword enables PAT. The router uses the outside interface's IP and assigns a unique source PORT for each session — allowing thousands of internal hosts to share one public IP. Most common Internet edge configuration.

front.text: What command shows the NAT translation table, and what's its default timeout?

back.text:
show ip nat translations
!
! Default dynamic translation timeout = 24 hours (86400 seconds)
! Override:
ip nat translation timeout 3600
!
! Clear all dynamic entries:
clear ip nat translation *

Output columns: Pro (protocol), Inside global, Inside local, Outside local, Outside global. Static entries show '---' for missing fields. Dynamic entries (e.g., ICMP, TCP, UDP) show all four fields with port numbers.

front.text: What are the three RFC 1918 private IP address ranges?

back.text: These addresses are NEVER routed on the public Internet — they require NAT to communicate with external networks. The same private ranges can be reused in any organization without IANA registration.

front.text: What 'show' commands verify each FHRP type?

back.text:
show standby [interface] [brief]      ! HSRP
show vrrp [brief]                     ! VRRP
show glbp [brief]                     ! GLBP
The 'brief' option provides a one-line summary per group. Without 'brief', detailed output includes timers, state changes, MAC address, priority, preemption, tracking, and group name.

cards.subtopic: Why HSRP Doesn't Preempt by Default

front.text: If two HSRP routers come up in different orders, who becomes active and why is preemption usually configured?

back.text: By default, HSRP does NOT preempt — whichever router becomes active FIRST stays active, even if a higher-priority router comes online later. This is to avoid disruption from repeated failover events. Operators almost always configure 'standby <id> preempt' to ensure the highest-priority router (typically the primary) takes the active role on every recovery.

standby 10 preempt
standby 10 preempt delay minimum 60   ! optional: wait 60s before preempting

front.text: How does PAT use TCP/UDP ports to track multiple internal sessions sharing a single global IP?

back.text: When two internal hosts initiate sessions, PAT preserves their source ports if unique — but if they collide (same source port), PAT modifies the second session's source port to ensure uniqueness. The router maintains a translation table mapping each unique inside-global-IP:port → inside-local-IP:port. When a return packet arrives, the router uses the destination port (and IP) to look up the original internal host. This allows ~65,000 simultaneous sessions per global IP per protocol (TCP, UDP each).

front.text: Final IP Services quick-recall checklist for the ENCOR exam.

items: NTP = UDP/123, hierarchical via stratum (1=top, 16=unsynchronized)
ntp server = client→server; ntp peer = bidirectional sync
ntp master = local authoritative source when no upstream available
PTP = sub-microsecond accuracy via IEEE 1588v2 — used in industrial/financial
PTPv2 not backward compatible with original PTP
FHRP = HSRP (Cisco), VRRP (IETF), GLBP (Cisco with built-in load balancing)
HSRP: priority default 100, preempt OFF by default, MAC 0000.0C07.ACxx
HSRPv2: groups 0-4095, ms timers, multicast 224.0.0.102
VRRP: master/backup terminology, preempt ON by default, MAC 0000.5e00.01xx
VRRPv3: supports IPv4 + IPv6 hierarchical config
GLBP: AVG handles ARP replies, AVFs (up to 4) forward traffic with unique virtual MACs
GLBP load-balancing: round-robin (default), weighted, host-dependent
Object tracking lets FHRPs react to interface or route state changes
NAT terms: inside local/global, outside local/global
Static NAT = 1-to-1 permanent; Pooled NAT = 1-to-1 dynamic; PAT = many-to-1 with ports
Default NAT translation timeout = 24 hours (86400s)
show ip nat translations = verify NAT entries
All NAT requires 'ip nat inside' and 'ip nat outside' on appropriate interfaces

front.text: List five reasons why network device time synchronization is critical.

items: Correlating security events across firewalls, routers, switches, AAA, and SIEM
Validating digital certificates that have notBefore/notAfter timestamps
Ensuring encryption key exchange and rollover happen at correct intervals
Managing time-based passwords and session timeouts
Troubleshooting and root-cause analysis using consistent log timestamps across devices

front.text: What's the difference between a hardware clock and a software clock on a Cisco router?

rows: Maintained by the OS — RESET on power loss or reload
Battery-backed RTC — preserved across reloads/power loss

back.text:
ntp update-calendar         ! sync hardware clock from software clock
clock calendar-valid        ! mark hardware clock as authoritative if no NTP source
Without a hardware clock, a device starts with whatever default time is built in (often Jan 1, 1993 or similar) until NTP sync.

front.text: Show how to configure NTP authentication using MD5.

back.text:
! On both server and client:
ntp authentication-key 1 md5 NtpSecret123
ntp authenticate
ntp trusted-key 1
!
! On client only — bind authentication to a specific server:
ntp server 192.168.1.1 key 1
NTP authentication prevents spoofed time updates. The server and client must share the same key ID and key string.

front.text: Why is it best practice to bind NTP queries to a loopback interface using the 'source' keyword?

items: Loopback IPs survive physical interface failure
Predictable source IP for ACLs and firewall rules
Easier to identify the device in NTP server logs
Avoids issues when multiple physical interfaces could egress toward the NTP server

back.text: ntp server 192.168.1.1 source Loopback0

front.text: What does this 'show ntp status' output indicate, and what are some warning signs?

Clock is unsynchronized, stratum 16, no reference clock
ntp uptime is 53400 (1/100 of seconds)
clock offset is 0.0000 msec
root dispersion is 7937.39 msec

items: 'Clock is unsynchronized' = NTP has not yet locked onto a server
Stratum 16 = effectively unsynchronized (max + 1)
No reference clock = no upstream NTP source available
Root dispersion above ~1000 ms = poor accuracy estimate
Common causes: ACL blocking UDP/123, wrong server IP, firewall in the path, no route to server

front.text: Decode the symbols at the start of each row in 'show ntp associations'.

rows: System peer — currently being used as the time source
Selected — passed sanity checks, ready as backup
False ticker — clock disagrees with the majority

front.text: What types of networks typically REQUIRE PTP rather than NTP?

items: Industrial automation (factory floor, manufacturing)
Financial trading networks (microsecond timestamping for transactions)
Power utility automation (smart grid, IEC 61850)
Cellular base station synchronization
Audio/Video Bridging (AVB) over Ethernet for media production
Test and measurement equipment

back.text: If millisecond accuracy is enough → use NTP. If you need microsecond/nanosecond precision → use PTP.

front.text: Define PTP Grand Master, Master, Slave, and Boundary clock roles.

rows: The most accurate clock in the entire PTP domain — typically synced to GPS or atomic source
Provides time to downstream slaves on a port
Synchronizes its clock to a master via PTP messages
Has multiple PTP ports — slave on the upstream port, master on the downstream port
Doesn't sync; measures and corrects the residence time of PTP packets passing through

front.text: How does PTP elect the Grand Master, and what does the Best Master Clock Algorithm (BMCA) consider?

items: Priority1 (lower wins; default 128)
Clock Class (e.g., GPS-synced wins over free-running)
Clock Accuracy (lower nanosecond uncertainty wins)
Clock Variance (smaller is more stable)
Priority2 (lower wins; default 128)
Clock Identity (lower MAC-derived ID wins as final tiebreaker)

back.text: BMCA runs continuously — Grand Master can change if a better clock is announced.

front.text: What are the default HSRP hello and hold timers, and how does the standby router detect active router failure?

back.text: If the standby router doesn't receive a hello from the active router within the hold time, it assumes the active is down and takes over. Use millisecond timers (HSRPv2) for sub-second failover in critical environments — at the cost of more CPU and bandwidth.

front.text: Why is it common best practice to use the VLAN ID as the HSRP group number?

items: Easy mental mapping: VLAN 10 → HSRP group 10
Eliminates ambiguity when troubleshooting on a multi-VLAN switch
Avoids accidental MAC overlap on the segment (each group gets a unique virtual MAC)
Supported with HSRPv1 only up to VLAN 255 — use HSRPv2 for VLANs > 255
Simplifies documentation and consistent design across VLANs

cards.subtopic: Multiple HSRP Groups for Load Sharing

front.text: How do you achieve HSRP load sharing without using GLBP?

back.text: Configure TWO HSRP groups on the same interface — Router A is active for group 1 (VIP-1), Router B is active for group 2 (VIP-2). Half of the hosts use VIP-1 as their gateway; the other half use VIP-2. Each router carries half the traffic; if either fails, the survivor handles both VIPs.

! Router A
interface vlan 10
 standby 1 ip 172.16.10.1
 standby 1 priority 110
 standby 1 preempt
 standby 2 ip 172.16.10.2
 standby 2 priority 100
!
! Router B (mirrored priorities)
interface vlan 10
 standby 1 ip 172.16.10.1
 standby 1 priority 100
 standby 2 ip 172.16.10.2
 standby 2 priority 110
 standby 2 preempt

front.text: Show how to configure VRRPv3 for IPv6 with a link-local VIP.

back.text:
fhrp version vrrp v3
!
interface vlan 10
 ipv6 address 2001:DB8:10::2/64
 vrrp 10 address-family ipv6
  address FE80::1 primary
  address 2001:DB8:10::1
  priority 110
For IPv6 VRRP, you must specify a link-local address (FE80::*) as the primary virtual address — IPv6 hosts use link-local for default gateway in router advertisements.

front.text: How many AVFs can be active per GLBP group, and what happens to additional routers?

back.text: Maximum of 4 AVFs per GLBP group. Additional routers in the same group go into 'Listen' state — they're ready to take over if any of the 4 active AVFs fail. The AVG (which can be a separate role) hands out only the MAC addresses of the active AVFs in ARP replies.

front.text: Show how to configure GLBP to lower an AVF's weight when an upstream interface fails.

back.text:
track 1 interface GigabitEthernet0/0 line-protocol
!
interface vlan 30
 glbp 30 ip 172.16.30.1
 glbp 30 load-balancing weighted
 glbp 30 weighting 100 lower 80 upper 90
 glbp 30 weighting track 1 decrement 30

Lower threshold (80) = AVF stops forwarding when weight drops below this. Upper threshold (90) = AVF resumes forwarding when weight reaches or exceeds this. Decrement (30) = how much the tracked failure reduces the weight (100-30=70 < 80, so AVF stops).

front.text: When does NAT translation occur — before or after routing?

back.text: This means inbound packets are translated FIRST, but outbound packets are routed FIRST. ACLs applied to inside vs outside interfaces must be carefully placed: the outbound ACL on the outside interface sees the post-translation (global) IP.

front.text: Show how to configure static port forwarding so external HTTPS (port 443) requests reach an internal web server at 10.10.10.5:443.

back.text: ip nat inside source static tcp 10.10.10.5 443 198.51.100.10 443

Static PAT (port forwarding) maps a specific outside global IP:port to an inside local IP:port. Common use: exposing internal web servers, mail servers, or VPN endpoints to the Internet via a single public IP.

front.text: Show how to combine NAT pool + PAT (overload) for thousands of inside hosts sharing multiple public IPs.

back.text:
ip access-list standard ACL-NAT-INSIDE
 permit 10.0.0.0 0.255.255.255
!
ip nat pool PUBLIC-POOL 198.51.100.10 198.51.100.15 prefix-length 29
!
ip nat inside source list ACL-NAT-INSIDE pool PUBLIC-POOL overload
The 'overload' keyword on a pool gives you many-to-many port translation across multiple public IPs — extending the available port space and supporting more concurrent sessions than single-IP PAT.

front.text: What command shows NAT translation counters and statistics?

back.text: show ip nat statistics

Output includes: total active translations, peak translations, translations created per second, hits, misses, NAT pool usage, expired translations, and inside/outside interface counters. Use it to identify NAT pool exhaustion, hits/misses imbalance, or unusually high translation churn.

front.text: What debug commands help troubleshoot NAT, and what's a key warning?

back.text:
debug ip nat
debug ip nat detailed
WARNING: NAT debugs are CPU-intensive on production routers — generate one log line per packet. Always limit scope with ACLs or use briefly. Use 'undebug all' to stop.

front.text: An inside host fails to reach the Internet — 'show ip nat translations' shows no entries. What should you check?

items: Verify 'ip nat inside' on the inside interface AND 'ip nat outside' on the outside interface
Check the ACL referenced by 'ip nat inside source list' — does it permit the host's source IP?
Confirm there's a route to reach the destination
If using 'pool' (no overload), check if the pool is exhausted
Check 'ip nat translation timeout' — possibly stale entries blocking new ones
Verify ICMP/TCP/UDP control plane traffic to the NAT router isn't being filtered upstream
Use 'debug ip nat' carefully to confirm packets reach the NAT engine

front.text: What is 'twice NAT' (or 'overlapping NAT'), and when is it used?

back.text: Twice NAT translates BOTH source AND destination IP addresses simultaneously. Common scenarios: connecting two organizations with overlapping IP address ranges (e.g., both use 10.0.0.0/8) — each side appears as a different range to the other. Configuration uses both 'ip nat inside source' AND 'ip nat outside source' rules. Often easier to deploy with NAT-PT or NAT64 in modern designs.

front.text: Why can NAT break routing protocols, and what's the workaround?

back.text: Most routing protocols (OSPF, EIGRP, BGP) embed router IPs in their hello/update packets. If NAT translates the source IP, the protocol breaks because the inner content doesn't match. Workarounds: (1) exclude routing protocol traffic from NAT via ACL, (2) use a separate untranslated path for control plane, or (3) terminate routing on the NAT device itself so internal protocol traffic never crosses the boundary.

front.text: What is PAT port exhaustion, and how do you mitigate it?

back.text: PAT supports ~64,000 simultaneous sessions per public IP per protocol (TCP/UDP each). When all available ports are in use, new sessions FAIL until existing translations time out. Common causes: very large user populations, applications opening many short-lived connections (web browsers with HTTP/1.1 keep-alives, P2P apps), or default 24-hour timeouts holding ports too long.

! Lower the timeout to free ports faster
ip nat translation timeout 1800
ip nat translation tcp-timeout 3600
ip nat translation udp-timeout 300
!
! Or expand the public IP pool
ip nat pool BIGGER-POOL 198.51.100.10 198.51.100.30 prefix-length 27

front.text: What states do HSRP, VRRP, and GLBP use to track FHRP role?

rows: Disabled → Init → Listen → Speak → Standby → Active

front.text: If a router is configured with three NTP servers (stratum 1, 2, and 3), which one will it sync to and why?

back.text: The stratum 1 server, because it has the lowest stratum (closest to authoritative time). NTP automatically prefers the lowest-stratum reachable source — operators don't need to manually rank them. If stratum 1 fails, the router falls back to stratum 2, then stratum 3. The 'prefer' keyword can override this in case of stratum ties.

front.text: How do you create a tracked object that depends on multiple sub-objects (boolean AND/OR)?

back.text:
track 1 interface GigabitEthernet0/0 line-protocol
track 2 ip route 10.1.1.0/24 reachability
!
track 10 list boolean and
 object 1
 object 2
!
track 11 list boolean or
 object 1
 object 2
'boolean and' = both must be UP for the parent track to be UP. 'boolean or' = either UP makes the parent UP. Useful for complex FHRP failover logic (e.g., fail only when BOTH WAN links go down).

front.text: How do FHRPs decide who becomes the active/master router when priorities are tied?

rows: Highest configured IP address on the segment wins
Highest configured IP address on the segment wins
Round-robin assignment by AVG; tied AVFs handled by AVG election logic

back.text: Always set explicit priorities in production. Relying on 'highest IP wins' makes future address changes risky — your FHRP roles can shift unexpectedly.

front.text: Quick-reference for NAT troubleshooting commands.

back.text:
show ip nat translations           ! all current translations
show ip nat translations verbose   ! detailed view with timers
show ip nat statistics             ! pool/interface counters
clear ip nat translation *         ! flush all dynamic entries
clear ip nat translation <ip>      ! flush a specific entry
ip nat translation timeout <sec>   ! adjust default 24h timer
debug ip nat                       ! per-packet NAT debug
debug ip nat detailed              ! verbose debug

front.text: Final IP Services master recap with key facts not yet covered explicitly.

items: NTP authentication uses MD5 with shared key on both ends
Loopback as NTP source = stable IP for ACLs/firewalls
Hardware clock = battery-backed; software clock resets on reload
PTP BMCA: Priority1 → Class → Accuracy → Variance → Priority2 → Identity
PTP roles: Grand Master → Master → Boundary → Slave; Transparent clocks correct residence time
HSRP default hello/hold = 3/10 seconds
HSRP load sharing = use TWO groups with reversed priorities
VRRPv3 IPv6 requires link-local primary VIP (FE80::*)
GLBP max 4 AVFs; extras stay in Listen state
Tracked objects can be combined with 'boolean and/or' for complex logic
FHRP tiebreaker = highest IP if priorities are equal
NAT order: outbound = route then translate; inbound = translate then route
Static PAT (port forwarding) = map outside IP:port to inside IP:port
PAT supports ~64K simultaneous sessions per public IP per protocol
NAT and routing protocols don't mix — exclude routing traffic from NAT
Use 'show ip nat statistics' for pool/interface counters
ip nat translation timeout = adjust 24h default for memory and port reuse
Twice NAT (overlapping NAT) = simultaneously translate source and destination — used for merger/M&A scenarios

front.text: How does NTP polling interval evolve, and what's the typical range?

items: Initial polling interval = 64 seconds (2^6)
Range: 16 seconds (2^4) to 1024 seconds (2^10) by default
NTP gradually increases the interval as the clock stabilizes — less polling = more efficient
If accuracy degrades, NTP shortens the interval again
Visible in 'show ntp status' as 'system poll interval'

back.text: This adaptive polling is why NTP can take hours or days to achieve maximum accuracy after initial sync.

front.text: What's the difference between NTP 'stepping' and 'slewing' clock adjustments?

rows: Abrupt jump to the correct time
When offset > 128 ms initially, or > 1000 seconds at startup
Gradual rate adjustment (slow drift toward correct time)
When offset is small (< 128 ms) — preferred to avoid time discontinuities

back.text: Stepping can disrupt applications that rely on monotonic time (logs, certificates, sessions). NTP prefers slewing whenever possible.

front.text: How do NTP broadcast and multicast modes differ from the default client/server mode?

back.text:
! Server side
interface vlan 10
 ntp broadcast
!
! Client side
interface vlan 10
 ntp broadcast client

rows: Default — client polls server with unicast queries
Server periodically broadcasts time to the LAN; clients listen passively
Same as broadcast but uses multicast group 224.0.1.1 — works across L3 with multicast routing

back.text: Broadcast/multicast = lower client CPU but less precise (no round-trip measurements). Useful in large LANs where many clients need rough time sync.

front.text: Show how to configure timezone and daylight saving time on a Cisco router.

back.text:
clock timezone EST -5
clock summer-time EDT recurring 2 Sun Mar 02:00 1 Sun Nov 02:00

NTP always operates in UTC. The local 'clock timezone' command applies an offset for display purposes only. 'recurring' applies the rule yearly. Always set timezone consistently across devices in the same region for clear log correlation.

front.text: What are PTP profiles, and why do they exist?

back.text: PTP profiles are industry-specific PTP configurations that specify which timing options apply for a given use case. Different profiles have different default values for sync intervals, message types, transport, and clock classes — tailored to that industry's accuracy and reliability needs.

rows: Mobile carrier and service provider sync

front.text: How do you provide first-hop redundancy for IPv6, and which FHRP supports it?

rows: Yes — IPv4 + IPv6 in hierarchical address-family

back.text: IPv6 also natively supports neighbor discovery and router advertisement (RA) for first-hop selection — but RA-based fail over is typically slower than FHRP. Use VRRPv3 or HSRPv2 for IPv6 in production.

front.text: Show how to configure HSRP MD5 key chain authentication.

back.text:
key chain HSRP-KEYS
 key 1
  key-string ENCOR2024
!
interface vlan 10
 standby 10 ip 172.16.10.1
 standby 10 authentication md5 key-chain HSRP-KEYS
HSRP authentication prevents rogue routers from joining the HSRP group on a LAN. Use key chains for production because they support key rotation and lifetimes.

front.text: How does FHRP keep ARP entries valid for the VIP after a failover?

items: FHRP virtual MAC remains the same regardless of which physical router is active
When the new active router takes over, it sends gratuitous ARPs (GARPs) advertising the virtual MAC and VIP
Switches update their MAC tables to point the virtual MAC to the new active router's port
Hosts' ARP entries don't need to change — they still use the same VIP/MAC mapping
Recovery is essentially transparent to the host

front.text: Show how to enable sub-second failover for HSRP and VRRP.

back.text:
! HSRPv2 sub-second timers
standby version 2
standby 10 timers msec 200 msec 750
!
! VRRPv3 sub-second timers
vrrp 20 address-family ipv4
 timers advertise msec 200

Sub-second failover (~200-750 ms) requires HSRPv2 or VRRPv3. Faster timers = more CPU/control plane load — only use where required (financial trading, voice, IPTV). Combine with BFD on the physical link for even faster detection.

front.text: Why is FHRP almost always configured on Switched Virtual Interfaces (SVIs) rather than physical interfaces?

items: Each SVI represents one VLAN and one Layer 3 broadcast domain — perfect for one VIP
Multiple VLANs can have multiple FHRP groups on the same physical uplink
FHRP MAC and VIP coexist naturally with the SVI's virtual MAC and IP
SVIs survive physical interface failures (as long as one VLAN member is up)
Allows per-VLAN load sharing (alternate active routers across VLANs)

front.text: When would you use 'ip nat outside source' instead of 'ip nat inside source'?

rows: Standard — translate INSIDE addresses (most common: hiding private IPs behind public)
Translate OUTSIDE addresses — used when external networks have IPs that conflict with internal addressing or to hide external IPs from internal users

back.text: Outside source NAT is mostly seen in M&A or partner connectivity scenarios where two networks have overlapping IP ranges. Combining inside + outside source NAT = 'twice NAT'.

front.text: Why does PAT require special handling for ICMP traffic?

back.text: ICMP doesn't use TCP/UDP ports, so PAT can't multiplex ICMP echo requests using port numbers. Cisco PAT instead uses the ICMP Identifier field in the ICMP header — each ping session gets a unique Identifier, allowing PAT to distinguish multiple internal hosts pinging the same destination through one public IP. This is also why ICMP entries in 'show ip nat translations' show 'icmp' protocol and an Identifier (often shown as a port-like number) instead of TCP/UDP ports.

front.text: What are the default NAT translation timeouts for different protocols?

back.text:
ip nat translation tcp-timeout 3600
ip nat translation udp-timeout 60
ip nat translation icmp-timeout 30

front.text: Decode this 'show ip nat translations' output:

items: Row 1: TCP session — internal host 10.10.10.5:53412 reached Google's HTTPS (8.8.8.8:443) via PAT through 198.51.100.10:53412
Row 2: UDP DNS query — internal host 10.10.10.6:5353 queried Cloudflare (1.1.1.1:53) via PAT through 198.51.100.10:5353
Row 3: Static NAT entry — internal host 10.10.10.100 is permanently mapped to public IP 198.51.100.20 (no protocol/port; --- in outside fields)

front.text: Why do VPN tunnels often require NAT exemption (no-NAT) for traffic crossing the tunnel?

back.text: When a router has both PAT (for Internet access) and a VPN tunnel (for site-to-site connectivity), traffic destined to the remote VPN site should NOT be NATed — otherwise the remote site sees public IPs instead of the original private IPs. NAT exemption (using a deny rule in the NAT ACL) ensures VPN traffic bypasses NAT while regular Internet traffic still gets translated.

ip access-list extended ACL-NAT-EXCEPT
 deny ip 10.1.1.0 0.0.0.255 10.2.2.0 0.0.0.255   ! VPN traffic — don't NAT
 permit ip 10.1.1.0 0.0.0.255 any                ! Internet traffic — do NAT
!
ip nat inside source list ACL-NAT-EXCEPT interface GigabitEthernet0/0 overload

front.text: What is NAT64, and why is it used?

back.text: NAT64 (RFC 6146) translates between IPv6 and IPv4 — allowing IPv6-only hosts to communicate with IPv4-only services. It works with DNS64 (which synthesizes AAAA records from A records by prepending an IPv6 prefix to IPv4 addresses). Common use: enabling IPv6-only mobile networks (like T-Mobile US) to reach the IPv4 Internet. Replaces older mechanisms like NAT-PT, which has been deprecated.

front.text: What states does a tracked object report, and what triggers each?

rows: Tracked condition is met (interface line-protocol up, route present, etc.)
Tracking is starting, not yet evaluated

back.text:
show track          ! all tracked objects
show track 1        ! specific object
show track brief    ! summary

front.text: Show how to use a 'track threshold' object to monitor metrics like interface RX rate or load.

back.text:
track 5 interface GigabitEthernet0/0 ip routing
!
track 6 stub-object
 object 1 weight 30
 object 2 weight 70
 threshold weight up 50 down 30

Threshold tracking aggregates multiple sub-objects with weights and triggers up/down based on the weighted total exceeding the threshold. Useful for sophisticated FHRP failover where you want graduated response based on multiple conditions.

front.text: Why is it best practice to align HSRP active routers with STP root bridges?

back.text: If the HSRP active router is on Switch A but STP root is on Switch B, traffic from a host hits Switch A as the L2 destination, but STP forwarding paths might force it through Switch B first. This creates suboptimal Layer 2 forwarding — packets traverse extra hops. Aligning them on the same switch (HSRP active = STP root) ensures the optimal forwarding path matches the gateway.
Same applies to multi-VLAN designs — interleave HSRP active and STP root pairings to load-share traffic across switches without creating asymmetric paths.

front.text: An HSRP pair is in 'speak/standby' state but never goes active. What should you check?

items: Verify the VIP is in the same subnet as the interface IP
Confirm both routers are configured with the same group ID and HSRP version
Check that the routers can ping each other on the segment (Layer 2 reachability)
Verify interface line-protocol is UP
Check authentication settings — must match on both ends
Verify HSRP timers are consistent
Check for ACLs blocking 224.0.0.2 (HSRPv1) or 224.0.0.102 (HSRPv2) multicast
Look for CPU spikes or control-plane policing dropping HSRP hellos
Use 'debug standby' to see hello packet exchange

front.text: What's the typical sequence of HSRP state transitions when a router boots up and joins an existing HSRP group?

items: Initial — interface comes up, HSRP starts initializing
Listen — listening for hellos from other HSRP routers, identifies active and standby
Speak — sending hellos, participating in election if no active exists
Standby (or Active) — elected role; standby waits to become active if active fails
Without preemption: stays standby unless active fails
With preemption + higher priority: transitions Standby → Active

front.text: When would you choose VRRP over HSRP, and vice versa?

rows: Multi-vendor environment (non-Cisco devices in the mix)
Need extensive object tracking with decrement
Want preemption OFF by default for stability

front.text: Compare NAT and PAT in terms of session capacity per public IP.

rows: Only one internal host per public IP
Many internal hosts share one public IP via unique source ports

front.text: How do you quickly view all NAT-related configuration on a router?

back.text:
show running-config | include nat
show running-config | section interface | include nat
show ip nat translations
show ip nat statistics
The 'include nat' filter shows all NAT commands at a glance — useful for auditing and verifying NAT policy is consistent across the device.

front.text: What is an Application Layer Gateway (ALG), and why is it needed for some protocols crossing NAT?

back.text: Some protocols embed IP addresses inside the application payload (e.g., FTP control messages contain the IP/port of the data channel; SIP signaling contains RTP endpoint IPs). Standard NAT only translates the IP/TCP/UDP headers, not the payload. ALGs are protocol-aware NAT helpers that inspect and rewrite the embedded IPs in the payload so the protocol works across NAT. Common ALGs: FTP, SIP, H.323, DNS, TFTP, RTSP.

front.text: How do you disable a NAT ALG (e.g., for SIP) when it's causing problems with newer protocols?

back.text:
no ip nat service sip tcp port 5060
no ip nat service sip udp port 5060

Older NAT ALGs sometimes interfere with modern SIP, RTP encryption (SRTP), or VPN protocols. Disabling them lets the application handle NAT traversal itself (using STUN/TURN/ICE for SIP, for example).

front.text: Best practices for designing NTP in an enterprise network.

items: Use 2-3 stratum 1 sources (Internet pool.ntp.org, GPS, or radio time)
Have 2 internal stratum 2 servers acting as authoritative time for the rest of the network
Configure peers between the two stratum 2 servers for backup synchronization
All other devices = stratum 3+ clients of the internal stratum 2 servers
Source NTP queries from loopback interfaces
Enable NTP authentication on production
Set timezone consistently across devices for log correlation
Monitor 'show ntp status' regularly for sync issues

front.text: Best practices for designing FHRP in production networks.

items: Always configure preempt + delay (avoid preempt storms during reload)
Use VLAN ID = HSRP/VRRP/GLBP group ID for clarity
Set explicit priorities — don't rely on 'highest IP wins' tiebreaker
Align FHRP active router with STP root bridge per VLAN
Use object tracking + decrement for upstream link/route failures
Configure HSRP/VRRP authentication for security
Use sub-second timers only when required (BFD is often a better choice)
Document HSRP group IDs, priorities, and tracked objects
Test failover regularly during maintenance windows

front.text: Best practices for designing NAT/PAT in production networks.

items: Always mark inside/outside interfaces explicitly with 'ip nat inside/outside'
Use named extended ACLs (not standard) for granular NAT control
Tune translation timeouts based on workload — long-lived sessions vs short bursts
Use NAT exemption for VPN traffic (deny match in the NAT ACL)
Combine NAT pool + overload to maximize PAT session capacity
Monitor 'show ip nat statistics' for pool exhaustion or unusual translation rates
Disable unnecessary ALGs that interfere with modern protocols
Use static NAT only for servers that need fixed public IPs
Avoid NAT on routing protocol traffic — exclude OSPF/EIGRP/BGP via ACL
Consider IPv6 deployment to reduce NAT complexity long-term

front.text: Final IP Services exam-day quick-recall — additional facts not yet covered.

items: NTP polling interval auto-adjusts from 64s up to 1024s as accuracy stabilizes
NTP stepping = abrupt jump (offset > 128 ms); slewing = gradual (preferred)
NTP broadcast/multicast modes = lower CPU but less precise
PTP profiles tailor PTP for specific industries (telecom, power, industrial)
HSRP authentication = MD5 key chains for production
FHRP virtual MAC stays the same — GARP refreshes MAC tables on failover
Sub-second FHRP requires HSRPv2 or VRRPv3 + millisecond timers
FHRP almost always on SVIs, not physical interfaces
'ip nat outside source' = M&A / overlapping IP scenarios
PAT uses ICMP Identifier field instead of ports for ICMP multiplexing
Default NAT timeouts: TCP=24h, UDP=5min, ICMP=60s, DNS=60s
NAT exemption for VPN: deny VPN traffic in the NAT ACL
NAT64 + DNS64 = IPv6-to-IPv4 translation
ALGs (FTP, SIP, H.323) rewrite embedded IPs in payloads — sometimes need to be disabled for modern apps
Align HSRP active with STP root for optimal forwarding paths
Always enable preempt + delay on FHRP for proper recovery behavior

front.text: What's the difference between an overlay network and an underlay network?

rows: The physical transport network that provides IP connectivity
Internet, MPLS, private WAN, OSPF/EIGRP/BGP routed network
A logical/virtual network built ON TOP of the underlay using tunneling

back.text: Overlays add features the underlay can't natively provide: encryption, segmentation, multi-tenancy, virtualization. SD-WAN, SD-Access, and ACI all use overlays.

front.text: What is GRE, what was its original purpose, and what is its modern primary use case?

back.text: Generic Routing Encapsulation (GRE) is a tunneling protocol that encapsulates packets in IP for transport over an IP network. Originally created to carry non-routable legacy protocols like IPX over IP networks. Today GRE is most commonly used to: build VPNs over the Internet, transport multicast/routing protocol traffic between sites, and connect discontiguous networks.
GRE adds 24 bytes of overhead. GRE has NO encryption — it must be combined with IPsec for secure VPNs.

front.text: Show a basic GRE tunnel configuration including all required and recommended commands.

back.text:
interface Tunnel100
 bandwidth 4000                       ! recommended for IGP cost
 ip address 192.168.100.1 255.255.255.0
 ip mtu 1400                          ! recommended; GRE adds 24B
 keepalive 5 3                        ! recommended for liveness
 tunnel source GigabitEthernet0/1
 tunnel destination 100.64.2.2
tunnel source + tunnel destination + IP address are REQUIRED. tunnel mode gre ip is the default — explicit configuration is optional.

front.text: What is GRE recursive routing, and what's the symptom and fix?

back.text: Recursive routing happens when a router learns the tunnel destination IP via the tunnel itself — it then tries to send tunnel traffic through the very tunnel that depends on that route. The router detects this loop and brings the tunnel DOWN with %TUN-5-RECURDOWN syslog. The tunnel cycles up/down repeatedly.
%TUN-5-RECURDOWN: Tunnel100 temporarily disabled due to recursive routing

Fix: NEVER advertise the tunnel source/destination IPs into the routing protocol that runs ACROSS the tunnel. Filter them out, use static routes for the underlay, or run different routing protocols on underlay vs overlay.

front.text: What four security services does IPsec provide, and what does each do?

rows: Encrypt data so eavesdroppers can't read it
Detect tampering with data in transit
Prevent attackers from replaying captured packets

front.text: Compare IPsec Authentication Header (AH) and Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP).

back.text: ESP is the standard choice. AH is rarely used because it lacks encryption and breaks across NAT.

front.text: Compare IPsec Tunnel mode and Transport mode.

rows: Site-to-site VPNs, remote access — most common
Only the payload (original IP header preserved)
GRE over IPsec — IPsec encrypts the GRE payload while GRE provides routing

back.text: Tunnel mode is the default. Transport mode is used when there's already a tunneling protocol (like GRE) providing the routing layer.

front.text: Which IPsec encryption and hashing algorithms should be AVOIDED, and which should be used?

back.text: IOS XE 17.11.1+ rejects weak crypto by default. Field Notice 72510 documents this.

front.text: What happens in IKEv1 Phase 1 vs Phase 2, and what modes does each use?

rows: Establish bidirectional ISAKMP SA between peers
Main Mode (6 msgs, secure) or Aggressive Mode (3 msgs, less secure)
Establish unidirectional IPsec SAs for actual data

items: Total messages: Main Mode = 9 (6+3); Aggressive Mode = 6 (3+3)
Aggressive Mode reveals peer identities — less secure
Phase 2 typically negotiates encryption algorithm, hash, and PFS settings
ISAKMP runs over UDP/500

front.text: What happens in each of the 6 IKEv1 Main Mode messages?

items: MM1: Initiator sends SA proposals (encryption, hash, auth, DH group, lifetime)
MM2: Responder accepts a proposal
MM3: Initiator starts Diffie-Hellman key exchange
MM4: Responder completes DH — encryption now established for ISAKMP SA
MM5: Initiator sends authentication info (PSK or certificate)
MM6: Responder authenticates back — ISAKMP SA is now established

front.text: What are the key improvements of IKEv2 over IKEv1?

rows: Both peers must use same method
Asymmetric — each peer can use different method

front.text: Why combine GRE with IPsec instead of using IPsec alone?

items: GRE supports multicast and routing protocol traffic; IPsec alone (in tunnel mode) doesn't natively
GRE provides a tunnel interface for routing protocols to form adjacencies
GRE supports non-IP protocols (legacy use case)
IPsec provides the encryption GRE lacks
Best of both: GRE for transport flexibility + IPsec for security

back.text: Modern alternative: Static VTI (sVTI) over IPsec — simpler config, supports routing protocols, no GRE overhead.

front.text: Compare Crypto Maps and IPsec Profiles for tunnel protection.

back.text: Use IPsec profiles for new deployments. Crypto maps still appear in the field due to legacy infrastructure.

front.text: Show a complete GRE over IPsec configuration using IPsec profiles with Pre-Shared Key.

back.text:
! Phase 1 — ISAKMP policy
crypto isakmp policy 10
 authentication pre-share
 hash sha256
 encryption aes
 group 14
!
crypto isakmp key CISCO123 address 100.64.2.2
!
! Transform set (transport mode for GRE over IPsec)
crypto ipsec transform-set AES_SHA esp-aes esp-sha-hmac
 mode transport
!
! IPsec profile
crypto ipsec profile IPSEC_PROFILE
 set transform-set AES_SHA
!
! Apply to tunnel
interface Tunnel100
 ip address 192.168.100.1 255.255.255.0
 ip mtu 1400
 tunnel source GigabitEthernet0/1
 tunnel destination 100.64.2.2
 tunnel protection ipsec profile IPSEC_PROFILE

front.text: Show how to convert a GRE over IPsec tunnel into a static VTI (sVTI) over IPsec tunnel.

back.text:
! Change transform set to tunnel mode
crypto ipsec transform-set AES_SHA esp-aes esp-sha-hmac
 mode tunnel
!
! Add tunnel mode ipsec ipv4 to the tunnel interface
interface Tunnel100
 tunnel mode ipsec ipv4              ! VTI for IPv4
 tunnel protection ipsec profile IPSEC_PROFILE

rows: Static VTI for IPv4 — no GRE header, less overhead

front.text: Compare the major Cisco IPsec VPN solutions.
What is LISP, and what core problem does it solve?

back.text: LISP (Locator/ID Separation Protocol) is a routing architecture and control/data plane protocol that SEPARATES IP addresses into two distinct namespaces: Endpoint Identifiers (EIDs) and Routing Locators (RLOCs). Traditionally, an IP address represents BOTH identity and location — when a host moves, its IP must change. LISP decouples these so the EID stays the same while the RLOC changes, enabling mobility, multihoming, and traffic engineering without injecting more specifics into the global routing table.
LISP is the foundation of Cisco SD-Access. Originally created to address Internet routing table scalability problems (BGP route churn, non-aggregable prefixes).

front.text: Define the major LISP architecture roles: EID, RLOC, ITR, ETR, xTR, MS, MR.

rows: The endpoint's IP address — used INSIDE the LISP site
Internet-/core-facing IP address of an ETR — used OUTSIDE the LISP site
LISP router that ENCAPSULATES traffic leaving the LISP site
LISP router that DE-ENCAPSULATES traffic entering the LISP site
Router that performs both ITR and ETR functions
Stores EID-to-RLOC mappings learned from ETRs
Receives map requests from ITRs and forwards them to the MS
Single device acting as both Map Server and Map Resolver
Proxy ETR / Proxy ITR — bridges LISP and non-LISP sites

front.text: How is the LISP control plane similar to DNS?

back.text: Unlike traditional routing protocols (push model), LISP only requests routing info when actually needed — much more scalable for large topologies.

front.text: What encapsulation does LISP use, and what UDP port?

back.text: LISP performs IP-in-IP/UDP encapsulation. The ITR adds an outer IP header with RLOCs as source/destination, plus a UDP header with destination port 4341 (the LISP data plane port). A 24-bit Instance ID is included in the LISP shim header for VRF-style segmentation. The original packet (with EID source/destination) is preserved as the inner packet.

rows: LISP control plane (map register, map notify, map request, map reply)

front.text: Describe the LISP map registration process between an ETR and the MS.

items: ETR sends Map Register message to MS containing EID prefix and the RLOC to use for that prefix
ETR optionally sets the proxy map reply flag (P-bit) to ask MS to answer map requests on its behalf
MS replies with Map Notify confirming the registration was processed
Map Notify uses UDP port 4342 (both source and destination)

front.text: Walk through what happens when an ITR receives a packet to a remote EID it doesn't have in its map cache.

items: Endpoint sends packet to its default gateway (the ITR), destined to a remote EID
ITR does FIB lookup — no specific route found, only default; checks if source IS an EID prefix
If source matches an EID, ITR sends an encapsulated Map Request (UDP 4342) to the MR
MR forwards Map Request to the MS, which forwards it to the authoritative ETR
ETR (or MS, if proxy reply) sends a Map Reply directly to the ITR with the EID-to-RLOC mapping
ITR caches the mapping in its local map cache and updates its FIB
ITR LISP-encapsulates subsequent packets to the destination ETR's RLOC (UDP 4341)
ETR de-encapsulates and delivers to the local destination EID

front.text: What's the difference between a Proxy ETR (PETR) and a Proxy ITR (PITR)?

rows: ITRs send LISP-encapsulated traffic to PETR after receiving a negative map reply; PETR de-encapsulates and forwards to non-LISP destination
PITR receives traffic destined to a LISP EID, queries the map system, encapsulates and forwards to the appropriate ETR

back.text: Together, PETR + PITR (sometimes a single PxTR) bridge the LISP world with non-LISP networks.

front.text: What is VXLAN, and what limitations of traditional Layer 2 networks does it solve?

back.text: VXLAN (Virtual eXtensible LAN) is an overlay encapsulation that extends Layer 2 segments over a Layer 3 underlay using MAC-in-IP/UDP tunneling. Each VXLAN segment is an isolated Layer 2 network.

rows: VXLAN uses a 24-bit VNI = ~16 million segments
Underlay is L3 routed — full ECMP, no STP
Large MAC tables on every switch
MAC learning is per-segment, only on VTEPs
VXLAN with control plane (EVPN, LISP) supports it

front.text: What's the VXLAN UDP port, and what is the VNI?

rows: Replaces 12-bit VLAN ID; carried in VXLAN shim header
Original Ethernet frame inside outer IP/UDP/VXLAN headers

front.text: What is a VTEP, and what are its two interface types?

back.text: Virtual Tunnel Endpoint (VTEP) is the device that originates and terminates VXLAN tunnels. It maps Layer 2 frames to VNIs and encapsulates them for transmission across the L3 underlay.

rows: Connects local hosts; bridges traffic into VXLAN segments
L3-facing IP that identifies the VTEP and encapsulates/de-encapsulates VXLAN packets

back.text: Hosts that don't speak VXLAN connect via a VXLAN gateway, which is a VTEP that bridges classic VLANs to VXLAN segments.

front.text: Why does VXLAN require a separate control plane, and what are the four common options on Cisco?

back.text: The VXLAN spec defines ONLY a data plane encapsulation. It needs a control plane to handle MAC/IP-to-VTEP mapping and host mobility. Cisco supports four control planes:

rows: Most popular in DCs and private clouds

front.text: Compare LISP and VXLAN encapsulation.

back.text: Cisco SD-Access uses LISP for the control plane and VXLAN for the data plane — getting the best of both: dynamic mappings + L2 overlays.

front.text: Why is MTU configuration critical for overlay tunnels, and what are the typical overhead values?

back.text: Each tunnel encapsulation adds overhead. If the inner packet + tunnel headers exceed the underlay's MTU, the packet is fragmented or dropped (especially if DF bit is set). Always configure 'ip mtu' on tunnel interfaces to account for overhead.
Common practice: set 'ip mtu 1400' on tunnel interfaces when underlay MTU is 1500. Allow space for any encapsulation overhead.

front.text: Final overlay tunnels quick-recall checklist for the ENCOR exam.

items: Overlay = logical network on top of underlay (physical transport)
VPN = overlay used for secure communication over untrusted networks
GRE: 24 bytes overhead, no encryption, supports multicast/routing protocols
GRE recursive routing: tunnel destination must NOT be advertised via the tunnel itself
IPsec: AH (proto 51, no encryption, no NAT-T) vs ESP (proto 50, encryption, NAT-T)
IPsec modes: Tunnel (most common) vs Transport (used with GRE)
Avoid DES/3DES, MD5, DH groups 1/2/5; use AES, SHA-256+, DH 14+
IKEv1: 9 messages (Main+Quick) or 6 (Aggressive+Quick); IKEv2: 4 messages
ISAKMP UDP port 500; LISP control 4342; LISP data 4341; VXLAN 4789
Crypto maps (legacy) vs IPsec profiles (modern, used with tunnel protection)
tunnel mode ipsec ipv4 = static VTI (no GRE header)
DMVPN (mGRE+IPsec+NHRP), GET VPN (tunnel-less), FlexVPN (IKEv2 unified)
LISP separates EID (identity) from RLOC (location)
LISP roles: ITR/ETR (xTR), MS, MR, PETR, PITR
LISP control plane = pull model (like DNS)
VXLAN: MAC-in-IP/UDP encapsulation, 24-bit VNI = 16M segments
VXLAN spec defines only data plane — needs separate control plane (EVPN, multicast, LISP)
VTEP = origin/termination point of VXLAN tunnels
SD-Access = LISP control plane + VXLAN data plane
Always size MTU to account for encapsulation overhead

front.text: What is the default tunnel mode on a Cisco router, and which two GRE-related commands are OPTIONAL in a basic GRE configuration?

back.text: The default tunnel mode is 'tunnel mode gre ip' — so explicit configuration is OPTIONAL. The 'keepalive' command is also optional. Required commands are 'tunnel source', 'tunnel destination', and 'ip address'.

interface Tunnel100
 ip address 192.168.100.1 255.255.255.0
 tunnel source GigabitEthernet0/1
 tunnel destination 100.64.2.2
 ! tunnel mode gre ip   <- default, not required
 ! keepalive 5 3        <- optional but recommended

front.text: How does TTL behave in a GRE tunnel, and what's the default TTL value for GRE encapsulation?

back.text: When a packet enters a GRE tunnel, the original packet's TTL is encapsulated as part of the payload and decremented by ONLY 1 for the entire tunnel — regardless of how many physical hops the packet takes through the underlay. The OUTER GRE header gets a default TTL of 255.

interface Tunnel100
 tunnel ttl 64    ! change default GRE outer TTL
This is why a 'traceroute' across a GRE tunnel typically shows the tunnel as a single hop — the underlay path is hidden.

front.text: Why should you configure 'bandwidth' on a GRE tunnel interface even though it's a virtual interface?

items: Routing protocols like OSPF and EIGRP use bandwidth to compute path metrics
Without explicit bandwidth, the tunnel uses a low default value (often 100 kbps), making it look like a slow link
QoS policies referenced via 'service-policy' use the configured bandwidth
Set bandwidth to reflect the realistic minimum throughput of the underlay path
Tunnel interfaces have no concept of latency or actual physical capacity

back.text:
interface Tunnel100
 bandwidth 4000    ! 4 Mbps reference for IGP/QoS

front.text: When does a GRE tunnel interface line protocol go DOWN even though both endpoints are configured?

items: Tunnel destination is not in the routing table
Tunnel source interface is administratively down or has no IP
Recursive routing detected (tunnel destination learned via the tunnel)
Keepalives configured but not received from peer (tunnel goes DOWN/DOWN)
Without keepalives, tunnel stays UP/UP as long as a route to the destination exists — even if remote end is unreachable

back.text: Without keepalives, GRE has no built-in failure detection — routing protocol timers (slower) are the only way to detect a dead peer. Always configure keepalives or run BFD over the tunnel.

front.text: Show how to configure a GRE tunnel that runs over an IPv6 underlay.

back.text:
interface Tunnel100
 ipv6 address 2001:DB8:100::1/64
 tunnel source GigabitEthernet0/1
 tunnel destination 2001:DB8::2
 tunnel mode gre ipv6

front.text: What UDP ports does ISAKMP/IKE use, and what's special about port 4500?

rows: ISAKMP/IKEv1/IKEv2 default — used for IPsec negotiation when no NAT
NAT Traversal (NAT-T) — IPsec encapsulated in UDP/4500 to traverse NAT devices

back.text: When NAT is detected during IKE negotiation, peers automatically switch from UDP/500 to UDP/4500 and add an extra UDP header to ESP packets so they can pass through PAT/NAT devices.

front.text: What is the purpose of Diffie-Hellman in IPsec, and why is it CPU intensive?

back.text: Diffie-Hellman (DH) is an asymmetric key exchange protocol that allows two peers to derive a shared secret key over an UNSECURE channel — without ever transmitting the secret. The shared secret is then used for symmetric encryption (e.g., AES). DH itself is CPU-intensive because it relies on modular exponentiation with large prime numbers; modern key sizes (DH 14 = 2048 bits, DH 19 = 256-bit ECDH) require significant computation.

front.text: What is Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS), and why is it valuable?

back.text: PFS forces a NEW Diffie-Hellman exchange for every Phase 2 (or CREATE_CHILD_SA in IKEv2) negotiation. Without PFS, all session keys derive from the same Phase 1 master key — if that master key is compromised, ALL past and future session keys are compromised. With PFS, each session has an independent DH-derived key, so a compromise of one key doesn't expose any others.

crypto ipsec profile IPSEC_PROFILE
 set transform-set AES_SHA
 set pfs group14
PFS adds CPU cost (extra DH exchange) but greatly improves long-term security. Recommended for any production IPsec deployment.

front.text: Decode this output:

R1# show crypto isakmp sa
IPv4 Crypto ISAKMP SA
dst             src             state          conn-id status
100.64.1.1      100.64.2.2      QM_IDLE           1008 ACTIVE

items: dst/src = local and remote ISAKMP endpoints
QM_IDLE = ISAKMP SA is in QUICK MODE IDLE state — Phase 1 is established and ready for Phase 2 (Quick Mode) negotiations
ACTIVE = SA is operational and authenticated
conn-id = unique connection identifier on this device
Other states: MM_NO_STATE (negotiation failed), MM_KEY_EXCH (DH in progress), MM_AUTH_REQ (waiting for auth)

front.text: What do these counters from 'show crypto ipsec sa' tell you about a tunnel's health?

#pkts encaps: 40, #pkts encrypt: 40, #pkts digest: 40
#pkts decaps: 38, #pkts decrypt: 38, #pkts verify: 38

items: #pkts encaps = outbound packets encapsulated by IPsec
#pkts encrypt = outbound packets encrypted
#pkts digest = outbound packets that had HMAC integrity check applied
#pkts decaps = inbound packets de-encapsulated
#pkts decrypt = inbound packets decrypted
#pkts verify = inbound packets whose integrity was verified
All three numbers per direction should match each other and increment in tandem if traffic is flowing properly

back.text: If encaps > 0 but decaps = 0 → traffic going out but nothing coming back. Likely a return-path or remote-end issue.

front.text: What three protocols combine to make DMVPN, and what does each provide?

rows: Single tunnel interface terminates many spoke tunnels — no per-spoke config on the hub
Maps spoke tunnel IPs (overlay) to spoke physical IPs (underlay); enables dynamic spoke-to-spoke discovery
Provides the encryption layer for confidentiality and integrity

back.text: DMVPN supports hub-and-spoke + dynamic spoke-to-spoke tunnels that come up on demand and tear down when idle. Massive scalability for branch deployments.

front.text: What makes Cisco GET VPN unique compared to other IPsec VPN solutions?

items: Tunnel-less — preserves original IP header (no encapsulation in a new IP header)
Uses a Group Domain of Interpretation (GDOI) Key Server distributing keys to all members
All group members share the same encryption keys
Native any-to-any encryption — no per-pair tunnel setup
Multicast traffic is preserved (huge for video/IPTV/mcast apps)
QoS markings preserved on the original header
Perfect for MPLS/private WAN with full mesh requirements
Cisco-proprietary

back.text: Trade-off: original IP header is exposed, so GET VPN works only on TRUSTED networks (private MPLS). Don't use over the public Internet — use DMVPN or FlexVPN instead.

front.text: What is FlexVPN, and what makes it different from older Cisco IPsec solutions?

back.text: FlexVPN is Cisco's unified IKEv2-based VPN solution that supports site-to-site, hub-and-spoke, partial mesh (spoke-to-spoke), and remote access — all under a single, modular framework. Uses virtual access interfaces (created dynamically per session) instead of per-spoke crypto maps. Replaces the complexity of separate DMVPN, EzVPN, and crypto-map-based site-to-site configurations.

items: IKEv2-only (no IKEv1 fallback)
Asymmetric authentication (peers can use different methods)
EAP support — perfect for remote-access VPN
Dynamic spoke-to-spoke tunnels via Cisco IKEv2 redirect mechanism
Compatible with legacy crypto map configurations on peers
Supports IKEv2-based dynamic route distribution

front.text: What is a LISP negative map reply, and why is it useful?

back.text: When an ITR sends a Map Request for a destination that has NO EID-to-RLOC mapping in the LISP database (e.g., a non-LISP Internet destination), the MS responds with a NEGATIVE Map Reply. The reply includes the shortest non-LISP prefix that covers the destination but doesn't overlap any registered LISP EID. The ITR caches this prefix and forwards future traffic for that range to a configured Proxy ETR (PETR), which de-encapsulates and forwards natively to the non-LISP world.
Without negative map replies, ITRs would re-query the MS for every non-LISP destination — terrible scaling. Caching negative results allows efficient bypass to PETRs.

front.text: What is the LISP map cache, and how does it differ from a traditional routing table?

back.text: An ITR's map cache only contains mappings for destinations it's actively talking to — making it dramatically smaller than a full Internet routing table.

front.text: Beyond Internet routing scalability, what are the modern use cases for LISP?

items: SD-Access (Cisco's enterprise fabric solution) — LISP control + VXLAN data plane
Data center mobility — VMs move between sites without IP changes (LISP host mobility)
Multi-cloud and hybrid cloud connectivity
IoT — millions of endpoints with on-demand mapping resolution
IPv4-to-IPv6 transition (encapsulating IPv4 EIDs over IPv6 RLOCs and vice versa)
Traffic engineering without modifying global routing tables
Network virtualization with per-VRF Instance IDs

front.text: What are the most common LISP verification commands?

back.text:
show lisp site                       ! registered EID prefixes (on MS)
show ip lisp                         ! LISP configuration summary
show ip lisp database                ! local EID prefixes registered
show ip lisp map-cache               ! cached EID-to-RLOC mappings
show ip lisp statistics              ! data plane / control plane counters
show ip lisp instance-id <id>        ! per-VRF info

front.text: How does VXLAN with multicast underlay (flood-and-learn) populate MAC tables?

items: Each VNI is mapped to a multicast group (e.g., VNI 10000 → 239.1.1.1)
All VTEPs join the multicast group for VNIs they participate in
When a VTEP receives an unknown unicast, broadcast, or multicast frame (BUM traffic), it encapsulates and sends it to the VNI's multicast group
All VTEPs in the group receive the frame, learn the source MAC → source VTEP mapping, and forward locally if needed
Subsequent unicast frames between the learned MACs are sent directly VTEP-to-VTEP via unicast (no flooding needed)

back.text: Drawback: requires multicast in the underlay, which is operationally complex. EVPN replaces flood-and-learn with control-plane-based learning.

front.text: What does MP-BGP EVPN provide as the VXLAN control plane, and why is it preferred for data center deployments?

items: Eliminates flood-and-learn — MAC/IP-to-VTEP mappings are advertised via BGP
No multicast in the underlay required
Faster convergence — explicit BGP withdrawals vs MAC aging
Per-VTEP policy control (route maps, communities)
Supports both Layer 2 and Layer 3 overlays in a single protocol
ARP suppression — VTEP can answer ARP locally using EVPN-learned info, reducing broadcast
Multi-tenant via Route Distinguishers and Route Targets
Industry standard (RFC 7432) — multivendor interoperable

front.text: What is a VXLAN gateway, and what's the difference between Layer 2 and Layer 3 gateways?

rows: Bridges classic VLAN segments to VXLAN segments — same broadcast domain extended
Provides routing between VXLAN segments (different VNIs); also routes between VXLAN and external L3 networks

back.text: L3 gateways can be CENTRALIZED (one device routes between all VNIs — easy but creates a bottleneck) or DISTRIBUTED (each VTEP acts as the gateway for local VNIs — anycast gateway IP, far better scaling). Distributed is the modern best practice for fabric designs.

front.text: Walk through the encapsulation when Host A on VTEP-1 sends a frame to Host B on VTEP-2 in the same VXLAN segment.

items: Host A sends an Ethernet frame destined to Host B's MAC
VTEP-1 receives the frame on a local LAN interface mapped to VNI 10000
VTEP-1 looks up Host B's MAC in its VXLAN bridging table → finds it on VTEP-2
VTEP-1 prepends VXLAN shim header with VNI=10000
VTEP-1 wraps that with an outer UDP header (dst port 4789)
