VTEP-1 wraps the UDP packet with an outer IP header (src=VTEP-1 IP, dst=VTEP-2 IP)
Underlay routes the packet from VTEP-1 to VTEP-2 via standard L3 forwarding
VTEP-2 strips the outer IP/UDP/VXLAN headers, exposing the original Ethernet frame
VTEP-2 forwards the original frame out the local port mapped to VNI 10000

front.text: What is the order of headers in a VXLAN packet, from outer to inner?

items: Outer Ethernet header (underlay L2)
Outer IP header (src/dst = VTEP IPs)
Outer UDP header (dst port 4789)
VXLAN header (8 bytes, including 24-bit VNI)
INNER Ethernet header (original L2 from the host)
Original IP header (if applicable)
Original payload

back.text: [Outer Eth | Outer IP | UDP/4789 | VXLAN | Inner Eth | Inner IP | Payload]

front.text: What underlay characteristics are needed for a VXLAN/EVPN fabric to operate well?

items: Layer 3 routed underlay (no STP)
ECMP-capable for load distribution between leaf and spine
Loopback interfaces on each VTEP as the source for VXLAN tunnels
Fast convergence routing protocol (OSPF, IS-IS, or eBGP per leaf)
Jumbo MTU support (≥ 1600 bytes recommended) to avoid fragmentation of overlay frames
BFD enabled for sub-second failure detection
Multicast NOT required if using EVPN control plane
Multicast IS required for flood-and-learn VXLAN

front.text: What are the major roles of devices in an SD-Access fabric, and how do they map to LISP terminology?

rows: Stores EID-to-RLOC mappings; resolves map requests
L3 underlay device with no fabric awareness

back.text: SD-Access uses VXLAN for the data plane (Layer 2 + Layer 3 segmentation), LISP for the control plane (mappings + mobility), and TrustSec/SGTs for policy enforcement.

front.text: What single command shows the protocol type, source, destination, and state of a tunnel interface?

back.text: show interfaces tunnel <num>

Tunnel100 is up, line protocol is up
  Keepalive set (5 sec), retries 3
  Tunnel source 100.64.1.1, destination 100.64.2.2
  Tunnel protocol/transport GRE/IP        ! GRE tunnel
  ! OR
  Tunnel protocol/transport IPSEC/IP      ! VTI IPsec tunnel
The 'Tunnel protocol/transport' line is the fastest way to confirm whether a tunnel is GRE, GRE/IPv6, IPSEC/IP, or another mode.

front.text: An IPsec tunnel fails to come up — Phase 1 never completes. What's a logical troubleshooting flow?

items: Verify ISAKMP/IKE policy parameters MATCH on both peers (encryption, hash, DH group, lifetime)
Check the PSK on both ends — must be identical and bound to correct peer IP
Ensure UDP/500 (and UDP/4500 if NAT) is open through firewalls and ACLs
Verify IP reachability between IKE source IPs (ping the peer's source from this side)
Check 'show crypto isakmp sa' state — MM_NO_STATE typically means proposals don't match
Run 'debug crypto isakmp' temporarily — look for 'no proposal chosen' or auth failure errors
Verify clock sync if using certificates (cert-based auth fails with bad time)
Check ACLs / crypto map ACLs on both ends if using crypto maps

front.text: Why does MP-BGP EVPN not require multicast in the underlay, while flood-and-learn VXLAN does?

back.text: Flood-and-learn VXLAN uses multicast to deliver BUM (broadcast, unknown unicast, multicast) traffic — there's no other way for a VTEP to know which other VTEPs need a copy. EVPN, by contrast, exchanges MAC/IP-to-VTEP mappings via BGP advertisements PROACTIVELY. So when a VTEP receives a frame, it already knows which VTEP owns the destination — no flooding needed. BUM traffic in EVPN can be handled by ingress replication (unicast copies to each VTEP) instead of multicast.
This is a major reason most modern data center deployments choose EVPN — eliminating multicast simplifies the underlay enormously.

front.text: Compare GRE, IPsec, LISP, and VXLAN encapsulation overhead and scope.

back.text: VXLAN is the only one of these that can extend Layer 2 segments — that's its defining characteristic.

front.text: Match each use case to the most appropriate overlay technology.

rows: Branch network with hundreds of sites + dynamic mesh
Enterprise MPLS WAN with any-to-any encryption + multicast
Data center fabric with multi-tenant L2 extension
Carry routing protocol over a public network
Connect IPv6 islands over an IPv4 underlay

front.text: Final overlay tunnels exam-day master recap with extra facts not yet covered.

items: tunnel mode gre ip is the default — explicit configuration is OPTIONAL
Tunnel TTL: outer GRE TTL defaults to 255; inner TTL decrements only by 1 across the tunnel
Always set 'bandwidth' on tunnel interfaces for IGP/QoS reference
ISAKMP UDP/500; NAT-T UDP/4500; LISP control 4342, data 4341; VXLAN 4789
PFS forces a fresh DH per Phase 2 — recommended for production
DMVPN = mGRE + NHRP + IPsec; GET VPN = tunnel-less group encryption (MPLS only)
FlexVPN = unified IKEv2 framework (replaces older Cisco IPsec solutions)
QM_IDLE in 'show crypto isakmp sa' = Phase 1 complete and ready
'#pkts encaps' should match '#pkts decaps' over time for healthy bidirectional traffic
LISP map cache = pull-based; only contains active conversations
Negative map reply caches non-LISP destinations for PETR forwarding
VXLAN flood-and-learn requires multicast underlay; EVPN does not
Distributed anycast gateway = modern best practice for L3 in VXLAN fabrics
Outer-to-inner VXLAN headers: Eth → IP → UDP/4789 → VXLAN → Inner Eth → Inner IP → Payload
SD-Access maps: Edge Node = xTR; Border Node = PxTR; Control Plane Node = MS/MR
Always verify MTU vs encapsulation overhead — set 'ip mtu 1400' as a safe default on tunnels
Use 'show interfaces tunnel <num>' to instantly verify tunnel mode and state

front.text: Why is GRE often used to carry routing protocol traffic, and what does this enable?

back.text: Many routing protocols (OSPF, EIGRP) rely on multicast hello packets that traditional IPsec tunnel mode cannot natively transport. GRE encapsulates ANY Layer 3 protocol — including multicast and even non-IP — into a unicast IP packet that can traverse the underlay. This makes GRE the standard choice when you need to run a dynamic routing protocol across a VPN, get full IGP convergence, and exchange route updates between sites.
Combining GRE with IPsec (GRE over IPsec) gives you both the encryption of IPsec AND the protocol/multicast flexibility of GRE.

front.text: What's the advantage of using a loopback interface as the GRE tunnel source instead of a physical interface?

items: Loopback survives physical interface failure — tunnel stays up if you have multiple paths to the loopback
Predictable source IP for ACLs, firewalls, and VPN policies
Not tied to a specific physical link — works seamlessly with redundant transport
Easier to identify the tunnel endpoint in logs and 'show' output

back.text:
interface Loopback0
 ip address 1.1.1.1 255.255.255.255
!
interface Tunnel100
 tunnel source Loopback0
 tunnel destination 2.2.2.2
Best practice for production: source tunnels from a loopback, advertise loopbacks via the underlay routing protocol, and you get instant tunnel resilience over redundant transport.

front.text: What is Path MTU Discovery (PMTUD), and why does it sometimes fail across overlay tunnels?

back.text: PMTUD allows endpoints to discover the smallest MTU along a path by sending packets with the DF (Don't Fragment) bit set; routers along the path that can't forward the packet send back ICMP 'Fragmentation Needed' messages, prompting the source to reduce its packet size. PMTUD breaks when:

items: Firewalls or ACLs block ICMP type 3 code 4 (Fragmentation Needed) messages
Tunnel encapsulation adds overhead but the inner DF bit is preserved — outer fragmentation needed messages may not get back to the original source
Source endpoint ignores ICMP responses (rare, but happens)

back.text:
interface Tunnel100
 ip mtu 1400
 ip tcp adjust-mss 1360
Workaround: configure 'ip tcp adjust-mss' on the tunnel — the router rewrites TCP MSS in SYN packets to a safe value, avoiding fragmentation entirely for TCP traffic.

front.text: What does 'ip tcp adjust-mss' do, and what value should you typically use on a tunnel interface?

back.text: 'ip tcp adjust-mss' rewrites the Maximum Segment Size (MSS) option in TCP SYN packets passing through the interface. By lowering the MSS, the sender uses smaller TCP segments that fit within the tunnel MTU after encapsulation overhead, avoiding fragmentation problems entirely (only for TCP — UDP/ICMP still rely on PMTUD).

interface Tunnel100
 ip mtu 1400
 ip tcp adjust-mss 1360

front.text: What is a GRE tunnel key, and when is it useful?

back.text:
interface Tunnel100
 tunnel key 1234
 tunnel source GigabitEthernet0/1
 tunnel destination 100.64.2.2

A tunnel key is a 32-bit value carried in the GRE header. It's used to differentiate multiple GRE tunnels that share the same source/destination IPs — for example, when you have multiple parallel tunnels for redundancy or different policies. Both ends must have matching keys for the tunnel to work. Tunnel keys are also used in mGRE (DMVPN) deployments where many spokes share a single hub tunnel interface.

front.text: How does multipoint GRE (mGRE) differ from point-to-point GRE, and what makes it suitable for DMVPN?

back.text:
interface Tunnel100
 tunnel mode gre multipoint
 tunnel source GigabitEthernet0/1
 ! NO tunnel destination — set dynamically by NHRP

front.text: What fields are in an ESP header, and where does the actual encryption happen?

rows: Identifies the SA — receiver uses it to know which key/algorithm to apply
Original packet content (in tunnel mode) or transport-layer payload (in transport mode)
Aligns the payload to block size (for block ciphers like AES)
Specifies the protocol of the encrypted payload
HMAC of the encrypted data — verifies integrity and authenticity

back.text: Encryption covers Payload + Padding + Pad Length + Next Header. ICV is calculated over the SPI, sequence number, and encrypted data — it's NOT encrypted itself but provides authentication.

front.text: How does IPsec prevent replay attacks, and what is the default anti-replay window size?

back.text: Each ESP packet includes a unique sequence number. The receiver maintains a sliding window (default 64 packets) of accepted sequence numbers. Packets with sequence numbers BELOW the window or already seen WITHIN the window are dropped. This prevents an attacker from capturing valid IPsec packets and replaying them later to inject duplicate traffic.
crypto ipsec security-association replay window-size 1024
Default window is 64. Increase for high-throughput tunnels where reordering is possible — e.g., over QoS-enabled paths or asymmetric routing. Common values: 128, 512, 1024.

front.text: What's the difference between Phase 1 and Phase 2 lifetimes, and what are typical default values?

rows: How long the bidirectional ISAKMP SA stays valid before re-authentication
How long IPsec SAs stay valid before fresh keys are negotiated
Time OR volume — both are negotiated

back.text: Lifetimes don't have to match exactly between peers — IPsec uses the LOWER of the two negotiated values. Shorter lifetimes = more re-keying = better security but more CPU cost.

front.text: What are common crypto ACL misconfigurations when using crypto maps, and why are they so problematic?

items: Mismatched ACLs between peers — both sides MUST mirror exactly (subnet ranges, source/dest order)
Overlapping crypto ACLs across multiple peers — IPsec gets confused which SA to use
Forgetting to permit GRE protocol (47) in the crypto ACL when encrypting GRE
Using 'permit ip any any' as a shortcut — encrypts everything, including return traffic that shouldn't be encrypted
Crypto ACLs eat TCAM space on switches — large ACLs cause hardware exhaustion

back.text: These pitfalls are the main reason CRYPTO MAPS ARE LEGACY. IPsec profiles + tunnel protection (sVTI or GRE over IPsec with profiles) eliminate the need for crypto ACLs entirely.

front.text: What roles exist in a GET VPN deployment, and what is the function of each?

rows: Authenticates GMs, generates and distributes group keys, controls policy
Encrypts/decrypts data using shared group keys; registers with KS
Multiple KSs for redundancy — they sync group state

back.text: GET VPN uses Group Domain of Interpretation (GDOI, RFC 6407) to distribute keys. All GMs in the group share the SAME encryption keys — no per-pair tunnel negotiation. This is what enables tunnel-less encryption.

front.text: Compare SSL/TLS-based remote access VPN with IPsec-based remote access VPN.

rows: Sometimes blocked at hotels, public Wi-Fi
Cisco Secure Client or third-party IKEv2 client
Slightly higher CPU due to TLS overhead

back.text: Most enterprises use AnyConnect over TLS/DTLS for remote access — better firewall traversal and more flexible auth options.

front.text: What's the precise difference between a LISP Map Server (MS) and a Map Resolver (MR)?

rows: Receives Map Register messages from ETRs; stores EID-to-RLOC mappings; can answer Map Requests on behalf of ETRs (proxy reply)
Receives Map Requests from ITRs; forwards them to the appropriate MS via the mapping database system
Single device performing both functions — common in small deployments

back.text: In production, MS and MR are usually combined on the same device (MS/MR) — and SD-Access calls this the 'Control Plane Node'.

front.text: How does LISP enable host mobility — moving an endpoint between sites without changing its IP?

items: An endpoint moves from Site A to Site B; the EID stays the same
Site B's ETR detects the new endpoint (e.g., via ARP, DHCP, or special detection mechanisms)
Site B's ETR sends a Map Register to the MS with the EID and Site B's RLOC
MS updates its EID-to-RLOC mapping for that EID
Site A's ETR detects the endpoint is no longer present and sends a Map Register to remove its old mapping
When ITRs send traffic destined to that EID, the MS returns the new RLOC (Site B's)
Existing ITRs with stale cache entries get notified via solicit-map-request to refresh

back.text: This is the foundation of SD-Access wired/wireless mobility: a user can roam without changing their IP, and traffic continues to flow.

front.text: What is the LISP Instance ID, and how is it analogous to MPLS VPNs?

back.text: The Instance ID is a 24-bit value carried in the LISP shim header that provides VRF-style segmentation. Different Instance IDs identify completely separate LISP virtual networks — even if they use overlapping EID address ranges. This is conceptually identical to how MPLS L3VPN uses VPN labels (or Route Distinguishers) to separate customer routes that may use the same private IP space.

rows: VRF tables tied to Instance ID

front.text: What is LISP DDT, and how is it analogous to DNS hierarchy?

back.text: LISP Delegated Database Tree (DDT) is a hierarchical mapping system for LISP that mirrors how DNS works. Instead of every MS knowing every EID, DDT introduces a tree structure where: root DDT nodes know about top-level prefix delegations; child nodes know about more specific prefixes; queries are referred down the tree until reaching the authoritative MS for a given EID. This makes LISP scalable to Internet-size deployments — just like DNS scales to billions of domains.
DDT is essential for LISP-based Internet deployments. Most enterprise SD-Access deployments don't need DDT because they use a flat or two-level hierarchy with limited EID ranges.

front.text: How is BUM (broadcast, unknown unicast, multicast) traffic handled in VXLAN, and what are the two main approaches?

rows: Each VNI mapped to a multicast group; VTEPs join the group; BUM traffic flooded to the group
Pros: efficient one-to-many. Cons: requires multicast routing
VTEP makes individual unicast copies for each remote VTEP that has the VNI
Pros: no multicast needed. Cons: replication scales poorly with many VTEPs

back.text: EVPN supports both methods. Ingress replication is more common in modern data center fabrics because it eliminates underlay multicast complexity.

front.text: What is VXLAN ARP suppression, and why does it require an EVPN control plane?

back.text: ARP suppression allows a local VTEP to ANSWER ARP requests on behalf of remote endpoints — using ARP/IP information learned via EVPN advertisements. This prevents ARP broadcast traffic from being flooded across the fabric. It REQUIRES an EVPN control plane because ARP/IP-to-MAC mappings must be learned and advertised proactively (not via flooding). With multicast/flood-and-learn, the VTEP doesn't have remote ARP info ahead of time, so ARP suppression isn't possible.
ARP suppression dramatically reduces broadcast traffic in large fabrics — essential for scaling to hundreds or thousands of VTEPs.

front.text: What is the distributed anycast gateway in VXLAN EVPN, and what problem does it solve?

back.text: In a centralized L3 gateway design, ALL routed traffic between VNIs must go to a single gateway switch — creating a bottleneck and adding latency for traffic that could be routed locally. With distributed anycast gateway, EVERY VTEP shares the same gateway IP and MAC for each subnet — so endpoints' default gateway is always 'on the local switch.' Traffic between subnets is routed locally at the ingress VTEP without traversing the fabric to a central point.

items: Same gateway IP and MAC on every VTEP (anycast)
Routed traffic stays local — no hairpin to a central gateway
Endpoints don't need to change config when moving between VTEPs
Massively scalable — gateway capacity grows with the fabric

back.text: Distributed anycast gateway is the standard for modern VXLAN/EVPN data center fabrics.

front.text: How do VLAN IDs map to VXLAN VNIs at a VTEP?

back.text: Each VTEP maintains a mapping table: 'VLAN X on local LAN interface = VNI Y in VXLAN segment.' When a frame arrives on a tagged port with VLAN X, the VTEP encapsulates it with VNI Y into VXLAN. When a VXLAN frame arrives with VNI Y, the VTEP de-encapsulates and forwards as VLAN X locally. The VLAN ID is LOCAL to each VTEP — different VTEPs can use different VLAN IDs for the same VNI, as long as they map them consistently.
This 'VLAN of significance only locally' is what makes VXLAN scale beyond 4094 segments — the VNI is the global identifier; VLANs are just per-device tags.

front.text: Compare symmetric and asymmetric IRB (Integrated Routing and Bridging) in VXLAN EVPN.

rows: Ingress VTEP routes between subnets locally if both VNIs exist; otherwise uses the source VNI to send across the fabric
Simpler, but every VTEP must have a copy of every VNI it might route from
Uses an L3 VNI as a transit segment between L2 VNIs; ingress VTEP routes onto the L3 VNI; egress VTEP routes off the L3 VNI
More scalable — VTEPs only need the VNIs of locally connected hosts + the L3 VNI

back.text: Symmetric IRB is the standard for modern Cisco VXLAN/EVPN fabrics — uses far less state per VTEP and scales to thousands of VTEPs.

front.text: What underlay routing protocols are commonly used for VXLAN/EVPN fabrics, and what are the trade-offs?

rows: Single-area scale limits at very large size
Highly scalable, simple to configure for clos topologies
Less common in enterprise — operational unfamiliarity
Massively scalable, vendor-neutral, granular policy control
More complex BGP configuration; one ASN per leaf

back.text: For very large fabrics (1000+ VTEPs), eBGP per leaf is the de facto standard. Smaller fabrics often use OSPF for simplicity.

front.text: In a VXLAN EVPN fabric, what's the difference between the underlay BGP and the overlay BGP?

rows: Provides reachability between VTEP loopbacks (the RLOCs)
Distributes MAC, IP, and prefix info between VTEPs as VPN routes

back.text: These are typically two separate BGP sessions (or address families on the same session). The underlay handles transport; the overlay handles tenant data plane state. Both run between the same pair of devices but serve completely different purposes.

front.text: What's the typical convergence flow when a VTEP fails in a VXLAN EVPN fabric?

items: Underlay routing protocol (e.g., OSPF, eBGP) detects the dead VTEP via timers or BFD
Underlay routes pointing to the dead VTEP's loopback are withdrawn
Remaining VTEPs lose reachability to the failed VTEP — VXLAN tunnels to that VTEP fail
Overlay BGP-EVPN withdraws MAC/IP routes that pointed to the dead VTEP
Local VTEPs remove those EVPN routes from their forwarding tables
If the failed endpoint moves to another VTEP, EVPN re-advertises with the new VTEP next-hop
Convergence end-to-end can be sub-second with BFD enabled in the underlay

back.text: BFD on the underlay is critical for fast convergence — without it, you're stuck with default routing protocol timers (10s+ for OSPF, 180s for default BGP).

front.text: Compare Layer 2 vs Layer 3 overlay characteristics.

rows: Original Ethernet frame inside outer headers
Original IP packet inside outer headers

front.text: What command enables Path MTU Discovery on a tunnel interface, and why use it?

back.text:
interface Tunnel100
 tunnel path-mtu-discovery

Enabling 'tunnel path-mtu-discovery' allows the tunnel interface to dynamically detect the smallest MTU on the underlay path between the two endpoints by sending packets with DF=1 and listening for ICMP 'fragmentation needed' messages. The tunnel then automatically adjusts its MTU based on what the path can handle. Helps avoid fragmentation issues without manually tuning MTU.
Best practice: combine with 'ip tcp adjust-mss' for TCP traffic and explicit 'ip mtu' as a safety net.

cards.subtopic: Tunnel Mode vs Transport with VTI

front.text: When using sVTI (static Virtual Tunnel Interface) over IPsec, what TRANSFORM SET MODE should you use, and why?

back.text:
crypto ipsec transform-set AES_SHA esp-aes esp-sha-hmac
 mode tunnel       ! REQUIRED for sVTI

sVTI requires TUNNEL mode in the transform set — the original packet is fully encapsulated in a new IP header. This is different from GRE over IPsec, where TRANSPORT mode is preferred because GRE provides the routing layer and IPsec just encrypts the GRE payload. Mixing them up is a common mistake: using transport mode with sVTI breaks routing because the original IP header is exposed instead of being encrypted.

front.text: A customer has 500 branches connecting to a hub data center, needs dynamic spoke-to-spoke for direct traffic, and wants to encrypt over the public Internet. Which VPN solution is best, and why?

back.text: DMVPN. Reasons:

items: 500 branches require massive scalability — DMVPN handles thousands of spokes
Dynamic spoke-to-spoke is core to DMVPN via NHRP
Public Internet underlay = need encryption (IPsec) — DMVPN integrates IPsec naturally
Hub-spoke registration handles spoke address changes (e.g., DHCP)
Routing protocols (EIGRP, OSPF, BGP) work natively over GRE
GET VPN won't work — it requires a trusted (private MPLS) underlay
FlexVPN works but DMVPN is more proven at this scale for branch deployments

front.text: Quick decision tree: which overlay technology should you use based on the requirement?

items: Need encryption over Internet, multi-vendor → IPsec sVTI or site-to-site IPsec
Need encryption + multicast/routing protocols over Internet → GRE over IPsec (or DMVPN at scale)
Need 100+ spokes with dynamic mesh over Internet → DMVPN or FlexVPN
Need any-to-any encryption over private MPLS preserving multicast → GET VPN
Need IKEv2-based unified VPN (S2S + RA) → FlexVPN
Need data center L2 extension between racks → VXLAN with EVPN
Need campus fabric with mobility and segmentation → SD-Access (LISP + VXLAN)
Need IP mobility without changing endpoint IP → LISP (or LISP-based SD-Access)
Need to carry IPv6 over IPv4 underlay (or vice versa) → GRE with appropriate mode, or LISP

front.text: Final overlay tunnels exam recap — extra facts to lock in.

items: Use loopback as tunnel source for resilience and predictable IPs
PMTUD relies on ICMP 'fragmentation needed' (type 3 code 4) — firewalls often break it
'ip tcp adjust-mss 1360' is a safe value for tunnels with MTU 1400
GRE tunnel key allows multiple parallel GRE tunnels between same endpoints (used in mGRE/DMVPN)
ESP fields: SPI, sequence, encrypted payload, padding, ICV
Default IPsec replay window = 64 packets; can extend to 1024 for high-throughput tunnels
ISAKMP lifetime default 24h; IPsec lifetime default 1h or 4.6 GB
Crypto ACLs are TCAM-heavy and easy to misconfigure — prefer IPsec profiles
GET VPN requires Key Server (KS) and Group Members (GMs); uses GDOI
AnyConnect (TLS/DTLS) preferred for remote access over IPsec — better firewall traversal
LISP MS = stores mappings; LISP MR = resolves queries; combined as MS/MR in production
LISP host mobility moves EID between sites without changing IP
LISP Instance ID = MPLS VPN label equivalent (24-bit segmentation)
DDT = LISP's hierarchical mapping system, analogous to DNS
VXLAN BUM = ingress replication (no multicast) or multicast underlay
EVPN ARP suppression eliminates broadcast ARP across the fabric
Distributed anycast gateway is the modern VXLAN gateway design
Symmetric IRB scales better than asymmetric IRB for VXLAN routing
eBGP per leaf (RFC 7938) is standard for very large VXLAN fabrics
Underlay BGP (IPv4) carries VTEP reachability; overlay BGP (EVPN) carries tenant data
Sub-second VXLAN convergence requires BFD on the underlay
sVTI requires TUNNEL mode in transform set; GRE over IPsec uses TRANSPORT mode

front.text: What is the relationship between EtherChannel, Port-Channel, and LACP/PAgP?

back.text: EtherChannel is the logical bundled interface. Port-Channel is the logical interface that gets created. LACP and PAgP are the negotiation/control PROTOCOLS that manage which physical links are allowed to join the bundle. They are NOT the bundle itself.

rows: The logical interface that gets created (e.g., Po1)
No protocol — static, manually formed bundle

back.text: Mental model: Port-Channel1 = the team. Gi0/3 + Gi0/4 = team members. LACP/PAgP = the coach deciding who can join. EtherChannel creates the bundle; LACP/PAgP help negotiate and maintain which links belong in it.

front.text: Common misconception: 'LACP creates EtherChannel.' Why is this inaccurate, and what's the correct framing?

back.text: EtherChannel creates the BUNDLE. LACP and PAgP only NEGOTIATE and maintain which links belong in that bundle. You can have EtherChannel WITHOUT either protocol by configuring 'mode on' — this creates a static EtherChannel where the switch forces the links into the bundle without any negotiation.

! With LACP — protocol negotiates membership
interface range gi0/3-4
 channel-group 1 mode active

! Static (no protocol)
interface range gi0/3-4
 channel-group 1 mode on

Both create the same logical Port-Channel interface — the only difference is whether a control protocol manages link inclusion. Static (mode on) is risky because there's no consistency checking — mismatched configs can create traffic-blackholing loops.

front.text: Decode this 'show etherchannel summary' output and identify the bundle, the control protocol, and the member status.

Group Port-channel  Protocol    Ports
1     Po1(SU)       LACP        Gi0/3(P) Gi0/4(P)

items: Po1 = the logical Port-Channel interface (the bundle)
(SU) = State: S = Layer 2 (switch port), U = In use / Up
LACP = the control protocol negotiating link membership
Gi0/3, Gi0/4 = the physical member interfaces
(P) = Port is bundled successfully into the EtherChannel
Other common port flags: (I) = Independent (not bundled), (s) = Suspended, (D) = Down, (H) = Hot standby

back.text: This output cleanly separates the BUNDLE (Po1) from the PROTOCOL managing it (LACP) from the MEMBER ports and their status. Always check that all expected member ports show (P) — anything else means the bundle is degraded or partially formed.

front.text: How does an RF signal propagate through free space, and why do the electric/magnetic fields keep moving outward?

back.text: A transmitter sends an alternating current into an antenna. This sets up oscillating electric and magnetic fields that are perpendicular to each other and travel together as a wave. The signal must keep CHANGING (cycling up and down) to keep the fields oscillating and pushing outward. The waves expand in three dimensions away from the antenna — analogous to ripples expanding when a pebble drops into water. The receiver's antenna intercepts these waves and converts them back into an electrical signal.

front.text: What is frequency, what unit measures it, and what are the standard frequency unit names?

back.text: Frequency = the number of complete cycles a signal makes in 1 second. Measured in Hertz (Hz), where 1 Hz = 1 cycle per second.
RF (radio frequency) is the range from ~3 kHz to 300 GHz. Wi-Fi operates primarily in 2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, and 6 GHz bands.

front.text: What are the three main Wi-Fi bands and their respective frequency ranges?

rows: U-NII-5 through U-NII-8; Wi-Fi 6E and Wi-Fi 7

back.text: U-NII = Unlicensed National Information Infrastructure. The 5 GHz band has a gap (5.350–5.470 GHz / U-NII-2B) that's not currently used for Wi-Fi.

front.text: Why are 2.4 GHz channels considered overlapping while 5 GHz and 6 GHz channels are non-overlapping?

back.text: 2.4 GHz channels are spaced only 5 MHz apart (channel center frequencies), but Wi-Fi signals use 22 MHz of bandwidth. That means adjacent channel numbers (e.g., 1 and 2) almost completely overlap. Only channels 1, 6, and 11 are far enough apart to NOT overlap. In contrast, 5 GHz and 6 GHz channels are spaced 20 MHz apart and use ~20 MHz signal bandwidth — so adjacent channels can be used simultaneously without interference.

front.text: What is signal phase, and what happens when two signals are in phase vs. 180° out of phase?

back.text: Phase = the time-shifted position of one signal relative to another, measured in degrees (0° = start of cycle, 360° = full cycle).

rows: Signals cancel each other out — destructive interference

back.text: Phase manipulation is the foundation of transmit beamforming (TBF) and many MIMO techniques.

front.text: What is wavelength, and how does it relate to frequency?

back.text: Wavelength (λ) = physical distance the wave travels during one complete cycle. Higher frequency = shorter wavelength because the wave cycles faster while traveling at constant speed (~speed of light in air).
Antenna physical size is typically a fraction of the wavelength (often 1/4 or 1/2 λ). This is why higher-frequency antennas are physically smaller.

front.text: What is a decibel (dB), and why is it useful for comparing RF power levels?

back.text: A decibel (dB) is a logarithmic ratio between two power values: dB = 10 log₁₀(P₂/P₁). It's useful because RF power values can span many orders of magnitude (e.g., 0.00001 mW to 100 mW = 1,000,000:1 ratio). The logarithmic scale compresses huge ratios into small, manageable numbers and allows multiplication/division of power levels to become simple addition/subtraction of dB values.
dB by itself is a RATIO. dBm, dBi, and dBd are dB values referenced to specific absolute units.

front.text: What are the three dB laws every wireless engineer should memorize?

back.text: These laws let you compare powers in your head without a calculator. Combine them: doubling 3 times = +9 dB; doubling then × 10 = +13 dB; ÷10 then ÷2 = -13 dB.

front.text: Transmitter A is 1 mW; transmitter B is 100 mW. How much louder is B in dB?

back.text: Answer: B is 20 dB greater than A. Mental shortcut: every multiply by 10 adds 10 dB; every double adds 3 dB. So 100× = 10 × 10 = +10 + 10 = +20 dB. No calculator needed.

front.text: What is dBm, and what value does 0 dBm represent?

back.text: dBm = decibel relative to 1 milliwatt. It's an ABSOLUTE power measurement, not a ratio. 0 dBm = exactly 1 mW. Negative dBm values represent fractions of a milliwatt; positive dBm values represent multiples of a milliwatt.
Wi-Fi transmit powers are typically +10 to +20 dBm (10–100 mW). Receive signal strengths are typically -30 to -90 dBm.

front.text: What is EIRP, and how is it calculated?

back.text: Effective Isotropic Radiated Power (EIRP) = the actual power radiated from the antenna, taking into account the transmitter power, cable loss, and antenna gain. It's the regulated value that government agencies cap to limit RF exposure and interference.
EIRP = Transmitter Power (dBm) - Cable Loss (dB) + Antenna Gain (dBi)
Example: 20 dBm TX – 2 dB cable loss + 5 dBi antenna gain = 23 dBm EIRP. This is the value that matters for both range AND regulatory compliance.

front.text: Transmitter = +20 dBm, cable loss = 2 dB, antenna gain = 5 dBi. What is the EIRP?

back.text:
EIRP = 20 dBm - 2 dB + 5 dBi
     = +23 dBm

EIRP = +23 dBm. This means the antenna is radiating the equivalent of an isotropic antenna fed with 200 mW (since +23 dBm = 200 mW). Note: receiver-side antennas and cables aren't part of EIRP — EIRP is purely about what's leaving the transmit antenna.

front.text: What's the difference between dBi and dBd, and how do you convert between them?

back.text: Conversion: dBi = dBd + 2.14
A dipole has 2.14 dBi gain over isotropic. So an antenna spec'd as '6 dBd' = 8.14 dBi. Always normalize to dBi when calculating EIRP.

front.text: What causes Free Space Path Loss (FSPL), and what two factors affect it?

back.text: FSPL is caused by SPREADING — RF energy expands spherically from the antenna, so the energy density (and signal strength) drops off with distance. It's NOT caused by air, magnetic fields, or absorption. Two factors affect FSPL:

items: DISTANCE — exponential function; loss is greater near the transmitter and tapers off at distance
FREQUENCY — higher frequency = greater FSPL at the same distance
Even at 1 meter, ~46 dB of free space path loss occurs at Wi-Fi frequencies

back.text: This is why 6 GHz has shorter range than 5 GHz, which has shorter range than 2.4 GHz — given equal transmit power. Antenna gain and receiver sensitivity can partially compensate but never fully eliminate the gap.

front.text: Given equal transmit power, which Wi-Fi band has the longest effective range, and why?

back.text: Higher bands offer more channels and less congestion but worse range. Trade-off: 6 GHz has cleaner air but smaller coverage cells — typically requires more APs for the same physical area.

front.text: What is RSSI, and what's the typical range of values you'd see in a Wi-Fi receiver?

back.text: RSSI (Received Signal Strength Indicator) = the receiver's measurement of incoming signal power. Defined as a relative 1-byte value (0–255) in the 802.11 standard, but virtually always reported in dBm in practice.
RSSI values are NOT standardized between vendors — Cisco's -65 dBm may differ from another vendor's -65 dBm. Use them as relative indicators within the same hardware.

front.text: What is SNR, and why is it more important than RSSI alone for received signal quality?

back.text: SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio) = the difference between the received signal strength and the noise floor, measured in dB. SNR matters more than RSSI because a strong signal in a noisy environment can be unintelligible — what matters is how much LOUDER the signal is compared to the surrounding noise.

SNR (dB) = RSSI (dBm) - Noise Floor (dBm)

Example:
RSSI = -54 dBm
Noise floor = -90 dBm
SNR = -54 - (-90) = 36 dB (excellent)

front.text: Which combination represents the BEST received signal quality: high SNR + high RSSI, low SNR + high RSSI, high SNR + low RSSI, or low SNR + low RSSI?

items: High SNR + high RSSI = BEST — strong signal AND quiet environment
High SNR + low RSSI = OK — quiet environment makes a weak signal usable
Low SNR + high RSSI = POOR — strong signal but noisy environment makes it unusable
Low SNR + low RSSI = WORST — weak signal lost in noise

back.text: Counterintuitive but important: a strong signal alone isn't enough. If the noise floor rises with the signal, performance degrades. SNR is what determines whether a signal can be demodulated reliably.

front.text: What is receiver sensitivity, and why does it differ across modulation rates?

back.text: Receiver sensitivity = the minimum received signal strength (in dBm) at which the receiver can still demodulate the signal correctly. Below this level, errors and retransmissions dominate. Sensitivity differs across modulation rates because:

items: Higher data rates use more complex modulation (e.g., 256-QAM)
More complex modulations require higher SNR to be demodulated correctly
Higher rates therefore have LESS sensitive thresholds (e.g., -65 dBm)
Lower rates can tolerate weaker signals (e.g., -90 dBm) — that's why DRS shifts down at the edge of coverage

front.text: What aspects of an RF carrier signal can be modulated to carry data, and what is QAM?

back.text: QAM (Quadrature Amplitude Modulation) is used in modern Wi-Fi (802.11n/ac/ax). Higher-order QAM (e.g., 256-QAM in 802.11ac, 1024-QAM in 802.11ax) packs more bits per symbol — but requires high SNR.

front.text: Compare DSSS and OFDM as physical-layer modulation schemes for Wi-Fi.

back.text: OFDM divides a channel into many narrow subcarriers (tones) and sends data in parallel. This makes it far more efficient and resilient than DSSS.

front.text: Match the major 802.11 amendments to their Wi-Fi generation, bands, and max data rates.
What does the notation '3×3:2 MIMO' mean?

items: 3 = number of TRANSMITTERS
3 = number of RECEIVERS
2 = number of unique SPATIAL STREAMS supported

back.text:
Format: TX × RX : Spatial Streams

Examples:
1×1 SISO       — one TX, one RX, single radio chain
2×2:2 MIMO    — two TX, two RX, two spatial streams
3×3:2 MIMO    — three TX, three RX, two spatial streams (extra radios for diversity/MRC)
4×4:4 MIMO    — four TX, four RX, four spatial streams (max throughput)
Spatial streams = independent data streams multiplexed over the radios. More streams = more throughput, but both ends must support the same number.

front.text: What is spatial multiplexing, and how does it allow multiple data streams to share the same channel without interfering?

back.text: Spatial multiplexing distributes data across multiple radio chains operating on the SAME channel — increasing throughput without using more bandwidth. The key is that the multiple signals take slightly different paths through the environment (different reflections, distances), arriving at the receiver's antennas with different phase and amplitude characteristics. Digital signal processing at the receiver can separate these streams based on their unique spatial signatures.
More spatial streams = proportionally more throughput. 802.11ax supports up to 8 spatial streams (~9.6 Gbps theoretical aggregate). Requires multiple antennas at both ends.

front.text: What is transmit beamforming (TBF), and how does it improve the signal at a specific receiver?

back.text: TBF uses multiple transmit antennas to send the SAME signal with deliberately adjusted phase shifts. The phases are chosen so that all signal copies arrive AT A SPECIFIC RECEIVER constructively in phase — boosting that receiver's SNR. Other receivers in different locations get the signals out of phase, so they don't see the same boost. TBF requires explicit feedback from the targeted receiver so the transmitter can compute the right phase adjustments. Cisco APs maintain a TBF table per client.
TBF is essentially 'aimed' Wi-Fi — directing signal energy toward a specific endpoint to improve link quality without wasting power broadcasting omnidirectionally.

front.text: What is maximal-ratio combining (MRC), and how does it improve receive performance?

back.text: MRC uses multiple receive antennas to capture multiple copies of the same transmitted signal. Each copy may have different SNR due to fading, multipath, or noise. The receiver intelligently COMBINES the copies — weighting each by its SNR — to reconstruct an output signal that's better than any single copy. The result: improved effective SNR and receiver sensitivity.
This is why MIMO devices with extra receive radios (e.g., 3×3:2 — three RX, two streams) outperform SISO. The 'extra' antennas don't carry their own data but boost reception quality through MRC.

front.text: What is dynamic rate shifting (DRS), and how does it respond to changing RF conditions?

back.text: DRS is the automatic process by which Wi-Fi devices adjust their modulation and coding scheme (and therefore their data rate) based on current RF conditions like RSSI and SNR. As a client moves AWAY from the AP, signal degrades — DRS shifts to a SIMPLER, more robust modulation scheme with a LOWER data rate that can survive lower SNR. As the client moves CLOSER, conditions improve — DRS shifts to MORE COMPLEX modulation with HIGHER data rates.

items: Better SNR = more complex modulation = higher rate (e.g., 256-QAM, 54 Mbps+)
Worse SNR = simpler modulation = lower rate (e.g., BPSK, 1 Mbps)
Happens in milliseconds, completely transparent to the user
Not standardized — each vendor implements its own DRS algorithm

front.text: What is a carrier signal, and how does it relate to data transmission?

back.text: A carrier signal is a basic, predictable RF wave (constant frequency, amplitude, and phase) that 'carries' data on top of itself. Without modulation, the carrier carries no information — it just oscillates. Modulation alters one or more characteristics of the carrier (amplitude, frequency, phase) in a controlled way, encoding data into those changes. The receiver demodulates by detecting those changes and decoding them back to data.

The carrier provides a predictable reference frequency that the receiver can lock onto. Even after modulation, the average frequency stays at the carrier's frequency — that's how the receiver knows where to tune.

front.text: What's the difference between Wi-Fi 6 and Wi-Fi 6E?

rows: Up to 160 MHz (more 80/160 MHz channels available)

back.text: Wi-Fi 6E unlocks new spectrum that's free of legacy 802.11a/b/g/n/ac devices — meaning cleaner air, more non-overlapping channels, and far less congestion. Same 802.11ax technology underneath.

front.text: Wireless signals & modulation quick-recall checklist for the ENCOR exam.

items: Frequency = cycles per second (Hz). Wi-Fi: 2.4, 5, 6 GHz bands
Wavelength shrinks as frequency rises; higher freq = shorter range due to FSPL
Channels: 2.4 GHz overlapping (only 1, 6, 11 are non-overlapping); 5/6 GHz non-overlapping
dB Laws: 0 dB = equal; +3 dB = 2×; +10 dB = 10×
dBm = absolute power referenced to 1 mW; 0 dBm = 1 mW
EIRP = TX power - cable loss + antenna gain (in dBi)
dBi (vs isotropic) is standard; dBd (vs dipole) → add 2.14 to convert to dBi
FSPL is caused by SPREADING; affected by distance and frequency only
Higher frequency = greater FSPL = shorter range (2.4 > 5 > 6 GHz range)
RSSI in dBm; SNR = RSSI - noise floor; HIGH SNR + HIGH RSSI = best
Modulation alters: amplitude, frequency, OR phase (or both via QAM)
QAM = quadrature amplitude modulation = both amplitude AND phase
DSSS (802.11b) = single carrier; OFDM (a/g/n/ac/ax) = multiple subcarriers in parallel
MIMO format: TX × RX : Spatial Streams (e.g., 3×3:2)
Spatial multiplexing = parallel data streams over multiple radio chains
TBF = phase-aligned signals at a specific receiver for stronger SNR
MRC = combining multiple receive copies for better effective SNR
DRS = automatic data rate adjustment based on RSSI/SNR; better SNR = higher rate
Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax) supports 2.4/5 GHz; Wi-Fi 6E adds 6 GHz (greenfield spectrum)

front.text: What's the fundamental physical difference between sending data over a wire vs sending it over the air?

rows: No — can hold a steady level
Radiates in all directions from antenna

back.text: Key insight: a wireless signal MUST keep cycling — a steady voltage cannot propagate as an EM wave. The textbook 'rope' analogy: lifting one end and holding still doesn't reach the far end, but waving rhythmically does.

front.text: What is an idealistic (isotropic) antenna, and why is it used as a reference even though it doesn't physically exist?

back.text: An idealistic antenna is a theoretical point that radiates RF energy equally in all directions in three-dimensional space — producing a perfect sphere of waves. It cannot actually be built (a real antenna has size, shape, and physical structure that all bias the radiation pattern). However, it serves as a universal mathematical reference point. Real antenna gain is measured RELATIVE to this idealistic baseline using dBi (dB-isotropic).

All real antennas have shape and direction — they focus energy in some directions more than others. Comparing them all to the same theoretical isotropic antenna lets us standardize antenna gain specifications across vendors and antenna types.

front.text: Why are frequencies grouped into 'bands' rather than referred to individually?

back.text: A 'band' is just a convenient name for a contiguous range of frequencies used for the same purpose. It's far easier to say 'the 2.4 GHz band' than '2400.0 MHz to 2483.5 MHz.' Bands also reflect regulatory boundaries — different bands have different rules for licensing, transmit power limits, and allowed uses. Examples include the AM band, FM band, ISM band (industrial/scientific/medical), and U-NII bands (Unlicensed National Information Infrastructure).

front.text: What do ISM and U-NII stand for, and how are they relevant to Wi-Fi?

back.text: Both designate UNLICENSED spectrum — meaning anyone can use them without applying for a license, but must follow the regulatory rules (e.g., transmit power limits, channel restrictions). ISM was the original unlicensed range and includes microwaves, cordless phones, baby monitors, etc., which is why 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi competes with so much interference. U-NII bands have stricter rules and less competing equipment, making 5/6 GHz cleaner spectrum.

front.text: What is signal bandwidth, and how does it differ from channel width?

rows: The frequency spacing between channel center points (defined by standard)
The actual width of the modulated signal — how much frequency space it occupies

back.text: These two values can mismatch. In 2.4 GHz, channels are spaced 5 MHz apart but Wi-Fi signals are 22 MHz wide — so adjacent channels overlap heavily. In 5/6 GHz, channels are spaced 20 MHz apart matching the typical signal bandwidth — so adjacent channels don't overlap.
Spectral mask: the regulatory boundaries that define how much signal energy can spill outside the assigned bandwidth. Devices ignore signal energy outside the mask boundaries when receiving.

front.text: What constitutes one complete cycle of an RF signal?

back.text: One cycle = the signal completes a full pattern from a starting point and returns to that same point, ready to repeat. You can measure a cycle from any reference point along the wave:

items: From zero rising → through positive peak → through zero falling → through negative peak → back to zero rising
From peak to peak (consecutive maximums)
From trough to trough (consecutive minimums)
From any specific phase angle to the next occurrence of that same angle

back.text: The number of complete cycles in 1 second = the signal's frequency in Hertz.

front.text: Why are logarithms used to express RF power, and what does log₁₀ specifically calculate?

back.text: RF power values can span many orders of magnitude (e.g., 0.00001 mW to 100,000 mW = 10 billion to 1 ratio). Linear math (subtraction) becomes meaningless when comparing such different scales. Logarithms COMPRESS exponential ranges into linear ones.

log₁₀(x) asks: 'How many times must I multiply 10 by itself to get x?' Examples: log₁₀(10) = 1; log₁₀(100) = 2; log₁₀(1000) = 3; log₁₀(10000) = 4. Each multiplication by 10 in the input adds 1 to the logarithm output.
This is why power changes by a factor of 10 always = ±10 dB, regardless of starting value: log₁₀(10) = 1, then × 10 = 10 dB.

front.text: Why is dB more useful than simple subtraction (mW – mW) when comparing RF power levels?

back.text: Subtraction tells you the ABSOLUTE difference, but doesn't reflect the proportional relationship. Examples:

All three scenarios have B = 10× A. Subtraction gives different mW differences but dB always shows +10 dB. dB captures the proportional relationship — what really matters in RF where signals are amplified, attenuated, and combined.

front.text: Why does cable length matter for RF signals, and how do vendors specify cable loss?

back.text: Coaxial cables and waveguides between transmitters and antennas always introduce some signal LOSS due to the cable's electrical characteristics. The longer the cable, the more loss. Vendors publish cable specifications as 'loss per foot' or 'loss per meter' at specific frequencies (loss is also frequency-dependent — higher frequencies experience more loss per unit length).
Example: LMR-400 cable @ 2.4 GHz = ~6.7 dB loss per 100 ft. So 25 ft of LMR-400 = ~1.7 dB loss.
When calculating EIRP, always factor in cable loss between the transmitter and antenna. Long runs can significantly reduce radiated power.

front.text: What is a link budget, and what components does it include?

back.text: A link budget tracks all gains and losses along the entire RF signal path from transmitter to receiver, calculating the expected received signal level. It includes:

items: + Transmitter power (dBm)
- Transmit cable loss (dB)
+ Transmit antenna gain (dBi)
- Free space path loss (dB)
+ Receive antenna gain (dBi)
- Receive cable loss (dB)
= Received signal strength (dBm)

back.text:
Example:
TX power: +20 dBm
TX cable: -2 dB → +18 dBm
TX antenna: +4 dBi → +22 dBm (EIRP)
FSPL: -69 dB → -47 dBm
RX antenna: +4 dBi → -43 dBm
RX cable: -2 dB → -45 dBm (received)

front.text: If a receiver's sensitivity is -82 dBm and the calculated received signal is -78 dBm, will the link work? What if it's -85 dBm?

rows: ABOVE -82 dBm threshold → link works (4 dB margin)
BELOW -82 dBm threshold → link likely fails or runs at lower data rate

back.text: Receiver sensitivity is the minimum signal strength needed to demodulate a signal at a given data rate. Higher data rates require BETTER (less negative) sensitivity values. As signal weakens, DRS automatically drops to a lower data rate with a more lenient sensitivity threshold.
Always design with margin (e.g., 6–10 dB) above sensitivity to handle fading, interference, and movement. A signal exactly at sensitivity is a weak signal at the edge of usability.

front.text: What is the noise floor in a wireless environment, and what raises it?

back.text: The noise floor is the average background RF energy at a given frequency — everything that's NOT the desired signal. Some noise is unavoidable thermal noise from the receiver hardware (typically around -100 dBm). Noise rises in real environments due to:

items: Other Wi-Fi traffic on the same/adjacent channels
Non-Wi-Fi sources: microwaves, Bluetooth, cordless phones, baby monitors (especially 2.4 GHz)
Industrial equipment, motors, wireless cameras
Other APs in the same band (cell overlap)

back.text: The higher the noise floor, the harder it is to demodulate weak signals — even if the signal is strong, low SNR can make it useless. Always survey RF environment before AP deployment.

front.text: What are common minimum RSSI targets for different Wi-Fi applications?

back.text: Cisco recommends -67 dBm as the minimum for high-quality voice/video. Below this, jitter, packet loss, and dropped calls become likely. Always design with multi-AP coverage to ensure each location has at least 2–3 APs above target RSSI.

front.text: What are the three main goals of any RF modulation scheme?

items: Carry data at a predefined rate (more bits = more capacity)
Be reasonably immune to interference and noise (work in real environments)
Be practical to transmit and receive (achievable with available hardware)

back.text: These three goals often trade off against each other. More complex modulation = higher data rate but lower noise immunity. Wi-Fi standards must balance all three with different schemes available for different conditions.

front.text: What is spread spectrum, and why is it required for high-data-rate Wi-Fi?

back.text: Spread spectrum = transmitting a signal over a wider range of frequencies than the minimum bandwidth required for the data alone. This provides:

items: Resilience to narrowband interference (interference affects only part of the signal)
Resistance to multipath fading (different frequencies fade differently)
Improved security (signal looks like noise to unintended receivers)
Higher aggregate data rates (more bandwidth = more data carrying capacity)

back.text: All modern Wi-Fi uses spread spectrum: DSSS (legacy 802.11b — direct sequence) or OFDM (802.11a/g/n/ac/ax — multiple parallel subcarriers). Without spread spectrum, Wi-Fi couldn't reach gigabit speeds in noisy real-world environments.

front.text: How does OFDM divide a 20 MHz channel, and why is this efficient?

back.text: OFDM splits a single 20 MHz Wi-Fi channel into many narrow subcarriers (also called subchannels or tones), each carrying a small piece of the data in PARALLEL.

More subcarriers = finer granularity. 802.11ax's narrower subcarriers allow OFDMA, where multiple clients can be assigned different subcarriers within the SAME channel simultaneously — improving spectral efficiency for many small flows.

front.text: What is OFDMA in 802.11ax, and how does it differ from OFDM in earlier standards?

back.text: OFDMA (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiple Access) extends OFDM by allowing the channel to be divided into 'resource units' (RUs), each of which can be assigned to a DIFFERENT client simultaneously. Earlier OFDM-only Wi-Fi could only serve ONE client at a time per channel.
OFDMA dramatically improves Wi-Fi efficiency in dense environments (offices, stadiums, classrooms) where many clients have small/intermittent traffic.

front.text: How does 802.11 maintain backwards compatibility across amendments?

back.text: Each new 802.11 amendment generally supports the modulation and data rates of previous standards in the SAME band. So an 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6) AP can serve 802.11a/n/ac clients in 5 GHz and 802.11b/g/n clients in 2.4 GHz.

items: AP must include support for older modulation/data rates
Older clients can connect at their highest supported rate
Newer clients can use the latest (faster) rates
Coexistence has performance cost — older clients tie up the channel longer

back.text: Best practice: disable legacy DSSS rates (1, 2, 5.5 Mbps) on modern APs to force clients to use OFDM at higher rates and reclaim air time.

front.text: An 802.11ac AP is configured for 5 GHz only. An 802.11n client tries to connect. Will it work, and at what rate?

back.text: Yes, it will work. Both 802.11n and 802.11ac use OFDM in the 5 GHz band, and 802.11ac is backward-compatible with 802.11n. The connection will be established at 802.11n rates (up to 150 Mbps per spatial stream, depending on channel width and SNR), since the client cannot use 802.11ac-specific modulation (256-QAM) or wider channels (80/160 MHz).
The AP and client always negotiate the BEST modulation and data rate that BOTH support — limited by whichever side is older or has lower capabilities.

front.text: How does channel width affect Wi-Fi speed and channel availability?

rows: Very few channels — usually only in 5/6 GHz with DFS

back.text: Wider channels carry more data but reduce the number of non-overlapping channels available — risking AP-to-AP interference in dense deployments. In 2.4 GHz, 40 MHz is rarely viable due to limited channel space. In 6 GHz Wi-Fi 6E, 160 MHz becomes practical thanks to abundant new spectrum.

front.text: An AP supports 4 spatial streams, but a client supports only 2. What rate will they use?

back.text: They negotiate using the LOWER number — 2 spatial streams. The AP cannot 'force' the client to receive 4 streams. The two extra TX radios on the AP can still be useful for transmit beamforming (TBF) and adding redundancy/diversity, but data throughput is limited to 2-stream capacity from this client's perspective.

Most mobile devices (phones, laptops) use only 1–2 spatial streams to save battery and cost. APs typically support 4–8 to serve multiple clients efficiently. The mismatch is normal — you don't need matching counts on both ends.

front.text: What is MU-MIMO, and how does it differ from SU-MIMO (single-user MIMO)?

rows: AP sends multiple spatial streams to ONE client at a time
AP sends streams to MULTIPLE clients SIMULTANEOUSLY using different spatial streams

back.text: MU-MIMO uses TBF and spatial multiplexing to direct different streams to different clients in different physical locations at the same time. A 4×4 AP can serve up to 4 clients simultaneously (each receiving 1 stream), or 2 clients with 2 streams each, etc. 802.11ax extended this to UPLINK MU-MIMO too — multiple clients can transmit to the AP simultaneously.
Major efficiency gain in dense environments — AP no longer has to wait for one client to finish before serving the next.

front.text: Why is 'just turn up the AP transmit power' often a bad idea to extend Wi-Fi range?

items: AP heard farther, but client TX power doesn't increase — clients still struggle to reach AP from far away (asymmetric link)
Higher AP power increases CO-CHANNEL INTERFERENCE between APs
Reduces channel reuse capacity in dense deployments
Clients hear MORE APs at once — can cause sticky-client problems and roaming delays
Regulatory power limits prevent unlimited increase
Cells overlap excessively, hurting overall capacity

back.text: Best practice: balance AP TX power to MATCH typical client TX power (~10–15 dBm). This ensures symmetric reception and proper cell sizing. Add MORE APs for coverage, not more power per AP.

front.text: As a wireless client walks away from an AP, what changes occur to its data rate, and why?

items: Close to AP: high RSSI, high SNR → uses complex modulation (e.g., 256-QAM), highest data rate
Mid-distance: RSSI drops, SNR drops → DRS shifts to less complex modulation (e.g., 64-QAM, 16-QAM), medium data rate
Far: RSSI low, SNR low → DRS uses simplest modulation (e.g., BPSK), lowest data rate but highest noise immunity
Edge of coverage: signal too weak even for lowest rate → connection drops

back.text: DRS is automatic and continuous — happens in milliseconds without user awareness. The trade-off: trade speed for reliability as conditions worsen. Each modulation has its own SNR requirement.

front.text: Order common Wi-Fi modulation schemes from LEAST complex (most robust) to MOST complex (highest throughput).

back.text: Each step up doubles the bits per symbol but requires significantly higher SNR. 1024-QAM typically only works within a few meters of the AP.

front.text: What is multipath, and how does MIMO turn this from a problem into an advantage?

back.text: Multipath = a signal traveling from transmitter to receiver via MULTIPLE paths due to reflections off walls, ceilings, floors, and objects. Each copy arrives slightly delayed and out of phase with the others. In SISO systems, this causes destructive interference and signal degradation (multipath fading).

rows: Multipath = advantage (independent paths enable spatial multiplexing)

back.text: MIMO requires multipath to work — without slightly different paths to each antenna, the receiver cannot separate the spatial streams. In open environments without reflective surfaces, MIMO performance actually degrades.

front.text: Practical test: at the same EIRP, the test receiver reaches -67 dBm at 140 ft on 2.4 GHz, 80 ft on 5 GHz. Approximately what range would it reach on 6 GHz?

back.text: Approximately 50 ft on 6 GHz. The pattern: as frequency increases, FSPL increases proportionally, reducing range. Ratio: 2.4 GHz → 5 GHz cuts range roughly in half (140→80 ft). 5 GHz → 6 GHz cuts again (80→50 ft).
This is why deploying Wi-Fi 6E (6 GHz) typically requires more APs for the same coverage area, but each cell is less likely to interfere with others — better trade-off in dense indoor environments.

front.text: What's a typical Cisco-recommended cell overlap percentage between adjacent APs, and why?

back.text: Cisco recommends 15–20% cell overlap between neighboring APs at the design RSSI threshold (e.g., -67 dBm for voice). Reasons:

items: Ensures clients can roam smoothly without losing connection
Provides redundancy if one AP fails
Allows clients to find a stronger AP before signal degrades too much
Too LITTLE overlap = roaming gaps, voice drops
Too MUCH overlap = co-channel interference, reduced capacity

back.text: For voice/video deployments, plan tighter overlap (~20%) and lower RSSI threshold. For data-only with looser performance requirements, less overlap is acceptable.

front.text: What is BSS coloring in 802.11ax, and what problem does it solve?

back.text: BSS coloring assigns a numeric 'color' (1–63) to each Basic Service Set (BSS) — a single AP and its associated clients. When a Wi-Fi 6 device hears a frame from a different-colored BSS, it can recognize it as a 'foreign' signal and react more intelligently — for example, ignoring it for clear channel assessment (CCA) at higher signal-strength thresholds and transmitting earlier.

items: Reduces unnecessary deferral when nearby APs are operating
Increases effective channel reuse and aggregate throughput in dense deployments
Particularly valuable in multi-AP environments (offices, stadiums, classrooms)

back.text: Before BSS coloring, an AP would defer transmission whenever it heard ANY signal, even a weak one from a distant AP. With BSS coloring, the AP can determine 'that's a different cell — I can ignore it' and transmit anyway.

front.text: Final wireless signals & modulation master recap — extra facts to lock in.

items: An RF signal MUST keep cycling — a steady voltage cannot propagate as an EM wave
Idealistic (isotropic) antenna = theoretical reference; can't be built, used for dBi calculations
ISM band = 2.4 GHz unlicensed; U-NII = 5/6 GHz unlicensed
Channel width vs signal bandwidth — these can mismatch (causes 2.4 GHz overlap)
Logarithm compresses huge ratios into linear values (key to dB usefulness)
Cable loss is frequency-dependent — higher freq = more loss per foot
Link budget tracks all gains and losses end-to-end
Voice/video Wi-Fi target: -67 dBm RSSI, 25 dB SNR
Noise floor rises with Wi-Fi traffic, microwaves, Bluetooth — RF surveys matter
Modulation goals: data rate + noise immunity + practical hardware
Spread spectrum required for high-throughput — DSSS (legacy) or OFDM (modern)
OFDMA (802.11ax) lets multiple clients share a channel via Resource Units
Each new 802.11 amendment is backward compatible in the same band
Wider channels = higher speed but fewer non-overlapping channels available
MU-MIMO = AP serves multiple clients simultaneously (vs SU-MIMO, one at a time)
Don't overcrank AP TX power — match client TX power for symmetric links
DRS automatically shifts modulation as RSSI/SNR change
Modulation hierarchy: BPSK → QPSK → 16-QAM → 64-QAM → 256-QAM → 1024-QAM
MIMO turns multipath fading into a feature (spatial multiplexing)
Frequency increases → range decreases (2.4 > 5 > 6 GHz at same EIRP)
Cisco recommends 15–20% AP cell overlap for voice/video
BSS coloring (Wi-Fi 6) reduces unnecessary deferral between cells

front.text: What is an autonomous AP, and what are the operational challenges in deploying many of them?

back.text: An autonomous AP is a self-contained, standalone wireless device — each one runs its own configuration, offers complete BSSs independently, and connects directly to a wired access switch via trunk links carrying both data VLANs and a management VLAN.

items: Each AP must be individually configured (SSIDs, VLANs, RF settings)
Data VLANs must be trunked from distribution layer down to every AP
Adding a new VLAN requires touching every switch and AP
STP must run to prevent loops with redundant uplinks
Roaming is limited to Layer 2 only (single VLAN broadcast domain)
Each AP needs a unique management IP address

back.text: Pro: data path between two clients on the same AP is short — traffic stays local. Con: doesn't scale well in large enterprises.

front.text: What is the split-MAC architecture between a Cisco lightweight AP and a WLC?

rows: Authentication, association management, mobility, QoS policy, RRM, security policy

back.text: The lightweight AP handles low-latency 802.11 MAC functions that must happen instantly, while the WLC handles higher-level management functions that can tolerate the round-trip latency to the controller. This split is what makes the architecture scalable and centrally manageable. APs and WLCs communicate via CAPWAP tunnels.

front.text: What are the two CAPWAP tunnels between a lightweight AP and a WLC, and what UDP ports do they use?

rows: AP-WLC management messages, configuration, AP join, encryption keys (always DTLS encrypted)
Wireless client data traffic encapsulated for transport (DTLS encryption optional)

back.text: RFC 5415 defines CAPWAP. The control tunnel is ALWAYS encrypted with DTLS; the data tunnel can optionally be encrypted but is often left unencrypted in trusted internal networks for performance.

front.text: Compare the major Cisco AP operating modes and their use cases.

rows: Provides BSSs and serves clients; scans other channels in idle time
Remote site AP — control via WLC, data switched LOCALLY at the AP
No transmissions — dedicated sensor for IDS, rogue detection, location services
Captures 802.11 packets and forwards to a remote analyzer (Wireshark)
Correlates wired-side MACs with airborne MACs to find rogue APs
Point-to-point or mesh links between locations
FlexConnect operation on a mesh AP
Spectrum analysis mode — feeds data to MetaGeek/Cisco Spectrum Expert

back.text: Local mode = serving clients. All other modes typically disable client BSSs to dedicate the AP to a specialized function.

front.text: What characterizes a centralized wireless LAN deployment, and what is the typical scale per WLC?

back.text: In a centralized deployment, one or more WLCs are placed near the network core or in a data center. All APs across the enterprise establish CAPWAP tunnels to the controller(s). Wireless data traffic is encapsulated all the way up to the WLC where it's decapsulated and routed normally.

rows: Centralized — data center or near network core
Only need to exist near the WLC, not at every AP
Easy — all APs join one WLC, so roaming is seamless

front.text: In a centralized deployment, what path does traffic take when one wireless client communicates with another wireless client on the SAME AP?

back.text: Traffic does NOT stay locally between the two clients. Instead, it traverses: Client A → AP (encapsulated in CAPWAP) → up through the network → WLC (decapsulated and inspected) → back down through the network → AP → Client B. The traffic essentially goes 'up to the controller and back' even for clients on the same AP.

This is by design — centralized policy enforcement requires traffic to reach the WLC. Trade-off: increased latency for client-to-client local traffic. Round-trip time (RTT) between AP and WLC must be < 100 ms for local mode to work properly.

front.text: Compare public cloud and private cloud WLC deployments.

rows: FlexConnect (data switched locally — high latency to WLC prevents Local)
Enterprise data center hosted virtual WLC

back.text: Both can scale to ~6,000 APs and 64,000 clients, similar to centralized hardware WLCs.

front.text: Compare distributed and controller-less (EWC) wireless deployments.

rows: Smaller standalone WLCs at branch sites
WLC software runs ON one of the APs
Up to 250 APs, 5,000 clients per WLC
Small/midsize/multi-site branch with no dedicated WLC
Lower than centralized but still hardware investment
No dedicated WLC hardware needed — uses an AP

back.text: EWC = Embedded Wireless Controller — perfect for small offices or branches where investing in a dedicated WLC is not justified.

front.text: List the AP boot-to-RUN state machine sequence.

items: AP Boots — small IOS image loads, gets IP via DHCP or static
WLC Discovery — uses multiple methods to find WLCs
CAPWAP Tunnel — DTLS-encrypted control tunnel built; mutual cert authentication
WLC Join — sends Join Request, receives Join Response
Download Image — if AP and WLC software versions don't match, AP downloads matching image and reboots
Download Config — pulls all config (RF, SSIDs, security, QoS) from WLC
RUN State — AP is now serving wireless clients
(Reset) — if reset by WLC, tear down tunnels, reboot, return to step 1

front.text: What four (5-step) methods does an AP use to discover available WLCs, in order?

items: Subnet broadcast — AP sends CAPWAP Discovery Request on its local wired subnet (UDP 5246). Routers can relay broadcasts using 'ip helper-address' to remote WLCs.
Primed entries / previous WLC list — AP remembers up to 3 manually configured WLCs (primary/secondary/tertiary), plus up to 8 from a list of 32 received from a previously joined controller.
DHCP option 43 — DHCP server can include WLC management IP addresses in option 43.
DNS lookup — AP attempts to resolve 'CISCO-CAPWAP-CONTROLLER.localdomain' (where localdomain is from DHCP).
If all fail — AP resets and starts the discovery process over.

front.text: After an AP discovers a list of candidate WLCs, how does it select which one to join?

items: If AP is primed with primary/secondary/tertiary WLCs, it tries those in order first
If no primed WLCs are configured but a 'master controller' has been designated, AP joins that one
Otherwise, AP joins the LEAST-LOADED WLC (lowest ratio of currently joined APs to total AP capacity)

back.text: If a WLC is at maximum AP capacity, it rejects new joins. APs can be assigned a priority (low/medium/high/critical); higher-priority APs can displace lower-priority ones if a WLC is at capacity.

front.text: If a lightweight AP needs a new software image, where does it get it from, and what determines the image version?

back.text: An AP downloads its image directly from the WLC it has joined. The WLC's own software version determines what version the AP runs — the AP is forced to match the WLC. APs cannot be independently upgraded.

items: Common scenarios where AP downloads new image: first join, WLC upgrade, AP rehoming to a different WLC running different software

Pre-download feature: while WLC still runs old image, you can pre-stage the new image to all APs. After WLC reboots on new image, APs already have the image staged and reboot quickly without re-downloading.
Without pre-download, hundreds of APs simultaneously downloading from a single WLC = significant downtime

front.text: What happens when a WLC supporting hundreds of APs fails, and how can SSO HA improve the failure scenario?

rows: All APs detect controller failure (~35s default)
Active WLC fails → standby takes over instantly (sub-second)
All APs scramble to discover new WLCs
APs continue tunneling to the same VIP (now hosted by ex-standby)
All APs must rejoin and re-download config
AP and client states already synchronized — transparent failover

back.text: HA SSO pairs two WLCs as a redundant pair. Both share state; the standby is hot-ready with current AP and client data. Best practice for mission-critical deployments.

front.text: What are the default AP-to-WLC keepalive (heartbeat) intervals, and how fast can a failure be detected?

back.text: Keepalive interval determines how often APs probe WLC liveness. After a missed regular keepalive, the AP escalates to fast heartbeats. If those also fail, the AP marks the WLC as down and starts seeking another one.

front.text: What are the three categories of AP configuration parameters in IOS XE WLCs?

rows: AP-WLC CAPWAP control, FlexConnect operation, VLAN bindings — per geographic site
Radio settings per band (2.4, 5, 6 GHz): data rates, MCS, transmit power, channel, coverage hole detection
SSIDs, security profiles, QoS, traffic policies

back.text: These three categories are configured separately and independently in IOS XE WLCs using profiles + tags — a much more granular approach than the older AireOS AP groups.

front.text: How do profiles and tags work together to configure APs in an IOS XE WLC?

back.text: Profiles define the actual configuration parameters; tags are labels applied to APs that map them to those profiles. Each AP gets exactly THREE tags.

rows: SSIDs to broadcast and traffic policies

back.text: Object-oriented model — change a profile once, and all APs/groups assigned the relevant tag automatically inherit the new config. Far easier than touching AP groups individually.

front.text: What three default tags are automatically assigned to every AP, and why should you create your own?

back.text: Editing the default profiles affects ALL APs globally. Best practice: create your own custom site, RF, and policy tags + profiles, and assign them granularly. This gives you flexibility to vary configuration by location, AP type, or use case.

front.text: What's the typical workflow to customize AP configuration with profiles and tags?

items: Configure AP profiles and Flex profiles, then map them to Site Tags
Configure RF profiles per band (2.4, 5, 6 GHz), then map them to RF Tags
Configure WLAN profiles (SSIDs, security) and Policy profiles (QoS, traffic policy), then map them to Policy Tags
Assign Site, RF, and Policy tags to specific APs (manually, by CSV, by location, or regex)

back.text: Once profiles + tags are set up, future changes are dramatically easier — modify a profile and all APs sharing the tag inherit the change automatically. No need to touch each AP.

front.text: What are the E plane and H plane in an antenna radiation pattern plot, and what does each show?

rows: Top-down view of radiation pattern (XY plane)
Side view of radiation pattern (XZ plane)

back.text: Polar plots represent each plane as concentric circles divided into 360° sectors. The outer ring = strongest signal; inner rings = weaker. Together, the H plane and E plane plots help you mentally reconstruct the 3D radiation pattern of an antenna.

front.text: How is antenna gain achieved if antennas are passive devices?

back.text: Antennas don't AMPLIFY signals — they have no electronic amplifier circuitry. Instead, they FOCUS RF energy in a specific direction, so the energy density (and apparent signal strength) increases in that direction at the expense of other directions. It's like the difference between a flashlight (focused beam = high gain) vs a bare lightbulb (omnidirectional = low gain). Both emit the same total energy, but the flashlight's focus makes it 'stronger' where you're aiming it.

front.text: What is antenna beamwidth, and how is it measured?

back.text: Beamwidth is the angle between the two points where signal strength drops by 3 dB from the peak (i.e., to half the peak power). It's measured separately in the H plane and E plane and listed in degrees.

items: Find the peak/strongest point on the radiation pattern (typically on the outer ring)
Trace the pattern in either direction until it falls -3 dB from the peak
Draw lines from the antenna's center to those two -3 dB points
Measure the angle between the lines = beamwidth

back.text: Smaller beamwidth = more focused antenna = higher gain. Larger beamwidth = wider coverage = lower gain. Trade-off.

front.text: What is antenna polarization, and what happens when transmitter and receiver polarizations don't match?

back.text: Polarization = the orientation of the electrical field wave relative to the horizon. Most Cisco antennas are vertically polarized — the E field oscillates vertically as the wave travels. The magnetic field is always perpendicular to the E field.
If someone knocks an AP antenna sideways from vertical to horizontal, the radiation pattern changes AND the polarization no longer matches client devices — leading to mysterious connectivity issues.

front.text: What is the radiation pattern shape of an omnidirectional antenna, and what's a typical gain?

back.text: Omnidirectional antennas (e.g., dipoles, integrated AP antennas) produce a DONUT-shaped radiation pattern. The pattern radiates equally in all horizontal directions (broad H plane) but has reduced energy directly above and below the antenna (the 'donut hole'). This makes them ideal for ceiling-mounted APs covering an entire floor.
Lower gain than directional antennas because they spread energy widely instead of focusing. Best for covering a single open area from a central location.

front.text: What is a patch antenna, where is it typically used, and what's its gain?

back.text: Patch antennas have a flat, rectangular shape and are wall- or ceiling-mountable. They produce a broad EGG-shaped radiation pattern that extends out away from the flat face of the antenna.

items: Wall-mounted patch — covers area in front of the wall (great for hallway ends, corner rooms)
Ceiling-mounted patch (downward-facing) — covers a small focused floor area below; good for high client density
Gain: ~6-8 dBi @ 2.4 GHz, ~7-10 dBi @ 5 GHz

back.text: Useful for situations where omnidirectional coverage would 'leak' into adjacent rooms or floors. Patch antennas confine coverage to where you want it.

front.text: What is a Yagi antenna, what's its radiation pattern, and what's a typical use case?

back.text: A Yagi antenna (or Yagi-Uda) is a directional antenna shaped like a thick cylinder, internally containing multiple parallel elements of increasing length. It produces a focused egg-shaped pattern extending out along the antenna's length.

items: Gain: ~10-14 dBi (much higher than omnidirectional or patch)
Use cases: long hallways, warehouse aisles, outdoor between two buildings
More focused than patch — narrower beamwidth

front.text: What is a parabolic dish antenna used for, and what's its gain range?

back.text: A parabolic dish antenna is the most directional Wi-Fi antenna type, with a highly focused, narrow elliptical radiation pattern. Used for line-of-sight wireless bridges between two distant points (building-to-building or campus-to-campus links).

rows: Aimed precisely at the remote endpoint

back.text: Trade-off: extreme focus = extreme range BUT a slight bump in alignment can take the link offline. Always use rigid mounting and aim carefully.

front.text: Match each common Wi-Fi scenario to the most appropriate antenna type.

rows: Long hallway with offices on both sides
Wall-mounted AP covering one large room
High-density area (auditorium, classroom) requiring small cell
Coverage extending out from a building

front.text: What is client density, and how can directional antennas be used to manage it?

back.text: Client density = number of devices per AP. Higher density per AP = less airtime per client = poorer per-client performance. Good wireless design distributes clients across multiple APs to keep density manageable.

items: Use directional antennas (patch, downward-facing) to create SMALLER cells in dense areas
More APs each covering smaller areas = lower client density per AP = better per-client throughput
Trade-off: more APs needed = higher cost and more complex RF planning
Common in stadiums, classrooms, conference centers, dense office environments

back.text: An omnidirectional antenna in a dense area would have hundreds of clients all competing for the same AP airtime — performance suffers. Switching to directional antennas creates cell isolation and improves capacity.

front.text: Show how to configure a router to relay AP CAPWAP discovery broadcasts to remote WLCs.

back.text:
router(config)# ip forward-protocol udp 5246
router(config)# interface vlan 10
router(config-if)# ip helper-address 10.10.20.5     ! WLC1 management IP
