router(config-if)# ip helper-address 10.10.20.6     ! WLC2 management IP

Without 'ip helper-address', AP CAPWAP discovery broadcasts (UDP 5246) won't cross subnet boundaries. Combined with 'ip forward-protocol udp 5246', the router converts broadcast to unicast forwarding to specific WLC addresses. Useful when APs and WLCs are on different subnets.

front.text: Wireless infrastructure quick-recall checklist for the ENCOR exam.

items: Autonomous APs = standalone, hard to scale, Layer 2 roaming only
Lightweight APs + WLC = split-MAC architecture, scalable
CAPWAP control = UDP 5246 (always DTLS); CAPWAP data = UDP 5247 (DTLS optional)
AP modes: Local (default, serves clients), FlexConnect (remote, local switching), Monitor, Sniffer, Rogue Detector, Bridge, Flex+Bridge, SE-Connect
Centralized deployment: 1 WLC, up to 6,000 APs and 64,000 clients
Distributed deployment: smaller WLCs at branches (250 APs / 5,000 clients each)
Embedded Wireless Controller (EWC): WLC software runs on an AP itself (100 APs / 2,000 clients)
Public/private cloud WLC: APs use FlexConnect when RTT > 100 ms
AP state machine: Boot → Discover → CAPWAP tunnel → Join → Image download → Config download → RUN
Discovery methods: subnet broadcast → primed entries → DHCP option 43 → DNS lookup
WLC selection: primed (primary/secondary/tertiary) → master → least-loaded
AP image is determined by WLC version; pre-download to avoid lengthy outages
HA SSO pairs WLCs for sub-second failover; APs and clients don't notice failure
Default keepalive 30s + 4×3s fast = ~35s detection; can tune to ~5s
IOS XE WLC config: 3 tags per AP (Site, RF, Policy) → mapped to profiles
Each tag maps to specific profiles: Site=AP+Flex, RF=per band, Policy=WLAN+Policy
Always create custom tags + profiles, don't edit defaults globally
Antenna gain achieved by FOCUSING energy, not amplification (passive devices)
Beamwidth = angle between -3 dB points relative to peak signal
Polarization mismatch = severely degraded signal (TX/RX must match)
Antennas: Dipole (omnidirectional, low gain) → Patch (mid-gain, directional) → Yagi (high-gain, directional) → Parabolic dish (highest gain, narrow beam, point-to-point)
Higher client density = use directional antennas to shrink cell size and add APs

front.text: In a topology with redundant Layer 2 paths, why does traffic not loop when RPVST is enabled?

back.text: RPVST prevents loops by electing a loop-free logical topology over a physically redundant network. It places redundant ports into an alternate/discarding state rather than forwarding, ensuring only one active forwarding path exists toward the root bridge per VLAN.

items: Root bridge election creates a single reference point.
Each non-root switch selects exactly one Root Port (best path to root).
Each segment elects one Designated Port.
All other redundant ports are placed in Discarding state.

back.text: The physical redundancy remains; only the logical forwarding topology is loop-free.

front.text: Why is an RSTP Alternate Port 'ready but not forwarding' before a failure occurs?

back.text: The Alternate Port already knows of a valid backup path to the root bridge through BPDUs received from a neighbor. However, it remains in the Discarding state because forwarding simultaneously with the primary Root Port would create a Layer 2 loop.

items: Receives inferior BPDUs that confirm a path to the root.
Stores this path information in memory.
Stays in Discarding to prevent a loop.
Upon Root Port failure, transitions to Forwarding almost instantly without timers.

back.text: This precomputed knowledge is the key to RSTP's sub-second convergence.

front.text: Map the legacy 802.1D STP states to their 802.1w RSTP equivalents.

back.text: RSTP collapses Disabled, Blocking, and Listening into a single Discarding state — but Learning still exists.

front.text: Diagram the RSTP port state transition flow for an active port.

back.text:
Discarding
     ↓
  Learning
     ↓
 Forwarding
Compare to legacy 802.1D, which required four transitions:
Blocking → Listening → Learning → Forwarding
Fewer states + handshake-driven transitions = sub-second convergence.

front.text: Differentiate between an RSTP Alternate Port and a Backup Port.

back.text: Backup ports are rare in modern point-to-point switched networks; Alternate ports are the workhorses of RSTP convergence.

front.text: Exam Trap: True or False — RSTP achieves faster convergence by skipping the Learning state.

back.text: FALSE. RSTP still uses the Learning state to populate the MAC address table before forwarding traffic.
What RSTP actually changed:

items: Removed the Listening state (merged into Discarding).
Replaced timer-driven transitions with proposal/agreement handshakes.
Introduced Alternate and Backup port roles for precomputed failover.
Allowed Edge Ports (PortFast) to skip directly to Forwarding.

back.text: Skipping Learning would cause unicast flooding — RSTP avoids that.

front.text: How does the RSTP Proposal/Agreement handshake accelerate convergence on point-to-point links?

items: A switch sends a Proposal BPDU on a designated port that wishes to forward.
The receiving switch syncs by placing all non-edge designated ports into Discarding.
Once synced, it replies with an Agreement BPDU.
The original switch immediately transitions its port to Forwarding.
The process cascades downstream switch by switch.

back.text: This handshake replaces the 30-second Forward Delay (Listening + Learning) used in legacy STP, achieving convergence in under a second.

front.text: Why is deterministic root bridge placement treated as a separate design task from L2 redundancy?

back.text: Root placement dictates the entire Layer 2 forwarding topology and must align with Layer 3 gateway placement and traffic engineering goals. Selecting the root first influences:

items: Root Port selection on every downstream switch.
Which redundant links are blocked (Alternate state).
Optimal alignment with HSRP/VRRP active gateways.
Predictable, troubleshootable traffic flow patterns.
Efficient use of high-bandwidth distribution/core links.

back.text:
! Force a switch to be the root for a VLAN
spanning-tree vlan 10 root primary
spanning-tree vlan 10 root secondary  ! On backup distribution switch
Without explicit configuration, the oldest switch (lowest MAC) often wins — usually the worst choice.

front.text: What are the components of the Bridge ID used in STP root election?

back.text: Effective priority must be a multiple of 4096 due to the Extended System ID field.

spanning-tree vlan 10 priority 4096
! Effective Bridge ID priority = 4096 + VLAN ID (10) = 4106

front.text: Compare BPDU Guard, Root Guard, and Loop Guard.

rows: Any BPDU received on edge port
BPDUs stop arriving on non-designated port

back.text: BPDU Guard protects edges; Root Guard protects the root election; Loop Guard protects against software/hardware failures that silence BPDUs.

front.text: When a wireless client roams from one AP to another, what is it actually leaving and joining?

back.text: The client is leaving one Basic Service Set (BSS) and joining another. Each AP provides its own BSS, and roaming moves the client's association from one BSS to a different one.

front.text: Which device makes the decision to roam from one AP to another?

back.text: The client device makes the roaming decision. It continuously evaluates signal quality and, when the current AP's signal degrades, scans for a better candidate AP and reassociates. The infrastructure (APs/WLCs) does not force a roam.

front.text: What is the difference between an Association Request and a Reassociation Request frame?

rows: Used to form a brand-new association with an AP/BSS
Used when roaming to a new AP, preserving the client's prior association status

front.text: In an autonomous AP environment, how are leftover frames destined for a roamed client handled?

back.text: The original AP forwards any leftover frames to the new AP across the wired infrastructure, because the client's MAC address now appears on the new AP's switch port. Each autonomous AP maintains its own client association table independently.

front.text: Define intracontroller roaming.

back.text: Intracontroller roaming occurs when a client roams between two APs that are both joined to the same wireless LAN controller (WLC). The roam is handled entirely within that single controller.

front.text: Approximately how long does an intracontroller roam take, and why is it so fast?

back.text: An intracontroller roam typically completes in less than 10 ms. It is fast because the same controller already has the client's full context; it only needs to update its client association table to point to the new AP's CAPWAP tunnel.

front.text: Besides reassociation, which two processes can occur during a roam that may impact roam time?

items: DHCP — the client may renew its lease or request a new IP address
Client authentication — the controller may require an 802.1X exchange with a RADIUS server, including new key generation

back.text: Authentication is usually the most time-consuming step, which is why fast-roaming key caching mechanisms exist.

front.text: Compare CCKM, key caching, and 802.11r as fast-roaming techniques.

rows: One controller maintains a client/key database and shares keys with other controllers and APs during roams
Requires Cisco Compatible Extensions (CCX) on the client
Client caches keys from prior AP associations and presents them when roaming
Limited to 8 AP/key entries; destination AP must already be in the list
Client caches a portion of the auth server's key and presents it on roam; QoS is preserved

front.text: What does CCKM stand for, and what does it do?

back.text: CCKM = Cisco Centralized Key Management. One controller maintains a database of clients and keys on behalf of its APs and shares it with other controllers and APs as needed during roaming, eliminating the need for full reauthentication.

front.text: What is the maximum number of AP/key entries a client can keep in its key cache for fast roaming?

back.text: Eight (8). The destination AP must already be in this cache for key caching to accelerate the roam.

front.text: What is intercontroller roaming?

back.text: Intercontroller roaming occurs when a client roams between two APs that are joined to different wireless LAN controllers, requiring the controllers to coordinate the move.

front.text: When does an intercontroller roam qualify as a Layer 2 (local-to-local) roam?

back.text: When the WLAN interfaces on both controllers map to the same VLAN/IP subnet. The client keeps its original IP address, and the roam typically completes in under 20 ms.

front.text: How does a Layer 3 intercontroller roam allow the client to keep its original IP address?

back.text: When the two controllers detect different VLAN IDs on their WLAN interfaces, they build a CAPWAP tunnel between themselves. Client traffic is tunneled back to the original (anchor) controller's subnet, so the client's IP address remains valid even though it is physically associated with a foreign controller.
The original controller is the anchor; the new controller is the foreign controller.

front.text: Compare the role of the anchor controller vs. the foreign controller in a Layer 3 roam.

rows: The client's original controller; owns the client's IP subnet and remains the data anchor point
The controller the client has roamed to; tunnels client traffic back to the anchor over CAPWAP

front.text: What tunneling protocol is used between Cisco controllers to support Layer 3 roaming?

back.text: CAPWAP (Control and Provisioning of Wireless Access Points). The same protocol used between APs and controllers is also used to build inter-controller tunnels for Layer 3 roams.

front.text: In a Layer 2 intercontroller roam, what tunnel is used to carry client data between the two controllers?

back.text: None. In a Layer 2 roam, both controllers map the WLAN to the same VLAN/subnet, so the client's database entry is simply moved to the new controller. No inter-controller tunnel is required for client data.

front.text: You want all guest wireless traffic to terminate behind a DMZ firewall regardless of which controller the guest first associates with. How can this be implemented?

back.text: Configure the DMZ controller as a static anchor controller for the guest WLAN. Other controllers in the network will tunnel guest traffic to this anchor via Layer 3 roaming tunnels, ensuring all guest traffic exits at the DMZ.

front.text: What is a mobility group?

back.text: A mobility group is a logical grouping of Cisco wireless LAN controllers that share the same mobility group name and trust each other for fast, efficient client roaming, including support for CCKM, key caching, and 802.11r.

front.text: What are the maximum number of controllers in a single mobility group and in a mobility list?

items: Mobility group: up to 24 controllers
Mobility list: up to 72 controllers (across multiple mobility groups)

front.text: What is a mobility domain?

back.text: A mobility domain is the collection of controllers defined in a controller's mobility list. It represents the controller's view of the outside world — only controllers in the mobility list are known and trusted for client roaming.

front.text: If two controllers are in the same mobility list but in different mobility groups, what happens during a roam between them?

back.text: Roaming is still possible (Layer 2 or Layer 3), but it is not as efficient. Credentials are not cached and shared across mobility groups, so CCKM and key caching do not work — clients must perform a full authentication during the roam.

front.text: What happens when a client tries to roam between two controllers that are not in each other's mobility list?

back.text: The controllers are unknown to each other, so no roaming coordination occurs. The client must associate and authenticate from scratch on the new controller, as if joining for the first time.

front.text: Rank the typical roaming types by how quickly they complete (fastest to slowest).

items: Intracontroller roam — < 10 ms (fastest)
Layer 2 intercontroller roam — usually < 20 ms
Layer 3 intercontroller roam — slower due to inter-WLC CAPWAP tunnel setup
Roam between controllers in different mobility groups — slowest, requires full reauthentication

front.text: Wireless VoIP users complain about brief audio dropouts every time they roam between APs on different controllers. The controllers are in the same mobility list but in different mobility groups. What is the likely cause and fix?

back.text: Because the controllers are in different mobility groups, fast-roaming key caching (CCKM/802.11r/PMK caching) does not work across them, forcing a full 802.1X reauthentication on every roam — which adds enough delay to disrupt voice.
Fix: Place all controllers serving the voice WLAN in the same mobility group, and enable a fast-roaming method (CCKM or 802.11r) supported by the phones.

front.text: After roaming between controllers, clients lose connectivity until they renew DHCP. What design issue is likely?

back.text: The WLAN is mapped to different VLANs/subnets on each controller, but no Layer 3 anchor/foreign tunnel is being established (e.g., controllers not in the same mobility list, or anchor not configured). The client ends up on a new subnet without a Layer 3 roam, invalidating its IP.
Fix: Either map the WLAN to the same VLAN on both controllers (Layer 2 roam), or ensure both controllers share a mobility list so a Layer 3 roam tunnel can form.

front.text: List the high-level sequence of events when a client performs a Layer 3 intercontroller roam.

items: Client detects degraded signal and sends a Reassociation Request to a new AP.
New AP forwards the request to its controller (foreign WLC).
Foreign WLC contacts the client's original (anchor) WLC via the mobility list.
Both WLCs compare WLAN VLAN IDs and detect a subnet mismatch.
A CAPWAP mobility tunnel is established between foreign and anchor WLCs.
Client traffic is tunneled back to the anchor controller, allowing the client to keep its original IP address.

front.text: At the most basic level, how can a wireless client be located?

back.text: By the AP it is currently associated with. However, this is coarse — a single AP may cover a large area, and clients sometimes stay associated with a distant AP rather than roam to a closer one.

front.text: What is Received Signal Strength (RSS), and how does it help locate a device?

back.text: RSS is the measured power level of an RF signal received by an AP. Because free space path loss attenuates a signal exponentially with distance and frequency, RSS can be used to estimate how far a client is from the receiving AP.

front.text: Why is RSS from a single AP insufficient to accurately locate a wireless device?

back.text: With one AP and an omnidirectional antenna, a given RSS value places the client somewhere along a circle of equal distance — but the bearing is unknown. Three or more APs are needed to correlate measurements and pinpoint where the distance circles intersect.

front.text: Which Cisco components work together to provide Real-Time Location Services (RTLS)?

items: APs — gather RF data and RSS from clients
WLCs — aggregate client info via split-MAC and send to management/location platforms
Management platform — Cisco DNA Center (Catalyst Center) or Cisco Prime Infrastructure
Location server — Cisco Spaces, Cisco MSE (Mobility Services Engine), or Cisco CMX (Connected Mobile Experiences)

front.text: How does the split-MAC architecture support real-time location services?

back.text: APs interface directly with clients at the real-time RF layer (collecting RSS, probe info, etc.), while WLCs handle data forwarding and aggregate client/RSS information from the APs. The WLCs then feed that data to a management platform and a location server, which performs the actual location calculations.

front.text: What is RF fingerprinting, and why is it used for indoor location?

back.text: RF fingerprinting is Cisco's technique that applies an RF calibration template to a mapped area so location calculations account for real-world signal attenuation caused by walls, furniture, and other physical obstacles. It is more accurate than free-space path-loss math alone.
Calibration can be done by manually walking with a measurement device or by selecting predefined models (e.g., cubes/walled offices, drywalled offices, indoor high ceilings, outdoor open space).

front.text: How can multiple APs on different channels all hear the same client for location purposes when the client is associated with only one AP?

back.text: Clients periodically send 802.11 Probe Requests on every channel/band they support, both to discover potential APs and to prepare for roams. Neighboring APs receive these probes on their respective channels and report the source MAC and RSS to the controller and location server.

front.text: A retail store wants to track shoppers' smartphones as they walk around, even though the phones never join the store's Wi-Fi. Is this possible with Cisco RTLS?

back.text: Yes. As long as Wi-Fi is enabled on the phones, they will send 802.11 Probe Requests. APs detect these probes and report the MAC + RSS, allowing the location server (e.g., Cisco Spaces) to estimate and display the device's location even though it never associates with an AP.

front.text: On a Cisco Spaces map, what is the difference between a green dot and a red dot representing a tracked device?

rows: Device successfully associated with an AP
Device not associated, but actively sending probe requests

front.text: How can the Cisco location infrastructure locate non-802.11 sources of interference such as cordless phones?

back.text: Cisco APs with dedicated spectrum analysis and the CleanAir feature can detect non-802.11 interference and measure its received signal strength on a channel. Multiple APs report this data to the location server, which triangulates and displays a probable location of the interference source on the map.

front.text: How are Wi-Fi RFID tags located by a Cisco wireless network?

back.text: Wi-Fi RFID tags either associate to the WLAN to send data, or periodically wake up to transmit 802.11 Probe Requests or multicast frames announcing their presence. APs receive these frames, record RSS, and the location server uses the data to compute the tag's position on a map.

front.text: Quick exam recall: how do you tell if a roam is intracontroller, Layer 2 intercontroller, or Layer 3 intercontroller?

items: Same WLC for both APs → Intracontroller roam (fastest, < 10 ms)
Different WLCs, same VLAN/subnet on the WLAN → Layer 2 intercontroller roam (< 20 ms)
Different WLCs, different VLANs/subnets → Layer 3 intercontroller roam (uses CAPWAP anchor/foreign tunnel)

front.text: List several real-world use cases for wireless device location services.

items: Retail: targeted advertising as customers approach product displays
Museums: delivering exhibit-relevant content to mobile devices
Healthcare: tracking critical medical equipment and patients
Large venues: wayfinding/navigation guidance for visitors
Security: locating rogue APs/clients and sources of RF interference
Asset tracking: monitoring high-value tagged assets

front.text: A campus has 30 controllers serving the same SSID. To allow seamless fast roaming for voice clients across all of them, what is the recommended mobility design?

back.text: A single mobility group can hold only 24 controllers, so split the 30 WLCs into two mobility groups (e.g., 15 + 15) but place all 30 in each controller's mobility list (max 72). This forms a single mobility domain.

Roams within the same mobility group support full fast-roaming (CCKM/key caching/802.11r). Roams between different mobility groups in the same mobility list still work but require full reauthentication, so try to align WLCs that serve adjacent coverage areas into the same mobility group.

front.text: Why must wireless clients be authenticated before being granted access to a wireless network?

back.text: To control access so that only trusted/expected devices reach corporate or sensitive resources. Guests should be limited to a separate WLAN, and rogue or unknown clients should be blocked from associating at all. Authentication validates either the device, the user, or both before network access is granted.

front.text: What is Open Authentication?

back.text: Open Authentication is the original 802.11 method that requires no credentials. The client sends an 802.11 authentication request and is allowed to associate. It validates only that the client is a valid 802.11 device — not the user's identity. Real user authentication must be added through other means (e.g., WebAuth).

front.text: Where is Open Authentication most commonly seen in practice?

back.text: On public/guest WLANs at hotels, coffee shops, airports, etc. Clients associate immediately, then a WebAuth portal typically prompts them to accept an Acceptable Use Policy or enter basic credentials before full network access is granted.

front.text: On an IOS XE WLC, where do you configure Open Authentication on a WLAN, and what setting indicates Open Auth?

items: Configuration > Tags & Profiles > WLANs
Add or edit a WLAN; set the SSID under the General tab
Go to Security > Layer 2 tab
Set Layer 2 Security Mode to None

back.text: When verified in the WLAN list, the Security column will show [open].

front.text: Compare WPA, WPA2, and WPA3 at a high level.

rows: Original Wi-Fi Alliance certification; uses TKIP. Now considered weak/deprecated.
Adds AES/CCMP encryption; widely deployed. Personal mode vulnerable to offline dictionary attacks against 4-way handshake.
Most secure; Personal mode replaces PSK exchange with SAE, adds forward secrecy and protection against dictionary attacks.

back.text: Always use the highest WPA version supported by your WLCs, APs, and clients.

front.text: What is the difference between WPA personal mode and WPA enterprise mode?

rows: Small environments, embedded devices; same key configured on all clients and APs
Larger enterprises with per-user credentials and a RADIUS authentication server

front.text: When PSK is used (without ISE), where must the key string be configured?

back.text: On all wireless clients AND on all APs/WLCs (i.e., on the WLAN configuration). Every device using the WLAN must have an identical pre-shared key. The key itself is never transmitted over the air.

front.text: If the PSK is never sent over the air, how do the client and AP agree on encryption keys?

back.text: The PSK is used directly as the Pairwise Master Key (PMK). The client and AP then perform a four-way EAPOL handshake that uses the PMK to derive a Pairwise Transient Key (PTK) for unicast traffic and a Groupwise Transient Key (GTK) for broadcast/multicast traffic. The handshake material can be exchanged in the clear because it cannot be used without the PMK.

front.text: What attack is WPA/WPA2 Personal mode vulnerable to, and how does WPA3 Personal mitigate it?

back.text: WPA/WPA2 Personal is vulnerable to offline dictionary attacks: an attacker captures the 4-way handshake and brute-forces the PSK. With the key, they can decrypt traffic or join the network.

WPA3 Personal replaces the PSK exchange with Simultaneous Authentication of Equals (SAE), which resists offline dictionary attacks and provides forward secrecy — captured traffic cannot be decrypted later, even if the password is compromised.

front.text: What is SAE in WPA3-Personal?

back.text: Simultaneous Authentication of Equals — a key exchange method where the client and AP authenticate as peers (equally and even simultaneously), rather than the client authenticating against the AP. SAE is resistant to offline dictionary attacks and enables forward secrecy.

front.text: What is forward secrecy, and which WPA mode provides it?

back.text: Forward secrecy means that even if the password/key is later compromised, an attacker cannot decrypt previously captured wireless traffic. WPA3-Personal provides forward secrecy; WPA2-Personal does not.

front.text: Outline the IOS XE WLC steps to configure a WLAN for WPA2-Personal (PSK) with AES.

items: Configuration > Tags & Profiles > WLANs > Add
On General tab, set Profile Name and SSID
Security > Layer 2 tab: select WPA+WPA2
Uncheck WPA Policy; check WPA2 Policy and WPA2 Encryption AES (CCMP128)
Under Auth Key Mgmt, check only PSK
Enter the Pre-Shared Key string
Apply to Device, then map WLAN to a Policy profile via a Policy tag and apply to APs

back.text: In the WLAN list, Security should display [WPA2][PSK][AES].

front.text: Exam tip: what are the operational drawbacks of using PSK in a large environment?

items: Every device must be configured with the same key
Changing the key requires touching every device
Key disclosure to one user effectively compromises everyone
No per-user accountability or identity
Vulnerable to offline dictionary attacks (WPA2-PSK)

front.text: Why should hybrid WPA modes (e.g., WPA+WPA2 with TKIP+AES) generally be avoided?

back.text: A WLAN is only as secure as its weakest enabled cipher. Mixing WPA/WPA2 with TKIP weakens overall security and has been deprecated. Hybrid modes can also cause client compatibility issues. Use WPA2 or WPA3 with AES/CCMP only.

front.text: What is EAP?

back.text: Extensible Authentication Protocol — a flexible authentication framework (not a single method). EAP defines common functions used by specific authentication methods (PEAP, EAP-TLS, EAP-FAST, etc.) so that 802.11 itself doesn't need to implement each method natively.

front.text: Name and describe the three 802.1X roles in a wireless deployment.

rows: Controls port/network access; relays auth via EAPOL/RADIUS
Validates credentials against user database/policy and grants/denies access

front.text: When a Cisco WLC is configured for 802.1X with an external RADIUS server, what role does the WLC play?

back.text: The authenticator. It sits between the supplicant (client) and the authentication server (RADIUS), relaying EAP messages over EAPOL toward the client and over RADIUS toward the server, and enforcing access based on the AS's decision.

front.text: In WPA2-Enterprise, where does the supplicant live?

back.text: On the wireless client device. The supplicant is the software (built-in OS supplicant or third-party) that requests authentication and presents credentials to the authenticator.

front.text: What is EAPOL, and where is it used?

back.text: EAP over LAN — the encapsulation used to carry EAP messages over wired and wireless LANs between the supplicant and the authenticator. The authenticator then translates EAP into RADIUS for the authentication server.

front.text: What is the purpose of the EAPOL four-way handshake?

back.text: To exchange and derive dynamic encryption keys between the client and AP after authentication. It does NOT authenticate the user — it derives the PTK (unicast key) and distributes the GTK (broadcast/multicast key) so the wireless link can be encrypted.

front.text: Explain the key hierarchy used to secure WPA2/WPA3 wireless traffic.

items: PMK (Pairwise Master Key) — derived from EAP auth (Enterprise) or directly from the PSK (Personal)
GMK (Groupwise Master Key) — generated on the AP for group key derivation
PTK (Pairwise Transient Key) — derived from PMK during the 4-way handshake; protects unicast traffic
GTK (Groupwise Transient Key) — derived from GMK; distributed via 4-way handshake; protects broadcast/multicast

front.text: List the high-level message exchange of the EAPOL four-way handshake.

items: Message 1: AP → Client — sends ANonce, allowing client to derive PTK
Message 2: Client → AP — sends SNonce + MIC; AP can now derive PTK
Message 3: AP → Client — sends GTK (encrypted with PTK) + MIC; instructs client to install keys
Message 4: Client → AP — acknowledgment that keys are installed

back.text: After all four messages succeed, 802.1X unblocks the port and encrypted traffic can flow.

front.text: Where are specific EAP methods (PEAP, EAP-TLS, EAP-FAST, etc.) configured — on the WLC or somewhere else?

back.text: They are configured on the RADIUS authentication server and must be matched on the client supplicant. The WLC only needs to know that 802.1X is in use; it does not need to know which specific EAP method is being negotiated, since it just relays EAP between client and server.

front.text: Name several common EAP methods supported in WPA Enterprise mode.

items: LEAP (legacy, Cisco proprietary, deprecated)
EAP-FAST
PEAP
EAP-TLS
EAP-TTLS
EAP-SIM

front.text: On an IOS XE WLC, where do you define an external RADIUS server, and what minimum parameters are required?

items: Configuration > Security > AAA > Servers/Groups
Click Add (or edit existing)
Specify server Name, IP address, shared Secret key
Confirm/adjust RADIUS port numbers (auth/acct)

front.text: When configuring 802.1X for a WLAN on IOS XE, what AAA method-list Type do you select, and what server group?

items: Configuration > Security > AAA > AAA Method List
Add a method list (or edit default)
Type: dot1x
Move radius from Available to Assigned Server Groups
Apply to Device, then reference this method list under the WLAN's Security > AAA tab as the Authentication List

front.text: Outline the WLAN-side settings on an IOS XE WLC to enable WPA2-Enterprise with 802.1X.

items: Configuration > Tags & Profiles > WLANs > Add
Security > Layer 2: select WPA+WPA2
Check WPA2 Policy; uncheck WPA Policy
Check WPA2 Encryption: AES (CCMP128)
Auth Key Mgmt: check 802.1x; uncheck PSK
Security > AAA: set Authentication List to your dot1x method list (e.g., myRadius)
Apply to Device

back.text: Verify in the WLAN list: Security column should show [802.1X].

front.text: What is Local EAP on a Cisco WLC, and when would you use it?

back.text: Local EAP allows the WLC itself to act as the authentication server, validating clients against a local user database. It's useful in small deployments or as a fallback when external RADIUS servers are unreachable. In larger networks, an external RADIUS server (e.g., Cisco ISE) is preferred.

front.text: What is WebAuth, and how does it differ from PSK or EAP authentication?

back.text: Web Authentication presents the user with a web page (AUP, login form, splash page) before granting network access. Unlike PSK or EAP, WebAuth is interactive — it directly engages the end user via a browser. It's commonly added on top of Open Authentication for guest networks.

front.text: Guests must read and accept an Acceptable Use Policy before getting network access. Which authentication method natively supports this?

back.text: WebAuth. It can present an AUP page in the user's browser; access is granted only after the user acknowledges/accepts. Open Authentication, WPA2/WPA3 PSK, and WPA Enterprise alone don't natively present user-facing content.

front.text: List the Local Web Authentication (LWA) modes available on a Cisco WLC.

items: LWA with internal database on the WLC
LWA with external database (RADIUS or LDAP)
LWA with external redirect after authentication
LWA with external splash page redirect, using internal DB on WLC
LWA with passthrough (user acknowledgment only)

front.text: Compare Local Web Authentication (LWA) and Central Web Authentication (CWA).

rows: Hosted on a central server (e.g., Cisco ISE)
Many WLCs with centralized policy and user DB

front.text: Outline the IOS XE steps to enable WebAuth on a WLAN.

items: Configuration > Security > WebAuth: create a parameter map (e.g., MyWebAuth) with banner/redirect/customization
Configuration > Security > AAA > AAA Method List: create a list of Type 'login' assigned to RADIUS server group (e.g., webauth)
Configuration > Tags & Profiles > WLANs: add WLAN; set SSID on General tab
Security > Layer 2: configure Layer 2 settings (often None for guest)
Security > Layer 3: enable WebAuth, select the parameter map and the login authentication list
Apply to Device, link WLAN profile to a Policy profile via a Policy tag, apply to APs

back.text: In the WLAN list, the Security column will show [Web_Auth].

front.text: Exam tip: which AAA method-list Type is used for 802.1X versus WebAuth?
Can WebAuth be combined with other authentication methods?

back.text: Yes. WebAuth is a Layer 3 mechanism and can be layered on top of Open Authentication, PSK, or EAP-based authentication. For example, a guest WLAN may use Open Authentication at Layer 2 plus WebAuth at Layer 3 to display an AUP and capture credentials.

front.text: How do you verify which security method a WLAN is configured for in IOS XE WLC GUI, and what values appear?

back.text: Configuration > Tags & Profiles > WLANs lists all WLANs and a Security column. Typical values:

front.text: Clients on a WPA2-Enterprise WLAN cannot authenticate. The WLC shows RADIUS timeouts. What are the most likely causes to check?

items: RADIUS server reachability from the WLC management/source interface (ping/route)
RADIUS shared secret mismatch between WLC and server
Wrong RADIUS auth/acct port numbers
WLC's NAD/source IP not configured as a network device on RADIUS (e.g., ISE)
ACL or firewall blocking UDP 1812/1813 between WLC and RADIUS
Wrong AAA method list referenced under WLAN Security > AAA

front.text: A client repeatedly fails to associate with a WPA2-PSK WLAN and never reaches IP connectivity. What is the most common cause?

back.text: PSK mismatch between client and WLAN configuration. The 4-way handshake fails because the PMK derived on each side doesn't match. Re-enter the PSK on the client and confirm it matches the WLAN configuration exactly (case-sensitive, no extra whitespace).

front.text: Recommend an authentication design for: (a) corporate employees, (b) IoT/embedded sensors, (c) guests.

rows: WPA2/WPA3 Enterprise (802.1X) with EAP-TLS or PEAP, RADIUS (ISE) for per-user identity
WPA2/WPA3 Personal (PSK), preferably PSK with ISE (iPSK) for unique per-device keys
Open Authentication + WebAuth (LWA or CWA) with AUP and optional sponsor/credential login

front.text: Quick exam recall: summarize the four wireless authentication methods covered in Chapter 20.

rows: Public/guest WLANs, often combined with WebAuth

front.text: What is the recommended first step when users report a wireless problem?

back.text: Gather more information to determine the scope of the problem. Start broad, then narrow down by asking pointed questions to find patterns. Don't reboot the WLC or close the ticket prematurely.

front.text: How does the scope of user reports help you decide where to focus troubleshooting?

rows: Many users in same physical area
Many users across many areas / single SSID
Client device and its interaction with an AP

front.text: What is the most useful piece of information to identify a single wireless client in the WLC?

back.text: The wireless (Wi-Fi adapter) MAC address of the client device. Usernames and application names are often unhelpful, and the wired Ethernet MAC isn't what's seen over the air.

front.text: List the three high-level conditions that must be met for a wireless client to successfully associate and use a WLAN.

items: The client is within RF range of an AP and asks to associate
The client authenticates (PSK, 802.1X/EAP, etc.)
The client requests and receives an IP address (DHCP)

front.text: Why should you not blindly trust a user's claim of which AP they are using?

back.text: The client device — not the user — selects which AP to associate with. The user might see one AP nearby, but their device may actually be associated with a different AP based on signal, load, or roaming behavior.

front.text: How do you quickly locate a specific wireless client in the IOS XE WLC GUI (e.g., Catalyst 9800)?

back.text: Enter the client's wireless MAC address in the search bar at the top right of the WLC GUI. If known, a 'Client MAC Result' match is shown; click the MAC address to open the Client 360 View.

front.text: You have multiple WLCs and a user has reported issues but doesn't know which AP/WLC they're on. What is the most efficient way to find the client?

back.text: Search for the client's wireless MAC address on each WLC. The controller that has the client in its database is the one currently servicing that client. Searching APs individually or trying to reproduce from your own laptop is far less efficient.

front.text: What kind of information is shown in the Client 360 View on a Catalyst 9800 WLC?

items: Username (if authenticated by user)
Wireless MAC address and connection uptime
WLAN/SSID currently in use
AP name and channel where the client is associated
Signal strength (dBm) and SNR
Channel width
Device type and 802.11 capabilities (e.g., 802.11n, spatial streams)
Connectivity and application information

front.text: How should you interpret a client's received signal strength of -43 dBm vs -75 dBm?

rows: Strong signal — supports high data rates and good performance
Weak signal — likely forces lower data rates and poor performance; client may be too far from AP

back.text: Closer to 0 dBm = stronger signal. -30 dBm is excellent; -80 dBm or worse is generally unusable.

front.text: What is SNR, and how does it affect achievable data rates?

back.text: SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio) is the difference in dB between the wanted signal level and the noise floor. Higher SNR is better — low SNR may still support low data rates, but higher SNR is required for higher modulation/coding schemes (and thus higher throughput).

front.text: Exam tip: is an SNR of 5 dB on the 2.4 GHz band good or bad?

back.text: Bad. An SNR of 5 dB is very low — the signal is barely above the noise floor, which typically results in poor throughput and unreliable connectivity. Higher SNR (e.g., 25 dB or more) is preferred for good performance.

front.text: On a 5 GHz radio, you see channel 60, 5 dBm Tx, 6 clients, SNR 45 dB, and channel utilization 85%. What is the most likely cause of poor performance?

back.text: Channel utilization is too high (85%). The channel is heavily occupied, leaving little airtime for clients to transmit. SNR is excellent (45 dB), so it is not a signal issue. Investigate interference, neighboring APs on the same channel, or high client load on adjacent APs.

front.text: What information can you verify under the General > Client Properties tab in the Client 360 View?

items: Client MAC and IP addresses
Policy profile applied on the AP
WLAN/SSID being used
AP BSSID
Connection uptime and session timeout
Current transmit data rate / MCS
QoS settings
Mobility/roaming activity

front.text: What does the General > AP Properties tab show about a client's serving AP?

items: AP wired MAC address
AP name
Client association status (associated or not)
802.11 protocol in use
Current channel number

front.text: What client wireless security parameters are shown in the General > Security Information tab?

items: WPA version (e.g., WPA2/WPA3)
Cipher (e.g., CCMP/AES)
Key management (PSK, 802.1X)
EAP method (if any)
Session timeout (e.g., 1800 seconds)

front.text: What is a Radioactive Trace on an IOS XE WLC?

back.text: A Radioactive Trace (RA Trace) is a per-MAC-address conditional debug feature on the WLC. It collects detailed event logs filtered to a specific client MAC over a chosen time window, so you can see only the messages relevant to that client without sifting through unrelated logs.

front.text: A user says she tried to connect to the WLAN several minutes ago but failed, even though she chose the correct SSID. What is the best WLC tool to find the root cause?

back.text: Run a Radioactive Trace targeted at the user's wireless MAC address. Generate a trace file for the relevant time window (e.g., last 10 or 30 minutes) and review the WLC logs to see exactly where in the association/auth/DHCP process the failure occurred.

front.text: List the steps to run and retrieve a Radioactive Trace for a wireless client on a Catalyst 9800.

items: Navigate to Troubleshooting > Radioactive Trace (or click the wrench icon under the client MAC in Monitoring > Wireless > Clients)
Add the client's wireless MAC address (and any others) to the trace list
Click Start to begin collecting logs
Reproduce the issue or wait for the relevant event
Click Stop to end collection
Click Generate next to the MAC; choose interval (10/30/60 min or since last reboot) and check 'Enable internal logs'
Use the download or document icon to save or display the trace file

front.text: Exam tip: what does a Radioactive Trace NOT do?

items: It does NOT trace a user's roaming path between APs
It does NOT trace the WLC's CPU or memory usage
It does NOT trace radio activity per AP
It DOES collect WLC event logs filtered for a specific MAC over a time window

front.text: What two basic connectivity requirements must a lightweight AP meet to provide a working BSS?

items: Connectivity to its access-layer switch (PoE, IP addressing, VLAN)
Connectivity to its WLC (CAPWAP) — unless operating in FlexConnect mode

front.text: What is the simplest way to verify that an AP is up and joined to a WLC?

back.text: Search for the AP's name in the WLC GUI search bar. If it appears in the live AP list with an Admin status of 'up' (green check) and a valid IP address, it is successfully joined.

front.text: After installing a new AP, what should you verify on the WLC before clients try to use it?

items: AP appears in the WLC's joined AP list (the AP is found)
AP has a valid IP address
AP has valid channel numbers listed for both 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz radios
AP's admin status is up and radios are enabled

front.text: What does the AP Join Statistics tab tell you?

back.text: It shows the AP's current status and details about the last events when the AP joined or left the controller, including the last reboot reason and last disconnect reason. For example, 'tag modified' is a common, expected disconnect reason after a configuration change.

front.text: AP join statistics show the AP recently disconnected with reason 'tag modified.' Should you be concerned?

back.text: Usually not. Modifying any tag (policy, site, or RF) applied to an AP causes the AP to refresh its connection or cycle radios/WLANs to commit the change. This is expected behavior any time you push tag/profile updates.

front.text: What information appears on the top portion of the AP 360 View?

items: AP location, IP address, model, and serial number
PoE status
Country code
WPA3 capability
VLAN tag number
Software version
Connection uptime

front.text: What radio-level statistics can you check in the AP 360 View's operational summary?

items: Whether each radio is enabled
Number of associated clients per radio
Channel in use
Transmit power level
Channel utilization
Transmit and receive utilization

front.text: What does a high channel utilization value indicate, and how does it affect clients?

back.text: A high channel utilization means the RF channel is heavily occupied (by clients, neighboring APs, or interference). This reduces airtime available for new transmissions, slowing communication and making it harder for stations to gain access to the medium — typically resulting in slow throughput and high latency.

front.text: Which three tags must each AP be mapped to on a Catalyst 9800 WLC?

items: Policy tag — links WLAN profiles to policy profiles
Site tag — defines AP join profile and flex/local site behavior
RF tag — defines RF profiles for 2.4 GHz / 5 GHz / 6 GHz

front.text: How do you quickly view all profiles and tags applied to a specific AP?

back.text: Click the small hierarchy icon next to the AP's name in the AP statistics page. The AP Operational Configuration Viewer displays a hierarchical view showing the policy tag, site tag, and RF tag, plus the individual profiles each tag references.

front.text: Users in one building report that a particular SSID is missing, but the same SSID works in other buildings. What configuration on the WLC should you check?

back.text: Check the policy tag applied to the APs in that building. If the policy tag does not include the WLAN profile/policy profile mapping for that SSID, the affected APs won't broadcast it. Use the AP Operational Configuration Viewer (hierarchy icon) to confirm which policy/site/RF tags are applied.

front.text: A user complains of poor performance after walking to a different floor. WLC shows RSSI -75 dBm and SNR 18 dB at the same AP. What is most likely happening?

back.text: The client has moved out of optimal range of the original AP but has not yet roamed to a closer one (sticky client). This forces low data rates and poor throughput.
Possible fixes: check AP coverage on that floor (may need an additional AP), tune RF profile/Tx power, or address client-side roaming behavior (driver/aggressiveness).

front.text: An AP is not joining the WLC. What basic items should you verify first?

items: PoE / power to the AP (port and switch)
Switchport configuration (correct VLAN, trunk vs access, no err-disable)
AP has obtained a valid IP address (DHCP)
DNS / DHCP option 43 / controller discovery is working
IP reachability between AP and WLC (CAPWAP UDP 5246/5247)
WLC and AP software versions are compatible
AP is authorized on the WLC (if AP authorization is enforced)

front.text: Why is WLC connectivity considered optional for an AP operating in FlexConnect mode?

back.text: FlexConnect APs can switch client traffic locally at the branch and continue to provide service for already-authenticated SSIDs even when the WLC is unreachable. WLC connectivity is still required for management and certain centralized auth/services, but local data forwarding can survive a WAN outage.

front.text: Many users across multiple buildings report problems on the same SSID. Where should you focus first?

back.text: On the WLC and WLAN configuration. A widespread problem tied to a single SSID points to a controller-side issue: WLAN settings, AAA/RADIUS reachability, ACL, policy profile, or VLAN/DHCP misconfiguration — not individual APs or clients.

front.text: Many users in the same physical area report problems but no other locations are affected. Where should you focus?

back.text: On the AP(s) covering that area. Likely causes: AP down or rebooted, radio disabled, hardware failure, channel/RF issue, high channel utilization, or local interference. Use the WLC to check the AP's status, radio details, and channel utilization.

front.text: A client associates and authenticates successfully but cannot get an IP address. Where do you investigate?

items: Verify the policy profile maps the WLAN to the correct VLAN
Check DHCP server reachability and scope availability for that VLAN
Ensure DHCP relay/helper is configured if DHCP server is off-subnet
Look at Radioactive Trace logs around the DHCP discover/offer phase
Confirm there's no ACL blocking UDP 67/68

front.text: Why is the client's current MCS (Modulation and Coding Scheme) or data rate useful when troubleshooting performance?

back.text: MCS reflects the modulation/coding the client is currently using, which is tightly tied to RF conditions (RSSI/SNR). A persistently low MCS indicates poor RF (low SNR/RSSI, distance, interference), even if the client is technically connected — explaining slow throughput despite a working association.

cards.subtopic: Two Ways to Open RA Trace

front.text: Name two GUI paths to start a Radioactive Trace for a wireless client on a Catalyst 9800.

items: Troubleshooting > Radioactive Trace (from the main WLC default screen)
Click the small wrench icon under the client's MAC in Monitoring > Wireless > Clients

front.text: What time windows can you select when generating a Radioactive Trace file?

items: Last 10 minutes
Last 30 minutes
Last 1 hour
Since the last WLC reboot

back.text: Don't forget to check 'Enable internal logs' to include the WLC's collected logs in the output.

front.text: Summarize a clean wireless troubleshooting workflow from chapter 21.

items: Gather information; determine scope (one user, one area, one SSID, or all)
Identify the affected client(s) by wireless MAC address
On the WLC, search for the MAC and open the Client 360 View
Verify association: AP, channel, RSSI, SNR, channel width, MCS
Verify Client Properties (IP, policy profile, WLAN), AP Properties, and Security Information
If root cause is unclear, run a Radioactive Trace for that MAC and analyze
If multiple clients are affected, investigate the AP (status, radios, channel utilization, tags) or WLAN/WLC config
Validate AP is up, joined, has IP, valid channels, and correct policy/site/RF tags via the AP 360 / Operational Configuration Viewer

front.text: What are the main benefits of a hierarchical LAN design model?

items: Improved performance
Simplified design
High scalability
Easier troubleshooting and faster problem isolation
Fault containment — failures affect a smaller portion of the network
Consistent, repeatable deployment via modular building blocks

front.text: Name the three layers of the hierarchical LAN design model and the role of each.

rows: Connects endpoints/users to the network (network edge)
Aggregates access switches; L2/L3 boundary; control/services boundary
High-speed interconnect between distribution layers/network blocks

front.text: What is the access layer also commonly called, and what is its primary function?

back.text: The access layer is also called the network edge. Its primary function is to provide endpoints (PCs, IP phones, printers, APs, cameras, etc.) high-bandwidth wired and wireless connectivity into the network. It is also where the QoS trust boundary and most endpoint-facing security mechanisms are enforced.

front.text: In a hierarchical LAN design, are access layer switches interconnected to each other? How do endpoints on different access switches communicate?

back.text: No — access layer switches are NOT directly interconnected. Communication between endpoints on different access switches must pass through the distribution layer. VLAN segmentation at the access layer separates devices for performance, management, and security reasons.

front.text: What two key boundaries does the distribution layer enforce?

items: Layer 2 boundary — limits STP domain and contains Layer 2 faults from propagating
Layer 3 boundary — provides a logical point to summarize routes into the core, reducing routing table size and speeding convergence

front.text: How many distribution switches should be deployed within a hierarchical LAN design building block, and why?

back.text: Two — distribution switches are deployed in pairs for redundancy. The pair should be interconnected via a Layer 2 or Layer 3 link to support failover and avoid a single point of failure for the entire access block.

front.text: When should an organization add a dedicated core layer to its design?

back.text: When the network grows beyond about three distribution layers in a single location, or when interconnecting multiple buildings/PINs (data center, WAN edge, Internet edge, network services). A dedicated core reduces the wiring complexity from N×(N-1) full mesh links to just N links per distribution block, while providing high-speed scalability, high availability, and fast convergence.

front.text: Compare two-tier (collapsed core) and three-tier hierarchical designs.

rows: More than ~3 distribution pairs or multiple PINs

front.text: What are the typical 'network blocks' or 'places in the network' (PINs) connected through the core?

items: Campus access (end-user/endpoint block)
Data center / server room
WAN edge (remote sites, dedicated cloud interconnects)
Internet edge (Internet, e-commerce, VPN, public cloud)
Network services edge (WLCs, ISE, CUCM, TelePresence Mgr, etc.)
Private/public cloud connectivity

front.text: Which network blocks can provide access to cloud providers for end users?

rows: Dedicated interconnects to AWS, Azure, GCP (e.g., Direct Connect, ExpressRoute)
Standard Internet/VPN-based access to cloud services

front.text: List network-level design guidelines for high availability.

items: Add redundant devices and links at each layer
Eliminate single points of failure (fault tolerance)
Simplify design with virtual clustering (VSS, SWV, StackWise)
Deploy network monitoring to detect capacity, hardware, and security issues early

front.text: List system-level high availability guidelines for routers/switches.

items: Redundant hardware: dual power supplies, fans/fan-trays, modular line cards, dual route/supervisor processors (RPs)
Hot-swappable / OIR (Online Insertion and Removal) components
Enable SSO and NSF (with GR or NSR) for control-plane resiliency
Use link-failure detection: BFD and UDLD
Use FHRPs at L3 boundaries: HSRP, VRRP, GLBP

front.text: Why can a route processor (RP) failure cause traffic loss in a router with dual RPs without HA features enabled?

back.text: The active RP holds the control plane, RIB, and (in centralized forwarding) the FIB and adjacency table. When it fails, routing protocol adjacencies drop, the FIB is rebuilt, and packets can be lost while the new active RP re-establishes neighbors and recomputes routes. HA features (SSO, NSF, GR/NSR) preserve forwarding state during the switchover.

front.text: What is SSO (Stateful Switchover)?

back.text: SSO is an internal router redundancy feature that checkpoints (synchronizes) router configuration, line card state, and Layer 2 protocol state from the active RP to the standby RP. On failover, the standby RP takes over without flapping interfaces or reloading line cards — but Layer 3 routing adjacencies still go down because L3 control-plane state isn't synced by SSO alone.

front.text: What is NSF (Nonstop Forwarding), and how does it relate to SSO?

back.text: NSF is an internal Layer 3 data-plane redundancy feature that checkpoints the FIB from the active RP to the standby RP. During an RP switchover, the new active RP keeps forwarding using the checkpointed FIB while the control plane recovers.
NSF is not separately configurable — it is automatically enabled when SSO is enabled. That's why it's referenced as 'SSO/NSF.'

front.text: Why isn't SSO/NSF alone enough to ensure traffic continues during an RP switchover?

back.text: SSO/NSF preserves the local FIB so the failing router can keep forwarding, but neighbor routers see the routing adjacency drop and stop forwarding traffic to it. To prevent neighbors from withdrawing routes, you need GR (which signals neighbors via routing-protocol extensions) or NSR (which keeps adjacencies up internally without notifying neighbors).

front.text: What is Graceful Restart (GR), and what makes it different from SSO/NSF?

back.text: GR (RFC 4724) is a standards-based set of routing-protocol extensions that lets a router undergoing an RP switchover signal its neighbors that it can keep forwarding even though its routing adjacency may briefly drop. It is the only HA mechanism that interacts with neighbor routers — SSO/NSF are internal-only features.

front.text: Define the three GR router categories.

rows: Has dual RPs and runs SSO/NSF; preserves FIB through switchover; also GR-aware
Neighbor router that supports GR extensions; preserves routes/adjacency state for the switching router. Doesn't need dual RPs.
Neighbor router without GR extensions support — will drop routes when it sees the adjacency go down

front.text: What is Nonstop Routing (NSR)?

back.text: NSR is an internal Cisco HA feature where the active RP continuously checkpoints all routing control-plane state (including routing adjacencies and TCP sockets) to the standby RP. On switchover, the new active RP maintains adjacencies without notifying neighbors. Unlike GR, NSR does not require routing protocol extensions or any neighbor support — it works even with GR-unaware peers.

front.text: Compare Graceful Restart (GR) and Nonstop Routing (NSR).

rows: External — uses routing protocol extensions, requires neighbor support
Briefly drops, neighbors keep forwarding via GR

back.text: Best practice in scaled deployments: use GR with GR-aware peers; use NSR for GR-unaware peers.

front.text: What are the four valid combinations of HA features used during an RP switchover?

items: SSO and NSF (alone — neighbors see adjacency drop)
SSO/NSF with GR (neighbors GR-aware)
SSO/NSF with NSR (no neighbor support needed)
SSO/NSF with both NSR and GR (recommended for scaled mixed environments)

front.text: True or false: NSF can be enabled without enabling SSO.

back.text: False. NSF is not a configurable feature on its own — it is automatically enabled when SSO is enabled. They are commonly referenced together as SSO/NSF.

front.text: Describe the traditional Layer 2 access / Layer 3 distribution design and its main drawbacks.

back.text: Access switches operate at L2, and the L2/L3 boundary lives at the distribution layer. The distribution pair runs HSRP/VRRP/GLBP for IP gateway redundancy.

items: If VLANs span multiple access switches, STP blocks one uplink — wasted bandwidth, slower convergence
HSRP/VRRP only use one active uplink; load balancing requires manual odd/even VLAN tuning
GLBP can balance across uplinks but only on loop-free topologies
FHRP timers must be tuned for sub-second convergence (CPU impact)

front.text: What is the Layer 3 (routed) access design and what are its advantages?

back.text: The L2/L3 boundary is moved to the access switch — access uplinks to distribution become Layer 3 point-to-point routed links.

items: No FHRP required (HSRP/VRRP/GLBP unnecessary)
No STP needed on those uplinks — no blocked links
Both uplinks fully utilized
Faster convergence using EIGRP or OSPF
Easier troubleshooting (end-to-end ping/traceroute)

back.text: Trade-off: VLANs cannot span multiple access switches, and L3-capable access switches cost more.

front.text: What technologies make up the 'simplified campus design,' and how do they help?

rows: Multiple switches stacked into 1 logical switch

back.text: All three combine multiple boxes into a single logical device with one control/management plane, enabling MEC (Multichassis EtherChannel) or cross-stack EtherChannels for loop-free, fully utilized uplinks.

front.text: List the key benefits of the simplified campus design (VSS/SWV/StackWise + MEC).

items: Simpler management — fewer logical boxes
No FHRP needed — single logical gateway
Reduced STP dependence (still kept as failsafe)
All uplinks active via EtherChannel (no blocked links)
Easier troubleshooting (logical hub-and-spoke topology)
Sub-second convergence on link/node failure
VLANs can span multiple access switches without blocking
Inter-chassis SSO/NSF for high availability

front.text: Compare Multichassis EtherChannel (MEC) and cross-stack EtherChannel.

rows: EtherChannel spanning the two physical switches in a logical pair
EtherChannel spanning multiple physical switches in a stack

back.text: Both let downstream/upstream devices treat the logical group as a single switch and bundle all uplinks for active/active forwarding.

front.text: At a high level, what is Cisco SD-Access, and what does it provide beyond traditional campus design?

back.text: SD-Access is Cisco's intent-based campus solution combining a campus fabric (overlay) with Cisco DNA/Catalyst Center for automation. It adds:

items: End-to-end automated segmentation (user/device/app) without re-IPing
Host mobility across the fabric
Enhanced security via SGTs/policy
Simplified provisioning and assurance via DNAC
Standard switching/routing on top of overlay (LISP/VXLAN underneath)

front.text: A growing enterprise has 5 buildings on a campus, each with multiple floors of access switches, plus a data center, WAN edge, and Internet edge block. Which hierarchical design is most appropriate, and why?

back.text: A three-tier design (access + distribution + core) is most appropriate. Each building gets its own pair of distribution switches (one per building reduces fiber runs), and a dedicated core interconnects all distribution blocks plus the data center, WAN edge, Internet edge, and network services blocks. With more than 2-3 distribution pairs and multiple PINs, a collapsed core would become a bottleneck and a scalability issue.

front.text: Quick exam recall: list the campus architecture options covered in chapter 22.

items: Two-tier design (collapsed core) — small campuses
Three-tier design — large/multi-building campuses
Layer 2 access (STP-based) — traditional, FHRP at distribution
Layer 3 access (routed access) — L3 to the access, no FHRP/STP on uplinks
Simplified campus (VSS / StackWise Virtual / StackWise + MEC) — fewer logical boxes, all uplinks active
Software-Defined Access (SD-Access) — fabric + DNA/Catalyst Center automation

front.text: Why is a modular layered design preferred over a flat, fully meshed network?

items: Limits the scope of network changes to a subset of the network (fault containment)
Components can be added/removed with minimal impact
Improves resiliency and simplifies management
Easier to troubleshoot and isolate problems
Each layer can be replicated/repeated as the network grows

front.text: Why does the access layer play a critical role in network security?

back.text: Because the access layer is where endpoints physically connect to the network, it is the first line of defense. It must:

items: Authenticate users/devices (e.g., 802.1X, MAB)
Restrict unauthorized access to services
Enforce the QoS trust boundary
Apply port security, DHCP snooping, DAI, IP Source Guard, and similar features

front.text: What is the recommendation for business-critical endpoints that connect to only a single access switch?

back.text: Use access switches with redundant supervisor engines so that an internal supervisor failure does not cause an outage for those endpoints. This complements the network-level redundancy provided by dual-homing devices to two access switches.

front.text: Why are distribution switches often placed in each building of a large campus instead of all being centralized?

back.text: Because per-building distribution pairs aggregate that building's access switches locally, then carry only the consolidated uplinks back to the core. This dramatically reduces the number of expensive long-haul fiber-optic runs between buildings, lowers cost, and simplifies cabling.

front.text: What does the N×(N-1) → N link reduction at the core layer mean?

back.text: Without a core, fully interconnecting N distribution blocks requires N×(N-1) links (a full mesh). With a dedicated core, each distribution block needs only one link (or a small number) into the core, so total complexity drops to N links per distribution block. This dramatically reduces cabling, cost, and operational overhead as the network grows.
Example: 8 distribution blocks need 8×7 = 56 mesh links without a core, vs. ~8 links with a core.

front.text: Why does the data center block typically use a leaf–spine design instead of a hierarchical three-tier design?

rows: Predictable (always 2 hops between any two leaves)

front.text: What is the role of the Internet edge block in an enterprise architecture?

items: General Internet access for users and applications
E-commerce / public-facing services
Remote-access VPN termination
Connectivity to remote branches over the Internet
Cloud connectivity that doesn't require dedicated interconnects

front.text: What is the role of the WAN edge block?

back.text: The WAN edge connects the campus to remote sites and external resources over private WAN circuits or dedicated cloud interconnects. Typical uses:

items: Connectivity to remote branches and remote data centers
Other campuses across an enterprise WAN
Dedicated interconnects to public cloud providers (AWS Direct Connect, Azure ExpressRoute, GCP Interconnect)

front.text: What devices typically reside in the network services edge block?

items: Wireless LAN Controllers (WLCs)
Cisco Identity Services Engine (ISE)
Cisco Unified Communications Manager (CUCM)
Cisco TelePresence Manager
Other shared infrastructure services (DNS, DHCP, NTP, AAA, etc.)

front.text: What is BFD, and why is it useful in a high-availability design?

back.text: Bidirectional Forwarding Detection (BFD) is a lightweight protocol that quickly detects forwarding-plane failures between two adjacent devices, often in milliseconds. Routing protocols (OSPF, EIGRP, BGP) can be tied to BFD so they react far faster than their native hello timers, dramatically reducing convergence time.

front.text: What problem does UDLD solve, and where is it typically deployed?

back.text: Unidirectional Link Detection (UDLD) detects unidirectional links — links where traffic flows in only one direction (often caused by miswired or partially failing fiber). It error-disables such links to prevent black holes and STP loops. UDLD is commonly enabled on fiber links between switches in the access/distribution/core layers.

front.text: Compare HSRP, VRRP, and GLBP at a high level.

rows: Most common; manual odd/even VLAN tuning to balance

front.text: Why must default FHRP timers usually be tuned, and what's the cost?

back.text: Default HSRP/VRRP timers (e.g., hello 3s, hold 10s) are too slow for sub-second convergence. Tuning down hello/hold timers (or using preemption and BFD) speeds failover, but the more frequent hellos consume more CPU on the switches — so timer tuning must balance convergence speed against CPU load.

front.text: Walk through what happens, in order, during an RP switchover with SSO/NSF + GR enabled.

items: Active RP fails; standby RP becomes the new active RP
SSO checkpoint state is used: configuration, line cards, and L2 state are preserved (no link flap, no line-card reload)
NSF keeps forwarding using the checkpointed FIB
Routing adjacencies briefly drop, but GR signals neighbors that the router is undergoing graceful restart
GR-aware neighbors keep forwarding to the restarting router and preserve routes/adjacency state
New active RP rebuilds its routing tables and re-establishes adjacencies
RIB/FIB are reconciled — traffic continues with little to no loss

front.text: Does a GR-aware (helper) router need dual RPs?

back.text: No. A GR-aware router only needs to support the GR routing protocol extensions so it can preserve routes and adjacency state for a neighbor that is performing graceful restart. It does not need to be SSO/NSF capable itself, and it does not need redundant route processors.

front.text: You are deploying a Cisco core router that peers with a third-party router that does not support GR extensions. How do you keep traffic flowing during an RP switchover?

back.text: Enable SSO/NSF with NSR on the Cisco router. NSR is fully self-contained: the standby RP is constantly checkpointed with control-plane state and adjacencies, so during switchover the routing peer never sees the adjacency drop. No GR support is required on the third-party neighbor.

front.text: What is the recommended HA strategy in a scaled deployment with a mix of GR-aware and GR-unaware peers?

back.text: Use SSO/NSF with GR for neighbors that are GR-aware (lower overhead) and SSO/NSF with NSR for neighbors that are GR-unaware. This keeps NSR's higher checkpointing overhead limited to only the peers that actually require it, while still providing seamless failover everywhere.

front.text: Compare a loop-free vs. looped Layer 2 access topology.

rows: STP blocks one link per loop
Less — VLAN can't follow user across switches

front.text: What is the key limitation of GLBP in a Layer 2 access design?

back.text: GLBP only works correctly in loop-free topologies. In looped Layer 2 designs (where a VLAN spans multiple access switches and STP blocks a link), GLBP cannot effectively load-balance across both uplinks because STP forces traffic onto the single forwarding link.

front.text: What are the main limitations of a Layer 3 (routed) access design?

items: VLANs cannot span multiple access switches
L3-capable access switches are typically more expensive than L2-only switches
Requires running a routing protocol (EIGRP/OSPF) all the way to the access — more configuration to manage
Some legacy applications that rely on L2 adjacency (clusters, broadcast services) may not work well

front.text: A campus needs the same VLAN to span multiple access switches without STP blocking links. Which design satisfies this best?

back.text: The simplified campus design (VSS, StackWise Virtual, or StackWise + MEC/cross-stack EtherChannel). VLANs can span multiple access switches with all uplinks active because the distribution/core appears as a single logical switch and EtherChannel — not STP — handles redundancy.
Routed access does NOT meet this requirement (VLANs are confined to one switch). Layer 2 looped designs do, but at the cost of blocked links.

front.text: Why is STP still kept enabled in a simplified campus design even though EtherChannel removes the loops?

back.text: STP is retained as a failsafe in case a misconfiguration or a backdoor cabling event creates a loop (for example, two access switches accidentally interconnected). It will not normally block any links during steady-state operation, but it provides loop protection if the topology changes unexpectedly.

front.text: Which clustering technology is typically used at which layer?

rows: Distribution and Core layers (two switches → one logical switch)

back.text: Technically, any of them can be used at any layer, but Cisco platforms tend to align this way based on the switch families that support each feature.

front.text: How does a VSS/StackWise Virtual pair stay highly available if one of the two physical chassis fails?

back.text: VSS/SWV uses inter-chassis SSO/NSF: the active chassis continuously synchronizes control-plane and forwarding state to the standby chassis. If the active fails, the standby takes over as the new active for the logical switch with seamless failover, while MEC keeps the EtherChannels active on the surviving chassis's links.

front.text: What problems does SD-Access aim to solve compared to a traditional campus design?

items: Manual VLAN/ACL/IP-based segmentation that doesn't scale
Difficult host mobility (IP changes when users roam between sites/closets)
Inconsistent policy enforcement across wired/wireless
Complex, error-prone manual provisioning across many switches
Limited end-to-end visibility and assurance

back.text: SD-Access addresses these via a fabric overlay (LISP/VXLAN/SGT), automated provisioning through DNA/Catalyst Center, and identity-based policy.

front.text: A small business occupies a single 3-floor building with a few hundred users, no remote sites, and a small server room. Which campus architecture is most appropriate?

back.text: A two-tier (collapsed core) design. A single pair of distribution/core switches aggregates the access layer and connects to the server room, Internet edge, and (optionally) network services block. There is no need for a separate core layer at this scale, and avoiding it saves cost without giving up most hierarchical-design benefits.

front.text: List the conditions that typically justify moving from a two-tier to a three-tier design.

items: More than 2–3 distribution pairs needed
Multiple buildings each requiring their own distribution layer
Growing density of WAN routers, Internet edge devices, data center servers, and network services
Geographic dispersion that would otherwise require many fiber runs back to a single collapsed core
Need for higher core-layer scalability, throughput, and fast convergence

front.text: Your distribution router has dual RPs. Its OSPF neighbor is a Cisco router that is GR-aware. What is the appropriate HA configuration?

back.text: Enable SSO (which automatically enables NSF) with Graceful Restart (GR). GR has lower overhead than NSR and is fully effective because the neighbor supports the GR routing-protocol extensions. NSR is unnecessary in this case and would add extra checkpointing load.

front.text: Compare Layer 2 access, Layer 3 (routed) access, and Simplified Campus across key design factors.
Briefly define: building block, network block, place in the network (PIN).

rows: A modular, repeatable unit of the campus design (e.g., an access/distribution pair) that can be replicated to scale the network
A functional grouping in the architecture (e.g., campus access, data center, WAN edge, Internet edge, network services) connected through the core
Cisco term for a logical area where a specific role/function lives (e.g., campus PIN, branch PIN, data center PIN, cloud PIN)

front.text: A single-floor branch office has 1 access switch and 50 users. Does it need a hierarchical design with all three layers?

back.text: No. At this scale, a single access switch (or even a small two-tier collapsed access/distribution) is sufficient. The hierarchical model's value comes from modularity at scale; for tiny sites, applying all three layers adds cost and complexity without meaningful benefit.

front.text: Why is the access layer typically the QoS trust boundary?

back.text: Because the access layer is the first hop where untrusted endpoint traffic enters the network. Establishing the trust boundary at the access switch ensures that DSCP/CoS markings from endpoints are validated, re-marked if needed, and protected end-to-end — preventing rogue endpoints from claiming high-priority QoS classes.

front.text: Why is the distribution layer the recommended place for IP route summarization?

back.text: Because it is the L2/L3 boundary that aggregates many access-layer subnets. Summarizing routes here keeps the core's routing table small, reduces routing-protocol overhead, contains the impact of access-layer flaps to the local block, and speeds convergence throughout the rest of the network.

front.text: What design principles should the core layer follow to maximize performance and reliability?

items: Keep it as simple as possible — avoid complex policies/ACLs (push those to distribution)
Use only Layer 3 routed links (no STP)
Use ECMP for active/active load sharing and fast failover
Provide redundancy (dual core devices and dual uplinks from each distribution block)
Use high-speed, low-latency hardware
Avoid attaching end devices directly to core

cards.subtopic: Why No End Devices on Core

front.text: Why are end devices and services not connected directly to the core layer?

back.text: The core's job is high-speed transit between distribution blocks. Connecting end devices or services directly adds policy, ARP, and L2 complexity that slows convergence, increases the blast radius of failures, and undermines the core's role as a fast, simple backbone. End devices belong at the access layer, services in the network-services or data-center block.

front.text: Why does the access layer provide such high-bandwidth ports even if endpoints rarely use them at full capacity?

back.text: End-user traffic is bursty. Endpoints don't sustain peak rates, but they need the headroom to burst (file transfers, video, software updates) without being throttled. Providing high-bandwidth Gigabit/Multigigabit and modern Wi-Fi rates improves user QoE and productivity, even if average utilization is low.

front.text: What's the difference between a single-homed and dual-homed endpoint at the access layer, and why does it matter?

rows: Endpoint connects to one access switch only
Loses connectivity if that switch fails — needs redundant supervisors for critical hosts
Endpoint connects to two access switches (e.g., NIC teaming)
Survives loss of either switch — preferred for business-critical servers/devices

front.text: Why are dual power supplies and redundant fans considered system-level HA requirements?

back.text: Power and cooling are common single points of failure for individual chassis. Dual PSUs (ideally fed from independent power feeds/UPS) protect against power supply failure or feed loss; redundant fans/fan trays protect against thermal-based shutdowns. Without these, a single component failure can take an entire device offline despite all the L2/L3 redundancy.

front.text: What does OIR mean, and why is it important for high availability?

back.text: OIR = Online Insertion and Removal. It allows hardware components (line cards, supervisors, power supplies, fans) to be replaced or added while the device is running, without rebooting or impacting traffic. This is critical for maintaining uptime during maintenance and hardware failures.

front.text: Why is BFD often preferred over aggressively tuning routing-protocol hellos for fast failure detection?

rows: High CPU per protocol; each protocol must be tuned separately
Subsecond detection; one mechanism shared by OSPF/EIGRP/BGP/static; offloaded to hardware on many platforms
Slightly more configuration complexity; both ends must support BFD

back.text: BFD provides faster, more efficient detection without forcing aggressive timers in every routing protocol.

front.text: Why is interface or object tracking commonly used with HSRP/VRRP/GLBP?

back.text: Without tracking, an FHRP active router will keep advertising itself even if its uplink to the core/WAN is down — sending host traffic into a black hole. By tracking the uplink interface or a route, the router can decrement its priority and force failover to the standby when its upstream path fails, keeping host traffic on a router that actually has reachability.

front.text: How can HSRP/VRRP achieve some load balancing across distribution uplinks?

back.text: By configuring multiple HSRP/VRRP groups (typically one per VLAN) and making one distribution switch active for odd-numbered VLANs and the other active for even-numbered VLANs.
