Hosts in odd VLANs use one uplink; hosts in even VLANs use the other. This is manual and per-VLAN — GLBP, in contrast, can balance per host on loop-free topologies.

front.text: In one sentence, what problem do all FHRPs solve?

back.text: They provide a single, virtual IP/MAC default gateway to hosts that is backed by two or more routers, so the gateway remains reachable if one router fails — without requiring hosts to detect the failure or reconfigure.

front.text: Categorize the major HA features by which network plane they protect.

rows: Control plane state across redundant RPs
Data forwarding plane during RP switchover
Routing adjacencies between neighbors during switchover
Routing adjacencies internally, transparent to neighbors

front.text: Why is HA typically implemented at multiple layers (network, system, protocol) rather than relying on a single mechanism?

items: Different failure modes (link, supervisor, power, software bug, peer failure) require different mitigations
Defense in depth — if one mechanism fails, another contains the impact
End-to-end uptime depends on the weakest link, so each plane needs its own resilience
Different parts of the network have different RTO/RPO requirements that one mechanism can't meet alone

front.text: Why does routed access typically converge faster than a Layer 2 access design with FHRPs?

back.text: Routed access uses fast IGPs (EIGRP, OSPF) with sub-second timers and ECMP. There's no FHRP failover delay, no STP recalculation, and no MAC table flush. A failed link causes an immediate route recomputation — often in well under a second — without depending on FHRP hello/hold timers or STP topology change events.

front.text: In routed access, where does the L2/L3 boundary move to, and what does that imply for VLANs?

back.text: The L2/L3 boundary moves from the distribution switch down to the access switch. VLANs (and their broadcast domains) are confined to a single access switch, which can't be extended to other access switches. Each access switch becomes the L3 gateway for its own subnets.

front.text: Why does the simplified campus design (VSS/SWV/StackWise + EtherChannel) provide such fast convergence on link failure?

back.text: All uplinks are bundled in an EtherChannel and are in a forwarding state. When one member link fails, traffic is rebalanced across the remaining members in sub-second time without any routing or STP recalculation. There's no FHRP failover, no SPF run, and no STP topology change.

front.text: Why does a VSS/StackWise Virtual pair eliminate the need for FHRPs?

back.text: Because the two physical chassis appear as a single logical switch with one control plane and one set of L3 SVIs. Hosts use a single physical default gateway IP/MAC owned by the logical switch — there is no second peer to fail over to in the FHRP sense. If one chassis fails, the other continues operating as that same logical switch with the same gateway addresses.

front.text: How does an access switch see a Multichassis EtherChannel (MEC) terminating on a VSS pair?

back.text: It sees a standard EtherChannel (typically LACP) with members on what looks like one neighbor. The fact that the bundle physically lands on two different chassis is hidden by the VSS logical switch, so no special configuration is needed on the access switch — it uses normal LACP/PAgP.

front.text: What happens to traffic if one of the two physical chassis in a VSS pair completely fails?

back.text: Inter-chassis SSO/NSF lets the surviving chassis instantly take over as the active for the logical switch. MEC bundles drop the failed-side members but keep forwarding on the surviving members, so attached devices stay connected. Convergence is sub-second, with no FHRP or STP recalculation needed.

front.text: What's the trade-off when you allow a VLAN to span multiple access switches in a Layer 2 access design?

back.text: You gain flexibility — devices in the same VLAN can be plugged into different access switches without changing IP. But STP must block at least one redundant path to prevent loops, which wastes bandwidth and slows convergence on topology changes. The simplified campus design solves this dilemma by removing the loop without blocking links.

front.text: When designing a Layer 2 access network with multi-vendor distribution switches, which FHRP is most appropriate?

back.text: VRRP. It's an IETF standard (RFC 5798) and is supported across vendors, unlike HSRP and GLBP, which are Cisco-proprietary. If all distribution switches are Cisco and you want load balancing on a loop-free topology, GLBP is a good option.

front.text: Exam recall: which campus layer is also commonly called the aggregation layer?

back.text: The distribution layer. It aggregates the access switches in a building/floor and connects them up to the core.

front.text: Exam recall: which campus layer is also called the backbone?

back.text: The core layer. It is the high-speed backbone that interconnects distribution layers and other major network blocks (data center, WAN edge, Internet edge, network services).

front.text: List examples of devices that typically connect at the access layer.

items: PCs and laptops
IP phones
Printers
Wireless access points (APs)
Personal telepresence/video devices
IP video surveillance cameras
Building/IoT devices

back.text: APs and IP phones often extend the access layer one hop further by daisy-chaining downstream devices (PC behind a phone, clients behind an AP).

front.text: How does the distribution layer help contain Layer 2 faults?

back.text: By acting as the L2/L3 boundary, it terminates the spanning-tree domain and prevents L2 issues (broadcast storms, loops, STP topology changes) in one access block from affecting other access blocks or the core. Each distribution block is its own STP and broadcast domain.

front.text: A campus has 4 buildings, each with several floors. Should each building have its own distribution layer, or should there be one centralized distribution serving all buildings?

back.text: Each building should have its own pair of distribution switches. This minimizes the number of long inter-building fiber runs (only the distribution-to-core uplinks cross between buildings), contains L2 faults to one building, and allows each building to scale and be maintained independently. A core layer interconnects all the per-building distribution blocks.

front.text: Can a single enterprise mix different campus architecture options (two-tier, three-tier, routed access, simplified, SD-Access)?

back.text: Yes. Because the design is modular per-block, different parts of the enterprise can use different architectures based on local requirements. For example, small branches may run two-tier, large campuses may run three-tier with a simplified core, certain critical buildings may run SD-Access, and high-density floors may use routed access — all interconnected through a common core.

front.text: Quick exam recall: match each campus design choice to its primary characteristic.

rows: Distribution and core combined; small/single-building campuses
Separate access, distribution, and core; multi-building / multi-PIN
VLANs at access; L2/L3 boundary at distribution; FHRP + STP
L2/L3 boundary at access; ECMP, no FHRP, no STP on uplinks
VSS/SWV/StackWise + MEC; one logical switch, all links active

front.text: Define the hierarchical LAN design model in one sentence.

back.text: A modular network design that divides the enterprise LAN into distinct functional layers (access, distribution, and optionally core) so that each layer has a specific role and can be replicated as the network scales.

front.text: What is a 'building block' in hierarchical LAN design?

back.text: A repeatable, self-contained unit of the architecture (typically an access layer plus its distribution pair) that can be replicated as a unit to scale the network. Each building block has a consistent design and connects upward to the core or to a collapsed core.

front.text: What does 'place in the network' (PIN) mean?

back.text: A PIN is a logical area of the architecture defined by its role — for example, the campus PIN, branch PIN, data center PIN, or cloud PIN. Each PIN typically uses its own design pattern but connects to the rest of the enterprise through a common core.

front.text: Why does a hierarchical LAN design scale better than a flat, fully meshed network?

items: Adding capacity means adding another building block, not redesigning
Layer roles are predictable and consistent everywhere
Aggregation/summarization at distribution and core keeps protocol state bounded
Failures are contained inside a single block
Cabling complexity grows linearly (N) instead of N×(N-1)

front.text: How does the distribution layer separate the L2 access domain from the L3 core domain?

rows: Terminates STP, hosts VLAN SVIs, runs FHRP, applies access policy
Routed links, runs IGP, summarizes routes, ECMP toward core

front.text: Why should the core be kept as 'stable and simple' as possible?

back.text: The core carries traffic between every block in the enterprise. Any instability or complexity (frequent route changes, ACLs, NAT, complex policy) directly impacts every user. Keeping the core simple — fast L3 switching, ECMP, stable IGP design, no end-device attachments — minimizes change windows, reduces blast radius, and maximizes uptime.

front.text: What does the core layer interconnect in a large enterprise?

items: Multiple campus distribution blocks
Data center / private cloud block
WAN edge (private WAN, dedicated cloud interconnect)
Internet edge (public Internet, VPN, public cloud)
Network services edge (WLCs, ISE, CUCM, etc.)
Other geographically dispersed campus cores (in worldwide enterprises)

front.text: Why must distribution switches be deployed in pairs?

back.text: Because the distribution layer is the choke point for an entire access block. A single distribution switch would be a single point of failure for every user/endpoint connected through it. Deploying a pair (interconnected to each other and dual-homed to the core) eliminates that single point of failure.

front.text: Why is the link between the two distribution switches important, and what type of link is it?

back.text: The inter-distribution link (Layer 2 trunk or Layer 3 routed link, depending on design) lets traffic flow between the two distribution switches when an access uplink fails or during partial outages. Without it, traffic could black-hole when a host's uplink lands on the 'wrong' distribution switch relative to the active FHRP/L3 path.

front.text: Does every campus need all three hierarchical layers?

back.text: No. Small campuses may need only access + distribution (two-tier / collapsed core). Larger multi-building campuses typically need all three (access + distribution + core). The number of layers is driven by scale, geography, and the number of network blocks that must be interconnected.

front.text: Define redundancy in the context of network high availability.

back.text: The deliberate duplication of components, links, devices, or functions so that the failure of any single element does not bring down the service. In networking, redundancy applies at multiple levels: hardware (PSU, fans, RPs), links (dual uplinks), devices (paired distribution switches), and protocols (FHRP, BFD).

front.text: What is a route processor (RP), and how does it relate to a supervisor engine?

back.text: An RP is the brain of a router or modular switch — it runs the control plane (routing protocols, RIB, management) and, in centralized forwarding architectures, programs the FIB. On Cisco modular switch platforms (e.g., Catalyst 9600, Catalyst 6500/6800), the equivalent component is called a supervisor engine. A device with two RPs/supervisors uses one as active and the other as standby for HA.

front.text: What does SSO specifically prevent during a route processor switchover?

items: Interface flapping (links stay up)
Line cards reloading
Loss of L2 protocol state (CDP/LLDP/STP/EtherChannel/etc.)
Loss of running configuration

back.text: SSO does NOT preserve L3 routing-protocol adjacencies — that's what GR or NSR is for.

front.text: What does NSF specifically protect during an RP switchover?

back.text: The L3 forwarding plane. NSF keeps a checkpointed FIB on the standby RP so that, when it becomes active, packets continue to be forwarded using existing CEF entries while the routing protocols rebuild adjacencies and the RIB.

front.text: Which RFC defines Graceful Restart?

back.text: RFC 4724. GR is a standards-based mechanism that uses routing-protocol extensions to coordinate non-disruptive RP switchovers between a router and its neighbors.

front.text: What's the difference between GR-capable and GR-aware routers?

rows: Has dual RPs and runs SSO/NSF; performs the graceful restart itself
Supports GR routing-protocol extensions and helps a neighbor restart by preserving routes/adjacency state. Does not need dual RPs.

front.text: What happens if a neighbor of an SSO/NSF-capable router is GR-unaware?

back.text: When the GR-capable router's adjacency briefly drops during the RP switchover, the GR-unaware neighbor will treat it as a normal neighbor failure: it withdraws routes, reroutes traffic away from the failing router, and may cause traffic loss. To protect against this, use NSR on the GR-capable router instead of (or in addition to) GR.

front.text: What is the main downside of using NSR everywhere?

back.text: NSR continuously checkpoints all routing control-plane state (adjacencies, TCP sockets, etc.) from the active to the standby RP. This adds CPU and memory overhead, especially in scaled deployments with many neighbors. That's why the recommendation is to use GR where peers support it and reserve NSR for GR-unaware peers.

front.text: You enable SSO/NSF on a Cisco core router but observe traffic loss during testing of an RP switchover. Neighbors are running OSPF and don't support GR extensions. What HA option should you add?

back.text: Enable NSR on the Cisco router. Because the OSPF neighbors are GR-unaware, GR alone won't help. NSR keeps the OSPF adjacency up internally during the switchover, so neighbors never see it drop and never withdraw routes. SSO/NSF + NSR is the right combination here.

front.text: Where is BFD typically deployed in an enterprise design?

items: On core-to-core and core-to-distribution L3 links to speed up IGP convergence
On routed-access uplinks where sub-second failure detection is required
On WAN edge BGP peerings to detect provider failures faster than BGP keepalives
Between SD-WAN/MPLS routers to support fast TE/path protection
Anywhere routing protocols need detection faster than their native hellos

front.text: Compare UDLD normal mode vs aggressive mode.

rows: Marks the port as undetermined; does not necessarily err-disable the link
After repeated probe failures, err-disables the port to prevent black holes and STP loops

back.text: Aggressive mode is recommended on inter-switch fiber links, where unidirectional failures are common and dangerous.

front.text: Why does the simplified campus design (VSS/SWV) eliminate the need for an FHRP, while a traditional Layer 2 access design requires one?

back.text: In a traditional design, two physical distribution switches each have their own IP/MAC, and an FHRP creates a virtual gateway between them for hosts. In VSS/SWV, the two physical switches behave as one logical switch with a single SVI/IP/MAC for each VLAN — there's only one default gateway from the host's perspective, so there's nothing to fail over and no FHRP is needed.

front.text: Why are EIGRP and OSPF specifically called out as good routing protocols for routed access?

back.text: Both protocols can converge in well under a second when properly tuned, support ECMP, and scale to large campuses. They allow both access uplinks to be active (no FHRP, no STP), and provide consistent end-to-end troubleshooting using common tools (ping/traceroute/show ip route).

front.text: How does routed access use both uplinks at the same time?

back.text: By using equal-cost multipath (ECMP). Both Layer 3 uplinks from each access switch to the distribution pair have the same routing cost, so the access switch installs both as next hops and load-balances per-flow. There are no blocked links and no FHRP active/standby behavior.

front.text: Why is the simplified campus design described as logically a hub-and-spoke topology?

back.text: Because each access switch sees the distribution VSS/SWV pair as a single logical switch via one MEC bundle, and the core sees the distribution pair as a single neighbor as well. The physical mesh is hidden behind logical 'hubs,' which simplifies design, troubleshooting, and configuration.

front.text: Quick exam recall: which campus design supports spanning VLANs across multiple access switches without blocking links?

headers: VLANs Across Switches Without Blocked Links?
rows: Yes, but STP blocks a link
Yes — fully supported, no blocked links

front.text: When might routed access be a better fit than the simplified campus design?

back.text: When VLANs do not need to span access switches, when the organization wants the operational simplicity of a pure L3 design end-to-end, when the distribution platform doesn't support clustering, or when the team prefers troubleshooting with standard L3 tools (ping/traceroute/IGP) over managing a logical clustered switch.

front.text: What benefits do you get by applying VSS/SWV/StackWise across all campus blocks (access, distribution, and core)?

items: Logical hub-and-spoke topology end-to-end
All uplinks active — maximum aggregate bandwidth
No FHRP anywhere
Sub-second failover throughout the campus
Fewer logical devices to manage and patch
Consistent operational model across layers

front.text: At a high level, what two main components define an SD-Access deployment?

rows: Data plane (VXLAN), control plane (LISP), policy plane (SGT/CTS) overlay on the underlay network
Automation, orchestration, assurance, and policy management for the fabric

front.text: Final recap: list six factors you should evaluate when choosing a campus architecture.

items: Size / scale (users, switches, buildings, PINs)
Reliability and availability requirements (RTO, sub-second convergence)
Need for VLANs to span multiple access switches
Security and segmentation requirements
Operational simplicity vs. flexibility trade-offs
Budget and platform capabilities (L3 access? Clustering support? SD-Access readiness?)

front.text: What is a fabric network?

back.text: A fabric network is an overlay (virtual) network built over an underlay (physical) network using overlay tunneling technologies such as VXLAN. It enables host mobility, automation, network virtualization, and segmentation while being more manageable, flexible, secure, and scalable than traditional networks.

front.text: What are the two main components of SD-Access?

items: Cisco Campus fabric solution (control, data, management, and policy planes)
Cisco DNA Center (now Catalyst Center) for automation and management

back.text: When the campus fabric is managed via CLI/NETCONF/YANG it is just 'campus fabric'. When managed by DNA Center, it is SD-Access.

front.text: What are the four layers of the Cisco SD-Access architecture?

items: Physical Layer — switches, routers, WLCs, APs, and controller appliances
Network Layer — underlay and overlay networks
Controller Layer — Cisco DNA Center (NCP, NDP) and Cisco ISE
Management Layer — UI/UX dashboard for design, policy, provision, and assurance

front.text: What is the recommended underlay design for SD-Access and which IGP is preferred?

back.text: A Layer 3 routed access campus design using IS-IS as the IGP.
IS-IS is preferred because it establishes neighbors without IP dependencies, peers using loopback addresses, and treats IPv4, IPv6, and non-IP traffic agnostically.

front.text: Compare a manual underlay vs. an automated underlay in SD-Access.
What protocols/technologies are used by the SD-Access control, data, and policy planes?
How does LISP simplify routing in SD-Access?

back.text: LISP separates the endpoint identity (EID) from its location (RLOC). Instead of every router carrying full routing information for all destinations, remote destination info is held in a centralized mapping database (LISP Map Server).
Routers maintain only their local routes and query the map system to resolve destination EIDs, providing smaller routing tables, host mobility, and address-agnostic mappings (IPv4, IPv6, MAC).

front.text: Why does SD-Access use VXLAN encapsulation for the data plane instead of LISP encapsulation?

back.text: VXLAN can encapsulate the original Ethernet header (MAC-in-IP), enabling support for both Layer 2 and Layer 3 virtual topologies. LISP encapsulation cannot carry the original Ethernet header.
VXLAN is IP/UDP based, so it works over any IP network and provides built-in segmentation (VRF/VN) and group-based policy support.

front.text: What is VXLAN-GPO and what fields were added to support SD-Access?

back.text: VXLAN Group Policy Option (VXLAN-GPO) is the enhanced VXLAN format used by SD-Access to carry Cisco TrustSec SGT tags. It can transport up to 64,000 SGT tags.

items: Group Policy ID (16-bit) — carries the SGT tag
G Bit — set to 1 when an SGT tag is present in the Group Policy ID field
D Bit — when set, egress VTEP must not learn the source address
A Bit — when set, indicates policy already applied; do not re-apply

front.text: How does Cisco TrustSec enforce policy in SD-Access?

back.text: TrustSec assigns Scalable Group Tags (SGTs) to authenticated groups of users or devices. Network policies (ACLs, QoS, PBR, segmentation) are enforced based on the SGT tag rather than IP/MAC addresses.

This allows network-address-independent, location-independent group-based policies for both wired and wireless traffic, and policies can be extended to external networks via SGT Exchange Protocol (SXP).

front.text: What are the five basic device roles in the SD-Access fabric overlay?

items: Control Plane Node — maintains EID-to-RLOC mappings (LISP MS/MR)
Fabric Border Node — connects external L3 networks to the fabric (LISP PxTR)
Fabric Edge Node — connects wired endpoints to the fabric (LISP xTR)
Fabric WLC — connects APs and wireless endpoints to the fabric
Intermediate Node — provides only underlay services (no fabric role)

front.text: What functions does a fabric edge node perform?

items: Authenticates wired endpoints (e.g., 802.1X)
Assigns endpoints to host pools (SVI/VRF) and SGTs
Registers EIDs (MAC, /32 IPv4, /128 IPv6) with the control plane node
Provides anycast Layer 3 default gateway for connected endpoints
Performs VXLAN encapsulation/de-encapsulation
Enforces group-based policy for traffic to fabric endpoints

front.text: Compare the three types of SD-Access fabric border nodes.

rows: Known internal areas only (WLC, firewall, data center)
Unknown external areas (Internet, public cloud) using a default route
Both transit/known and unknown areas — combines both functions

front.text: How does SD-Access wireless differ from a traditional centralized WLC deployment?

rows: Distributed via VXLAN from AP to fabric edge
At the fabric edge (same as wired)

front.text: What is an Anycast Gateway in SD-Access?

back.text: A pervasive Layer 3 default gateway where the same SVI (with the same IP and MAC address) is provisioned on every fabric edge node.
This stretches an IP subnet across the fabric so endpoints can move between edge nodes without changing their IP or default gateway — the fabric behaves like one large logical switch.

front.text: What are the three main controller subsystems in SD-Access?

items: Cisco Network Control Platform (NCP) — automation/orchestration via NETCONF/YANG, SNMP, SSH/Telnet (in DNA Center)
Cisco Network Data Platform (NDP) — data collection, analytics, and assurance using NetFlow, SPAN, etc. (in DNA Center)
Cisco Identity Services Engine (ISE) — identity/policy services, NAC via 802.1X, MAB, WebAuth; programs group-based policies

front.text: What are the four primary workflows in Cisco DNA Center (Catalyst Center)?

items: Design — network hierarchy, settings, image repository, profiles
Policy — group-based access control, VNs, QoS, traffic copy
Provision — assign devices, fabric domains, host onboarding
Assurance — health dashboards, Client/Device 360, issues

front.text: What are the four mandatory components of the Cisco SD-WAN (Viptela) solution?

items: SD-WAN edge devices (cEdge/vEdge routers) — data plane
vManage NMS — single pane of glass management
vSmart controller — control plane (OMP routes and policies)
vBond orchestrator — authentication and orchestration

back.text: vAnalytics is an OPTIONAL analytics and assurance service.

front.text: What are the main functions of the vBond orchestrator?

items: Authentication — authenticates every device joining the SD-WAN fabric using certificates and RSA
NAT detection — uses STUN (RFC 5389) to detect devices behind NAT
Load balancing — balances sessions across multiple vSmart/vManage controllers
Orchestrates initial connectivity between edge devices, vSmart, and vManage

front.text: What protocol does the vSmart controller use with SD-WAN edge devices, and what does it do?

back.text: vSmart uses Overlay Management Protocol (OMP) over a permanent DTLS tunnel with each edge device. OMP is a Cisco proprietary routing protocol similar to BGP.

OMP advertises routes, next hops, encryption keys, and policy information needed to establish and maintain the SD-WAN fabric. vSmart also enforces all centralized control-plane policies (topology, VPN membership, AAR, traffic data).

front.text: Compare cEdge vs. vEdge routers in Cisco SD-WAN.

rows: Supported on most platforms (IOS XE containers)

front.text: What is the sequence by which an SD-WAN edge device joins the fabric?

items: Edge locates vBond by IP or FQDN
Edge authenticates to vBond using certificates/RSA over DTLS
vBond performs NAT detection (STUN) on each transport
vBond directs the edge to the appropriate vManage and vSmart
Edge tears down the vBond session and establishes DTLS tunnels with vManage and vSmart
Edge forms OMP neighborships with vSmart to exchange routes
Edge establishes IPsec tunnels with other edge devices to form the data plane

front.text: Compare Local Policy vs. Centralized Policy in Cisco SD-WAN.

rows: ACLs, QoS, routing policies, on-device security
Topology, VPN membership, AAR, traffic data policies
Control plane via OMP; data plane in edge volatile memory

front.text: What is Application-Aware Routing (AAR), and what mechanism does it use to measure tunnel quality?

back.text: AAR is a data plane policy that forwards application traffic across a tunnel that meets defined SLA thresholds for loss, latency, and jitter. If the preferred transport exceeds those thresholds, AAR moves traffic to a different transport.
AAR uses Bidirectional Forwarding Detection (BFD) probes within SD-WAN tunnels to measure packet loss, latency, jitter, and IPsec MTU.

front.text: How does Cloud OnRamp for SaaS measure path quality, and what is vQoE?

back.text: CoR for SaaS does not use BFD because there is no SD-WAN device on the SaaS side. Instead, edge devices send small HTTP probes to the SaaS application across each available path to measure latency and loss.

Path quality is reported as a Viptela Quality of Experience (vQoE) score from 0 (worst) to 10 (best), viewable in vManage. The path with the highest vQoE for a given application is selected on a per-application basis.

front.text: Compare DIA, Gateway, and Client site types in Cloud OnRamp for SaaS.

rows: Site with direct Internet circuit(s) — sends SaaS traffic directly to the Internet
Regional hub with Internet access; reports HTTP loss/latency to remote sites via OMP so they can compare paths
Site without local Internet (e.g., dual MPLS); chooses the best gateway path based on vQoE

front.text: A wired client authenticates via 802.1X at a fabric edge but cannot reach an endpoint at another edge node in the same VN. What SD-Access components/registrations should you verify?

items: Verify the edge node registered the client EID (MAC, /32, /128) with the control plane (LISP MS).
Confirm both edges resolve EID-to-RLOC mappings from the control plane node.
Check VXLAN tunnel reachability between source and destination RLOCs (underlay routing health).
Validate the same VN (LISP instance ID/VNI) is used at both edges and the anycast SVI is consistent.
Confirm SGT assignment and that no SGACL is denying the traffic between the two scalable groups.

front.text: An SD-WAN edge router has reachability to vBond but cannot establish OMP with vSmart. What should you investigate?

items: Confirm the certificate chain and root CA are valid on edge, vBond, vSmart, and vManage.
Verify the device is authorized in vManage (valid serial number list pushed to controllers).
Confirm the DTLS tunnel to vSmart establishes (firewall/NAT may be blocking the control plane port).
Check that vBond is reachable on every transport — NAT detection must succeed on each circuit.
Verify the edge has been directed to the correct vSmart by vBond and that vSmart has spare control connection capacity.
Use 'show control connections' and 'show control connection-history' on the edge for failure reasons.

front.text: A branch has both MPLS and Internet transports. Voice traffic must use MPLS, but if MPLS exceeds 1% loss or 150 ms latency, voice should fail over to Internet. What feature implements this and how?

back.text: Use an Application-Aware Routing (AAR) policy configured on vManage and pushed via vSmart.

items: Define an SLA class with loss ≤ 1% and latency ≤ 150 ms.
Match voice traffic (e.g., DSCP EF or app-list).
Set MPLS as the preferred transport with the SLA class applied.
BFD probes continuously measure each tunnel; if MPLS exceeds the SLA, AAR re-routes voice to the Internet tunnel automatically.

front.text: Compare a Virtual Network (VN), Host Pool, and Scalable Group in SD-Access.

rows: VRF instance + LISP instance ID + VXLAN VNID
SVI per host pool on edge nodes (anycast gateway)
Micro-segmentation of endpoints with similar policy
TrustSec SGT carried in VXLAN-GPO header; enforced via SGACL

back.text: There is a one-to-one relationship between host pools and scalable groups, and scalable groups operate within a VN by default.

front.text: Exam Tip: Memorize the SD-Access plane-to-protocol mapping. What does the exam expect?

back.text: If you see a question asking 'what is the control plane of SD-Access?' the answer is LISP — not VXLAN.

front.text: Exam Tip: Why VXLAN over LISP encapsulation in SD-Access?

back.text: VXLAN supports Layer 2 encapsulation (MAC-in-IP), allowing SD-Access to support Layer 2 overlays. LISP encapsulation does not carry the original Ethernet header.
Common distractor: 'VXLAN supports IPv6' — both do. The correct reason is L2 (MAC-in-IP) support.

front.text: Exam Tip: What is the standard VXLAN header vs. the SD-Access VXLAN header?

back.text: SD-Access uses VXLAN-GPO (Group Policy Option), which is NOT identical to the original VXLAN header. New fields were added in the first 4 bytes to carry SGT tags.

items: Group Policy ID (16-bit) — carries the SGT
G Bit — indicates SGT is present
D Bit — Don't Learn
A Bit — Policy Already Applied

back.text: If asked 'what field carries the SGT in VXLAN-GPO?' the answer is Group Policy ID.

front.text: Exam Tip: What environments was SD-Access designed for?

back.text: Enterprise CAMPUS and BRANCH only.
SD-Access is NOT designed for data center, service provider, WAN, or cloud environments. Watch for these distractors on the exam.

front.text: Exam Tip: Which devices are part of the SD-Access fabric architecture?

items: Cisco switches (wired access)
Cisco routers (WAN/branch)
Cisco WLCs
Cisco APs
Cisco DNA Center (Catalyst Center)
Cisco ISE

back.text: Firewalls and IPS systems are NOT part of the SD-Access fabric architecture — they may be distractors.

front.text: Exam Tip: Which LISP roles map to which SD-Access fabric nodes?
Exam Tip: What is the recommended underlay IGP for SD-Access automated underlay?

back.text: IS-IS — used by DNA Center LAN Automation.
If you see OSPF or EIGRP as the answer for an automated SD-Access underlay, it's likely a distractor. Manual underlay can use other IGPs, but automated must be IS-IS.

front.text: Exam Tip: How is wireless data plane traffic carried in SD-Access vs. traditional CAPWAP?

back.text: SD-Access: AP encapsulates wireless client traffic in VXLAN directly to the fabric edge — distributed data plane.
Traditional: AP tunnels everything to a centralized WLC via CAPWAP — centralized data plane.
Remember: SD-Access wireless control plane stays centralized at the WLC, but the data plane is distributed via VXLAN.

front.text: Exam Tip: Campus Fabric vs. SD-Access — what's the difference?

back.text: Same underlying technology. The differentiator is DNA Center management. No DNA Center = not SD-Access.

front.text: Exam Tip: What are the three types of border nodes? Memorize this.

items: Internal Border — connects to KNOWN internal areas (WLC, firewall, DC)
Default Border — connects to UNKNOWN external areas (Internet, public cloud); has default route
Internal + Default Border — connects to BOTH known and unknown (anywhere)

front.text: Exam Tip: How does anycast gateway work in SD-Access?

back.text: Every fabric edge node has the SAME SVI with the SAME IP address and SAME MAC address for a given host pool.
This allows endpoints to roam between edges without changing their IP or default gateway. The fabric behaves like one giant logical switch.

front.text: Exam Tip: SGT vs. Scalable Group Tag — are they the same?

back.text: Yes. In Cisco TrustSec they are called Security Group Tags. In SD-Access they are referred to as Scalable Group Tags.
Both use the same SGT acronym. Don't get tripped up by the different names — they refer to the same tag.

front.text: Exam Tip: What are the four DNA Center workflows you must memorize?

items: Design
Policy
Provision
Assurance

back.text: Mnemonic: 'DPPA' — Design, Policy, Provision, Assurance.

front.text: Exam Tip: NCP vs. NDP vs. ISE — know your DNA Center subsystems.

rows: Identity and policy (NAC, 802.1X, MAB, WebAuth, SGT)

front.text: Exam Tip: What are the FOUR mandatory SD-WAN components?

items: vManage NMS
vSmart controller
vBond orchestrator
SD-WAN routers (cEdge/vEdge)

back.text: vAnalytics is OPTIONAL — not mandatory. Cisco ISE and DNA Center are NOT SD-WAN components (they belong to SD-Access).

front.text: Exam Tip: What is the 'single pane of glass' in SD-WAN?

back.text: vManage.
Common distractors: vSmart (control plane), vBond (orchestration/auth), DNA Center (SD-Access — wrong solution).

front.text: Exam Tip: What is the main function of the vBond orchestrator?

back.text: Authenticates the vSmart controllers and the SD-WAN routers and orchestrates connectivity between them.
Mnemonic: vBond = Bouncer. It authenticates devices at the door and tells them where to go (vSmart/vManage).

front.text: Exam Tip: Does vSmart establish IPsec tunnels with SD-WAN routers?

back.text: FALSE. vSmart establishes permanent DTLS (or TLS) tunnels — NOT IPsec — with SD-WAN routers.
IPsec is used between SD-WAN routers (data plane), not between routers and vSmart (control plane).

front.text: Exam Tip: Does SD-WAN only work over the Internet or MPLS?

back.text: FALSE. SD-WAN is transport-independent. It works over any IP-based network: Internet, MPLS, 3G/4G LTE, satellite, dedicated circuits, etc.

front.text: Exam Tip: What is OMP and what does it do?

back.text: Overlay Management Protocol — Cisco proprietary routing protocol similar to BGP, used between vSmart and SD-WAN edge routers over DTLS.
OMP advertises routes, next hops, encryption keys, and policy information.
Think 'BGP for the SD-WAN overlay'.

front.text: Exam Tip: SD-WAN persona summary — what does each persona do?

rows: Forwards traffic, IPsec to other edges

front.text: Exam Tip: What protocol does vBond use to detect NAT?

back.text: STUN — Session Traversal Utilities for NAT (RFC 5389).
vBond should ideally NOT be behind NAT. If it must be, use a 1:1 static NAT.

front.text: Exam Tip: AAR uses what protocol to measure tunnel quality, and what does it measure?

back.text: Application-Aware Routing uses BFD (Bidirectional Forwarding Detection) probes inside SD-WAN tunnels.

items: Packet loss
Latency
Jitter
IPsec tunnel MTU
Path liveliness (up/down)

front.text: Exam Tip: How does Cloud OnRamp for SaaS measure path quality (since BFD can't reach SaaS)?

back.text: HTTP probes — small probes from edge devices toward the SaaS app to measure latency and loss.
Quality is reported as a vQoE score (0–10, higher is better) viewable in vManage.
Don't confuse: AAR uses BFD inside SD-WAN tunnels. CoR for SaaS uses HTTP probes outside the fabric.

front.text: Exam Tip: Local Policy vs. Centralized Policy — where is each pushed?

rows: ACLs, QoS, routing policies, on-device security
Topology, VPN membership, AAR, traffic data

back.text: Centralized policies are processed by vSmart before OMP advertises routes to edges.

front.text: Exam Tip: VN, Host Pool, Scalable Group — how do they relate?

back.text: 1:1 relationship — host pools and scalable groups operate within a VN by default.

front.text: Exam Tip: What is the SD-Access map server / map resolver based on?

back.text: LISP MS/MR — defined in RFC 6830.
It maps endpoint identifiers (EIDs) to routing locators (RLOCs) and runs on the SD-Access control plane node.

front.text: Exam Tip: SGACLs vs. ACLs — what's the policy enforcement difference in SD-Access?
Exam Tip: CoR for SaaS site classifications — DIA, Gateway, Client.

rows: Local Internet — sends SaaS traffic directly out its own circuit
Regional hub with Internet — reports HTTP probe results to remote sites via OMP
No local Internet — picks the best gateway based on vQoE

front.text: Exam Tip: Quick recall — match the SD-WAN persona to its job.

items: vManage = Manage (GUI / NMS / single pane of glass)
vSmart = Smart (brains — OMP, policies, control plane)
vBond = Bond (introduces and authenticates devices, NAT detection)
cEdge/vEdge = Edge (data plane, forwards user traffic)

back.text: If a question asks about advertising routes → vSmart. About authentication → vBond. About a GUI/dashboard → vManage. About forwarding traffic → edge router.

front.text: What is a fabric network, and what tunneling technology is typically used to build it?

back.text: A fabric network is an overlay network (a virtual network/VN) built on top of an underlay (physical) network using overlay tunneling technologies such as VXLAN. Fabrics enable host mobility, automation, virtualization, and segmentation, and are more manageable, flexible, secure, and scalable than traditional networks.

front.text: Which network environments was Cisco SD-Access designed for?

back.text: SD-Access was designed for enterprise campus and branch network environments. It was NOT designed for data center, service provider, or WAN environments.
Exam tip: A common distractor is choosing 'data center' or 'WAN' — SD-Access is campus/branch only. SD-WAN handles WAN; ACI handles data center.

front.text: What is the difference between a 'campus fabric' solution and an 'SD-Access' solution?

back.text: When the campus fabric is managed via Cisco DNA Center, it is called SD-Access. The protocols are the same — only the management approach changes.

front.text: What are the four basic layers of the Cisco SD-Access fabric architecture?

items: Physical Layer — switches, routers, WLCs, APs, controller appliances
Network Layer — underlay + overlay (fabric)
Controller Layer — Cisco DNA Center (NCP, NDP) and Cisco ISE
Management Layer — UI/UX dashboard for design, policy, provision, assurance

front.text: What is the recommended underlay design for SD-Access, and which IGP is preferred?

back.text: The recommended underlay for SD-Access is a Layer 3 routed access campus design using IS-IS as the IGP. Layer 2 designs running STP are supported but NOT recommended.

items: IS-IS forms neighbors without IP dependencies
IS-IS can peer using loopback addresses
IS-IS is agnostic to IPv4, IPv6, and non-IP traffic

back.text: Exam tip: If you see 'recommended SD-Access underlay IGP' on the exam, the answer is IS-IS — even though OSPF can be configured manually.

front.text: Compare the manual underlay vs. automated underlay models in SD-Access.
What are the three planes of operation in the SD-Access fabric and what protocol underpins each?

back.text: Exam tip: Memorize this triad — LISP / VXLAN / TrustSec map to control / data / policy. It appears repeatedly in ENCOR questions.

front.text: How does LISP simplify routing in SD-Access, and what do EID and RLOC represent?

back.text: LISP separates an endpoint's identity from its location:

items: EID (Endpoint Identifier) = the endpoint's IP address (its identity)
RLOC (Routing Locator) = the IP address of the fabric edge/border router (its current location)

back.text: Remote destination info is moved to a centralized mapping database — the LISP Map Server (control plane node). Routers only need local routes and query the map system to find destination EIDs, resulting in smaller routing tables, native host mobility, and address-agnostic mappings (IPv4/IPv6/MAC).

front.text: Why does SD-Access use VXLAN rather than LISP for data-plane encapsulation?

back.text: VXLAN can encapsulate the original Ethernet header (MAC-in-IP), so it supports Layer 2 overlays. LISP data encapsulation cannot — it does not carry the original Ethernet header.

items: VXLAN supports both Layer 2 and Layer 3 virtual topologies
VXLAN can run over any IP-based network
VXLAN-GPO carries built-in segmentation (VNID = VRF) and group-based policy (SGT)

back.text: Exam tip: The exact reason on the exam is 'VXLAN supports Layer 2 networks' — that is the single best answer.

front.text: What is VXLAN-GPO, and which new fields were added compared to standard VXLAN?

back.text: VXLAN Group Policy Option (VXLAN-GPO) is the SD-Access enhancement to standard VXLAN that adds fields to the first 4 bytes of the VXLAN header to carry TrustSec SGTs (up to 64,000 tags). Defined in IETF draft draft-smith-vxlan-group-policy-05.

rows: 1 = SGT is present in Group Policy ID
1 = egress VTEP must not learn source address
1 = policy already applied; do not re-apply

front.text: How does Cisco TrustSec enforce policy in the SD-Access fabric, and what advantage does it have over traditional ACLs?

back.text: TrustSec assigns Scalable Group Tags (SGTs) to authenticated users/devices. Policy (security, QoS, PBR, segmentation) is applied based on the SGT — not on MAC/IPv4/IPv6 addresses.

items: Policy is decoupled from network addressing — simpler and more scalable
Enforcement is dynamic and follows the user (wired or wireless)
Works over legacy/third-party networks via VXLAN-GPO
Extends to external networks (cloud, data center) via SXP (SGT Exchange Protocol)

front.text: What are the five basic device roles in the SD-Access fabric overlay?

items: Control Plane Node — LISP map server/resolver; holds EID-to-RLOC mappings
Fabric Border Node — connects external L3 networks (LISP PxTR)
Fabric Edge Node — connects wired endpoints (LISP xTR); anycast gateway
Fabric WLC — connects APs and wireless endpoints to the fabric
Intermediate Node — pure underlay transport; no fabric role

back.text: Exam tip: A single physical device can play multiple roles (e.g., a core switch acting as both border and control plane node).

front.text: What functions does a fabric edge node perform in SD-Access?

items: Acts as a LISP tunnel router (xTR)
Provides the anycast gateway (same SVI/IP/MAC across all edges)
Authenticates wired endpoints (e.g., 802.1X)
Assigns endpoints to host pools (SVI/VRF) and scalable groups (SGT)
Registers EIDs (MAC, /32 IPv4, /128 IPv6) with the control plane node
Performs VXLAN encapsulation/de-encapsulation for connected endpoints
Enforces group-based policy for traffic to fabric endpoints

front.text: What are the three types of SD-Access fabric border nodes and how do they differ?

rows: Known areas only — WLC, firewall, data center
Specific routes are known to the control plane
Unknown external areas — Internet, public cloud
Configured with a default route; destinations are not in the control plane
Combines both functions in one node

front.text: How does SD-Access fabric wireless differ from a traditional centralized WLC deployment?

rows: Distributed — VXLAN from AP to fabric edge
Must connect to fabric edge or extended node

back.text: Distributing the data plane via VXLAN improves performance and scalability because client traffic does not have to traverse the WLC; the fabric edge makes the routing decision.

front.text: What is the SD-Access anycast gateway and what does it enable?

back.text: The anycast gateway is a pervasive Layer 3 default gateway provisioned with the SAME SVI IP and MAC address on every fabric edge node. It allows an IP subnet to be stretched across the entire fabric so an endpoint can move between edge nodes (or even buildings) without changing its IP address or default gateway.
The fabric behaves like one giant logical switch spanning the campus.

front.text: Compare Virtual Network (VN), host pool, and scalable group in SD-Access.

rows: VRF instance + LISP instance ID + VXLAN VNID
IP subnet for endpoints with shared default gateway
SVI on every edge node; assigned via 802.1X or static port
SGT tag carried in VXLAN-GPO; enforced by SGACLs

back.text: Scalable groups operate within a VN; there is a one-to-one relationship between host pools and scalable groups.

front.text: What are the three main controller subsystems of the SD-Access controller layer, and what does each do?

rows: DNA Center subsystem for underlay/fabric automation & orchestration via NETCONF/YANG, SNMP, SSH/Telnet
DNA Center subsystem for analytics & assurance — collects from NetFlow, SPAN, etc.; correlates events
Identity & policy services — 802.1X, MAB, WebAuth; assigns endpoints to scalable groups; programs policy on devices

front.text: List the four primary Cisco DNA Center workflows, in the order they are typically used.

items: Design — network hierarchy, settings, image repository, profiles
Policy — VNs, scalable groups, group-based access control, app QoS, ERSPAN
Provision — assign devices to sites, set up fabric domains, define fabric roles, host onboarding
Assurance — monitor health, troubleshoot Client 360 / Devices 360, manage issues

front.text: A wired client connected to an SD-Access fabric edge can authenticate via 802.1X but cannot reach hosts on other fabric edges. The underlay routing is verified healthy. What is the most likely cause?

back.text: Most likely the fabric edge is not registering the client's EID with the control plane node, or the control plane node is not reachable / has stale mappings. Without EID-to-RLOC registration, other edges cannot resolve the destination and VXLAN encapsulation will fail.

items: Verify the edge has reachability to the control plane node's loopback (RLOC)
Check LISP registration state for the EID on the edge
Verify the control plane node's host tracking database contains the EID
Confirm the client's host pool/VN is provisioned consistently across edges

front.text: Why are enterprises adopting SD-WAN over traditional MPLS/DMVPN/IWAN designs?

items: Centralize device configuration and network management
Lower cost and risk via WAN automation/orchestration
Seamlessly extend the enterprise into public clouds (AWS, Azure)
Optimize SaaS application user experience (Office 365, Salesforce)
Use any transport (Internet, MPLS, 4G/LTE, satellite) — transport-independent
Enhanced application visibility and intelligent path control for SLAs
End-to-end segmentation and encryption

front.text: What are the four mandatory components of the Cisco SD-WAN (Viptela) solution, and the one optional component?

rows: Single pane of glass GUI; configuration, monitoring, APIs
Distributes routes/policies via OMP over DTLS
Authenticates and orchestrates initial connectivity; NAT detection

back.text: Exam tip: Memorize 'vManage / vSmart / vBond / SD-WAN routers' as the four mandatory components — vAnalytics is optional.

front.text: What is the main function of the vBond orchestrator in Cisco SD-WAN?

back.text: The vBond authenticates the vSmart controllers and the SD-WAN routers (edge devices) and orchestrates connectivity between them. As a device comes online, it must authenticate to vBond, which determines its eligibility to join the fabric and directs it to the appropriate vSmart and vManage.

items: Authentication via certificates and RSA cryptography
NAT detection using STUN (RFC 5389)
Load balancing of sessions across multiple vSmarts/vManages
Maintains permanent DTLS control connection with every vSmart

front.text: What is OMP, and how does it relate to the vSmart controller?

back.text: Overlay Management Protocol (OMP) is a Cisco-proprietary routing protocol similar to BGP. It runs inside DTLS tunnels between vSmart controllers and SD-WAN edge devices to advertise routes, next hops, encryption keys, and policy information needed to build/maintain the SD-WAN fabric.

vSmart processes OMP routes from edges (and other vSmarts) and re-advertises reachability to the rest of the fabric. It also pushes control-plane policies (topologies, service chaining, VPN segmentation) and translates vManage policies into something edges can enforce.

front.text: What types of tunnels are used between SD-WAN components, and between edges themselves?

rows: Temporary DTLS for auth/NAT detection, then torn down

back.text: Exam tip: vSmart does NOT keep permanent IPsec to every edge — it uses DTLS. Data-plane IPsec is built directly between edges.

front.text: What is the difference between vEdge and cEdge routers in Cisco SD-WAN?

rows: Full security stack (URL filtering, IPS, etc.)

front.text: Compare local policy and centralized policy in Cisco SD-WAN.

rows: Applied at vSmart (control plane) / sent to edge memory (data plane)
ACLs, QoS, routing policies, on-device security
Topology, VPN membership, AAR, traffic-data policies
Volatile memory (data plane) / processed at vSmart (control)

back.text: Centralized policy components include Topology, VPN Membership (control plane), Application-Aware Routing, and Traffic Data (data plane).

front.text: How does Application-Aware Routing (AAR) work in Cisco SD-WAN, and what role does BFD play?

back.text: AAR uses Bidirectional Forwarding Detection (BFD) probes inside the SD-WAN tunnels to continuously measure each tunnel's loss, latency, jitter, and IPsec MTU. Path selection is then based on whether a tunnel meets the SLA defined for an application — not just on traditional metrics like bandwidth or hop count.

items: BFD measures real-time path quality (up/down + loss/latency/jitter)
AAR can prefer one transport (e.g., MPLS) but fail over if it breaches thresholds
Provides SLA-driven forwarding for business-critical apps during brownouts/soft failures

back.text: Exam tip: AAR = data-plane policy; BFD = the measurement engine. CoR for SaaS uses HTTP probes instead because there's no edge on the SaaS side.

front.text: A branch site has dual DIA Internet circuits (ISP1, ISP2) and uses Cloud OnRamp for SaaS for Office 365. ISP1 has high latency today. Walk through how CoR for SaaS chooses the path.

items: The branch edge sends small HTTP probes to the SaaS app over BOTH ISP1 and ISP2 circuits
It measures loss and latency on each path
Each path is given a Viptela Quality of Experience (vQoE) score from 0–10
ISP2 (better metrics) gets the higher vQoE score for that application
The edge forwards Office 365 traffic out ISP2 — decision is per-application
Probing continues; if ISP2 degrades, the edge re-evaluates and may switch back

back.text: A site forwarding SaaS traffic directly to the Internet is classified as a CoR DIA site. CoR for SaaS can also load-balance across circuits with similar scores.

front.text: A new SD-WAN edge device is staged and powered up but never appears in vManage. The device has Internet reachability and the certificate is valid. What is the first thing to verify?

back.text: Verify that the edge can resolve and reach the vBond orchestrator. The edge MUST authenticate to vBond first — vBond then directs it to the appropriate vSmart and vManage.

items: Confirm DNS resolution of the vBond FQDN (preferred) or its IP
Check that UDP/TCP traffic to vBond is not blocked by upstream firewalls/NAT
If vBond is behind NAT, ensure 1:1 static NAT is configured (vBond behind NAT is not recommended)
Verify the device's certificate chain matches what vBond expects
On the edge, check control connection state to vBond before troubleshooting vSmart/vManage

back.text: Exam tip: Onboarding flow is always 'edge → vBond → (vSmart + vManage)'. If vBond auth fails, nothing else proceeds.

front.text: What problems in traditional campus networks does SD-Access aim to solve?

items: Slow, error-prone manual configuration of each device
Inconsistent policies between wired and wireless networks
Difficulty maintaining user credentials and policy at scale
Hard to locate and troubleshoot mobile users
Security vulnerabilities from policy gaps
ACL-based policies that don't scale (tied to IPs/subnets)

front.text: List the seven main capabilities/features that SD-Access brings to a campus network.

items: Network automation — single-point provisioning via Cisco DNA Center
Network assurance and analytics — telemetry-driven prediction
Host mobility — wired and wireless
Identity services — via Cisco ISE
Policy enforcement — group-based via SGACLs
Secure segmentation — guest, corporate, IoT, facilities
Network virtualization — VRFs as Virtual Networks (VNs)

front.text: What is an SD-Access extension node?

back.text: An SD-Access extension node is a Cisco access-layer switch that does NOT actively participate in the SD-Access fabric (it lacks the required ASIC/FPGA support) but is part of the SD-Access deployment because it is automated and managed through DNA Center.
Exam tip: Extension nodes provide fabric reach to access ports without being true fabric nodes — they hang off a fabric edge.

front.text: What is the difference between the underlay and overlay networks in SD-Access?

rows: Virtual network interconnecting all fabric nodes

back.text: The fabric overlay is always automated by DNA Center, regardless of whether the underlay is manual or automated.

front.text: What is the LAN Automation feature in DNA Center, and what does it deploy?

back.text: LAN Automation is a Cisco DNA Center feature that builds a fully automated SD-Access underlay. It uses Cisco Network Plug and Play (PnP) to discover devices and deploys an IS-IS routed access campus design with both unicast and multicast routing configured for efficient overlay delivery.

items: Eliminates misconfigurations
Greatly speeds underlay buildout
Trade-off: no manual customization for special designs

front.text: What is the LISP Map Server (MS), and what role does it play in SD-Access?

back.text: The LISP Map Server is a centralized mapping database that stores EID-to-RLOC mappings. In SD-Access it is implemented by the control plane node. Edge and border nodes register their local EIDs with the MS and query it to resolve destination EIDs to RLOCs (the IP of the destination edge/border).
Exam tip: Combined map-server/map-resolver = MS/MR. The control plane node performs both functions.

front.text: What enhancements has Cisco added to the original LISP specification for SD-Access?

items: Distributed Anycast Gateway — same SVI/IP/MAC on every fabric edge
VN Extranet — controlled inter-VRF leaking between virtual networks
Fabric Wireless integration — APs build VXLAN directly to fabric edges
Address-agnostic mappings (IPv4, IPv6, MAC)

front.text: What kind of encapsulation does VXLAN use, and what is a VTEP?

back.text: VXLAN performs MAC-in-IP/UDP encapsulation. The original Ethernet frame is wrapped inside a VXLAN header, then UDP, then an outer IP header so it can traverse any IP-based network.
A VTEP (Virtual Tunnel Endpoint) is the device that performs VXLAN encap/decap. In SD-Access, fabric edge nodes (and borders) act as VTEPs.

front.text: What does each of the G, D, and A bits in the VXLAN-GPO header indicate?

rows: An SGT is being carried in the Group Policy ID field
Egress VTEP must NOT learn the source address of the encapsulated frame
Group policy already applied; downstream nodes must not re-apply

back.text: Exam tip: A bit is only valid when G bit = 1. Network devices set A=1 after applying policy so it isn't enforced twice.

front.text: How many SGT tags can VXLAN-GPO transport, and in which field?

back.text: VXLAN-GPO can transport up to 64,000 SGT tags using the 16-bit Group Policy ID field added to the first 4 bytes of the VXLAN header.

front.text: What is SXP, and why is it used with Cisco TrustSec?

back.text: SXP (SGT Exchange Protocol) is a TrustSec control protocol that propagates IP-to-SGT mappings between TrustSec-aware devices when inline SGT tagging is not possible (e.g., across non-TrustSec capable hops or to external networks like cloud and data center).
Exam tip: SXP extends TrustSec policy enforcement beyond the SD-Access fabric.

front.text: Why is the fabric edge node referred to as a LISP xTR?

back.text: An xTR is a LISP tunnel router that performs both ITR (Ingress Tunnel Router) and ETR (Egress Tunnel Router) functions. The fabric edge encapsulates traffic from connected endpoints (ITR role) and decapsulates traffic destined for them (ETR role), so it operates as both — hence xTR.

front.text: What is a LISP PxTR, and how does it relate to the SD-Access fabric border node?

back.text: A PxTR (Proxy xTR) is a LISP tunnel router that acts as a proxy for non-LISP networks — it ingresses/egresses LISP traffic on behalf of devices that don't speak LISP. SD-Access fabric border nodes are PxTRs: they connect external L3 networks to the fabric and translate reachability and policy info (VRFs, SGTs) between domains.

front.text: Why is platform selection important for the SD-Access control plane node, and where can it be deployed?

back.text: The control plane node maintains all endpoint mappings (EID-to-RLOC) for the entire fabric. It must be a switch or router with sufficient hardware/software scale for the host count expected. It can be located either inside or outside the SD-Access fabric, and is often colocated with the border node on smaller designs.
Exam tip: Undersizing the control plane node directly limits how many endpoints the fabric can support.

front.text: How are endpoints assigned to host pools in SD-Access?

items: Dynamically — through 802.1X authentication (ISE returns the host pool)
Statically — by configuring the fabric edge port to a specific host pool

back.text: Each fabric edge has an SVI per host pool, which serves as the anycast default gateway for endpoints in that pool. Host pools map to a VRF (VN) and are advertised per LISP instance ID.

front.text: What three identity/access methods does Cisco ISE support for endpoints joining the SD-Access fabric?

items: 802.1X — supplicant-based authentication (most secure)
MAC Authentication Bypass (MAB) — for devices that can't run a supplicant (printers, IoT)
Web Authentication (WebAuth) — captive portal for guest/BYOD

back.text: ISE then assigns the endpoint to a scalable group (SGT) and host pool, and pushes group-based policy to the network devices.

front.text: How do DNA Center (NCP/NDP) and Cisco ISE share information, and what does each contribute?

back.text: DNA Center and ISE integrate through APIs to exchange contextual information:

items: NDP shares analytics/contextual data with ISE and NCP
NCP shares automation context with ISE and NDP
ISE shares identity and policy context with both NCP and NDP
Combined context is presented to the user in the DNA Center management layer

front.text: What are the main tools available in the Cisco DNA Center Design workflow?

items: Network Hierarchy — geolocation, buildings, floors, site IDs
Network Settings — DNS, DHCP, AAA, credentials, IP pools, wireless
Image Repository — software images, version compliance, deployment
Network Profiles — LAN, WAN, WLAN connection profiles (e.g., SSIDs)

front.text: What is configured in the Cisco DNA Center Provision workflow?

items: Devices — assign to site ID, set image, deploy underlay configs
Fabrics — create fabric domains (or use the default LAN fabric)
Fabric Devices — assign roles: control plane, border, edge, WLC
Host Onboarding — define authentication type and assign host pools to VNs

front.text: What does Cisco DNA Center's Assurance workflow provide?

items: Dashboard — global health scores for fabric and non-fabric devices/clients
Client 360 — per-client status, onboarding, app experience
Devices 360 — per-device resource usage, loss, latency
Issues — reactive open issues and proactive trend detection

front.text: What are Cisco's two SD-WAN solutions, and when is each recommended?

rows: Cloud-first orgs needing granular segmentation, advanced routing, advanced security, complex topologies
Orgs needing UTM (firewall, VPN, IPS, AV, anti-spam, web filtering) in one box, or existing Meraki customers

back.text: Exam tip: ENCOR focuses on Cisco SD-WAN (Viptela).

front.text: What does 'transport-independent' mean in Cisco SD-WAN, and which transports are supported?

back.text: Transport-independent means the SD-WAN underlay can be ANY type of IP-based network. The fabric runs on top of whatever WAN media is available.

items: Internet (broadband)
MPLS
3G/4G LTE
Satellite
Dedicated circuits (e.g., AWS DX, Azure ER)

back.text: Exam tip: 'SD-WAN only works over Internet or MPLS' is FALSE — it works over any IP transport.

front.text: Why is using an FQDN preferred over an IP address when configuring vBond, and how does vBond detect NAT?

back.text: FQDN is preferred because it allows horizontal scaling of vBond devices and gives flexibility if a vBond's IP must change — devices keep working without reconfiguration.

vBond detects NAT using STUN (Session Traversal Utilities for NAT, RFC 5389). It compares the source IP/port the edge presents with what vBond actually receives. A separate session is built across every transport on the edge so NAT detection happens per circuit.

front.text: What functions does the vManage NMS provide in Cisco SD-WAN?

items: Single pane of glass GUI for the entire SD-WAN fabric
Stores and pushes all edge device configurations
Controls software updates
Used to author control plane and data plane policies
Provides REST APIs for programmatic configuration
Monitors fabric health and alarms

front.text: What kinds of information does OMP advertise between vSmart and SD-WAN edges?

items: Routes (prefixes reachable at each site)
Next-hop / TLOC (Transport Location) information
Encryption keys for IPsec sessions
Policy information (data and control plane)
Service routes (firewalls, IPS, etc. for service chaining)

back.text: Exam tip: OMP is similar to BGP but is proprietary — and it carries crypto keys, which BGP does not.

front.text: What standard router features do SD-WAN edge devices retain alongside SD-WAN overlay functions?

items: OSPF, EIGRP, BGP routing protocols
ACLs
QoS
Routing policies
Local intelligence for HA, ARP management, interface decisions

back.text: Edges form DTLS to vSmart for OMP, and IPsec to other edges for the data plane. They make site-local forwarding decisions; vSmart provides remote-site reachability.

front.text: What are the four types of centralized policy components in Cisco SD-WAN, and which plane does each affect?

rows: Drops or modifies routing — change metrics or next-hop
Controls advertisement of VPN prefixes to specific sites
Forwards an app's traffic based on tunnel SLA characteristics
Filters/modifies flows — NAT, service-chaining, QoS, packet duplication

front.text: Compare the three CoR for SaaS site classifications: DIA site, gateway site, and client site.

rows: Sends SaaS traffic directly to the Internet from its own circuits and probes them with HTTP probes
Has Internet exit AND advertises its loss/latency to remote sites via OMP so they can use it as an Internet gateway
No local Internet (only private transports). Picks the best gateway based on per-app vQoE scores received via OMP

front.text: What is vQoE, what is its scale, and where is it used?

back.text: Viptela Quality of Experience (vQoE) is a score from 0–10 that quantifies the quality of a path to a SaaS application based on HTTP probe results (loss and latency). 0 = worst, 10 = best.

items: Calculated per application, per path
Used by Cloud OnRamp for SaaS to pick the best Internet exit
Visible in the vManage GUI
Drives load-balancing across paths with similar scores

front.text: A network engineer must ensure voice traffic always uses MPLS unless MPLS exceeds 1% loss or 150 ms latency, in which case voice should fail over to broadband Internet. Which SD-WAN feature accomplishes this, and where is it configured?

back.text: This is exactly the use case for Application-Aware Routing (AAR), a centralized data-plane policy.

items: Configure the AAR policy on vManage with SLA class: loss ≤ 1%, latency ≤ 150 ms
Define application = voice (e.g., RTP / DSCP EF)
Set preferred color = mpls; backup color = biz-internet
vManage pushes policy to vSmart, which distributes it to applicable edges
BFD probes continuously measure loss/latency/jitter on each tunnel
If MPLS breaches the SLA, edges automatically forward voice over the broadband tunnel

back.text: Exam tip: AAR + BFD = the classic answer for SLA-driven WAN failover. Standard routing metrics (cost, AD) do NOT factor in real-time path quality.

front.text: Exam tip: What is the fastest way to remember the SD-Access plane-to-protocol mapping?

back.text: LISP / VXLAN / TrustSec → Control / Data / Policy

rows: 'Trust' = who is allowed to do what

back.text: If you see any question that says 'SD-Access uses ___ for the data plane,' the answer is VXLAN — never LISP.

front.text: Exam tip: Why does SD-Access use VXLAN instead of LISP for the data plane?

back.text: Single best answer on the exam: 'VXLAN supports Layer 2 networks.'

VXLAN can encapsulate the original Ethernet header (MAC-in-IP), enabling Layer 2 overlays. LISP data encapsulation cannot. Distractors like 'VXLAN supports IPv6' or 'VXLAN has a smaller header' are tempting but wrong — pick the L2 answer.

front.text: Exam tip: For which network environments was Cisco SD-Access designed?

back.text: ENTERPRISE CAMPUS AND BRANCH ONLY.

items: ❌ Data center → that's ACI
❌ WAN → that's SD-WAN
❌ Service provider → not SD-Access
❌ Public/private cloud → not SD-Access
✅ Enterprise campus and branch

back.text: Watch for distractor answers that include data center or WAN — they are always wrong for SD-Access scope.

front.text: Exam tip: Which IGP is recommended for the SD-Access underlay, and why?

back.text: IS-IS — always the recommended underlay IGP, and the ONLY IGP used by DNA Center LAN Automation.

items: Forms neighbors without IP dependency
Peers using loopback addresses
Agnostic to IPv4, IPv6, and non-IP traffic

back.text: OSPF is allowed only in a manually configured underlay. If the question says 'recommended' or 'automated' — answer IS-IS.

front.text: Exam tip: How do SD-Access fabric roles map to LISP roles?

rows: Performs PxTR registration on behalf of wireless clients

back.text: If a question asks 'which device acts as the LISP map server?' the answer is the control plane node — not the border, not the edge.

front.text: Exam tip: Which field carries the SGT in the VXLAN-GPO header, and how big is it?

back.text: Group Policy ID — 16 bits — supports up to 64,000 SGTs.
Watch for distractors like 'Scalable Group ID,' 'Group Based Tag,' or 'Group Based Policy' — all wrong. The exam answer is exactly 'Group Policy ID.'

items: G bit = SGT present (1) or not (0)
D bit = Don't Learn — egress VTEP must not learn source addr
A bit = Policy Applied — only valid when G=1

front.text: Exam tip: Why does the SD-Access anycast gateway use the SAME SVI IP and MAC on every fabric edge?

back.text: So endpoints can roam between edges (and even buildings) without changing IP or default gateway — the fabric behaves like one giant logical switch.

If the exam asks how SD-Access enables seamless host mobility for wired and wireless clients, the answer involves the distributed anycast gateway plus LISP EID-to-RLOC re-registration on the new edge.

front.text: Exam tip: What are the four MANDATORY components of Cisco SD-WAN, and which one is optional?

back.text: Mandatory (4): vManage, vSmart, vBond, SD-WAN routers (edges). Optional (1): vAnalytics.
Common trap: 'Cisco DNA Center' and 'Cisco ISE' are NOT SD-WAN components — they belong to SD-Access.

front.text: Exam tip: What is the MAIN function of the vBond orchestrator?

back.text: To authenticate the vSmart controllers AND the SD-WAN routers, and orchestrate connectivity between them.
Be careful — distractors will swap parties (e.g., 'authenticates vManage and routers' or 'authenticates vSmart and vManage'). The correct pair on the exam is vSmart + SD-WAN routers.

items: Also performs NAT detection via STUN (RFC 5389)
Provides load balancing across multiple vSmart/vManage instances
Maintains permanent DTLS to every vSmart

front.text: Exam tip: Which tunnel type is used where in the SD-WAN fabric? (Common true/false trap)

back.text: vSmart ↔ edges = DTLS (carries OMP). Edge ↔ edge data plane = IPsec. vSmart does NOT build IPsec to edges.

rows: Temporary DTLS — torn down after auth/NAT detection

back.text: Classic exam trap: 'The vSmart establishes permanent IPsec connections to all SD-WAN routers.' → FALSE. It uses DTLS, not IPsec.

front.text: Exam tip: What is the 'single pane of glass' for the SD-WAN solution?

back.text: vManage — the GUI/NMS for the entire SD-WAN fabric.
Distractor watch: DNA Center is the single pane of glass for SD-ACCESS, not SD-WAN. Don't confuse the two.

front.text: Exam tip: True or false — 'SD-WAN only works over the Internet or MPLS networks.'

back.text: FALSE. Cisco SD-WAN is transport-independent.

It runs over ANY IP-based underlay: Internet, MPLS, 3G/4G LTE, satellite, or dedicated circuits like AWS Direct Connect and Azure ExpressRoute. If the exam restricts SD-WAN to just two transports, it is wrong.

front.text: Exam tip: When is a deployment called 'campus fabric' vs 'SD-Access'?

back.text: Same protocols, different management. Managed by CLI/API (NETCONF/YANG) = campus fabric. Managed by Cisco DNA Center = SD-Access.
If a question describes LISP/VXLAN/TrustSec without DNA Center, it's the campus fabric solution — not full SD-Access.

front.text: Exam tip: How do you tell the three SD-Access border node types apart?

back.text: Memory hook: 'Default border = default route.' If the question mentions Internet/public cloud, it's the default border.

front.text: Exam tip: In SD-Access fabric wireless, where is the control plane and where is the data plane?

back.text: Control plane = CENTRALIZED at the WLC (CAPWAP). Data plane = DISTRIBUTED via VXLAN from AP directly to fabric edge.
Classic exam phrasing: 'The wireless data plane is distributed but the control plane remains centralized.' That's the SD-Access wireless model and a frequent test point.

items: AP must be directly connected to a fabric edge (or extended node)
Wired and wireless policy is enforced at the SAME place — the fabric edge
WLC registers wireless EIDs with the control plane node on behalf of edges

front.text: Exam tip: How do macro- and micro-segmentation map to SD-Access constructs?

rows: VRF + LISP instance ID + VXLAN VNID
TrustSec tag in VXLAN-GPO; enforced via SGACL

back.text: Memory hook: 'VN for Big slices, SGT for Small slices.' Scalable groups always operate INSIDE a VN.

front.text: Exam tip: What's the easy way to remember NCP vs NDP vs ISE?

back.text: NCP = aCtions (automation). NDP = Data (analytics). ISE = Identity (who and what).

front.text: Exam tip: In what order does Cisco DNA Center expect you to use its four workflows?

back.text: Design → Policy → Provision → Assurance. Memorize as 'D-P-P-A.'

items: Design — sites, settings, images, profiles
Policy — VNs, scalable groups, SGACLs, app QoS
Provision — assign sites, build fabric, set roles, onboard hosts
Assurance — health, Client 360 / Devices 360, issues

back.text: If a question lists workflows out of order, eliminate it. The exam tests this sequence directly.

front.text: Exam tip: What's the difference between how AAR and CoR for SaaS measure path quality?

rows: HTTP probes to the SaaS app
There's no SD-WAN edge on the SaaS side to form BFD with

back.text: If the question mentions BFD measuring loss/latency/jitter → AAR. If it mentions HTTP probes and a vQoE score (0–10) → CoR for SaaS.

front.text: Exam tip: How do you tell vEdge and cEdge apart on the exam?

back.text: 'v' = Viptela OS (legacy). 'c' = Cisco IOS XE (current).
If a question highlights advanced on-box security features (URL filtering, IPS, AMP), the answer points to cEdge / IOS XE — not vEdge.

front.text: By default, how many ICMP echo packets does the Cisco IOS ping command send, and what is the default timeout?

back.text: By default, ping sends 5 ICMP echo packets of 100 bytes each with a 2-second timeout. Successful replies are shown as '!' and timeouts as '.'.

front.text: Which ping command would you use to test for MTU issues using a 1500-byte packet sourced from Loopback101 to 22.22.22.22, repeated 10 times?

back.text: R1# ping 22.22.22.22 source loopback 101 size 1500 repeat 10
Combine size, source, and repeat options. Add df-bit (in extended ping) to detect MTU issues along the path.

front.text: By default, how many hops will traceroute attempt before quitting, and what does an asterisk (*) in the output indicate?

back.text: Traceroute attempts up to 30 hops by default. An asterisk (*) indicates that a probe timed out and no response was received from that hop.

front.text: During a traceroute, you see the response '!H'. What does this indicate?

back.text: !H indicates the router received an ICMP 'destination host unreachable' message. This typically means the next-hop router has no route to the destination IP address.

front.text: An OSPF neighbor is stuck in EXSTART/EXCHANGE state. Which debug command would quickly reveal an MTU mismatch, and what message would you look for?

back.text: R1# debug ip ospf adj
Look for a message like 'Nbr x.x.x.x has smaller interface MTU' or 'has larger interface MTU'. This indicates an MTU mismatch causing DBD exchange to fail.

front.text: Compare the default hello and dead intervals for the four OSPF network types.
How do you use conditional debugging to limit 'debug ip packet' output to traffic matching a specific subnet, and why is this important in production?

back.text:
R1(config)# access-list 100 permit ip any 192.168.14.0 0.0.0.255
R1(config)# access-list 100 permit ip 192.168.14.0 0.0.0.255 any
R1# debug ip packet 100
Unrestricted debug ip packet on a production router can flood the CPU and console, potentially causing the device to crash or become unresponsive.

front.text: Exam tip: After issuing 'undebug all', will an interface debug condition (e.g., 'debug interface Loopback0') still be active?

back.text: Yes. Interface conditions persist after 'undebug all'. You must explicitly remove them using 'undebug interface loopback0'. The system will warn that removing the last condition may cause a flood of debug messages.

front.text: Compare authentication and encryption capabilities of SNMPv1, SNMPv2c, and SNMPv3.

back.text: SNMPv3 with authPriv is the recommended best practice for production environments.

front.text: Configure SNMPv2c on a router to allow read-only and read/write access only from NMS host 192.168.14.100, and enable config-change traps to that host.

back.text:
access-list 99 permit 192.168.14.100
snmp-server community READONLY ro 99
snmp-server community READWRITE rw 99
snmp-server enable traps config
snmp-server host 192.168.14.100 traps READONLY

front.text: List the eight syslog severity levels from 0 to 7 with their keywords.
Configure a router to send debugging-level (level 7) syslog messages to a remote collector at 192.168.14.100.

back.text:
logging host 192.168.14.100
logging trap 7
By default, syslog uses UDP port 514. Ensure NTP is configured for accurate timestamps before relying on logs for troubleshooting.

front.text: What seven key fields traditionally define a NetFlow flow?

items: Source IP address
Destination IP address
Source port number
Destination port number
Layer 3 protocol type
Type of Service (ToS)
Input logical interface

back.text: A flow is a unidirectional traffic stream matching this combination of fields.

front.text: Configure traditional NetFlow v9 on Ethernet0/1 (ingress and egress) and export flows to collector 192.168.14.100 on UDP port 9999.

back.text:
ip flow-export version 9
ip flow-export destination 192.168.14.100 9999
!
interface Ethernet0/1
 ip flow ingress
 ip flow egress

front.text: List the four main components of Flexible NetFlow and their roles.

rows: Applied to interfaces; performs traffic monitoring
Exports cached flow data to a collector (NetFlow v9)
Samples partial flow data to reduce CPU/memory load

front.text: In a Flexible NetFlow flow record, what is the difference between the 'match' and 'collect' commands?

back.text: 'match' selects key fields that uniquely identify a flow (e.g., IP destination address). 'collect' selects non-key fields that are gathered for the matched flow (e.g., counter bytes, counter packets, TCP flags).

front.text: What is the correct sequence of steps to enable Flexible NetFlow on an interface?

items: Create a flow record (match key fields, collect non-key fields)
Create a flow exporter (destination, version, transport port)
Create a flow monitor and assign the flow record
Attach the flow exporter to the flow monitor
Apply the flow monitor to interface(s) with 'ip flow monitor NAME input/output'

front.text: Compare local SPAN, RSPAN, and ERSPAN.

rows: Source and destination on same switch
Source and destination on different switches
Source and destination across L3 boundaries

front.text: Configure a local SPAN session that monitors both directions of traffic on Gi1/0/1 and Gi1/0/2 and sends mirrored copies to Gi1/0/9.

back.text:
monitor session 1 source interface gi1/0/1 - 2
monitor session 1 destination interface gi1/0/9

front.text: What two special behaviors does an RSPAN VLAN have that differ from a normal VLAN?

items: MAC addresses are NOT learned on ports associated with the RSPAN VLAN.
Traffic is flooded out all ports associated with the RSPAN VLAN.

back.text: These behaviors ensure mirrored traffic reaches the destination port without interfering with normal forwarding.

front.text:
monitor session 1 type erspan-source
 description SOURCE-PC-D-TRAFFIC
 source interface GigabitEthernet 1/0/4 rx
 filter vlan 34
 no shutdown
 destination
  ip address 10.123.1.100
  erspan-id 2
  origin ip address 10.34.1.4
Interpret this configuration. What is being captured and where is it sent?
