back.text: Ingress (rx) traffic on Gi1/0/4 belonging to VLAN 34 is captured and tunneled (Layer 3) to a remote analyzer at 10.123.1.100. The ERSPAN ID 2 distinguishes this session, and the source/origin IP 10.34.1.4 identifies the sending device.

front.text: Name at least four metrics IP SLA can monitor.

items: Delay (round-trip and one-way)
Jitter (directional)
Packet loss (directional)
Packet sequencing
Connectivity (directional)
Voice quality scores (e.g., MOS)
Server/website download time
Per-hop path

front.text: Configure an IP SLA ICMP echo probe to 192.168.14.100 sourced from Loopback0 every 300 seconds, and schedule it to run forever starting now.

back.text:
ip sla 1
 icmp-echo 192.168.14.100 source-interface Loopback0
 frequency 300
!
ip sla schedule 1 life forever start-time now
Verify with: show ip sla configuration 1

front.text: Why is IP SLA more useful than relying on a service provider's SLA for end-to-end performance?

back.text: A provider SLA only measures performance across the provider's network. IP SLA generates synthetic probes from customer-edge devices, providing end-to-end visibility across the entire path including the LAN, CPE, and provider network.

front.text: What is Cisco DNA Center 'Network Time Travel' and how does it help troubleshooting?

back.text: Network Time Travel acts like a DVR for the network. Using streaming telemetry, it records network state over time so an engineer can replay past conditions to investigate user-reported issues that occurred earlier (e.g., 'last Tuesday at 3pm I couldn't connect').

front.text: What does Cisco DNA Center Path Trace provide beyond a traditional CLI traceroute?

items: Visual topology view of the path between two endpoints
Continuous or periodic refresh at a configurable interval
Detection of ACLs blocking traffic, including ACL name, interface, direction, and result (permit/deny)
Integration with client/device context (user, MAC, IP, SSID)

front.text: A user named Grace reports that her iPad keeps dropping from the wireless network. How can Cisco DNA Center Assurance Client 360 accelerate root-cause analysis compared to traditional CLI troubleshooting?

back.text: Searching for 'Grace' (via AD/ISE integration) returns all her devices with health scores, MAC, IP, VLAN, SSID, connected AP/switch, and last-known location. A timeline shows historical issues with guided remediation steps and impact analysis—often before an engineer could log into a single device.

front.text: Name three third-party or Cisco systems Cisco DNA Center commonly integrates with via open APIs to enrich Assurance data.

items: Active Directory (user identity)
Cisco Identity Services Engine (ISE) for policy and identity context
ServiceNow for helpdesk ticketing and automated issue enrichment
Infoblox for IPAM/DNS context

front.text: Exam tip: Why is it valuable to disable console logging and instead use 'logging buffered debugging' with an increased buffer size when running debugs?

back.text: Console logging is synchronous and can severely impact CPU during heavy debugs, potentially making the device unresponsive. Sending debug output to an enlarged logging buffer (e.g., 100000 bytes) lets you review messages with 'show logging' without flooding the console, while preserving more history than the default 4096-byte buffer.

no logging console
logging buffered 100000
logging buffered debugging

front.text: What is the tradeoff of using a Flow Sampler in Flexible NetFlow rather than full collection?

back.text: Sampling reduces CPU and memory load on the device by analyzing only a subset of packets, but accuracy decreases—events between sampled intervals may be missed. It is acceptable when trend visibility is sufficient (e.g., capacity planning) but unsuitable where every flow matters (e.g., billing or detailed security forensics).

front.text: What does setting the DF bit in an extended ping accomplish, and how is it used to detect MTU issues?

back.text: Setting the Do-Not-Fragment (DF) bit prevents intermediate routers from fragmenting the packet. If a hop has an MTU smaller than the packet size, the packet is dropped and an ICMP 'fragmentation needed' message returns. By incrementally testing with the DF bit and increasing sizes, you can identify the path MTU.

front.text: In an extended ping, what do the Loose, Strict, Record, Timestamp, and Verbose options do?

rows: Specifies hops the packet should traverse (others allowed)
Same as Loose, but only listed hops are allowed
Records the IP addresses of the first 9 hops traversed
Displays round-trip time at each hop
Default; auto-selected with any other option

front.text: Run a traceroute to 22.22.22.23 from Loopback101, using port 500, sending 5 probes per hop with a 10-second timeout.

back.text: R1# traceroute 22.22.22.23 port 500 source loopback101 probe 5 timeout 10

front.text: By default, what destination port number and how many probes per hop does Cisco IOS traceroute use?

back.text: Default destination port is 33434 (UDP), and 3 probes are sent per hop. The timeout per probe defaults to 3 seconds, with maximum TTL of 30.

front.text: OSPF neighbors are stuck in INIT state. 'debug ip ospf hello' on R4 shows: 'Mismatched hello parameters... Mask R 255.255.255.0 C 255.255.255.248'. What is the issue and fix?

back.text: The interfaces have mismatched subnet masks. R4's interface is configured with /29 while R1 is sending hellos with /24. OSPF requires matching masks on broadcast network types. Fix by aligning the mask on R4's interface to match the rest of the segment.

interface Ethernet0/1
 ip address 192.168.14.4 255.255.255.0

front.text: R1 (broadcast) and R4 (point-to-multipoint) are not forming an OSPF adjacency. The hello debug shows mismatched timers. Why, and what is the simplest fix?

back.text: Different OSPF network types use different default hello/dead intervals (broadcast = 10/40, point-to-multipoint = 30/120). Either align the network types with 'ip ospf network broadcast' on R4 or manually configure matching hello and dead intervals on both sides.

interface Ethernet0/1
 ip ospf network broadcast

front.text: Exam tip: What is the safest way to terminate all active debugs on a router?

back.text:
R1# undebug all
R1# u all
Always test debug commands in a lab environment first. Note that 'undebug all' does NOT remove interface debug conditions—use 'undebug interface <name>' separately.

front.text: Match each SNMP operation to its purpose: get-request, get-next-request, get-bulk-request, set-request, trap.

rows: Retrieve value of a specific variable
Retrieve next value within a table
Retrieve large blocks of data efficiently
Store a value in a specific variable
Unsolicited message from agent to manager when an event occurs

front.text: What is an SNMP MIB, and what kind of information does it contain?

back.text: The Management Information Base (MIB) is a hierarchical repository of device parameters (objects) that can be polled or used to trigger traps. Examples include sysDescr, sysUpTime, sysContact, sysName, and sysLocation. Each object has a unique OID and access level (read-only or read-write).

front.text: Exam tip: Why should SNMPv3 be preferred over SNMPv2c in production, and what does 'authPriv' provide?

back.text: SNMPv3 uses username-based authentication and supports message integrity (HMAC-MD5/SHA) and encryption (DES/3DES/AES). 'authPriv' provides BOTH authentication AND privacy (encryption). SNMPv2c only uses cleartext community strings, which can be sniffed.

front.text: By default, where are syslog messages sent on a Cisco router, and on which UDP port are they sent to a remote collector?

back.text: By default, all syslog messages are sent to the console. Remote syslog collectors receive messages on UDP port 514 (configurable). The buffer is disabled by default and must be enabled with 'logging buffered'.

front.text: Why must NTP be properly configured before relying on syslog for troubleshooting?

back.text: Syslog messages carry timestamps. Without accurate clocks (synchronized via NTP), correlating events across multiple devices becomes unreliable, making root-cause analysis significantly harder—especially during distributed network events.

front.text: What is the latest commonly deployed version of NetFlow, and what key capability does it add over earlier versions?

back.text: NetFlow Version 9 introduces a flexible, template-based export format. This allows new fields to be added without redefining the entire flow record format and enables Flexible NetFlow and IPFIX (NetFlow v10).

front.text: Configure NetFlow Top Talkers to display the top 10 flows on a router, sorted by bytes.

back.text:
ip flow-top-talkers
 top 10
 sort-by bytes
!
show ip flow top-talkers

front.text: Build a custom Flexible NetFlow record named CUSTOM1 that matches IPv4 destination address and collects byte and packet counters.

back.text:
flow record CUSTOM1
 description Custom Flow Record for IPv4 Traffic
 match ipv4 destination address
 collect counter bytes
 collect counter packets
Verify with: show flow record CUSTOM1

front.text: Build a Flexible NetFlow exporter named CUSTOM1 that sends NetFlow v9 data to 192.168.14.100 over UDP port 9999.

back.text:
flow exporter CUSTOM1
 description EXPORT-TO-NETFLOW-COLLECTOR
 destination 192.168.14.100
 export-protocol netflow-v9
 transport udp 9999

front.text: Apply Flexible NetFlow monitor CUSTOM1 to capture ingress traffic on Ethernet0/1, and show the verification command.

back.text:
interface Ethernet0/1
 ip flow monitor CUSTOM1 input
!
show flow monitor CUSTOM1 cache

front.text: How does Flexible NetFlow benefit security operations beyond traditional NetFlow?

items: Tracks all parts of the IP header and packet, normalizing them into flows
Dynamically creates individual caches per flow type
Can filter ingress traffic destined for a single host
Useful for detecting DoS attacks and worm propagation
Allows multiple monitors on the same traffic for different teams' analyses

front.text: What types of sources can be configured for a local SPAN session?

items: One or more specific switch ports (access or trunk)
A port channel (EtherChannel)
A VLAN (all traffic received by hosts in that VLAN; not the SVI itself)

back.text: Direction can be specified with rx (received), tx (sent), or both (default).

front.text: What does the 'encapsulation replicate' keyword do on a SPAN destination, and when would you use it?

back.text: Normally a SPAN session strips 802.1Q tags and Layer 2 control protocols (STP BPDUs, CDP, VTP, DTP, PAgP, LACP). 'encapsulation replicate' preserves these tags and protocols on the mirrored traffic—useful when analyzing trunk ports or troubleshooting Layer 2 control-plane issues.

front.text: Configure RSPAN: source switch SW2 mirrors Gi1/0/3 to RSPAN VLAN 99; destination switch SW1 forwards VLAN 99 traffic to local port Gi1/0/9.

back.text:
! On both switches
vlan 99
 name RSPAN_VLAN
 remote-span
!
! SW2 (source)
monitor session 1 source interface gi1/0/3
monitor session 1 destination remote vlan 99
!
! SW1 (destination)
monitor session 1 source remote vlan 99
monitor session 1 destination interface gi1/0/9

front.text: Exam tip: What are key SPAN session limitations to remember?

items: STP is disabled on the SPAN destination port (avoid forwarding loops)
A destination port cannot be reused between two SPAN sessions
A source port CAN be reused across two sessions
If source ports send more data than the destination can transmit, packets will be dropped at the destination
Most platforms support at least two SPAN sessions

front.text: You need to capture traffic from a remote branch site and analyze it at headquarters across the routed WAN. Which SPAN technology should you use, and why?

back.text: Use ERSPAN. It encapsulates mirrored traffic in GRE and routes it across Layer 3 boundaries (such as a WAN), so the analyzer can be located anywhere with IP reachability. SPAN and RSPAN are limited to a single switch or Layer 2 domain respectively.

front.text: Configure an IP SLA HTTP GET probe to http://192.168.14.100 every 90 seconds and schedule it to run forever starting now.

back.text:
ip sla 2
 http get http://192.168.14.100
 frequency 90
!
ip sla schedule 2 start-time now life forever

front.text: Why must an IP SLA operation be scheduled separately, and what command activates it?

back.text: Defining an IP SLA operation only creates the probe configuration—it does not run until scheduled. The 'ip sla schedule' command activates it, with options for life (forever or seconds), start-time (now, after, specific time, or pending), and recurring intervals.
ip sla schedule 1 life forever start-time now

front.text: Beyond simple monitoring, what advanced use cases enable IP SLA to influence routing or interface state?

items: Object tracking via 'track' command, which monitors IP SLA reachability state
Influencing static route installation when a tracked path goes down
Triggering HSRP/VRRP/GLBP failover based on tracked SLA state
Sending SNMP traps to an NMS via the CISCO-RTTMON-MIB

front.text: How does Cisco DNA Center Assurance simplify troubleshooting compared to traditional polling-based tools? (Pick two)

items: It uses streaming telemetry to gain near-real-time insight from devices, rather than periodic SNMP polling.
It uses open APIs to integrate with other platforms (AD, ISE, ServiceNow, Infoblox) to enrich data with contextual information.

front.text: What are the three primary workflow areas of Cisco DNA Center?

items: Design — site hierarchy, network settings, and image management
Provision — deploy devices, fabric, and policies
Assurance — monitor health, troubleshoot issues with telemetry and AI

front.text: Exam tip: True or false—Cisco DNA Center Assurance can only manage routers and switches.

back.text: False. Assurance manages routers, switches, wireless LAN controllers, access points, and integrates with ISE, AD, and ServiceNow to provide context for both wired and wireless clients.

front.text: Cisco DNA Assurance flags a P1 'Onboarding' issue impacting one building and seven wireless clients. What does Assurance provide to accelerate resolution?

items: Detailed root-cause description of the issue
Impact analysis (locations, number of users/devices affected)
Suggested remediation steps based on Cisco TAC knowledge and machine learning
Lists of affected clients with MAC, name, and connected AP
Optional integration that auto-populates ServiceNow tickets with this data so engineers begin work already knowing the cause and fix

front.text: What do the common Cisco IOS ping reply characters mean: !, ., U, M, and ?

rows: Could not fragment (DF bit set, MTU too small)

front.text: When you issue a ping without specifying a source, what source IP does the router use?

back.text: The router uses the IP address of the egress interface determined by the routing table for the destination. To force a specific source (e.g., to test return-path reachability or routing for a specific subnet), use the 'source' option.

front.text: Why might setting Type of Service to 184 in an extended ping be useful, and what is a caveat when traversing a service provider?

back.text: ToS 184 (decimal) corresponds to DSCP EF (Expedited Forwarding), used to test real-time QoS policies for voice/video. Caveat: many service providers do not honor or remark ICMP traffic with EF, so end-to-end tests may not reflect real application behavior.

front.text: During OSPF neighbor establishment, in which state are Database Descriptor (DBD) packets exchanged, and which adjacency issue commonly appears here?

back.text: DBDs are exchanged during the EXSTART/EXCHANGE states. MTU mismatches between OSPF neighbors most commonly manifest here, causing the adjacency to flap between EXSTART/EXCHANGE and INIT. Use 'debug ip ospf adj' to confirm.

front.text: Which command quickly verifies OSPF network type, hello/dead intervals, area, cost, and neighbor count for a specific interface?

back.text: show ip ospf interface ethernet0/1

front.text: Exam tip: Why is broad debugging risky on production routers, and how do conditional debugs mitigate the risk?

back.text: Unrestricted debugs (especially 'debug ip packet') can flood the CPU, hitting the process-switching path for every matched packet. This can cause unresponsiveness or outages. Conditional debugging (ACLs, interfaces, MAC, etc.) restricts the volume of messages and CPU impact to only relevant traffic.

front.text: If you configure 'logging buffered warnings' (level 4), which severity messages will be captured?

back.text: All messages at the configured severity AND lower numeric values (more severe) are captured. With level 4 (warnings), levels 0–4 are logged: emergencies, alerts, critical, errors, and warnings.

front.text: Which command displays the current syslog configuration, including console/buffer/trap levels and any configured remote logging hosts?

back.text: show logging

front.text: Exam tip: Beyond using SNMPv3, what two best practices reduce risk when using community strings (SNMPv1/v2c)?

items: Bind community strings to an ACL that permits only known NMS hosts
Use long, non-default, hard-to-guess community strings (avoid 'public' / 'private')

front.text: Compare SNMP read-only (RO) vs read-write (RW) community strings.

rows: Read AND modify device configuration via SNMP

back.text: Avoid RW community strings when possible; use SNMPv3 with authPriv and tightly scoped views.

front.text: What design considerations should you evaluate before enabling NetFlow on a router?

items: Memory consumption — flow cache resides in DRAM and is platform dependent
CPU impact, especially on older or software-based platforms
Direction of capture (ingress, egress, or both)
Bandwidth used by NetFlow Data Export (NDE) toward the collector
Sampling vs. full collection trade-off

front.text: Which three commands quickly verify traditional NetFlow configuration, export status, and current cache contents?

back.text:
show ip flow interface
show ip flow export
show ip cache flow

front.text: What does 'cache timeout active 60' configure within a Flexible NetFlow flow monitor?

back.text: It tells the device to export records for active flows every 60 seconds, even if the flows are still ongoing. This balances export freshness for long-lived flows with cache size and export bandwidth.

front.text: A security team and a capacity planning team both want different views of the same interface traffic. How does Flexible NetFlow accommodate both?

back.text: Flexible NetFlow supports applying multiple flow monitors to the same interface simultaneously. Each team can have its own flow record (key/non-key fields), monitor, and exporter — sending data to different collectors with different policies — all on the same traffic.

front.text: What two predefined Flexible NetFlow records are commonly available, and when would you choose them over a custom record?

items: 'netflow' — traditional NetFlow collection schemes
'netflow-original' — traditional IPv4 input NetFlow with origin AS

back.text: Use predefined records when you need backward-compatible traditional NetFlow behavior; use custom records when you need specific match/collect fields tailored to your use case.

front.text: On a SPAN source command, what do the keywords rx, tx, and both control, and what is the default?

rows: Only traffic received on the source
Only traffic transmitted by the source

front.text: When SPANning a trunk port, how do you restrict the captured traffic to only VLANs 10 and 20?

back.text: monitor session 1 filter vlan 10,20
Filtering reduces noise on the analyzer and helps avoid saturating the destination port.

front.text: By default the SPAN destination port drops ingress traffic. When and how would you allow ingress on the destination port?

back.text: Use the 'ingress' option when the analyzer is a host that also needs network access (e.g., RDP back to a Windows analyzer). Choose 'untagged vlan X' to associate incoming traffic with VLAN X, or 'dot1q vlan X' to require 802.1Q tagging.
monitor session 1 destination interface gi1/0/2 ingress untagged vlan 123

front.text: Which command displays only local SPAN sessions, and which shows only remote (RSPAN) sessions?

back.text:
show monitor session local
show monitor session remote

front.text: What is the purpose of the ERSPAN ID, and why is it important when multiple ERSPAN sessions are running?

back.text: The ERSPAN ID uniquely identifies a session within the GRE-encapsulated traffic. The destination analyzer uses this ID to demultiplex multiple ERSPAN sessions arriving at the same collector. Source and destination configurations must use the same ERSPAN ID.

front.text: Compare three common IP SLA probe types: ICMP echo, UDP jitter, and HTTP.

rows: Jitter, packet loss, and one-way delay (often for voice/video planning)
Application reachability and page download time

front.text: What is the role of the IP SLA Responder, and why is it required for accurate jitter and one-way delay measurements?

back.text: The IP SLA Responder is a Cisco IOS feature enabled on the destination router. It timestamps probes precisely (accounting for processing delay) and replies, allowing the source to compute accurate jitter and one-way delay. Without a Responder, only round-trip data is available.
ip sla responder

front.text: Which two commands verify IP SLA configuration and current operational statistics?

back.text:
show ip sla configuration
show ip sla statistics

front.text: You want a default route via 10.1.1.1 to be removed automatically when the next hop becomes unreachable. How do you combine IP SLA with object tracking to accomplish this?

back.text:
ip sla 10
 icmp-echo 10.1.1.1
 frequency 5
ip sla schedule 10 life forever start-time now
!
track 1 ip sla 10 reachability
!
ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 10.1.1.1 track 1
If IP SLA 10 fails, track 1 transitions Down, and the static default route is removed from the routing table — enabling failover to a backup route.

front.text: What does a Cisco DNA Center Assurance 'health score' represent, and what general scopes are scored?

back.text: A health score is a 1–10 composite metric (color-coded) derived from KPIs such as connectivity, performance, errors, and onboarding success. Scopes include overall network health, individual device health, client health (wired and wireless), and application health.

front.text: How are Cisco DNA Center Assurance wireless sensors used, and what value do they add beyond passive telemetry?

back.text: Sensors (dedicated or AP-based) actively test the network as a synthetic client — onboarding, authentication (PSK/802.1X), DHCP, DNS, RADIUS, web reachability, throughput, etc. This proactively detects issues before users complain, complementing passive telemetry.

front.text: How are issues prioritized in Cisco DNA Center Assurance, and what is a P1 issue?

back.text: Issues are categorized P1–P4 by impact severity. A P1 (Priority 1) issue is highest impact — typically affecting many clients or critical infrastructure (for example, an onboarding issue affecting an entire building). P4 issues are informational/low impact.

front.text: What value do AI/ML-driven analytics add to Cisco DNA Center Assurance compared to static thresholds?

items: Establishes per-site/per-client baselines instead of one-size-fits-all thresholds
Detects anomalies relative to learned normal behavior
Reduces alert fatigue by surfacing only meaningful deviations
Identifies trends (e.g., gradual onboarding degradation) before users notice
Provides predictive insights from historical telemetry

front.text: Exam tip: What is the recommended order of basic diagnostic tools when investigating a connectivity issue?

items: Gather facts (IP info, scope, time of issue)
Verify Layer 1/2 (interface status, errors)
Use ping for basic reachability
Use traceroute to identify where in the path traffic is failing
Inspect routing/ACLs/firewalls if Layer 3 path looks broken
Use debug (preferably conditional) only after the above narrow the scope

front.text: Your operations team uses ServiceNow for tickets. How can Cisco DNA Center Assurance reduce mean-time-to-resolution (MTTR) through API integration?

back.text: Cisco DNA Center exposes open REST APIs. Webhooks/intent APIs can automatically create or enrich ServiceNow tickets with the detected issue, root cause, impacted clients, locations, path-trace results, and suggested remediation. Engineers receive context-rich tickets and skip the discovery phase, dramatically reducing MTTR.

front.text: What does Cisco SAFE stand for, and what is its purpose?

back.text: Cisco SAFE stands for Secure Architectural Framework. It is a Cisco-developed framework that helps design secure solutions across different Places in the Network (PINs) by integrating security services modularly.

front.text: List the six Places in the Network (PINs) defined by Cisco SAFE.

items: Branch
Campus
Data Center
Edge
Cloud
WAN (Wide Area Network)

front.text: Name the six secure domains defined by Cisco SAFE used to evaluate each PIN.

items: Management
Security Intelligence
Compliance
Segmentation
Threat Defense
Secure Services

front.text: Compare the Before, During, and After phases of the Cisco SAFE attack continuum.

rows: Establish policies and prevention to reduce risk
Detect, block, and defend attacks in progress

front.text: What is Cisco Talos?

back.text: Cisco Talos is the Cisco threat intelligence organization — an elite team of security experts supported by sophisticated systems that detect, analyze, and protect against known and emerging threats. It was formed from IronPort SecApps, Sourcefire VRT, and the Cisco TRAC team.

front.text: What is Cisco Secure Malware Analytics (formerly Threat Grid), and how does it analyze files?

back.text: Cisco Secure Malware Analytics is Cisco's sandbox malware analysis solution. It performs static analysis (filenames, MD5 hashes, file types) and dynamic/behavioral analysis by running suspicious files in a controlled sandbox to observe their behavior. It evades anti-sandbox detection by avoiding typical instrumentation and integrates threat intelligence from Talos.

front.text: What are the main architectural components of Cisco Advanced Malware Protection (AMP)?

items: AMP Cloud (private or public) — stores file dispositions
AMP connectors (Secure Endpoint, Secure Email, Secure Web Appliance, AMP for Networks, AMP for Meraki MX)
Threat intelligence from Cisco Talos and Cisco Secure Malware Analytics

front.text: How does Cisco AMP determine a file's disposition without using local signature databases?

back.text: AMP connectors remain lightweight by sending a hash (fingerprint) of the file to AMP Cloud. AMP Cloud returns a verdict — clean, malicious, unknown, or custom. Unknown files can be sent to Secure Malware Analytics for sandbox analysis. Dispositions can change retrospectively as new intelligence is received.

front.text: How does Cisco Umbrella provide a first line of defense against Internet-based threats?

back.text: Cisco Umbrella (formerly OpenDNS) blocks requests to malicious Internet destinations (domains, IPs, URLs) at the DNS layer — before an IP connection is established or a file is downloaded. It is 100% cloud delivered, uses Anycast DNS for global reach, and analyzes ~500 billion daily DNS requests.

front.text: According to Gartner, what four capabilities must a Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW) include?

items: Standard firewall capabilities such as stateful inspection
An integrated IPS
Application-level inspection (to block malicious or risky apps)
Ability to leverage external security intelligence to address evolving threats

front.text: Compare the three software image options supported on Cisco Secure Firewall appliances.

rows: Full NGFW, supported on Secure Firewall and 5500-X (except 5585-X)

front.text: What are the core components of Cisco Secure Network Analytics (formerly Stealthwatch Enterprise)?

items: Network Analytics Manager (formerly SMC) — control center and aggregator
Flow Collectors — collect and analyze NetFlow/IPFIX telemetry
Flow Rate License — required for flow collection and aggregation

back.text: It relies primarily on NetFlow/IPFIX data for security analysis.

front.text: What is Cisco pxGrid and what role does ISE play in it?

back.text: pxGrid (Platform Exchange Grid) is an IETF framework that enables Cisco and third-party security platforms to share contextual information through a single API to identify, contain, and remediate threats. Cisco ISE acts as the central pxGrid controller (server). pxGrid 2.0 uses WebSocket and REST API over STOMP 1.2 (replacing XMPP-based pxGrid 1.0).

front.text: Identify the three roles in an 802.1x deployment and their functions.

rows: Software on the endpoint that provides identity credentials via EAPoL
NAD (switch/WLC) that brokers EAP between supplicant and auth server
RADIUS server that validates identity and returns accept/deny

front.text: How does an authenticator transport EAP messages between the supplicant and the authentication server?

back.text: Between the supplicant and authenticator, EAP messages are encapsulated in EAPoL (Layer 2). The authenticator copies the EAP payload from the EAPoL frame into a RADIUS AV-pair and forwards it to the authentication server (and vice versa). The authenticator is unaware of the EAP method in use — authentication is transparent to it.

front.text: Compare EAP-TLS, PEAP, and EAP-FAST in terms of certificates, tunneling, and unique features.

rows: Faster re-auth via PACs; supports EAP chaining

front.text: What is EAP chaining and which EAP method supports it?

back.text: EAP chaining (supported only by EAP-FAST) allows both machine and user authentication inside a single outer TLS tunnel and combines them into a single overall authentication result. This enables policies like granting greater privileges only when a user authenticates from a corporate-managed device.

front.text: Describe how MAC Authentication Bypass (MAB) works as a fallback to 802.1x.

items: Switch sends EAPoL identity requests every 30s; after 3 timeouts (~90s), it concludes no supplicant is present.
Switch opens the port to learn the source MAC from the first packet, then discards the packet.
Switch builds a RADIUS Access-Request using the MAC as the identity.
RADIUS server authorizes (or denies) and may return dACL, dVLAN, or SGT tag.

back.text: MAC addresses are easily spoofed — MAB-authorized endpoints should be given restricted access.

front.text: Compare Local Web Authentication (LWA) and Centralized Web Authentication (CWA) with Cisco ISE.
What are the three phases of Cisco TrustSec?

items: Classification — assign SGT tags (dynamically via 802.1x/MAB/WebAuth, or statically)
Propagation — communicate SGT/IP mappings (inline tagging or SXP)
Enforcement — apply policy at egress (SGACL on switches/routers, SGFW on firewalls)

front.text: Compare inline (native) SGT tagging and SXP propagation.

rows: Inserted into Layer 2 frame (Cisco metadata header)

front.text: What are the two main types of TrustSec egress enforcement?

items: Security Group ACL (SGACL) — enforced on routers and switches; filters by source and destination SGT
Security Group Firewall (SGFW) — enforced on Cisco Secure Firewalls using locally defined tag-based rules

back.text: Endpoints are NOT aware of SGT tags — tagging is only known and applied within the network infrastructure.

front.text: What is MACsec, and why is it described as hop-by-hop?

back.text: MACsec is an IEEE 802.1AE Layer 2 encryption standard. It is hop-by-hop because traffic is encrypted only on the wire between two MACsec peers and decrypted internally at each switch. This allows the switch to inspect frame contents (e.g., SGT tags, QoS) before re-encrypting on the next hop. Encryption uses AES-GCM via onboard ASICs.

front.text: Compare the two MACsec keying mechanisms: SAP and MKA.

rows: Default for uplink MACsec between Cisco switches
Used for downlink MACsec (endpoint↔switch) and switch↔switch

front.text: What is the difference between Downlink MACsec and Uplink MACsec?

rows: MKA (requires MACsec-capable supplicant like Cisco Secure Client)
Cisco SAP by default; can be manual or dynamic via 802.1x

front.text: What 'before-attack' protections does the Cisco Secure Web Appliance (WSA) provide?

items: Web reputation filters — score sites from -10 to +10 based on 200+ parameters
Web (URL) filtering — blocks known-bad URLs from a 50M+ database; uses Dynamic Content Analysis for unknown URLs
Application Visibility and Control (AVC) — granular control over 4,000+ apps and 150,000+ micro-applications

front.text: What problem does Enhanced FlexAuth (Access Session Manager) solve, and how does it relate to IBNS 2.0?

back.text: By default, switches try 802.1x → MAB → WebAuth sequentially, which delays endpoints lacking 802.1x supplicants. Enhanced FlexAuth allows multiple authentication methods to run concurrently (e.g., 802.1x and MAB simultaneously) so endpoints come online faster. It is a key component of Cisco Identity-Based Networking Services (IBNS) 2.0, which combines FlexAuth, C3PL, and Cisco ISE for end-to-end access policy.

front.text: Name four advanced threat protection capabilities of Cisco Secure Email (ESA).

items: Reputation filtering and spam protection (CASE engine — >99% catch rate, <1 in 1M false positives)
Forged email detection and Cisco Advanced Phishing Protection (CAPP) for BEC/social engineering
Outbreak filters that rewrite suspicious URLs and redirect them through the Secure Web Appliance
Cisco Domain Protection (CDP) and graymail detection / Safe Unsubscribe

front.text: Which protocols does Cisco ISE use for network access control vs. device administration?

rows: Network Access Control (802.1x, MAB, WebAuth) for users/endpoints
Device administration (AAA for routers/switches CLI access)

back.text: Exam tip: If the question is about user/endpoint network access → RADIUS. If it's about logging into a network device's CLI → TACACS+.

front.text: Quick exam recall: Which Cisco solutions map to these descriptors? (1) Threat intel team, (2) Sandbox malware analysis, (3) NetFlow-based security analytics, (4) DNS-layer security, (5) Centralized NAC policy.

items: Cisco Talos — threat intelligence organization
Cisco Secure Malware Analytics (Threat Grid) — sandbox malware analysis
Cisco Secure Network Analytics (Stealthwatch) — NetFlow/IPFIX security analytics
Cisco Umbrella — DNS-layer security (formerly OpenDNS)
Cisco Identity Services Engine (ISE) — centralized NAC and policy

back.text: Memorize the rebranding: AnyConnect→Secure Client, AMP for Endpoints→Secure Endpoint, Threat Grid→Secure Malware Analytics, Stealthwatch→Secure Network Analytics, Firepower NGFW→Secure Firewall.

front.text: Why are branch offices considered prime targets for security breaches in the Cisco SAFE framework?

back.text: Branches are typically less secure than campus and data center PINs because the large number of branches makes it cost-prohibitive to apply the same security controls. Top branch threats include endpoint malware (e.g., POS malware), wireless infrastructure exploits (rogue APs for MitM/DoS), unauthorized/malicious client activity, and exploitation of trust.

front.text: Why is the Edge considered the highest-risk PIN in Cisco SAFE, and what are typical threats?

back.text: The Edge is the primary ingress/egress point for Internet traffic and is most important for e-commerce, making it the highest-risk PIN. Typical threats include web server vulnerabilities, distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks, data loss, and man-in-the-middle (MitM) attacks.

front.text: What makes data centers particularly difficult to secure, and what threats are typical there?

back.text: Data centers contain the most critical information assets (intellectual capital), making them primary targets. They typically host hundreds or thousands of servers, making it very difficult to create and manage proper access rules. Typical threats include data extraction, malware propagation, unauthorized network access (application compromise), botnet infestation (scrumping), data loss, privilege escalation, and reconnaissance.

front.text: Compare traditional segmentation with advanced (TrustSec) segmentation in the Cisco SAFE Segmentation domain.
From which three security research teams was Cisco Talos formed?

items: IronPort Security Applications (SecApps)
Sourcefire Vulnerability Research Team (VRT)
Cisco Threat Research, Analysis, and Communications (TRAC) team

front.text: What is Glovebox in Cisco Secure Malware Analytics?

back.text: Glovebox is a sandbox environment within Cisco Secure Malware Analytics that lets analysts safely upload and interact with suspicious files directly to observe malware behavior. It's used for manual investigation when automatic submission is unavailable.

front.text: What is the Cisco Security Connector (CSC), and on what platform does it run?

back.text: The Cisco Security Connector (CSC) is the name for Cisco Secure Endpoint on Apple iOS. It incorporates both Cisco Secure Endpoint (AMP) and Cisco Umbrella into a single iOS application.

front.text: Beyond VPN, what built-in modules and capabilities does Cisco Secure Client (formerly AnyConnect) provide?

items: VPN Posture (HostScan) module — checks AV/antispyware/firewall compliance
ISE Posture module — works with Cisco ISE for endpoint compliance
Web security via Cisco Cloud Web Security
Network visibility into endpoint flows for Cisco Secure Network Analytics
Roaming protection via Cisco Umbrella (even when VPN is disconnected)

front.text: What is the simplest way to deploy Cisco Umbrella for an entire corporate network, including guest devices?

back.text: Change the DHCP configuration on all Internet gateways (routers, APs) so that all devices forward their DNS traffic to Umbrella's global network. For off-network laptops, use Cisco Secure Client's roaming security module or the Umbrella roaming client to send DNS queries to Umbrella.

front.text: What 'during-attack' capabilities does the Cisco Secure Web Appliance (WSA) provide?

items: Cloud access security via CASB partners (e.g., Cisco CloudLock)
Parallel antivirus (AV) scanning with multiple engines
Layer 4 traffic monitoring to detect spyware 'phone-home' communications
File reputation and analysis with Cisco AMP
Data Loss Prevention (DLP) via ICAP integration with third-party DLP vendors

front.text: What is Global Threat Analytics (GTA) and what data does it analyze?

back.text: Global Threat Analytics (GTA), formerly Cognitive Threat Analytics (CTA), uses machine learning to identify malicious activity before sensitive data can be exfiltrated. It analyzes web traffic, endpoint data from Cisco Secure Endpoint, and network data from Cisco Secure Network Analytics.

front.text: How do Cisco Secure Email outbreak filters protect against malicious URLs in messages?

back.text: Outbreak filters can rewrite URLs in suspicious emails. When a user clicks the rewritten URL, they are redirected to the Cisco Secure Web Appliance, which actively scans the website content. If malware is found, a block screen is displayed. Cisco Secure Email also generates Web Interaction Tracking reports showing top users who clicked malicious URLs and the top malicious URLs clicked.

front.text: According to Gartner, what five capabilities define a Next-Generation IPS (NGIPS)?

items: Real-time contextual awareness
Advanced threat protection
Intelligent security automation
Unparalleled performance and scalability
Application visibility and control (AVC) and URL filtering

front.text: What three remediation actions can the FMC apply to compromised hosts via integration with Cisco ISE?

items: Quarantine — limits or blocks an endpoint's access to the network
Unquarantine — removes the quarantine action
Shutdown — shuts down the switch port that the compromised endpoint is connected to

front.text: In Cisco documentation, what is the difference between 'FirePOWER' (uppercase) and 'Firepower' (lowercase)?

rows: Cisco Secure IPS (NGIPS) services software or NGIPS services ASA module
Cisco Secure Firewall or the Firepower Threat Defense (FTD) unified image

back.text: Exam tip: Watch the capitalization on the exam — it can hint whether the question is about the IPS module or the unified firewall image.

front.text: What is the role of the Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center (FMC)?

back.text: The FMC is a centralized management platform that aggregates and correlates threat events, contextual information, and network performance data. It provides a single pane of glass for event collection and policy management for Cisco Secure Firewall (physical and virtual), FTD for ISR, and ASA with FirePOWER Services.

front.text: Compare the Cisco Secure Network Analytics UDP Director with the Cisco Telemetry Broker.

rows: Multiple telemetry types (e.g., AWS VPC Flow Logs)
Receives and replicates UDP streams to multiple destinations
Ingests, filters, and transforms telemetry into target formats (e.g., IPFIX)
Single NetFlow export replicated to many tools
Cross-platform telemetry transformation for Stealthwatch, Splunk, etc.

front.text: How does Encrypted Traffic Analytics (ETA) within Cisco Secure Network Analytics detect threats in encrypted traffic?

back.text: ETA pinpoints malicious patterns in encrypted traffic without having to decrypt it. It analyzes metadata such as packet length sequences, byte distributions, and TLS handshake characteristics — combined with machine learning — to identify threats and accelerate response while preserving privacy and compliance.

front.text: What are the two deployment models supported by Cisco Secure Cloud Analytics?

rows: Cisco Secure Cloud Analytics Public Cloud Monitoring
Visibility and threat detection in AWS, GCP, and Azure using native telemetry like VPC flow logs
Visibility and threat detection for on-premises networks delivered from the cloud

back.text: Cisco Secure Cloud Analytics consumes only metadata — actual packet payloads are never retained or transferred outside the network.

front.text: What types of compliance checks can Cisco ISE perform via its endpoint posture service?

items: Latest OS patches installed
Endpoint firewall enabled
Anti-malware definitions up to date
Disk encryption enabled
Mobile PIN lock configured
Rooted/jailbroken phone status

back.text: Non-compliant devices can be remediated (restricted from network/apps/services) until they become compliant.

front.text: What is the high-level sequence of a successful 802.1x authentication?

items: Authenticator detects port up; sends EAP-Request/Identity (or supplicant sends EAPoL-Start).
Authenticator relays EAP messages between supplicant and RADIUS server, encapsulating EAP into RADIUS AV-pairs until an EAP method is selected.
Authentication occurs using the chosen EAP method.
On success, RADIUS returns Access-Accept with EAP-Success and authorization options (e.g., dACL, dVLAN, SGT).
Authenticator opens the port for the endpoint.

front.text: Why is EAP-MD5 considered a poor choice as an authentication method?

back.text: EAP-MD5 hashes credentials with MD5 and sends them to the authentication server. However, it has no mechanism for mutual authentication — the supplicant cannot verify whether the authentication server is trustworthy. This makes it vulnerable to rogue authentication servers and man-in-the-middle attacks.

front.text: What is one major difference between EAP-TTLS and PEAP?

back.text: PEAP only supports EAP-based inner authentication methods (EAP-MSCHAPv2, EAP-GTC, EAP-TLS). EAP-TTLS supports those plus legacy non-EAP inner methods such as PAP, CHAP, and MS-CHAP. However, PEAP is much more widely supported across vendors than EAP-TTLS.

front.text: What is a Protected Access Credential (PAC), and which EAP method uses it?

back.text: A Protected Access Credential (PAC) is similar to a secure cookie stored locally on the host as 'proof' of a successful prior authentication. EAP-FAST uses PACs to enable faster re-authentications and faster wireless roaming compared to PEAP.

front.text: What authorization options can a Cisco switch apply to a MAB-authenticated endpoint based on the RADIUS server's response?

items: Downloadable ACLs (dACLs)
Dynamic VLAN assignment (dVLAN)
Security Group Tags (SGT) tags

front.text: What is the sequence of steps for Centralized Web Authentication (CWA) with Cisco ISE?

items: Endpoint connects without a configured (or properly configured) supplicant.
Switch performs MAB and sends RADIUS Access-Request to ISE.
ISE returns RADIUS result with a URL redirect to the centralized portal on ISE.
Endpoint receives IP, DNS, and gateway via DHCP.
User opens browser, enters credentials in the centralized ISE portal — credentials are tied to the MAB session.
ISE sends a CoA-reauth to the switch.
Switch sends new MAB request with the same session ID; ISE returns final authorization (e.g., dACL).

front.text: When dynamic SGT assignment via 802.1x/MAB/WebAuth is not possible (e.g., in a data center), what static mapping options are available?

items: IP to SGT
Subnet to SGT
VLAN to SGT
Layer 2 interface to SGT
Layer 3 logical interface to SGT
Port to SGT
Port profile to SGT

back.text: Cisco ISE can also centrally maintain a database of IP-to-SGT mappings that TrustSec-capable devices download.

front.text: What are the speaker and listener roles in SXP, and how many hops can SXP support?

back.text: In SXP (SGT Exchange Protocol), the speaker is the SXP peer that sends IP-to-SGT bindings, and the listener is the peer that receives them. SXP is TCP-based and peer-to-peer, supporting both single-hop and multi-hop connections, allowing IP-to-SGT mappings to be communicated between switches that don't support inline tagging in hardware.

front.text: What two additional fields does MACsec add to an Ethernet frame, and what is their size?

items: 16-byte MACsec Security Tag field (802.1AE header) — includes EtherType 0x88e5, TCI/AN, SL, Packet Number, and SCI
16-byte Integrity Check Value (ICV) field

back.text: MACsec provides authentication using GMAC, or authenticated encryption using AES-GCM. All devices in the MACsec path must support 802.1AE for the frame to be processed.

front.text: Scenario: An SGACL matrix shows source SGT 'Employees' meeting destination SGT 'Employees' with a Permit_FTP ACL. What does this mean operationally, and where is the ACL enforced?

back.text: Employee-to-employee traffic is restricted to FTP only (TCP port 21); all other traffic between employees is denied. Enforcement happens at the egress point of the TrustSec network — for two employees on the same switch, the ingress/egress switch enforces the SGACL. The matrix direction is always source SGT (rows) to destination SGT (columns).
Exam tip: Remember 'permit IP' = permit all, 'deny IP' = deny all in SGACL matrices. SGACLs filter based on source AND destination SGTs.

front.text: What types of threats are campuses most exposed to in the Cisco SAFE framework?

back.text: Campuses contain a large number of users — employees, contractors, guests, and partners — making them easy targets for phishing, web-based exploits, unauthorized network access, malware propagation, and botnet infestations.

front.text: How is security in the Cloud PIN dictated, and what are the primary cloud threats?

back.text: Cloud security is dictated by service-level agreements (SLAs) with the cloud service provider and requires independent certification audits and risk assessments. Primary threats include web server vulnerabilities, loss of access, data loss, malware, and MitM attacks.

front.text: What are the typical threats in the WAN PIN according to Cisco SAFE?

back.text: The WAN connects all PINs together, and managing security across hundreds of branches is challenging. Typical threats include malware propagation, unauthorized network access, WAN sniffing, and MitM attacks.

front.text: Why is the Management secure domain considered critical in the Cisco SAFE framework?

back.text: Management of devices and systems using centralized services is critical for consistent policy deployment, workflow change management, and keeping systems patched. It coordinates policies, objects, and alerting across the network.

front.text: What does the Security Intelligence domain in Cisco SAFE provide?

back.text: Security Intelligence provides detection of emerging malware and cyber threats. It enables an infrastructure to enforce policy dynamically because reputations are augmented by the context of new threats — providing accurate and timely security protection.

front.text: What kinds of visibility does the Threat Defense secure domain provide in Cisco SAFE?

items: Network traffic telemetry
File reputation
Contextual information (device type, location, user, identity, role, privilege level, login status, posture status)

back.text: This visibility enables assessment of the nature and potential risk of suspicious activity so the correct next steps can be taken.

front.text: Why is the Cisco SAFE framework described as modular?

back.text: SAFE is modular because PINs that don't exist in a network can simply be removed from the design. Each PIN is evaluated independently using the secure domains, allowing organizations to apply only the components relevant to their environment.

front.text: Approximately how much daily telemetry does Cisco Talos analyze?

items: ~16 billion web requests per day
~600 billion emails per day
~1.5 million unique malware samples per day

back.text: Talos uses this scale of telemetry to feed reputation and intelligence into Cisco security products in near real time.

front.text: Name several intelligence feeds that Cisco Talos uses to enrich its threat intelligence.

items: Advanced Microsoft and industry disclosures
Advanced Malware Protection (AMP) community
ClamAV, Snort, Immunet, SpamCop, SenderBase, Cisco Secure Malware Analytics, and Talos user communities
Honeypots
Sourcefire AEGIS program (Awareness, Education, Guidance, and Intelligence Sharing)
Private and public threat feeds
Dynamic analysis

front.text: Match each Cisco AMP connector to its renamed/current product name.

rows: AMP for Email / Email Security Appliance (ESA)
AMP for Web / Web Security Appliance (WSA)
Runs on Cisco Secure Firewall and dedicated AMP appliances

front.text: What are the four file disposition categories used by Cisco AMP?

items: Clean
Malicious (malware)
Unknown
Custom

back.text: Dispositions can change over time — files previously classified as clean can later be reclassified as malicious based on new intelligence.

front.text: How does Cisco AMP provide protection across the Before, During, and After phases of the attack continuum?

rows: Global threat intel from Talos and Secure Malware Analytics
File reputation and sandboxing to identify threats during attack
Retrospection, indicators of compromise (IoCs), breach detection, tracking, analysis, and surgical remediation

front.text: How does the Cisco Secure Web Appliance score websites for reputation, and what range is used?

back.text: WSA analyzes more than 200 web traffic and network-related parameters (domain owner, hosting server, site age, type) and assigns a reputation score from -10 to +10 — instead of a binary good/bad classification. Based on this score and policy, sites are blocked, allowed, or delivered with a warning. Talos refreshes web reputation data every three to five minutes.

front.text: What does the Dynamic Content Analysis (DCA) engine in WSA do?

back.text: The DCA engine accurately identifies inappropriate content in real time for ~90% of unknown URLs. It scans text, scores the text for relevancy, calculates model document proximity, and returns the closest category match — enabling URL filtering on uncategorized sites.

front.text: Scenario: A company wants to allow employees to use Facebook but block them from clicking 'Like' or watching certain YouTube channels. Which WSA capability enables this?

back.text: Cisco Application Visibility and Control (AVC) enables this granular policy. AVC identifies and classifies more than 4,000 web/mobile applications and 150,000+ micro-applications (such as Facebook Messenger or YouTube channels), giving administrators fine-grained control over how apps and their sub-features are used.

front.text: How does the Cisco Secure Web Appliance integrate with third-party DLP solutions?

back.text: WSA uses Internet Content Adaptation Protocol (ICAP) to integrate with third-party DLP appliances. Outbound traffic is directed to the DLP appliance, which inspects content for sensitive data (credit card numbers, customer PII, confidential files) and allows or blocks uploads to cloud services like Box, iCloud, and Dropbox based on third-party rules.

front.text: What is the Cisco Context Adaptive Scanning Engine (CASE), and what is its performance level?

back.text: CASE is the spam-blocking engine in Cisco Secure Email. It delivers a spam catch rate greater than 99% with a false-positive rate of less than 1 in 1 million messages.

front.text: What is Cisco Domain Protection (CDP) and what does it prevent?

back.text: Cisco Domain Protection (CDP) for external email helps prevent phishing emails from being sent using a customer's own domain. It is part of Cisco Secure Email's defense against domain spoofing and impersonation attacks.

front.text: What detection engine powers Cisco Secure IPS, and what is notable about it?

back.text: The Cisco Secure IPS detection engine is Snort — the world's most powerful open-source IPS engine. Cisco gained Snort through its acquisition of Sourcefire in 2013.

front.text: What are the management options available for Cisco Secure Firewall running FTD or Cisco Secure IPS Services software?

items: Cisco SecureX
Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center (FMC) — formerly Firepower Management Center
Cisco Secure Firewall Device Manager (FDM) — formerly Firepower Device Manager, for small appliances

back.text: FTD CLI configuration is NOT supported — CLI is only available for initial setup and troubleshooting.

front.text: What management options are available for Cisco ASA software?

items: Command-line interface (CLI)
Cisco Security Manager (CSM)
Cisco Secure Firewall Adaptive Security Device Manager (ASDM)
Cisco Defense Orchestrator

front.text: List the key benefits of Cisco Secure Network Analytics.

items: Real-time threat detection
Incident response and forensics
Network segmentation
Network performance and capacity planning
Ability to satisfy regulatory requirements

front.text: Compare the centralized Data Store model with the distributed Flow Collector model in Cisco Secure Network Analytics.

rows: Centralized storage on Data Node appliances

front.text: Can Cisco ISE act as a certificate authority, and what is one practical use?

back.text: Yes — Cisco ISE includes an internal certificate authority (CA). It's particularly useful for streamlining device onboarding (e.g., automated 802.1x supplicant provisioning and certificate enrollment) so endpoints can use certificate-based EAP methods like EAP-TLS without requiring an external PKI.

front.text: Why is EAP-MSCHAPv2 (PEAPv0) the most common inner method for PEAP in enterprise environments?

back.text: EAP-MSCHAPv2 allows simple transmission of username/password — or computer name/computer password — encrypted within an MSCHAPv2 session inside the PEAP TLS tunnel. The RADIUS server can then authenticate those credentials against Microsoft Active Directory, which is widely deployed in enterprises.

front.text: What is the relationship between guest VLAN and Web Authentication (WebAuth) on Cisco switches?

back.text: Guest VLAN and WebAuth (both LWA and CWA) are mutually exclusive — they cannot be used at the same time on the same configuration. Guest VLAN provides Internet access for endpoints without 802.1x supplicants, while WebAuth requires manual user credential entry through a browser.
Exam tip: If a question mentions both guest VLAN and WebAuth on the same port, that's a misconfiguration — they're mutually exclusive.

front.text: Why are SGT tags typically given business-relevant names like 'Mac_Corporate' or 'Mac_Guest' instead of just numeric values?

back.text: SGT tags represent the context of the user, device, use case, or function — making them easier to manage when named after roles or business cases. The named SGT is used in ISE and on network devices to build readable security group policies. The actual frame carries the numeric value (decimal or hex), but administrators interact with the friendly name.

front.text: What are SGT tags called in Cisco Software-Defined Access (SD-Access)?

back.text: In Cisco SD-Access, SGT tags are referred to as Scalable Group Tags (SGTs). The acronym stays the same, but the 'S' switches from Security to Scalable. The function is identical.
Exam tip: 'Security Group Tag' (TrustSec) and 'Scalable Group Tag' (SD-Access) are the same thing — both abbreviated SGT.

front.text: What encryption algorithm does MACsec use, and where is encryption performed for performance?

back.text: MACsec uses AES-GCM (Galois/Counter Mode Advanced Encryption Standard) for authenticated encryption — typically AES-GCM-128. Encryption and decryption are performed by onboard ASICs on the switch rather than offloading to a software crypto engine (as with IPsec), enabling line-rate performance.

front.text: Scenario: A switch port has a MACsec encryption policy of 'optional' configured at the CLI, but ISE returns an authorization with 'must-secure' (force encryption). Which policy wins?

back.text: The ISE-issued policy wins. If Cisco ISE returns an encryption policy as part of the authorization result, it overrides anything configured at the switch CLI. This allows centralized, identity-based control over MACsec enforcement — for example, requiring encryption only for corporate-managed endpoints while allowing optional for others.
Exam tip: Manual port-level MACsec config is uncommon in production — dynamic ISE-driven policy is the typical deployment model.

front.text: Exam tip: How can you quickly recall the six Cisco SAFE PINs?

back.text: Use the mnemonic 'BCDECW' — Branch, Campus, Data center, Edge, Cloud, WAN. Notice that 'Internet' is NOT a PIN — that's a common distractor on the exam. The Edge is the ingress/egress to the Internet, but Internet itself is not a SAFE PIN.
Exam trap: If you see 'Internet' listed as a PIN choice, it's wrong. The correct answer is 'Edge'.

front.text: Exam tip: Which word is NOT a Cisco SAFE secure domain — Segmentation or Segregation?

back.text: Segregation is NOT a SAFE secure domain. The six secure domains are: Management, Security Intelligence, Compliance, Segmentation, Threat Defense, and Secure Services. 'Segregation' is a classic distractor that sounds plausible but is incorrect.
Exam trap: Watch for 'Segregation' vs 'Segmentation' — only Segmentation is correct.

front.text: Exam tip: If asked 'What is Cisco's threat intelligence organization?' — what's the right answer, and what's the trap?

back.text: The correct answer is Cisco Talos. The trap is the 'Cisco Threat Research, Analysis, and Communications (TRAC) team' — TRAC was one of three teams that merged to FORM Talos (alongside IronPort SecApps and Sourcefire VRT), but TRAC by itself is not Cisco's current threat intel organization.
Exam trap: 'TRAC team' is a wrong-answer distractor. Talos is the correct answer.

front.text: Exam tip: How do you distinguish Cisco Secure Malware Analytics from Cisco Secure Network Analytics on the exam?

back.text: Exam trap: The names are very similar. Remember: Malware = sandbox; Network = NetFlow.

front.text: Exam tip: Which Cisco security product relies on NetFlow data for security analysis?

back.text: Cisco Secure Network Analytics (Stealthwatch Enterprise) is the answer. It collects NetFlow, IPFIX, and other flow telemetry from routers, switches, firewalls, and endpoints. Talos, Secure Malware Analytics, and Secure Web Appliance do NOT primarily rely on NetFlow.
Exam keyword: 'NetFlow' → Secure Network Analytics.

front.text: Exam tip: True or False — Without Cisco ISE, it would not be possible to implement pxGrid.

back.text: True. Cisco ISE is the central pxGrid controller (server). All Cisco and third-party security platforms (pxGrid nodes) interface with ISE to publish, subscribe to, and query contextual information. Without ISE, there is no pxGrid implementation.
Exam tip: ISE = pxGrid controller. Memorize this association.

front.text: Exam tip: What protocol does pxGrid 1.0 use vs pxGrid 2.0?

back.text: Exam tip: Starting with ISE 3.1, all pxGrid connections must be 2.0. If you see XMPP → that's pxGrid 1.0.

front.text: Exam tip: When does Cisco ISE use RADIUS vs TACACS+?

back.text: Exam trick: 'Network access' = RADIUS. 'Device administration' = TACACS+.

front.text: Exam tip: Which EAP method supports EAP chaining?

back.text: Only EAP-FAST supports EAP chaining. EAP-TTLS, EAP-GTC, and PEAP do NOT support chaining. EAP chaining allows machine AND user authentication inside a single outer TLS tunnel, producing a combined authentication result.
Exam trap: The other EAP methods sound similar but only EAP-FAST chains. Memorize: FAST = chaining + PACs.

front.text: Exam tip: True or False — SGT tags extend all the way down to the endpoints.

back.text: False. Endpoints are NOT aware of SGT tags. SGT tags are only known and applied within the network infrastructure (switches, routers, firewalls). The infrastructure inserts and removes the tags transparently.
Exam trap: A common 'gotcha' is assuming endpoints see SGTs. They don't.

front.text: Exam tip: Which of these are valid TrustSec phases — Classification, Enforcement, Distribution, Aggregation, Propagation?

back.text: The three valid TrustSec phases are: Classification, Propagation, and Enforcement. 'Distribution' and 'Aggregation' are wrong-answer distractors.
Exam trap: 'Distribution' sounds like 'Propagation' but is incorrect. Memorize the order: Classify → Propagate → Enforce.

front.text: Exam tip: What happens when a tagged frame arrives at a device that does NOT support inline tagging in hardware?

back.text: The frame is dropped. Inline (native) tagging requires Cisco hardware with ASIC support for TrustSec. Devices without that ASIC support cannot process the SGT field and will drop tagged frames. Use SXP as the alternative for non-inline-capable devices.
Exam tip: Inline tagging = ASIC required = Cisco-only. SXP = software-based = wider compatibility.

front.text: Exam tip: Why is MACsec called hop-by-hop, and why does that matter?

back.text: MACsec encrypts traffic only on the wire between two adjacent MACsec peers. At each switch, the frame is decrypted internally so the switch can inspect SGT tags, apply QoS, and enforce policy — then re-encrypted on the next hop. This is the opposite of IPsec, which is end-to-end.
Exam tip: MACsec = Layer 2, hop-by-hop, AES-GCM via ASIC. IPsec = Layer 3, end-to-end.

front.text: Exam tip: Which keying protocol is used for downlink MACsec vs uplink MACsec by default?

back.text: Exam tip: Downlink = MKA (standards-based, supports endpoints). Uplink = SAP (Cisco-only by default).

front.text: Exam tip: What's the difference between 'FirePOWER' and 'Firepower' in Cisco documentation?

rows: Cisco Secure IPS (NGIPS) services software / NGIPS ASA module
Cisco Secure Firewall or FTD unified image

back.text: Exam tip: Capitalization matters! UPPERCASE POWER = IPS-only module. lowercase power = unified firewall.

front.text: Exam tip: How long does MAB fallback take after 802.1x is enabled, and why?

back.text: Approximately 90 seconds. The switch sends EAP-Request/Identity messages every 30 seconds; after 3 timeouts (3 × 30s = 90s), it concludes no supplicant exists and falls back to MAB. If 802.1x is NOT enabled, MAB starts immediately at link-up. Enhanced FlexAuth eliminates this delay by running 802.1x and MAB concurrently.
Exam tip: 90-second delay = sequential 802.1x → MAB. FlexAuth = concurrent = no delay.

front.text: Exam tip: If a question asks about WebAuth + posture, AUP, dVLAN, or CoA — which type is required?

back.text: Centralized Web Authentication (CWA) with Cisco ISE is required. LWA does NOT support VLAN assignment, CoA, posture, AUP acceptance, password change, or self-registration. Only CWA supports those advanced services.
Exam tip: Any 'advanced' WebAuth feature → CWA + ISE. Basic switch portal → LWA.

front.text: Exam tip: Cisco rebranded most of its security portfolio. What are the most common name swaps you'll see on the exam?

back.text: Exam tip: Both old and new names may appear on the exam — recognize both.

front.text: Exam tip: What hardware or software must be installed on-premises to deploy Cisco Umbrella?

back.text: None. Cisco Umbrella is 100% cloud-delivered with no hardware to install or software to maintain on-premises. Deployment is as simple as changing the DHCP-assigned DNS servers to point to Umbrella. For roaming clients, the Umbrella module in Cisco Secure Client or the standalone roaming client can be used.
Exam keyword: 'cloud-delivered DNS-layer security' → Cisco Umbrella.

front.text: Exam tip: 'Security Group Tag' or 'Scalable Group Tag' — are they the same thing?

back.text: Yes — both abbreviate to SGT and refer to the same tagging mechanism. 'Security Group Tag' is the TrustSec terminology, and 'Scalable Group Tag' is the Cisco SD-Access terminology. Functionally they are identical.
Exam tip: TrustSec = Security; SD-Access = Scalable. Both = SGT.

front.text: What is the function of the implicit deny ACE found at the end of every ACL?

back.text: It denies all packets that did not match any previous permit or deny statements in the ACL.

front.text: How do you convert a subnet mask of 255.255.255.0 into a wildcard mask for an ACL?
What is the primary difference between a PACL and a VACL in terms of application?

back.text: PACLs (Port ACLs) are applied to Layer 2 switch ports, while VACLs (VLAN ACLs) are applied to entire VLANs.

front.text: Which password types are considered 'uncrackable' and recommended by Cisco?

back.text: Type 8 (PBKDF2 with SHA-256) and Type 9 (scrypt).

front.text: What is the difference between 'login' and 'login local' on a terminal line?

back.text: 'login' enables line password authentication, while 'login local' enables username-based authentication using the local user database.

front.text: What commands are available at privilege level 0?

back.text: disable, enable, exit, help, and logout.

front.text: Compare TACACS+ and RADIUS regarding encryption.

back.text: TACACS+ encrypts the entire payload, whereas RADIUS encrypts only the password field.

front.text: Why is TACACS+ preferred over RADIUS for network device administration?

back.text: TACACS+ separates authentication, authorization, and accounting, allowing for granular command-level authorization, which RADIUS cannot do effectively.

front.text: What is the purpose of the 'if-authenticated' keyword in AAA authorization commands?

back.text: It ensures that if the AAA server is unreachable, commands are authorized as long as the user has successfully authenticated locally.

front.text: What are the two system-built zones in ZBFW?

back.text: The 'self' zone (router's own IP addresses) and the 'default' zone (interfaces not assigned to a specific security zone).

front.text: List the five steps to configure ZBFW.

items: 1. Configure security zones
2. Define inspection class maps
3. Define inspection policy maps
4. Configure zone pairs and associate policy maps
5. Apply zones to interfaces

front.text: What are the three possible firewall actions in a ZBFW policy map?

items: drop
pass
inspect

front.text: What is the primary purpose of Control Plane Policing (CoPP)?

back.text: To protect the router's CPU from unexpected extreme rates of traffic by policing traffic destined for the control plane.

front.text: How do you apply a CoPP policy to the control plane?

back.text:
control-plane
 service-policy input POLICY-NAME

front.text: Why should you disable CDP and LLDP on public-facing interfaces?

back.text: They provide unnecessary topology information to routers outside your control, increasing the information available to potential attackers.

front.text: What is the risk of leaving 'ip proxy-arp' enabled on an interface?

back.text: It can facilitate man-in-the-middle attacks where a host spoofs the router's MAC address to intercept traffic.

front.text: What is the benefit of using the 'start-stop' keyword in AAA accounting?

back.text: It ensures that accounting logs are generated at both the start and the end of a user session, providing a complete audit trail.

front.text: How many inbound and outbound ACLs can be applied to a single interface?

back.text: Only one inbound ACL and one outbound ACL per interface.

front.text: What are the three mandatory configuration steps to enable SSH on a router?

items: 1. Set a hostname
2. Set a domain name
3. Generate RSA crypto keys

front.text: If you are using the 'pass' action in ZBFW, what must you remember regarding zone pairs?

back.text: The 'pass' action is unidirectional; you must configure a second zone pair for the return traffic.

front.text: Which TCP port does TACACS+ use for communication?

back.text: TCP port 49.

front.text: Why is 'service password-encryption' considered an insecure method for password protection?

back.text: It uses Type 7 encryption, which is a weak, easily reversible proprietary algorithm.

front.text: Why is 'violate-action transmit' often used initially when deploying CoPP?

back.text: To avoid accidentally dropping legitimate traffic while establishing a baseline for normal traffic flows.

front.text: Which interface command disables the sending of ICMP redirect messages?

back.text: no ip redirects

front.text: In the command 'aaa authentication login default group ISE-TACACS+ local', what happens if the TACACS+ server is unreachable?

back.text: The device falls back to local username-based authentication.

front.text: How does a router process an ACL when a packet arrives?

back.text: It evaluates the packet against the ACEs sequentially from top to bottom. The first match determines the action (permit/deny), and processing stops.

front.text: What is the function of the 'inspect' action in ZBFW?

back.text: It provides stateful inspection, allowing return traffic automatically without needing an explicit return policy.

front.text: What command configures a 5-minute idle timeout for a terminal line?

back.text: exec-timeout 5 0

front.text: What is the command to enable command authorization for privilege level 15?

back.text: aaa authorization commands 15 default group ISE-TACACS+ if-authenticated

front.text: Why should 'no service config' be configured on a router?

back.text: It prevents the device from automatically loading configurations from remote servers via TFTP, which is a potential security vulnerability.

front.text: What is the range for numbered standard ACLs?
What is the range for numbered extended ACLs?
What is the primary CLI syntax difference when creating a Named ACL versus a Numbered ACL?

back.text: Named ACLs use the 'ip access-list [standard|extended] [name]' command, which enters a specific sub-mode, whereas numbered ACLs are configured directly in global configuration mode.

front.text: What is a major limitation of PACLs regarding traffic direction?

back.text: PACLs only support filtering incoming traffic; they do not support outbound filtering.

front.text: What command is used to apply a VLAN access map to specific VLANs?

back.text: vlan filter [map-name] vlan-list [list]

front.text: What is the default privilege level for Privileged EXEC mode?

back.text: Level 15.

front.text: How do you assign a specific command to a custom privilege level?

back.text: privilege [mode] level [level] [command string]

front.text: What does the 'transport input none' command achieve on a VTY line?

back.text: It blocks both Telnet and SSH access to that line.

front.text: What is the difference between 'exec-timeout' and 'absolute-timeout'?

back.text: 'exec-timeout' triggers after a period of inactivity. 'absolute-timeout' terminates the session regardless of activity after the timer expires.

front.text: Which command displays a warning to a user before an absolute timeout terminates their session?

back.text: logout-warning [seconds]

front.text: Which UDP ports are used by the industry-standard implementation of RADIUS?

back.text: UDP 1812 for authentication/authorization and UDP 1813 for accounting.

front.text: What is the global command required to enable AAA functionality on an IOS XE device?

back.text: aaa new-model

front.text: Why is AAA authorization for the console line disabled by default?

back.text: To prevent inexperienced users from locking themselves out of the device if the AAA server is unreachable.

front.text: What command enables AAA authorization for configuration mode commands?

back.text: aaa authorization config-commands

front.text: What command enables command accounting for privilege level 15?

back.text: aaa accounting commands 15 default start-stop group [group-name]

front.text: In a method list, how does the router process multiple authentication methods?

back.text: It processes them sequentially from left to right. If the first method fails or is unreachable, it moves to the next.

front.text: How does ZBFW differ from a traditional static ACL?

back.text: ZBFW is stateful and inspects Layers 4-7, whereas static ACLs are stateless and only filter based on headers.

front.text: What is the 'self' zone in ZBFW?

back.text: A system-level zone that includes all the router's own IP addresses.

front.text: What is the 'default' zone in ZBFW?

back.text: A system-level zone that automatically contains any interface not assigned to a specific security zone.

front.text: What is the difference between 'match-all' and 'match-any' in a ZBFW class map?

back.text: 'match-all' requires all conditions to be met (Boolean AND), while 'match-any' requires only one condition to be met (Boolean OR).

front.text: Why is the order of zones in a 'zone-pair' command significant?

back.text: The first zone specified is the source zone, and the second is the destination zone.

front.text: What command allows you to view traffic statistics for a specific ZBFW zone pair?

back.text: show policy-map type inspect zone-pair [zone-pair-name]

front.text: What does the 'police' command's 'conform-action' do?

back.text: It defines the action taken for traffic that is within the defined rate (typically 'transmit').

front.text: How can you identify necessary traffic classes for CoPP if they are unknown?

back.text: Use the Cisco Embedded Packet Capture (EPC) feature to capture traffic and analyze it in a PCAP file.

front.text: What command disables the Maintenance Operation Protocol (MOP) service globally?

back.text: no mop enabled

front.text: What command disables the Packet Assembler/Disassembler (PAD) service?

back.text: no service pad

front.text: What is the benefit of enabling 'service tcp-keepalive-in'?

back.text: It ensures that orphaned or half-open connections are removed from the device, freeing up resources.

front.text: What command disables the automatic loading of configuration files from remote devices?

back.text: no service config

front.text: What happens to traffic between interfaces in the same security zone by default?

back.text: It is permitted to communicate freely.

front.text: What happens to traffic between interfaces in different security zones by default?

back.text: It is dropped unless a specific policy is configured.

front.text: What does a wildcard bit of 0 represent in an ACL mask?

back.text: It means the corresponding bit in the IP address must be matched exactly.

front.text: What does a wildcard bit of 1 represent in an ACL mask?

back.text: It means the corresponding bit in the IP address is ignored (a 'don't care' bit).

front.text: What is the equivalent command for 'access-list 1 permit 0.0.0.0 255.255.255.255'?

back.text: access-list 1 permit any

front.text: What is the equivalent command for 'access-list 1 permit 192.168.1.1 0.0.0.0'?

back.text: access-list 1 permit host 192.168.1.1

front.text: Can PACLs filter Layer 2 control packets like STP or CDP?

back.text: No, PACLs cannot filter Layer 2 control packets.

front.text: What algorithm does a Type 5 password use?

back.text: MD5 hashing with password salting.

front.text: What algorithm does a Type 8 password use?

back.text: PBKDF2 with SHA-256 hashing and password salting.

front.text: What algorithm does a Type 9 password use?

back.text: scrypt hashing and password salting.

front.text: What is the recommended command to create a username with Type 9 password hashing?

back.text: username [name] algorithm-type scrypt secret [password]

front.text: Why should Type 4 passwords never be used?

back.text: They contain a known security flaw (cisco-sa-20130318-type4) and have been deprecated.

front.text: What is the default privilege level for User EXEC mode?

back.text: Level 1.

front.text: Which AAA protocol supports EAP for 802.1X authentication?

back.text: RADIUS.

front.text: Does TACACS+ support EAP?

back.text: No, TACACS+ does not support EAP.

front.text: What is the command to add a TACACS+ server on IOS XE 16.12.2 and later?

back.text:
tacacs server [name]
 address ipv4 [ip]
 key [key]

front.text: What happens if you configure AAA command authorization but the AAA server is unreachable and you have no fallback method?

back.text: The user will be unable to execute any further commands.

front.text: What command creates a security zone?

back.text: zone security [zone-name]

front.text: What command associates an interface with a security zone?

back.text: zone-member security [zone-name]

front.text: What is the default action for the implicit class-default in a ZBFW policy map?

back.text: Drop.

front.text: When should you use the 'pass' action in ZBFW?

back.text: For inherently secure protocols with predictable behavior, such as IPsec or ESP.

front.text: What command verifies the configuration of inspection class maps?

back.text: show class-map type inspect

front.text: In a CoPP policy, what is the purpose of the 'violate-action'?

back.text: It defines the action to take when traffic exceeds the policed rate and the exceed threshold (usually 'drop').

front.text: Why should you include a 'class-default' in a CoPP policy map?

back.text: To catch and monitor unknown traffic that doesn't match any defined classes.

front.text: What command verifies the statistics of a CoPP policy applied to the control plane?

back.text: show policy-map control-plane input

front.text: What command disables the Cisco Discovery Protocol (CDP) on an interface?

back.text: no cdp enable

front.text: What command disables LLDP transmission on an interface?

back.text: no lldp transmit

front.text: What command disables LLDP reception on an interface?

back.text: no lldp receive

front.text: What command ensures the device sends TCP keepalives for outbound sessions?

back.text: service tcp-keepalive-out

front.text: What command disables proxy ARP on an interface?

back.text: no ip proxy-arp

front.text: What is the purpose of the 'aaa authorization console' command?

back.text: It explicitly enables AAA authorization for the console line, which is disabled by default.
