front.text: What is the difference between 'log' and 'log-input' in an extended ACL?

back.text: 'log' logs the packet information; 'log-input' logs the packet information plus the input interface and source MAC address.

front.text: What is the implicit action at the end of every ACL, and why does it matter?

back.text: Every ACL ends with an implicit deny any. Any packet that does not match an explicit permit ACE earlier in the list is dropped. This is why you must include an explicit 'permit any' (or specific permits) when you only intend to filter a few sources.

front.text: How do you convert a subnet mask to a wildcard mask, and what is the wildcard for 255.255.128.0?
Compare the number ranges used for numbered standard vs. numbered extended IPv4 ACLs.
Which command applies an ACL to an interface, and how many inbound/outbound ACLs are allowed per interface?

back.text:
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 ip access-group {acl-number | name} {in | out}
Cisco routers allow only one inbound and one outbound ACL per interface.

front.text: List the processing order of PACL, VACL, and RACL for traffic routed across VLANs.

items: Inbound PACL on the ingress switch port
Inbound VACL on the ingress VLAN
Inbound ACL on the ingress SVI
Outbound ACL on the egress SVI
Outbound VACL on the egress VLAN

back.text: Outbound PACLs are not supported.

front.text: Name three common restrictions of Port ACLs (PACLs).

items: Filter only inbound traffic (no outbound)
Cannot filter L2 control packets like CDP, VTP, DTP, PAgP, UDLD, STP
Supported only in hardware
Do not filter IPv6, ARP, or MPLS traffic

front.text: Show the four-step CLI flow to create and apply a VACL that drops ICMP on VLAN 20.

back.text:
ip access-list extended ICMP
 permit icmp any any
!
vlan access-map VACL_20 10
 match ip address ICMP
 action drop
vlan access-map VACL_20 20
 match ip address OTHER
 action forward
!
vlan filter VACL_20 vlan-list 20

front.text: Identify the three CLI access line types on IOS XE and how each is used.
Compare Cisco IOS XE password types 0, 5, 7, 8, and 9 and identify which are recommended.
What does 'service password-encryption' actually do, and what is its key limitation?

back.text: It converts existing and future type 0 (plaintext) passwords in the configuration to type 7 encryption. The limitation is that type 7 uses a weak Cisco Vigenere cipher that can be decrypted in seconds. It only protects against shoulder-surfing, not real attackers.

front.text: What is the recommended command to create a local user with the strongest password hashing?

back.text:
username NETADMIN privilege 15 algorithm-type scrypc secret cisco123
! algorithm-type options: md5 (type 5), sha256 (type 8), scrypt (type 9)
scrypt (type 9) is the Cisco-recommended option.

front.text: What is the difference between the line commands 'login' and 'login local'?
What commands are available at IOS XE privilege level 0, and what are the default uses of levels 1 and 15?

items: Level 0: disable, enable, exit, help, logout
Level 1: User EXEC mode (R1>) — show commands but no configure terminal
Level 15: Privileged EXEC (R1#) — full access
Levels 2–14: Available for custom RBAC via 'privilege' command

front.text: Show two ways to restrict vty inbound access to SSH only.

back.text:
! Method 1: transport input
line vty 0 4
 transport input ssh

! Method 2: access-class
ip access-list extended SSH-IN
 permit tcp 10.0.0.0 0.0.0.255 any eq 22
line vty 0 4
 access-class SSH-IN in

front.text: List the steps required to enable SSH on an IOS XE router.

items: Set a hostname (not 'Router')
Set an IP domain name: ip domain-name cisco.com
Generate RSA keys: crypto key generate rsa (≥768 bits for SSHv2; 2048+ recommended)
Configure local users or AAA
Enable login local under line vty
Optionally force SSHv2 only: ip ssh version 2

front.text: Define the three functions of the AAA framework.

items: Authentication: Verifies user identity before access is granted
Authorization: Defines what an authenticated user is allowed to do
Accounting: Logs user activity (logins, commands, start/stop times)

front.text: Compare RADIUS and TACACS+ across protocol, encryption, AAA separation, and primary use case.
Show the IOS XE 16.12.2+ syntax to configure a TACACS+ server, group, and login authentication with local fallback.

back.text:
aaa new-model
!
tacacs server ISE-PRIMARY
 address ipv4 10.10.10.1
 key my.S3cR3t.k3y
!
aaa group server tacacs+ ISE-TACACS+
 server name ISE-PRIMARY
!
aaa authentication login default group ISE-TACACS+ local
aaa authorization exec default group ISE-TACACS+ if-authenticated
aaa authorization commands 15 default group ISE-TACACS+ if-authenticated
aaa accounting commands 15 default start-stop group ISE-TACACS+

front.text: Why is the 'if-authenticated' method recommended at the end of every AAA authorization command?

back.text: When all AAA servers become unreachable, login may fall back to local authentication, but authorization can still try to reach the AAA server, blocking commands. 'if-authenticated' lets authenticated users execute commands when the AAA servers are unreachable, preventing lockout.
if-authenticated and 'none' are mutually exclusive — 'none' disables authorization entirely.

front.text: By default, does AAA authorization apply to the console line, and what command changes that?

back.text: No. Console authorization is disabled by default to prevent accidental lockout. To enable it, configure 'aaa authorization console' in global configuration mode.

front.text: What is ZBFW and what is its core operating principle for inter-zone traffic?

back.text: Zone-Based Firewall is an integrated stateful firewall in IOS XE. Interfaces are placed into security zones. Traffic within the same zone is allowed; traffic between different zones is dropped by default until an explicit policy (zone-pair + service-policy) permits or inspects it.

front.text: What are the two system-built ZBFW zones, and how do they behave?

items: self zone — represents the router's own IP addresses (control/management plane). Traffic to/from self is permitted by default until a policy is applied to a self zone-pair.
default zone — any interface not assigned to another zone is placed here. Allows policies between unzoned and zoned interfaces.

front.text: Compare the three ZBFW policy-map actions: drop, pass, and inspect.
List the five steps to configure a ZBFW policy.

items: Define security zones with 'zone security ZONE-NAME'
Create inspect class-maps that match traffic (often via ACLs)
Create an inspect policy-map that applies drop/pass/inspect to each class
Create a zone-pair (source → destination) and apply the service-policy
Assign interfaces to zones with 'zone-member security ZONE-NAME'

front.text: After applying an OUTSIDE-to-SELF policy, the router can no longer ping 8.8.8.8 from itself. Why, and how do you fix it?

back.text: Once any policy is applied to a zone-pair involving the self zone, traffic in the opposite direction also requires an explicit policy. Locally originated pings need a SELF-to-OUTSIDE zone-pair with a policy that inspects (or passes) ICMP.

zone-pair security SELF-TO-OUTSIDE source self destination OUTSIDE
 service-policy type inspect POLICY-SELF-TO-OUTSIDE

front.text: What is Control Plane Policing (CoPP) and what does it protect?

back.text: CoPP is a QoS policy applied to traffic destined for (or sourced by) the router's route processor / CPU. It rate-limits known control and management traffic classes to protect the CPU from spikes, malformed traffic, or DoS attacks that could destabilize the device.

front.text: Does CoPP support input policies, output policies, or both? Which is typically used?

back.text: CoPP supports both input and output policies, but typical implementations only use an input policy to rate-limit traffic going to the control plane CPU.

front.text: Show the commands to apply a CoPP policy-map named POLICY-CoPP and verify it.

back.text:
control-plane
 service-policy input POLICY-CoPP
!
! Verify
show policy-map control-plane input

front.text: You're rolling out CoPP for the first time and don't know correct rates yet. What strategy minimizes risk while you build a baseline?

items: Set 'violate-action transmit' on critical classes (routing, IPsec, management) so traffic is not dropped during baselining
Set 'violate-action drop' on low-rate, well-bounded classes (ICMP, DHCP)
Allow a small class-default with monitoring so unexpected traffic is visible
Use 'show policy-map control-plane input' over time to observe conform/exceed/violate counters
Tighten violate actions to 'drop' once normal rates are characterized

front.text: Exam tip: Which IOS XE features and services should be disabled on Internet-facing interfaces to harden a router?

items: Topology discovery: 'no cdp enable', 'no lldp transmit', 'no lldp receive'
ICMP redirects: 'no ip redirects'
Proxy ARP: 'no ip proxy-arp' (mitigates man-in-the-middle)
Auto-config: 'no service config'
MOP: 'no mop enabled' (global and per-interface)
PAD service: 'no service pad'
Enable TCP keepalives: 'service tcp-keepalive-in' / 'service tcp-keepalive-out'

back.text: Common ENCOR exam targets: CDP/LLDP disable and proxy-ARP disable on untrusted interfaces.

front.text: What do the ACL keywords 'any' and 'host' expand to in wildcard form?
Why are named ACLs preferred over numbered ACLs, and what command creates one?

back.text: Named ACLs let you describe the ACL's purpose in the name, which makes large configurations easier to read and audit.

ip access-list extended BLOCK-TELNET
 deny tcp any any eq 23
 permit ip any any

front.text: How does an IOS ACL evaluate packets, and what happens after the first match?

back.text: Evaluation is top-down (lowest sequence first). On the first matching ACE, the permit/deny action is applied and processing stops. ACE order is therefore critical — more-specific entries should appear before more-general ones.

front.text: Which command applies an ACL to a vty line, and how does it differ from 'ip access-group'?

back.text:
line vty 0 4
 access-class MGMT-IN in
'access-class' is used on vty lines to filter inbound or outbound terminal sessions. 'ip access-group' is used on interfaces to filter transit/data traffic.

front.text: What is a downloadable ACL (dACL), and what happens if both a PACL and a dACL are configured on the same switch port?

back.text: A dACL is a per-user ACL pushed dynamically from a RADIUS server (typically Cisco ISE) after successful 802.1X/MAB authentication. If both a static PACL and a dACL are present on the same switch port, the dACL overrides the PACL.

front.text: Map each 'transport input' keyword to what it allows on a vty line.
Exam tip: How should the AUX port be hardened on an IOS XE router?

back.text:
line aux 0
 no exec
 transport input none
'no exec' disables the EXEC process; 'transport input none' blocks reverse Telnet into the AUX port.

front.text: Compare 'exec-timeout' and 'absolute-timeout' on a line.

rows: Disconnects only after the session is idle (default 10 min)
Disconnects after the time limit even if the session is active
Warns user before absolute-timeout forces disconnect

back.text: 'exec-timeout 0 0' disables idle timeout — fine for labs, not production.

front.text: You need a NOC user that can shut/no shut interfaces and set IP addresses, but nothing else. Build a custom privilege level 5 with the user 'noc'.

back.text:
username noc privilege 5 algorithm-type scrypt secret cisco123
privilege exec level 5 configure terminal
privilege configure level 5 interface
privilege interface level 5 shutdown
privilege interface level 5 no shutdown
privilege interface level 5 ip address
When you assign a multi-keyword command (like 'no shutdown') to a level, the leading keywords ('no', 'shutdown') are also assigned to that level so the command is reachable.

front.text: Compare SSHv1 vs SSHv2, and what does the 'SSH 1.99' log message mean?

items: SSHv1 — original; has security flaws; avoid
SSHv2 — full rework, FIPS 140-1/140-2 certified; required for compliance
SSH 1.99 — IOS supports both v1 and v2 simultaneously; force v2 only with 'ip ssh version 2'

front.text: Exam tip: What RSA modulus is required for SSHv2, and what's the modern Cisco default behavior?

back.text: SSHv2 requires at least a 768-bit RSA modulus. Starting in IOS XE 17.11.1, RSA keys smaller than 2048 bits are rejected by default as cryptographically weak (per Cisco Field Notice 72510). 2048+ is the practical minimum today.

front.text: How are AAA method lists processed, and what is the difference between 'default' and a named (custom) list?

back.text: Methods are tried left-to-right; the next method is used only if the previous one is unavailable (server unreachable). The 'default' list applies to all lines automatically. A named list applies only when explicitly attached to a line with 'login authentication NAME', 'authorization exec NAME', etc.
aaa authentication login default group ISE-TACACS+ local enable

front.text: What does the 'start-stop' keyword do for AAA accounting, and why is it preferred?

back.text: 'start-stop' sends an accounting record when a session/command starts and another when it ends. It gives the AAA server real-time visibility into active sessions and a clear audit trail of who did what and for how long, without delaying the session start (unlike 'wait-start').

front.text: Show the commands required to enable per-command TACACS+ authorization for privilege levels 0, 1, and 15, including config-mode commands.

back.text:
aaa authorization commands 0  default group ISE-TACACS+ if-authenticated
aaa authorization commands 1  default group ISE-TACACS+ if-authenticated
aaa authorization commands 15 default group ISE-TACACS+ if-authenticated
aaa authorization config-commands
'aaa authorization config-commands' is required for global-config and sub-config commands to be authorized, not just EXEC commands.

front.text: Exam tip: What two safeguards should you always configure before enabling AAA on a production device?

items: Create a local privilege 15 user as a fallback: 'username admin privilege 15 algorithm-type scrypt secret ...'
Add 'local' (or 'enable') as a fallback method on every authentication/authorization method list
Open a second session (e.g., console) before enabling AAA so you can recover if locked out

front.text: After enabling 'aaa authorization commands 15 default group ISE-TACACS+', users see 'Command authorization failed' when ISE is unreachable. What's wrong and how do you fix it?

back.text: The method list has no fallback. When ISE is unreachable, no method authorizes the command, so every command is denied. Add 'if-authenticated' (or 'local') as a fallback so authenticated users can keep working when AAA is down.
aaa authorization commands 15 default group ISE-TACACS+ if-authenticated

front.text: Which IOS XE commands help verify and troubleshoot AAA operations?

back.text:
show aaa servers
show aaa method-lists all
show tacacs
show privilege
debug aaa authentication
debug aaa authorization
debug tacacs

front.text: Why is a ZBFW typically a better choice than ACLs alone at a branch edge?
In a ZBFW inspect class-map, what is the difference between 'match-all' and 'match-any', and which is the default?

rows: Boolean AND — all match conditions must be true
Boolean OR — any one condition is sufficient

front.text: Which IOS XE commands verify ZBFW class-maps, policy-maps, and zone-pair counters?

back.text:
show class-map type inspect
show policy-map type inspect
show policy-map type inspect zone-pair [ZONE-PAIR-NAME]
show zone security
show zone-pair security

front.text: Inside hosts can reach the Internet, but Internet replies are dropped. Both interfaces are in zones and a zone-pair INSIDE → OUTSIDE exists with 'inspect'. What's likely wrong?

back.text: If the policy uses 'inspect', return traffic should be permitted automatically. Common causes: the policy uses 'pass' instead of 'inspect' (pass is one-way, requires a reverse zone-pair); the inspect class-map doesn't actually match the originating traffic; or interfaces aren't members of the expected zones. Verify with 'show policy-map type inspect zone-pair' and 'show zone security'.

front.text: Define the policer's three rate buckets used in CoPP: conform, exceed, and violate.

rows: Traffic above CIR but within burst (Bc/Be)
Traffic above CIR + burst — typically dropped

front.text: Why should the class-default in a CoPP policy be policed at a small rate rather than dropped outright?

back.text: Allowing a small amount of traffic in class-default lets you discover unexpected or new control-plane traffic by watching the counters. Dropping everything would silently break legitimate but uncategorized traffic and remove visibility into what's hitting the CPU.

front.text: Exam tip: How does CoPP differ on Catalyst 9000 series switches compared to ISR/ASR routers?

back.text: Catalyst 9000 series platforms ship with a built-in default CoPP policy that protects the CPU out of the box and typically does not need to be modified. On routers, you usually build CoPP from scratch. Always check platform-specific documentation for restrictions before customizing.

front.text: Which IOS XE feature is recommended for identifying control-plane traffic classes when designing a CoPP policy?

back.text: Cisco Embedded Packet Capture (EPC). It captures live traffic on the device and can export to PCAP, allowing you to identify protocols and rates hitting the control plane so you can build accurate match criteria and CIRs for each CoPP class.

front.text: What is proxy ARP, why is it a security risk, and how do you disable it?

back.text: Proxy ARP lets a router answer ARP requests on behalf of another device (typically a remote subnet's router). An attacker can spoof the router's MAC and intercept traffic — a man-in-the-middle attack. Disable on each interface with 'no ip proxy-arp'.

front.text: When does an IOS XE router send an ICMP redirect, and how do you disable that behavior?

back.text: It sends an ICMP redirect when traffic 'hairpins' — arrives and leaves on the same interface — to inform the host of a more direct next-hop. On untrusted/Internet-facing interfaces, disable with the interface command 'no ip redirects' to avoid leaking topology hints.

front.text: How do you disable CDP and LLDP on a single Internet-facing interface without disabling them globally?

back.text:
interface GigabitEthernet0/2
 no cdp enable
 no lldp transmit
 no lldp receive
This keeps neighbor discovery available on internal LAN ports while preventing topology leaks toward the Internet.

front.text: Exam tip: What's the difference between the ACL keywords 'log' and 'log-input', and what should you watch out for?

rows: Above plus the ingress interface and source MAC

back.text: ACL logging punts matching packets to the CPU and can cause CPU spikes — pair with CoPP and use sparingly in production.

front.text: Scenario: You're handed a freshly racked branch router. List a baseline secure-management config that combines this chapter's hardening guidance.

back.text:
! Identity & SSH
hostname BR1
ip domain-name example.com
crypto key generate rsa modulus 2048
ip ssh version 2

! Local fallback user
username admin privilege 15 algorithm-type scrypt secret S0meStr0ng!

! AAA with TACACS+ and local fallback
aaa new-model
tacacs server ISE-1
 address ipv4 10.10.10.1
 key <secret>
aaa group server tacacs+ ISE-TACACS+
 server name ISE-1
aaa authentication login default group ISE-TACACS+ local
aaa authorization exec default group ISE-TACACS+ if-authenticated
aaa authorization commands 15 default group ISE-TACACS+ if-authenticated
aaa authorization config-commands
aaa authorization console
aaa accounting commands 15 default start-stop group ISE-TACACS+

! VTY hardening
ip access-list standard MGMT-IN
 permit 10.0.0.0 0.255.255.255
line vty 0 4
 access-class MGMT-IN in
 transport input ssh
 exec-timeout 5 0

! AUX hardening
line aux 0
 no exec
 transport input none

! Disable unused services
no service pad
no mop enabled
no service config
service tcp-keepalive-in
service tcp-keepalive-out

! Internet-facing interface hardening
interface GigabitEthernet0/2
 no cdp enable
 no lldp transmit
 no lldp receive
 no ip proxy-arp
 no ip redirects
 no mop enabled

front.text: Beyond traffic filtering, list four other Cisco features that use ACLs as classification tools.

items: QoS classification (class-maps matching access-group)
NAT (matching addresses to translate)
Route maps / routing protocol prefix selection
SNMP server access control
VTY line access (access-class)

front.text: What do the extended ACL operators 'eq', 'lt', 'gt', and 'range' do, and which protocols support them?

rows: Match all ports less than the value
Match all ports greater than the value
Match any port not equal to the value

back.text: These port operators are valid only for TCP and UDP ACEs.

front.text: What does the 'established' keyword in an extended TCP ACL match, and what is its limitation?

back.text: It matches TCP segments with the ACK or RST flag set, which approximates 'return traffic of an existing session.' It is stateless — the router does not actually track sessions. An attacker can craft packets with ACK set and bypass the rule. Use a stateful firewall (ZBFW) for real session enforcement.

front.text: How do you insert a new ACE between existing entries in a named ACL without rebuilding it?

back.text:
ip access-list extended INBOUND
  5 permit tcp 10.1.1.0 0.0.0.255 any eq 443
 15 deny ip any any log

! Sequence numbers can be re-spaced with:
ip access-list resequence INBOUND 10 10
Inside the named ACL, prefix a new entry with a sequence number to place it precisely. 'ip access-list resequence' renumbers existing entries (start, increment).

front.text: Which 'show' commands let you verify ACL contents, hit counts, and where ACLs are applied?

back.text:
show access-lists [name|number]
show ip access-lists [name|number]
show ip interface GigabitEthernet0/1   ! shows applied inbound/outbound ACLs
show running-config | include access-group|access-class

front.text: Users report that an ACL is 'blocking everything.' 'show access-lists' shows hits only on the implicit deny. What's the most likely cause and how do you fix it?

back.text: The ACL is missing an explicit 'permit any' (or specific permits) — every packet is hitting the implicit deny at the end. Either add an explicit permit for legitimate traffic, or reorder ACEs so permits come before denies. Use 'show ip access-lists' to confirm hit counts on each ACE.

front.text: What is a time-based ACL, and which feature is required for them to work reliably?

back.text: A time-based ACL only enforces specific ACEs during defined time ranges (e.g., business hours). Configure with 'time-range NAME' and reference it on each ACE with 'time-range NAME'. Accurate time is critical, so the device must be synchronized via NTP.

time-range BUSINESS-HOURS
 periodic weekdays 8:00 to 17:00
!
ip access-list extended WEB
 permit tcp any any eq 80 time-range BUSINESS-HOURS

front.text: Which IOS command rate-limits failed login attempts to mitigate brute-force attacks?

back.text:
login block-for 120 attempts 4 within 60
login delay 2
login on-failure log
login on-success log
After 4 failed attempts in 60 seconds, the router enters quiet mode for 120 seconds and blocks new logins (except hosts allowed by 'login quiet-mode access-class').

front.text: Exam tip: Which login banner should display before login, and which after, and why does it matter legally?

back.text: Never use 'Welcome' wording in pre-login banners — most jurisdictions require an explicit 'authorized users only' notice for prosecution.

front.text: Which SSH server commands harden authentication beyond just enabling SSHv2?

back.text:
ip ssh version 2
ip ssh time-out 60                  ! login negotiation timeout (seconds)
ip ssh authentication-retries 3     ! max attempts per session
ip ssh logging events
ip ssh server algorithm mac hmac-sha2-256 hmac-sha2-512
ip ssh server algorithm encryption aes128-ctr aes192-ctr aes256-ctr
ip ssh dh min size 2048

front.text: How do you configure SSH public-key authentication for a local user on IOS XE?

back.text:
ip ssh pubkey-chain
 username netadmin
  key-string
   AAAAB3NzaC1yc2EA...   ! base64 of public key
   exit
  exit
 exit
Once configured, the user can authenticate with their matching private key instead of a password — stronger and immune to password brute-force.

front.text: Can AAA be used without a remote server? When would you do that?

back.text: Yes. With 'aaa new-model' you can use 'local' as a method (e.g., 'aaa authentication login default local'). This is useful for very small networks or as a transitional configuration before deploying ISE/TACACS+, while keeping the AAA framework in place so accounting and method lists work consistently.

front.text: How do you make TACACS+ requests always source from a specific (e.g., loopback) interface?

back.text: ip tacacs source-interface Loopback0
This ensures the AAA server sees a stable source IP even if a physical interface flaps, and lets you tightly scope the AAA server's network device list to a single trusted address.

front.text: Exam tip: What part of a TACACS+ packet is encrypted, and what part of a RADIUS packet is encrypted?

rows: Entire payload (header is in clear; body is encrypted with shared key)

front.text: A user complains they can authenticate but cannot run any commands. Which debug commands isolate authentication vs authorization issues?

back.text:
debug aaa authentication
debug aaa authorization
debug tacacs
debug tacacs authentication
debug tacacs authorization
show aaa servers
If 'authentication' succeeds but 'authorization' fails, the issue is in the AAA server's command set policy (or a missing 'if-authenticated' fallback when ISE is unreachable).

front.text: After applying any policy involving the self zone, what side effect should you plan for?

back.text: Before any self-zone policy exists, all traffic to/from self is permitted (so SSH, SNMP, BGP, OSPF, EIGRP all just work). Once any policy is applied to a zone-pair involving self, the implicit allow is lost in that direction. You must explicitly permit/inspect required management and routing protocols in BOTH directions (e.g., self-to-OUTSIDE and OUTSIDE-to-self), or you'll lose neighbor adjacencies and remote management.

front.text: Can multiple interfaces belong to the same ZBFW zone, and how does traffic between them behave?

back.text: Yes. Multiple interfaces can be members of the same security zone (a many-to-one relationship). Traffic between interfaces in the same zone is permitted freely without going through any policy — it is treated as intra-zone.

front.text: A new interface was added to the WAN router and is dropping all traffic, even though no zone-pair denies it. Why?

back.text: If an interface is not assigned to a security zone, traffic between it and any zone-member interface is dropped by default. The fix is to assign it to a zone (or enable the default zone) so a zone-pair policy can govern it.

interface GigabitEthernet0/3
 zone-member security INSIDE

front.text: Briefly compare ZBFW with the legacy reflexive ACL / 'ip inspect' (CBAC) approaches.
Show how to define a CoPP class-map for routing protocol traffic that matches OSPF, EIGRP, BGP, and PIM.

back.text:
ip access-list extended ACL-CoPP-Routing
 permit tcp any eq bgp any established
 permit tcp any any eq bgp
 permit eigrp any host 224.0.0.10
 permit ospf any host 224.0.0.5
 permit ospf any host 224.0.0.6
 permit pim  any host 224.0.0.13
 permit igmp any any
!
class-map match-all CLASS-CoPP-Routing
 match access-group name ACL-CoPP-Routing

front.text: In the CoPP policer 'police 64000 bc 2000 be 2000', what do CIR, Bc, and Be represent?

rows: Committed Information Rate, in bps — sustained rate
Committed burst — bytes that can be sent above CIR briefly
Excess burst — additional headroom that triggers 'exceed'

back.text: Think of two token buckets — Bc determines conform/exceed boundary, Be determines exceed/violate boundary.

front.text: Exam tip: What's the most common operational mistake when first deploying CoPP?

back.text: Setting violate-action to 'drop' on routing/management classes before baselining real traffic. This can drop legitimate BGP/OSPF/SSH bursts and break neighbor adjacencies or lock you out. Always start with 'violate-action transmit' on critical classes, monitor counters, then tighten.

front.text: On Catalyst switches, where is CoPP enforced — software or hardware — and why does it matter?

back.text: On Catalyst 9000 platforms, the default CoPP is enforced in hardware (ASIC/FED), so it can rate-limit massive control-plane spikes without taxing the CPU itself. On software-forwarding platforms like older ISRs, CoPP is enforced in software on the route processor, so you must size policer rates conservatively to avoid CPU exhaustion before policing kicks in.

front.text: How do ACLs, ZBFW, and CoPP layer together as defense in depth on a branch router?

items: ACLs (RACL/PACL/VACL): coarse stateless filtering at L2/L3 — first line of defense
ZBFW: stateful inspection of transit traffic between zones — guards user/application flows
CoPP: protects the route processor from control-plane abuse, even if other layers leak traffic
Device hardening (no proxy-arp, no CDP on WAN, SSHv2 only) reduces the attack surface itself

front.text: What is Unicast Reverse Path Forwarding (uRPF), and what are its two modes?

rows: Drops packet unless source is reachable via the same interface it arrived on
Drops packet only if the source has no route in the FIB at all (any interface)

back.text:
interface GigabitEthernet0/2
 ip verify unicast source reachable-via rx   ! strict
! or
 ip verify unicast source reachable-via any  ! loose

front.text: Why and how should IP source routing be disabled on an IOS XE router?

back.text: no ip source-route
IP source routing lets the originator dictate the path, which attackers can use to bypass firewalls or routing policy. It has no legitimate modern use and should be disabled globally.

front.text: Why might you disable ICMP unreachables on an Internet-facing interface, and how?

back.text:
interface GigabitEthernet0/2
 no ip unreachables

Generating unreachables for every dropped packet (e.g., during a scan) is CPU-intensive and also leaks information about ACL/firewall behavior. Disabling them reduces both the DoS amplification and the reconnaissance surface.

front.text: Exam tip: Why is 'no ip directed-broadcast' a standard hardening command, and which classic attack does it mitigate?

back.text: Directed broadcasts let a remote attacker target a subnet's broadcast address. Combined with spoofing, this enables the Smurf attack — flooding a victim with ICMP replies from every host on the subnet. 'no ip directed-broadcast' is the default on modern IOS but should always be confirmed on edge interfaces.

front.text: Briefly: how do DHCP snooping and Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) protect a Layer 2 access network?

items: DHCP snooping — designates 'trusted' uplink ports to legitimate DHCP servers; drops DHCP server messages on untrusted access ports; builds a binding table of IP-to-MAC-to-port
DAI — uses the DHCP snooping binding table to validate ARP replies on access ports; drops gratuitous/forged ARPs to mitigate ARP spoofing and man-in-the-middle attacks
Together they protect against rogue DHCP servers, ARP poisoning, and IP/MAC spoofing

front.text: Exam tip recap: What are the seven biggest Chapter 26 takeaways for the ENCOR 350-401 exam?

items: ACLs are top-down, first-match wins, with an implicit deny at the end — wildcard masks are inverted subnet masks.
PACL → VACL → RACL is the order for routed traffic; outbound PACLs don't exist.
Type 9 (scrypt) is the strongest password; 'service password-encryption' only delivers weak type 7.
TACACS+ (TCP/49, full payload encryption, AAA separated) is for device admin; RADIUS (UDP, password-only encryption, AAA combined, EAP-capable) is for network access.
Always include local fallback and 'if-authenticated' on AAA method lists to avoid lockout.
ZBFW: zones + zone-pairs + policy-maps; once self-zone has any policy, both directions must be explicitly handled.
CoPP protects the CPU; baseline with violate-action transmit, then tighten — and remember Catalyst 9K has a default hardware CoPP policy.

front.text: Exam tip: How do you write a wildcard mask that matches only odd-numbered or only even-numbered IP addresses in a subnet?

back.text: Wildcard bits don't have to be contiguous. Setting only the low-order bit to 0 in the wildcard forces that bit to match exactly, isolating odd vs even.

front.text: Exam tip: Where should you place a standard ACL versus an extended ACL, and why?

items: Standard ACL — match source only, so place it as close to the destination as possible to avoid blocking legitimate traffic to other destinations.
Extended ACL — match source, destination, protocol, and ports, so place it as close to the source as possible to drop unwanted traffic before it consumes bandwidth.

front.text: Exam tip: How do you decide between applying an ACL inbound vs outbound on an interface?

back.text: Direction is always relative to the router. Inbound ACLs are processed before routing — they save CPU on dropped packets and can protect the router itself. Outbound ACLs are processed after routing — useful when one ACL must filter traffic from many ingress interfaces toward a single destination.
Only inbound ACLs can protect the router's own control plane on that interface.

front.text: Exam tip: When using 'access-class' on a vty line, why does a standard ACL usually work fine even though the traffic is technically TCP/22?

back.text: 'access-class' on a vty matches against the source IP of the inbound terminal connection, not the protocol or destination. A standard ACL keyed on the source address is sufficient to permit/deny who can SSH/Telnet in. Use an extended ACL only if you need to restrict by SSH vs Telnet — but 'transport input ssh' usually handles that more cleanly.

front.text: Exam tip: If both 'enable password' and 'enable secret' are configured, which one is used?

back.text: 'enable secret' always takes precedence over 'enable password' when both are configured. 'enable password' is type 0 (or type 7 with service password-encryption) and is considered legacy; 'enable secret' uses type 5/8/9 hashing. Best practice: configure only 'enable secret' (preferably algorithm-type scrypt) and remove 'enable password'.

front.text: Exam tip: Why does a user defined as 'username admin privilege 15 …' land directly at the # prompt without typing 'enable'?

back.text: Assigning privilege 15 to a username means the user enters EXEC mode at level 15 immediately on login — no 'enable' step is needed. This is convenient but also a common audit finding: any compromise of that one credential is full administrative access. Many sites prefer privilege 1 by default and require an explicit 'enable' (with separate enable secret or AAA enable authentication).

front.text: Exam tip: Why is setting a hostname AND ip domain-name required before generating RSA keys for SSH?

back.text: IOS names the RSA key pair using hostname.domain-name (e.g., R1.cisco.com). If either is missing or set to the default 'Router', key generation fails or produces an unusable key. Set both, then run 'crypto key generate rsa' — and use a modulus of 2048 or higher per modern guidance (Field Notice 72510).

front.text: Exam tip: A method in an AAA list only fails over to the next method when what condition is true?

back.text: Failover only happens when the current method is unavailable (e.g., AAA server unreachable). If the server replies 'access-reject', that's a valid response — the next method is NOT tried. So a wrong password on TACACS+ does not silently fall through to local authentication.

front.text: Exam tip: Why does AAA authorization NOT apply to the console line by default, and how is this commonly tested?

back.text: Cisco intentionally excludes the console from AAA authorization to prevent accidental lockout (e.g., misconfigured ISE policy). Exam questions often present a scenario where TACACS+ command authorization seems to fail on the console — the answer is to add 'aaa authorization console' globally, or to apply a custom 'authorization' list to line con 0.

front.text: Exam tip: Memorize the four RADIUS UDP ports and which are Cisco-legacy vs IETF-standard.
Exam tip: Be ready to justify why TACACS+ — not RADIUS — is preferred for device administration.

items: TACACS+ separates Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting — each can use a different policy
TACACS+ supports per-command authorization in real time; RADIUS would have to push every allowed command in the initial response (memory-intensive)
TACACS+ encrypts the entire payload; RADIUS only encrypts the password field
TACACS+ uses TCP (reliable) on port 49, vs RADIUS UDP

front.text: Exam tip: What is the default action for inter-zone traffic in a ZBFW when no zone-pair exists?

back.text: Drop. Traffic between two different zones is denied unless an explicit zone-pair with a policy-map permits or inspects it. This is the inverse of legacy ACL-based filtering, where unconfigured traffic flows by default.

front.text: Exam tip: When should you use 'pass' instead of 'inspect' in a ZBFW policy?

back.text: Use 'pass' for inherently secure or non-stateful protocols whose return traffic is unpredictable or asymmetric — examples: ESP (IPsec), GRE, OSPF, EIGRP, and routing protocol multicasts. 'inspect' would attempt to track state for these and may incorrectly drop legitimate traffic. Note: 'pass' is one-way — you must create a reverse zone-pair if both directions are needed.

front.text: Exam tip: Why is it usually a bad idea to add 'log' to the 'drop' action in a ZBFW class-default?

back.text: Class-default catches a high volume of unmatched traffic, especially on Internet-facing zone pairs (scans, leaked broadcasts, etc.). Logging every dropped packet floods syslog and can spike CPU. Log specific named class-maps for visibility, but leave class-default as a silent drop.

front.text: Exam tip: 'Input' on the control plane means traffic going where?

back.text: Input on the control plane means traffic going TO the route processor / CPU (i.e., punted from the data plane). Output means traffic generated BY the route processor leaving toward the data plane. Almost all CoPP deployments use 'service-policy input' because the goal is to protect the CPU from inbound storms.

front.text: Exam tip: What's a subtle gap in a CoPP routing class that only matches 'permit ospf any 224.0.0.5' and 'permit ospf any 224.0.0.6'?

back.text: It only matches multicast OSPF Hellos and DR/BDR multicasts. It does NOT match unicast OSPF traffic — virtual links, sham links, or unicast neighbor LSAs over point-to-point or NBMA networks. The same gap applies to unicast EIGRP and BGP-over-non-direct-neighbors. Add explicit unicast permits for any routing protocol that may run unicast in your environment.

front.text: Exam tip: When tuning a CoPP management class, why should SSH and SNMP rates be different from routing rates?

back.text: Management traffic (SSH bulk transfers, SNMP walks, NetFlow exports) is bursty and human-driven, while routing traffic is steady and protocol-driven. A single shared rate forces you to over-provision (waste) or under-provision (drop). Split into separate class-maps so each can be sized to its actual profile, and so an SNMP storm can't starve OSPF Hellos.

front.text: Exam tip: When hardening a router, which order minimizes the risk of cutting yourself off?

items: Open a console session you can't lose
Configure local fallback user (privilege 15, scrypt secret)
Configure SSHv2, RSA keys, vty access-class, transport input ssh
Enable AAA with local fallback methods
Apply ACLs to interfaces (not vtys yet)
Then enable ZBFW and CoPP, baselining before tightening
Finally, disable unused services (CDP, LLDP, proxy-ARP, etc.)

front.text: Exam tip: Don't confuse these similarly named hardening commands.

back.text: Many of these are off by default on modern IOS XE — verify with 'show running-config all' rather than assuming.

front.text: Exam tip: Five distractor patterns to watch for in Chapter 26 questions.

items: 'service password-encryption' = strong encryption (FALSE — it's weak type 7).
RADIUS encrypts the entire packet (FALSE — only the password field).
'login local' authenticates against the line password (FALSE — it uses the local username database).
ZBFW 'pass' is bidirectional (FALSE — pass is one-way; create a reverse zone-pair).
CoPP class-default should be 'drop' immediately on day one (FALSE — keep some allowance for visibility while baselining).

front.text: What is a hypervisor?

back.text: Virtualization software that creates virtual machines and performs the hardware abstraction allowing multiple VMs to run concurrently on the same physical server.

front.text: Compare Type 1 and Type 2 hypervisors.

rows: On top of a host OS

front.text: Name four popular hypervisors used in the server virtualization market.

items: VMware vSphere (ESXi)
Microsoft Hyper-V
Citrix XenServer
Red Hat Kernel-based Virtual Machine (KVM)

front.text: What is a virtual machine (VM)?

back.text: A software emulation of a physical server with an operating system. From an application's perspective, it looks and behaves like a real physical server, complete with virtualized CPU, memory, and NICs.

front.text: What two key benefits does VM migration provide?

items: Maintenance without downtime — VMs can be moved to other hosts during hardware upgrades while preserving transactional integrity.
High availability — if a host fails, VMs can be spun up on other hosts in the cluster.

front.text: What is a bare-metal server?

back.text: A physical server running a single operating system dedicated to a single user/workload — no hypervisor or virtualization layer. CPU utilization is typically only 10–25%, which is one of the main drivers behind server virtualization.

front.text: What is a container?

back.text: An isolated environment where containerized applications run. It includes the application plus the dependencies (binaries, libraries) it needs to run, and shares the kernel of the host OS — it does NOT include its own guest OS.

front.text: Compare VMs and containers.

rows: Shares host OS kernel — no guest OS

front.text: Name several container engines used to create, run, and manage containers.

items: Docker (most popular)
rkt ("rocket")
Open Container Initiative (OCI)
LXD (Canonical)
Linux-VServer
Windows Containers

front.text: What is a container image?

back.text: A file created by a container engine that includes the application code along with its dependencies. Container images become containers when they are run by the container engine, and they are highly portable.

front.text: What is a virtual switch (vSwitch)?

back.text: A software-based Layer 2 switch that operates like a physical Ethernet switch. It enables VMs to communicate with each other inside a virtualized server and with external networks via the host's physical NICs (pNICs).

front.text: What are two key rules about multiple vSwitches on a single virtualized host?

items: Network traffic cannot flow directly from one vSwitch to another vSwitch within the same host (it must go through a VM/VNF, e.g., a virtual firewall).
vSwitches on the same host cannot share the same pNIC.

front.text: What problem does distributed virtual switching solve, and what are its main benefits?

back.text: Standard vSwitches must be configured individually on every host. A distributed vSwitch aggregates the vSwitches across a cluster and treats them as a single switch.

items: Centralized management of vSwitch config across multiple hosts
Networking statistics and policies migrate with the VM during live VM migration
Configuration consistency across all hosts in the distributed switch

front.text: How does Docker network containers within a single host by default?

back.text: Docker creates a default virtual bridge called Docker0, assigned the subnet 172.17.0.0/16. Each container gets a virtual Ethernet (veth) interface on Docker0 that appears as eth0 inside the container, with an IP from the bridge subnet. Containers on the same host can communicate; containers on other hosts are not reachable by default and require OS-level routing or an overlay network (e.g., Kubernetes).

front.text: What is Network Functions Virtualization (NFV)?

back.text: An architectural framework created by ETSI that defines standards to decouple network functions from proprietary hardware-based appliances and run them in software on standard x86 servers. It also defines how to manage and orchestrate those functions.

front.text: What is a Virtual Network Function (VNF)?

back.text: The virtual or software version of a network function (such as a firewall, router, load balancer, or IDS) that typically runs on a hypervisor as a VM. VNFs are most often used for L4–L7 services but can also provide L2/L3 functions.

items: Cisco Catalyst 8000V
Cisco Secure Firewall ASA Virtual
Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense Virtual

front.text: Distinguish NF, VNF, NFV, and NFVI.

rows: Function performed by a physical appliance (e.g., firewall, router)
Software/virtual version of an NF, typically a VM on a hypervisor
ETSI architectural framework that decouples NFs from proprietary HW
All HW + SW components that make up the platform where VNFs run

front.text: What is service chaining in the context of NFV?

back.text: Connecting two or more VNFs together in a chain to deliver an NFV service or end-to-end solution (for example: external switch → vLB → vFW → vWAAS → vRouter → external network). The VIM is responsible for VNF service chaining.

front.text: What is NFV MANO and what are its two main components?

back.text: MANO = Management and Orchestration. It consists of:

items: NFV Orchestrator — creates, maintains, and tears down VNF network services; stitches multiple VNFs into end-to-end services.
VNF Manager — manages the lifecycle of one or more VNFs and handles FCAPS for the VNF's virtual components.

front.text: What does the Virtualized Infrastructure Manager (VIM) do in the ETSI NFV framework?

items: Manages and controls NFVI hardware (compute, storage, network) and virtualized resources
Collects performance measurements and fault information
Handles lifecycle management (setup, maintenance, teardown) of NFVI resources
Performs VNF service chaining

front.text: What is an Element Manager (EM/EMS) in NFV?

back.text: The component responsible for the functional management of VNFs, performing FCAPS (Fault, Configuration, Accounting, Performance, Security) functions. A single EM can manage one or many VNFs, and an EM can itself be a VNF.

front.text: Compare north–south and east–west traffic patterns in an NFV host.

rows: pNIC → VNF → pNIC (in/out of the host)
pNIC → VNF → another VNF (service chained), possibly more VNFs, then out via pNIC

back.text: Understanding the pattern helps choose the right I/O technology (OVS-DPDK, PCI passthrough, SR-IOV) for optimal throughput.

front.text: What is OVS-DPDK and how does it improve VNF performance?

back.text: Open vSwitch enhanced with the Data Plane Development Kit (DPDK) libraries. OVS-DPDK runs entirely in user space and uses a Poll Mode Driver (PMD) that polls the pNIC for packets — bypassing the kernel network stack and CPU interrupts. Packets stay in user space, so they can be switched directly to the appropriate VNF.
Key trade-off: PMD requires one or more dedicated CPU cores for polling.

front.text: What is PCI passthrough and what is its main downside?

back.text: PCI passthrough gives a VNF direct, exclusive access to a physical PCI device (such as a pNIC), bypassing the hypervisor. From the VNF's perspective, the pNIC is directly attached.
Benefits

items: Bypasses hypervisor overhead
Direct access to I/O resources
Lower CPU utilization and system latency
Higher I/O throughput

back.text: Downside
The entire pNIC is dedicated to a single VNF (1:1), so the number of VNFs using it is limited by the number of pNICs in the system.

front.text: What is SR-IOV and how does it relate to PCI passthrough?

back.text: Single-Root I/O Virtualization (SR-IOV) is an enhancement to PCI passthrough that allows multiple VNFs to share the same pNIC. SR-IOV emulates multiple PCIe devices on a single physical PCIe device:

items: Physical Function (PF) — the actual physical PCIe device (e.g., the pNIC)
Virtual Function (VF) — the emulated PCIe devices that VNFs attach to via PCI passthrough

front.text: Compare VEB and VEPA switching modes for an SR-IOV-enabled pNIC.

rows: Traffic between VNFs on the same pNIC is hardware switched directly by the pNIC
Traffic between VNFs on the same pNIC is sent up to an external switch which switches it back

front.text: What are the four main components of the Cisco Enterprise NFV (ENFV) solution architecture?

items: Management and Orchestration (MANO) — Cisco DNA Center / Catalyst Center
VNFs — Cisco and third-party virtual network functions
NFVIS — Network Functions Virtualization Infrastructure Software (the OS)
Hardware resources — x86 compute (e.g., Cisco ENCS, Catalyst 8200 uCPE)

front.text: What is Cisco NFVIS and what core components does it include?

back.text: NFVIS is a standard-Linux-based OS extended with functions for virtualization, VNF lifecycle management, monitoring, device programmability, and hardware acceleration.

items: Linux (drives the underlying hardware)
KVM-based hypervisor with QEMU and Libvirt
Open vSwitch (OVS) for vSwitching and service chaining
Embedded Elastic Services Controller (ESC-Lite) for VIM/VNF lifecycle management
Plug and Play client (registers with DNA/Catalyst Center)
Orchestration via REST, CLI, HTTPS, and NETCONF/YANG
HTTPS web server, device management tools, and RBAC

front.text: Which platform plays the role of orchestrator in Cisco's Enterprise NFV solution, and how does it onboard new branch sites?

back.text: Cisco DNA Center (Catalyst Center) provides MANO functionality. Branches are onboarded using Plug and Play (PnP):

items: An admin builds a network profile (LAN/WAN interfaces, VNFs, service chaining, CPU/memory, custom config templates).
Branch sites are assigned to a network profile that matches their requirements.
When a new ENFV platform powers up, its PnP client registers with the PnP server in DNA Center.
DNA Center matches the site to its profile and provisions/onboards the device automatically (zero-touch).

front.text: Scenario: A branch x86 host runs four VNFs (vRouter, vFW, vWAAS, vWLC) that must service-chain east–west traffic at high throughput. The host has only two pNICs. Which I/O technology should you choose, and why not the others?

back.text: Best choice: SR-IOV (or OVS-DPDK if heavy east–west service chaining inside the host is required).

items: PCI passthrough → rejected: it dedicates an entire pNIC to one VNF (1:1). With only two pNICs you could not serve four VNFs.
Standard OVS → rejected: kernel/user-space context switches and CPU interrupts cap throughput on high-speed NICs.
SR-IOV → multiple VFs on one PF allow several VNFs to share a pNIC with near-passthrough performance.
OVS-DPDK → user-space PMD bypasses the kernel and is ideal when most traffic is service-chained between VNFs inside the host (east–west).

back.text: Exam tip: PCI passthrough = 1 pNIC ↔ 1 VNF. SR-IOV = 1 pNIC ↔ many VNFs (PF/VF). OVS-DPDK = kernel bypass via user-space polling, dedicated CPU cores required.

front.text: An engineer installs VMware Fusion on their MacBook to run a Windows VM for testing. Which hypervisor type is this?

back.text: Type 2 (hosted) hypervisor — it runs on top of a host OS (macOS) rather than directly on the hardware. Type 2 hypervisors are typically used on client devices, not production servers.

front.text: What was the main driver behind server virtualization?

back.text: Server hardware was being underutilized. Bare-metal servers typically ran a single OS with a single application and used only about 10–25% of CPU resources. VMs and containers maximize the use of available hardware, increasing efficiency and cost-effectiveness.

front.text: What virtualized components does a VM present to its guest OS and applications?

items: Virtual CPU (vCPU)
Virtual memory (RAM)
Virtual NICs (vNICs)
Virtual storage
Other emulated server hardware

back.text: From the application's perspective, the VM looks and feels like a real physical server.

front.text: A developer says "It works on my laptop but not on the production server because the libraries are missing." How do containers solve this?

back.text: A container image bundles the application along with all of its dependencies (binaries and libraries). Because everything the app needs to run is inside the image, it runs identically across machines. This eliminates the classic "works on my machine" problem and removes the need to install missing libraries on the host OS.

front.text: Why does a container start in seconds while a VM typically takes minutes?

back.text: A VM must first boot its full guest OS before the application can start. A container leverages the kernel of the host OS (which is already running) and starts only the application and its dependencies — no guest OS boot is required.

front.text: Name the most popular virtual switches used in server virtualization.

items: Open vSwitch (OVS)
VMware vSphere Standard Switch (VSS)
VMware vSphere Distributed Switch (VDS)
VMware NSX vSwitch
Microsoft Hyper-V Virtual Switch
Libvirt Virtual Network Switch

front.text: VM1 is connected to vSwitch2 (no pNIC attached). VM0 is connected to vSwitch1 (linked to pNIC1). VM1 cannot reach VM0 or the external network. Why, and what is the typical fix?

back.text: Network traffic cannot flow directly between two vSwitches on the same host. vSwitch2 also has no pNIC, so VM1 has no path off the host.
Typical fix
Insert a VM/VNF (commonly a virtual firewall like NGFWv) with vNICs on both vSwitches to bridge the traffic between vSwitch2 and vSwitch1, which then forwards to the external network via pNIC1.

front.text: Two Docker hosts both use the default Docker0 bridge. Containers on Host A cannot reach containers on Host B even though the hosts can ping each other. Why?

back.text: Both hosts use the same default subnet (172.17.0.0/16) and assign overlapping container IPs from that range. The default Docker bridge only provides connectivity within a single node — containers on other nodes are not reachable by default.
Resolutions

items: Customize the bridge subnet on each host so they don't overlap
Use OS-level routing on a node-by-node basis
Use an overlay network
Use a container orchestrator such as Kubernetes to manage networking and IP scheduling at scale

front.text: What is a veth interface in Docker container networking?

back.text: A virtual Ethernet (veth) interface is created on the Docker0 bridge for each container. The veth appears as eth0 inside the container and is assigned an IP from the bridge's subnet (default 172.17.0.0/16). It provides the container's L2/L3 connectivity to the bridge and to other containers on the same host.

front.text: List the primary business and operational benefits of NFV.

items: Reduced capex and opex (less hardware, space, power, cooling)
Faster time to market — VMs/containers deploy faster than hardware
Improved ROI on new services
Elasticity — scale up/out and down/in on demand
Opens the market to virtual appliances and pure-software networking vendors
Lower-risk testing and deployment of innovative services

front.text: Compare OSS and BSS in the ETSI NFV framework.

rows: Supports network systems and services — inventory, provisioning, device configuration, fault resolution
Runs business operations — product management, customer management, billing, order management

back.text: In SP environments, OSS and BSS work together to improve overall customer experience.

front.text: Define IRQ, DMA, and device driver in the context of NIC packet processing.

rows: Hardware signal an I/O device sends to the CPU to indicate it has data to transfer; the CPU saves state, runs the interrupt handler, then resumes work
Memory access method that lets an I/O device send/receive data directly to/from main memory, bypassing the CPU, to speed up operations
Computer program that controls an I/O device and lets the CPU communicate with it (e.g., a NIC driver)

front.text: What is the difference between kernel space and user space, and why does it matter for VNF performance?

rows: The OS kernel — directly manages CPU, RAM, NICs, storage; runs in protected memory
Applications and their libraries — including most VNF processes

back.text: Standard packet flow crosses the kernel/user-space boundary multiple times, which is expensive in CPU cycles. I/O acceleration technologies (OVS-DPDK, PCI passthrough, SR-IOV) reduce or eliminate those context switches and CPU interrupts.

front.text: Put the high-level steps of standard OVS packet flow (pNIC → VM application) in order.

items: pNIC receives the packet and places it in an Rx queue
pNIC DMAs the packet and packet descriptor into main memory
pNIC sends an IRQ to the CPU
CPU runs the pNIC driver, which moves the packet up the network stack into a socket receive buffer
Packet is copied from the socket buffer to OVS
OVS forwards the packet to the VM (kernel ↔ user space switching — expensive)
Packet arrives at the VM's vNIC Rx queue
vNIC DMAs packet/descriptor into virtual memory
vNIC sends an IRQ to the vCPU
vCPU runs the vNIC driver, packet enters the VM's network stack and a socket buffer
Packet data is delivered to the application inside the VM

front.text: Why does the standard interrupt-driven I/O model become a bottleneck on high-speed NICs (e.g., 40 Gbps)?

back.text: Each received packet generates an IRQ. The CPU must save state, run the interrupt handler, process the packet, then restore state and resume prior work. As NIC speed increases and packet size shrinks, packets-per-second skyrockets and the CPU spends most of its time servicing interrupts and switching contexts. This caps packet throughput, which is exactly what kernel-bypass technologies (OVS-DPDK, SR-IOV, PCI passthrough) are designed to overcome.

front.text: What is required for OVS-DPDK to operate, and how does it bypass kernel overhead?

items: Physical NIC must support DPDK
One or more CPU cores must be dedicated to the DPDK Poll Mode Driver (PMD)
OVS-DPDK runs entirely in user space

back.text: Instead of waiting for IRQs, the PMD continuously polls the pNIC for packets, bypassing the kernel network stack and CPU interrupts. Once in OVS (user space), packets can be switched directly to the appropriate VNF.

front.text: Compare standard OVS, OVS-DPDK, PCI passthrough, and SR-IOV for VNF I/O.

rows: Kernel + user-space switching, IRQ-driven — lowest performance
User-space PMD polls NIC; needs dedicated CPU cores
Highest perf but pNIC dedicated to a single VNF
Multiple VFs on one PF; near-passthrough perf with sharing

front.text: Define Physical Function (PF) and Virtual Function (VF) in SR-IOV.

rows: The actual physical PCIe device, such as the pNIC itself
An emulated PCIe device hosted on the PF; VNFs attach directly to a VF using PCI passthrough

back.text: SR-IOV emulates many VFs on a single PF, allowing multiple VNFs to share one pNIC with near-passthrough performance.

front.text: What problem in enterprise branch offices does Cisco ENFV address?

back.text: Branches typically need many physical devices — WAN accelerator, firewall, WLC, IPS, collaboration, router/switch — often deployed redundantly. Across many branches, this creates large operational complexity and cost. Cisco ENFV consolidates these functions as VNFs on a single x86-based host, dramatically reducing the number of physical devices, truck rolls, and management overhead.

items: Fewer physical devices in the branch — saves space, power, maintenance, cooling

Centralized management via Cisco DNA/Catalyst Center
Operational flexibility — VM moves, snapshots, upgrades
Supports Cisco SD-WAN cEdge and vEdge virtual router onboarding
Supports third-party VNFs

front.text: How can managed service providers (MSPs) extend the Cisco ENFV solution with OSS/BSS capability?

back.text: MSPs can layer an OSS/BSS component on top of the ENFV stack using:

items: Cisco Network Services Orchestrator (NSO)

front.text: A single high-throughput vRouter VNF on a branch host must achieve maximum I/O performance with minimum latency, and no other VNFs need to share that NIC. Which I/O technology fits best, and what is the trade-off?

back.text: Best fit: PCI passthrough — the entire pNIC is mapped 1:1 to the vRouter VNF, bypassing the hypervisor for direct hardware access.
Benefits

items: Bypassed hypervisor and reduced CPU utilization
Lower system latency
Maximum I/O throughput

back.text: Trade-off
The pNIC is dedicated to that one VNF — no other VNF can use it, so scale is limited by the number of pNICs in the host.

front.text: Quick-recall exam tips for ENCOR Chapter 27 (Virtualization).

items: Type 1 hypervisor = bare metal (ESXi, Hyper-V, KVM, XenServer). Type 2 = hosted (VMware Fusion/Workstation).
VM = virtualized physical server WITH a guest OS. Container = isolated app environment that SHARES the host kernel — no guest OS.
vSwitch is L2 only. Two vSwitches on the same host CANNOT talk directly and CANNOT share a pNIC.
Distributed vSwitch = centralized vSwitch config across a cluster + policies follow VMs during live migration.
NFV = ETSI framework. VNF = the virtual function. NFVI = all the HW+SW infrastructure. MANO = NFV Orchestrator + VNF Manager.
VIM (e.g., ESC-Lite in NFVIS) handles NFVI lifecycle, FCAPS, and service chaining.
I/O acceleration: PCI passthrough = 1 pNIC ↔ 1 VNF. SR-IOV = 1 pNIC ↔ many VNFs (PF/VF). OVS-DPDK = user-space PMD, kernel bypass, dedicated cores.
SR-IOV switching modes: VEB (NIC switches internally) vs VEPA (external switch hairpins traffic).
Cisco ENFV orchestrator = Cisco DNA / Catalyst Center. NFVIS = Linux + KVM/QEMU + OVS + ESC-Lite + PnP client.
ENFV hosts = Cisco ENCS and Catalyst 8200 Edge uCPE.

front.text: Where does a Type 1 hypervisor sit in the software stack, and what does it abstract?

back.text: A Type 1 (bare-metal/native) hypervisor runs directly on the physical server hardware — no host OS underneath. It abstracts CPU, memory, storage, and NICs and presents them to multiple guest VMs running on top, so each VM gets the look and feel of its own dedicated physical server.

front.text: You are choosing a hypervisor for a production data center hosting hundreds of VMs. Should you choose Type 1 or Type 2? Why?

back.text: Type 1 (bare-metal). It runs directly on the hardware with no host OS overhead, providing better performance, density, and reliability for production workloads. Type 2 hypervisors (e.g., VMware Fusion/Workstation) are intended for client devices and labs because they require — and depend on — a host OS.

front.text: How do VMs improve the cost-effectiveness of physical server hardware?

back.text: Bare-metal servers typically use only 10–25% of available CPU. By running multiple VMs on a single physical host, the hypervisor multiplexes CPU, memory, storage, and NIC resources across many workloads, raising overall utilization, consolidating hardware, and reducing space, power, cooling, and licensing costs.

front.text: An ESXi host running 10 VMs suffers a hardware failure. How does virtualization provide high availability for those workloads?

back.text: Because VMs are software-defined and decoupled from any one host, the cluster can spin up the failed host's VMs on other hosts that have available CPU, memory, and storage. With shared storage and a clustered hypervisor, the VMs restart on healthy hosts automatically — preserving service availability without manual rebuild of physical servers.

front.text: What does it mean that VMs can be migrated "while preserving transactional integrity," and why is that valuable?

back.text: Live VM migration moves a running VM from one host to another without dropping its active sessions or losing in-flight transactions. CPU and memory state are copied to the destination host, then the VM resumes there seamlessly. This enables zero-downtime maintenance — for example, upgrading memory in a host or evacuating a host before patching — because workloads keep running through the move.

front.text: How does the isolation boundary differ between VMs and containers?

rows: Hypervisor + separate guest OS per VM
OS kernel namespaces / cgroups; all containers share host kernel
Bound to that VM's guest OS
Kernel exploit can affect all containers on host
Each VM can run a different OS
All containers share the host OS kernel

front.text: Why are container images considered "extremely portable"?

back.text: A container image bundles the application code together with every binary and library dependency it needs. Because the image is self-contained and only relies on a compatible host kernel, it can be moved to and run on any host with a container engine — without installing missing libraries or worrying that the host OS lacks a required component.

front.text: What two communication paths does a vSwitch enable inside a virtualized server?

items: VM-to-VM communication within the same virtualized server (east–west traffic between VMs on that vSwitch)
VM-to-external-network communication via the host's physical NICs (pNICs) — north–south traffic

front.text: How is a vSwitch similar to and different from a physical Layer 2 switch?

rows: Connects VM vNICs and host pNICs
vSwitches on same host CANNOT exchange traffic directly
Two vSwitches on same host cannot share the same pNIC

front.text: Your team manages 50 ESXi hosts and is tired of configuring identical port groups, VLANs, and policies on every host. Which feature solves this, and what additional benefit does it provide for live VM migration?

back.text: Use distributed virtual switching. It aggregates the per-host vSwitches into a single logical switch managed centrally.

items: Centralized vSwitch configuration for the entire cluster
Configuration consistency across all hosts in the distributed switch
During a live VM migration, the VM's networking statistics and policies migrate with it — no manual re-creation on the destination host

front.text: Inside a Docker container, what interface does the application see, and where is its peer end attached?

back.text: Inside the container, the application sees an interface called eth0 with an IP from the bridge subnet (default 172.17.0.0/16 on Docker0). The peer end of that veth pair lives in the host's network namespace and is attached to the Docker0 virtual bridge, which forwards traffic between containers and (via the host) to the outside world.

front.text: How does NFV differ from generic server virtualization, even though both run workloads as VMs/containers on x86?

rows: Decouple network functions from proprietary appliances
Network functions (FW, router, LB, IDS, WAN opt, etc.)

front.text: A branch service chain steers Internet traffic through: vLoad Balancer → vFirewall → vWAN-Optimizer → vRouter. Which ETSI NFV component is responsible for stitching these VNFs together, and what is this connection called?

back.text: The Virtualized Infrastructure Manager (VIM) is responsible for connecting (chaining) VNFs together as part of NFVI lifecycle management. Connecting two or more VNFs in a chain to deliver an end-to-end NFV service is called service chaining.

front.text: What does FCAPS stand for, and which ETSI NFV component performs FCAPS for VNFs?

back.text: Element Managers (EM/EMS) perform FCAPS for VNFs. The VNF Manager also handles FCAPS for the VNF's virtual components within MANO.

front.text: Briefly describe the layered structure of the ETSI NFV architectural framework.

items: NFVI (bottom) — physical compute, storage, network plus the virtualization layer
VNFs and their Element Managers (EMs) — virtualized network functions and per-function FCAPS
OSS/BSS — operations and business support systems used by SPs and large enterprises
MANO (right side, spanning all layers) — NFV Orchestrator, VNF Manager, and VIM

front.text: Which network functions are most commonly delivered as VNFs?

back.text: VNFs are most commonly used for L4–L7 services, but they can also implement L2/L3.

items: Load balancers (LB) and application delivery controllers (ADCs)
Firewalls
Intrusion detection/prevention systems (IDS/IPS)
WAN optimization appliances
Routers and switches (L2/L3)

front.text: A VNF on a host receives all user traffic from the WAN, processes it, and sends it directly back out to the WAN. Another VNF on the same host receives a copy of that traffic for logging only. Identify each pattern and why classifying them matters.

back.text: User-data path (WAN → VNF → WAN) is north–south. The copy-to-logging-VNF path is east–west (VNF → VNF inside the host).

Classifying these patterns matters because it drives I/O technology choice — north–south flows benefit greatly from kernel-bypass technologies like SR-IOV or PCI passthrough on the pNIC, while heavy east–west flows benefit from OVS-DPDK, which keeps packets in user space when switching between VNFs on the same host.

front.text: A VNF on a host using standard OVS suffers severely degraded throughput as small-packet traffic increases, even though the pNIC is rated for 40 Gbps. What are the two main bottlenecks, and which technologies remove them?

back.text: Bottlenecks

items: Per-packet IRQs to the CPU — high packet-per-second rates flood the CPU with interrupts
Kernel ↔ user-space context switches when OVS forwards each packet to the VM

back.text: Mitigations

items: OVS-DPDK — user-space PMD polls the NIC, eliminating interrupts and kernel hops
SR-IOV / PCI passthrough — bypass the hypervisor and vSwitch entirely for direct VNF access to the NIC

front.text: What is the main resource trade-off introduced by OVS-DPDK?

back.text: OVS-DPDK requires one or more CPU cores to be dedicated full-time to the Poll Mode Driver (PMD), which constantly polls the pNIC for packets. Those CPU cores are no longer available for VNFs or applications. You trade some general-purpose CPU capacity for dramatically higher and more predictable packet throughput, lower latency, and elimination of interrupt overhead.

front.text: A host has 2 pNICs configured with PCI passthrough — one assigned to the vRouter, one to the vFirewall. The team now wants to add a vWAAS and a vWLC to the same host with similar I/O performance. Why won't PCI passthrough work, and what should they use instead?

back.text: PCI passthrough provides exclusive 1:1 mapping between a pNIC and a single VNF. With only 2 pNICs, no more VNFs can claim a dedicated NIC.
Solution

Use SR-IOV. SR-IOV emulates many Virtual Functions (VFs) on a single Physical Function (PF), allowing multiple VNFs to share the same pNIC with near-passthrough performance via PCI passthrough to each VF.

front.text: Two VNFs on the same SR-IOV-enabled pNIC need to talk to each other, but the security team wants every flow inspected by the upstream physical switch. Which SR-IOV switching mode meets that requirement?

back.text: Use VEPA (Virtual Ethernet Port Aggregator). In VEPA mode, traffic between VNFs on the same pNIC is hairpinned out to the external switch, which switches it back. That ensures the upstream switch (and any ACLs/IPS on it) inspects every flow.
VEB (Virtual Ethernet Bridge) would switch the traffic locally on the pNIC and never expose it to the external switch — the wrong choice here.

front.text: What is a key prerequisite for using OVS-DPDK, PCI passthrough, or SR-IOV on a host?

back.text: The physical NIC itself must support the I/O technology you want to use. OVS-DPDK requires DPDK-compatible NICs with poll-mode driver support; PCI passthrough requires PCIe NICs assignable directly to a guest; SR-IOV requires NICs that can expose multiple Virtual Functions on a Physical Function. Software support alone is not enough — verify the NIC datasheet and platform compatibility before designing the VNF I/O architecture.

front.text: A retail enterprise has 500 branches, each with a separate router, firewall, WAN optimizer, and WLC. Operations are overwhelmed. How does the Cisco ENFV solution change this picture?

items: Replaces multiple physical appliances with VNFs running on a single x86 host (e.g., ENCS or Catalyst 8200 uCPE)
Reduces space, power, cooling, and on-site truck rolls
Enables zero-touch deployment via Plug and Play with Cisco DNA / Catalyst Center
Centralizes design, provisioning, and lifecycle management of VNFs across all 500 branches
Allows new services and updates to be rolled out in minutes via network profiles

rows: Embedded Elastic Services Controller (ESC-Lite) inside NFVIS
Cisco ENCS, Catalyst 8200 Edge uCPE, x86 hosts
Cisco (Cat 8000V, ASAv, FTDv, vWAAS, 9800-CL, etc.) and 3rd-party VNFs

front.text: In a Cisco ENFV deployment, what is the division of responsibility between ESC-Lite (in NFVIS) and Cisco DNA / Catalyst Center?

rows: VIM — local lifecycle of VNFs on that host: create/delete VNFs, allocate CPU/memory/storage, monitor and auto-restart VNFs, send SNMP/syslog alarms
