front.text: Final exam tips for ENCOR Chapter 27 — virtualization quick recall.

items: Bare-metal server = single OS, single app, ~10–25% CPU usage — the original problem virtualization solves.
Hypervisor types: Type 1 = bare metal (production), Type 2 = hosted (clients).
VM = full guest OS + virtual HW. Container = app + dependencies sharing the host kernel.
Containers boot in seconds (kernel already running); VMs boot in minutes (must boot guest OS).
Two vSwitches on the SAME host cannot exchange traffic directly and cannot share a pNIC.
Distributed vSwitch = single management plane across the cluster + policies follow VMs during migration.
Default Docker bridge = Docker0 / 172.17.0.0/16 — same on every host, which is why containers across hosts need overlays or Kubernetes.
ETSI NFV terms: NF (function), VNF (virtual function), NFVI (HW+SW infra), VIM (manages NFVI), MANO = NFV Orchestrator + VNF Manager.
EM/EMS performs FCAPS (Fault/Config/Accounting/Performance/Security) for VNFs.
Service chaining = stitching VNFs together; performed by the VIM.

I/O acceleration cheat sheet: PCI passthrough = 1 pNIC ↔ 1 VNF (max perf, no sharing). SR-IOV = 1 PF, many VFs, many VNFs share one pNIC. OVS-DPDK = user-space PMD, kernel bypass, dedicated CPU cores.
SR-IOV switching: VEB = NIC switches internally; VEPA = hairpin to external switch.
Cisco ENFV stack: DNA/Catalyst Center (MANO) + NFVIS (Linux + KVM/QEMU + OVS + ESC-Lite) + VNFs + ENCS or Catalyst 8200 Edge uCPE.
MSP add-on for OSS/BSS = Cisco NSO and Cisco MSX.
NFVIS = standard Linux + virtualization, VNF lifecycle, monitoring, programmability, and HW acceleration features.

front.text: Exam tip: Type 1 vs Type 2 hypervisors — fast recall.

items: Type 1 = bare metal / native — runs DIRECTLY on hardware. No host OS.
Type 1 examples: VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, KVM, Citrix XenServer.
Type 2 = hosted — runs ON TOP of a host OS.
Type 2 examples: VMware Fusion, VMware Workstation.
Production data centers = Type 1. Laptops/labs = Type 2.
If the question mentions 'runs on top of Windows/macOS' → Type 2.

front.text: Exam tip: VM vs container — what to remember.

items: VM = software emulation of a PHYSICAL server WITH its own guest OS.
Container = isolated environment for an app — SHARES the host kernel, NO guest OS.
Containers ≠ 'lightweight VMs' (don't say this).
VM boot = minutes (guest OS must load). Container start = seconds (kernel already up).
Container image = app + all dependencies. Becomes a container when run by the engine.
Most popular container engine = Docker. Others: rkt, LXD, OCI, Linux-VServer, Windows Containers.

front.text: Exam tip: vSwitch behavior rules you must know.

items: vSwitch = software-based LAYER 2 switch (not multilayer).
Multiple vSwitches CAN exist on a single host.
Two vSwitches on the SAME host CANNOT exchange traffic directly — must go through a VM/VNF (e.g., NGFWv).
Two vSwitches on the SAME host CANNOT share the same pNIC.
Distributed vSwitch = central management across cluster + policies/stats follow the VM during live migration.
Common vSwitches: OVS, VMware VSS/VDS/NSX, Hyper-V vSwitch, Libvirt.

front.text: Exam tip: Docker default container networking facts.

items: Default Docker bridge name = Docker0.
Default subnet = 172.17.0.0/16.
Each container gets a veth on Docker0; inside the container it appears as eth0.
Containers DO need vSwitches/bridges to communicate (don't fall for the 'no vSwitch needed' trick).
Default config makes containers reachable only WITHIN the same host.
Cross-host container reachability needs OS-level routing, overlay networks, or an orchestrator like Kubernetes.

front.text: Exam tip: NFV vocabulary — don't mix these up.

rows: Function performed by a physical appliance (FW, router, etc.)
Software/virtual version of an NF — usually a VM on a hypervisor
ETSI framework that decouples NFs from proprietary HW
All HW + SW infrastructure that hosts VNFs
Manages NFVI resources + does service chaining + collects perf/fault data
Connecting two or more VNFs to deliver an end-to-end service

back.text: Standards body for NFV = ETSI (memorize this).

front.text: Exam tip: MANO and FCAPS quick recall.

items: MANO = NFV Orchestrator + VNF Manager.
NFV Orchestrator = creates/maintains/tears down VNF network services across multiple VNFs.
VNF Manager = lifecycle of one or more VNFs + FCAPS for the VNF's virtual components.
VIM is NOT part of MANO in the strict definition — but it manages NFVI and does service chaining.
FCAPS = Fault, Configuration, Accounting, Performance, Security.
EM/EMS = does FCAPS for VNFs (and an EM can itself be a VNF).

front.text: Exam tip: choosing between OVS, OVS-DPDK, PCI passthrough, and SR-IOV.

rows: Kernel bypass via PMD in user space

items: 'Multiple VNFs need direct NIC access' → SR-IOV.
'Single VNF, max performance, no sharing' → PCI passthrough.
'Heavy east–west between VNFs on the host' → OVS-DPDK.
All three require NIC hardware support.

front.text: Exam tip: SR-IOV PF/VF and VEB vs VEPA.

items: PF (Physical Function) = the actual physical PCIe device (the pNIC itself).
VF (Virtual Function) = emulated PCIe devices on the PF; VNFs attach via PCI passthrough.
VEB = Virtual Ethernet Bridge — pNIC switches VNF-to-VNF traffic INTERNALLY in hardware.
VEPA = Virtual Ethernet Port Aggregator — VNF-to-VNF traffic is hairpinned to the EXTERNAL switch and back.
If question says 'all traffic must be inspected by the upstream switch' → VEPA.
If question says 'lowest latency / no external hop' → VEB.

front.text: Exam tip: Cisco ENFV components and roles.

rows: Cat 8000V, ASAv, FTDv, vWAAS, 9800-CL, vEdge, ThousandEyes, Meraki vMX, 3rd-party

items: ENFV orchestrator = Cisco DNA / Catalyst Center (NOT APIC, NOT APIC-EM).
Plug and Play = zero-touch onboarding of new branch ENFV devices.

front.text: Exam tip: NFVIS — what it is and what's inside.

items: NFVIS = standard Linux + extras for virtualization, VNF lifecycle, monitoring, programmability, HW acceleration. (TRUE on the DIKTA quiz.)
Hypervisor inside NFVIS = KVM (with QEMU and Libvirt).
vSwitch inside NFVIS = Open vSwitch (OVS).
VIM inside NFVIS = Elastic Services Controller Lite (ESC-Lite) — handles VNF create/delete, CPU/memory/storage allocation, auto-restart, SNMP/syslog alarms.
Northbound interfaces = REST, CLI, HTTPS web portal, NETCONF/YANG.
Built-in PnP client registers with DNA / Catalyst Center for true zero-touch.
Includes RBAC and a resource manager for CPU core tracking.

front.text: Why is using the CLI to configure a large number of network devices considered difficult to scale?

items: CLI is used on a device-by-device basis
CLI is prone to human error and misconfiguration
Only one command can be executed at a time
Commands and syntax can change between software versions and platforms

front.text: Compare the pros and cons of using the CLI to manage network devices.

rows: Executes one command at a time

front.text: What is the difference between Northbound and Southbound APIs in network automation?

rows: Used by GUIs and apps to interact with the controller (often REST)
Pushes configuration changes from the controller down to routers, switches, APs

front.text: What is a RESTful API?

back.text: A RESTful API is an API that uses HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE) to gather and manipulate data. Because HTTP has a defined structure, REST provides a consistent way to interact with APIs across multiple vendors.

front.text: Match each HTTP function to its action: GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE.

rows: Submits data to a specific destination

front.text: What does CRUD stand for and what is each function used for?
Identify the meaning of these HTTP status codes: 200, 201, 400, 401, 403, 404.

rows: URL/page does not exist or is hidden

front.text: What HTTP status code indicates 'Unauthorized'?

back.text: 401 Unauthorized — the client is not authenticated to access the site or API call.

front.text: What are the key characteristics of JSON data format?

items: Stores information in key/value pairs
Each JSON object starts with { and ends with }
Easy to read and create — cleaner than XML
Indentation improves readability but is not required
Cisco DNA Center expects incoming API data in JSON format

front.text: What are the basic structural rules of XML?

items: Tag-based language using < and > symbols
Every start tag (e.g., <interface>) requires a matching end tag (</interface>)
Readable by both humans and applications
Indentation is recommended but not required
Commonly used in web services and NETCONF

front.text: What HTTP method and authentication type are used to authenticate to the Cisco DNA Center Token API?

items: HTTP Method: POST
Authentication: Basic Authentication (username/password)
Content-Type header: application/json
Returns a token used for all subsequent API calls

back.text: POST https://sandboxdnac.cisco.com/api/system/v1/auth/token

front.text: How is the token returned from the Cisco DNA Center Token API used, and how long is it valid?

back.text: The token is generated from the user's login credentials and is unique per authenticated session. It must be passed in the X-Auth-Token header of subsequent API calls. A new authentication produces a new token; tokens are valid only for the current authenticated session.

front.text: Which Postman headers are needed to call the Cisco DNA Center Network Device API after authentication?

back.text: GET https://sandboxdnac.cisco.com/api/v1/network-device

front.text: How do the limit and offset query parameters affect a Cisco DNA Center Network Device API call?

items: ?limit=1 — returns only one record from the inventory
&offset=2 — starts at the second record in the inventory
Combined: returns the second device only

back.text: GET https://sandboxdnac.cisco.com/api/v1/network-device?limit=1&offset=2

front.text: What Content-Type and request body are used to authenticate to the Cisco vManage Authentication API?

items: HTTP Method: POST
Headers Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
Body keys: j_username and j_password
Successful auth returns a JSESSIONID (Java session ID) used for subsequent API calls

front.text: What is YANG and what are its key structural characteristics?

items: YANG (Yet Another Next Generation) is a data modeling language defined in RFC 6020
Alternative to SNMP MIBs; standard for data definition
Uses a hierarchical tree structure built from modules
Distinguishes between configuration data and operational state
Common elements include containers, leafs, lists, and types

front.text: Which of the following are part of the YANG model: Type, Leaf, Container, String, Method?

back.text: Leaf and Container are core elements of a YANG model. (Type/String are data types referenced inside leafs, but the canonical YANG building blocks are container, list, leaf, and choice/case.)

front.text: What is NETCONF, what transport does it use, and what data format does it use?

items: IETF standard protocol defined in RFC 4741 / RFC 6241
Uses YANG data models to communicate with devices
Runs over SSH (typical), TLS, or SOAP (uncommon)
Uses XML-formatted RPC messages
Distinguishes configuration data from operational state (unlike SNMP)

front.text: Compare NETCONF and SNMP across resources, data models, encoding, and transport.
List the common NETCONF operations and what each one does.

rows: Requests running config and state info

front.text: What is RESTCONF and how does it relate to NETCONF and YANG?

items: Defined in RFC 8040
Provides a RESTful HTTP-based interface to YANG-modeled data
Uses the same YANG models and datastore concepts as NETCONF
Does NOT replace NETCONF — both coexist
Supports HTTP methods: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, OPTIONS
Requests/responses can use JSON or XML

front.text: What are the two main reasons to use three quotation marks (""") in Python?

items: To begin a multiple-line string
To end a multiple-line string

back.text: Triple-quoted strings are also commonly used to write docstrings/comments that span multiple lines.

front.text: What is a Python dictionary, and what does one look like?

back.text: A dictionary is a collection of key/value pairs enclosed in curly braces { }. Keys map to values, similar to JSON.

front.text: What is a Python function and how is one identified in code?

back.text: A function is a block of code built to perform a specific action that can be reused. Functions are defined using the 'def' keyword and can take arguments and return values. The print() built-in is also a function.

def dnac_login(host, username, password):
    url = "https://{}/api/system/v1/auth/token".format(host)
    response = requests.request("POST", url,
        auth=HTTPBasicAuth(username, password),
        headers=headers, verify=False)
    return response.json()["Token"]

front.text: True or false: Python is considered one of the most difficult programming languages to learn and adopt.

back.text: False. Python is one of the easier languages to start with, and it has become one of the most common languages in network programmability.
Exam tip: Cisco emphasizes Python for ENCOR because it is approachable AND widely used in real network automation tooling.

front.text: What is the Cisco DevNet Documentation page used for?

items: Provides API documentation for Cisco solutions (DNA Center, SD-WAN, IoT, Collaboration, etc.)
Acts as a starting point for learning how to programmatically interact with Cisco platforms
Also serves as a gateway into learning labs and API references

front.text: What is the purpose of a GitHub repository?

items: Provides a place to store a developer's code
Gives the option to share code with other users
Provides documentation on code examples
Supports version control, bug tracking, and collaborative peer review

front.text: List the steps to authenticate to Cisco DNA Center using Postman and the Token API.

items: Enter URL: https://sandboxdnac.cisco.com/api/system/v1/auth/token
Select HTTP method: POST
Authorization tab → set Type to Basic Auth
Enter username 'devnetuser' and password 'Cisco123!'
Headers tab → add key 'Content-Type'
Set value to application/json
Click Send to receive an authentication token (HTTP 200 OK)

front.text: An engineer authenticates to Cisco DNA Center successfully but receives HTTP 401 Unauthorized when calling the Network Device API in a separate Postman tab. What is the most likely cause?

back.text: The X-Auth-Token header is missing or contains an invalid/expired token. The token returned from /auth/token must be added as the X-Auth-Token header value on subsequent API calls. Tokens are session-specific, so re-authenticating produces a new token that must be reused.
Verify Content-Type is application/json and that the token was copied without trailing whitespace.

front.text: Key ENCOR exam tips for Chapter 28 (Foundational Network Programmability).

items: Know the 5 HTTP methods (GET/POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE) and CRUD mapping
Memorize key HTTP status codes: 200, 201, 400, 401, 403, 404
DNA Center Token API = POST + Basic Auth + Content-Type application/json
vManage Authentication API = POST + Content-Type application/x-www-form-urlencoded with j_username/j_password
Subsequent DNA Center calls require the X-Auth-Token header
YANG building blocks include containers and leafs; data models distinguish config vs state
NETCONF uses XML over SSH; RESTCONF uses HTTP and supports JSON or XML
JSON uses { } and key/value pairs; XML uses paired <tags>
Python triple quotes (""") begin/end multi-line strings; dictionaries use { }

front.text: What is the leading cause of network outages according to Chapter 28's discussion of CLI management?

back.text: Human error — specifically misconfigurations due to lack of network understanding. The majority of network outages are not caused by hardware or software failure, but by people making mistakes via the CLI.

front.text: What is an API in the context of network automation?

back.text: An Application Programming Interface (API) is a mechanism used to communicate with applications, software, and network components programmatically. APIs can be used to configure or monitor specific components of a network through software.

front.text: Describe a Northbound API in the context of Cisco DNA Center.

items: Used to communicate from a network controller to its management software/apps
Cisco DNA Center's GUI uses a Northbound REST-based API
Best practice: encrypt with TLS between management software and controller
Allows applications above the controller to consume controller capabilities

front.text: Describe a Southbound API in the context of Cisco DNA Center.

back.text: Southbound APIs push configuration changes from the controller down to managed network devices such as routers, switches, and wireless access points. When an operator changes a switch configuration in the controller GUI, the controller uses a Southbound API to apply that change to the device.

front.text: How are URLs related to functions in a RESTful API?

back.text: Each REST API call maps to an individual URL for a particular function. Every configuration change or data poll has a unique URL — whether the operation is a GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, or DELETE — making each function uniquely addressable via HTTP.

front.text: What are the key sections of the Postman Builder dashboard described in Chapter 28?

items: History — list of all recent API calls (can be cleared)
Collections — folder-like groups for organizing API calls (can be favorited)
New Tab — independent tab for each API call with its own URL/parameters
URL bar — where the API endpoint is entered for each call

front.text: What is a Postman 'collection' and why is it useful?

back.text: A collection is a named group of saved API calls organized in a folder hierarchy. Collections help during testing by making API calls easy to find and sort. For example, a 'DNA-C' collection could store all Cisco DNA Center API calls. Collections can be marked as favorites with the star icon.

front.text: Why is it important to use a test lab or the Cisco DevNet sandbox when learning APIs?

back.text: API calls have the same effect as logging in via CLI and making changes. A DELETE call really removes data. Authenticated API users can impact production systems — always practice in a sandbox or non-production lab to avoid accidental outages.

front.text: Compare JSON and XML data formats.

rows: Established but being replaced in many cases

front.text: Which of the following snippets is valid JSON?

A) { "user": "root", "father": "Jason" }
B) <users><user><name>root</name></user></users>
C) root\nJason\nJamie
D) [users[root|Jason|Jamie]]

back.text: A — JSON uses key/value pairs enclosed in curly braces { }. B is XML, and C and D are not standard structured data formats.

front.text: What authentication method is used by the Cisco DNA Center Token API?

back.text: Basic Authentication — the username and password are passed via HTTP Basic Auth in a POST request to the Token API endpoint, which returns a token used in the X-Auth-Token header for subsequent API calls.

front.text: You need to retrieve only the third device from the Cisco DNA Center inventory. What URL parameters would you append to the Network Device API?

back.text: GET https://sandboxdnac.cisco.com/api/v1/network-device?limit=1&offset=3
limit=1 returns a single record; offset=3 starts at the third record in the inventory.

front.text: What is the JSESSIONID returned by the Cisco vManage Authentication API and how is it used?

back.text: JSESSIONID is the Java session ID returned after a successful authentication POST to vManage. It functions similarly to the DNA Center token — it must be passed in subsequent API calls to identify and authorize the user's session.

front.text: What types of information are returned from a successful HTTP GET to the Cisco vManage Fabric Device API?

items: Device ID
System IP
Hostname
Reachability
Status
Device type
Site ID

back.text: The data is returned in JSON format under the 'data' key.

front.text: How does YANG distinguish between configurable and non-configurable elements in a model?

back.text: Every YANG element has a defined type. Configurable elements can be changed (e.g., interface admin state up/down), while operational state elements cannot. Non-configurable leafs are explicitly marked with 'config false', as in an 'observed-speed' leaf that reflects the auto-detected speed rather than a user-set value.

front.text: Why are YANG models considered powerful for multi-vendor environments?

back.text: YANG models create a uniform way to describe configuration data, operational state, administrative actions, and notifications. This allows network operators to configure, monitor, and interact with devices holistically — across vendors — using a consistent data definition language.

front.text: What are the key behaviors of NETCONF transactions?

items: All-or-nothing — entire transaction succeeds or fails as a unit
No order of operations or sequencing within a transaction
Configuration is deployed all at the same time
Transactions process the same way every time on every device
Parallel transactions on different config areas don't interfere with each other

front.text: List common use cases for NETCONF.

items: Collecting the status of specific fields
Changing the configuration of specific fields
Taking administrative actions
Sending event notifications
Backing up and restoring configurations
Testing configurations before finalizing the transaction

front.text: Which HTTP methods does RESTCONF support, and what data formats can it use?

items: Methods: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, OPTIONS
Data formats: JSON or XML
Provides a RESTful API experience while leveraging NETCONF's device abstraction
Uses the same YANG models as NETCONF and IOS XE

front.text: Interpret the following RESTCONF GET response. What is being retrieved?

back.text: The RESTCONF GET retrieved the configured logging monitor severity level from a Cisco IOS XE device. HTTP 200 indicates success, and the JSON body shows the severity is set to 'critical'.

front.text: What is found on the Cisco DevNet Learn page?

items: Guided learning tracks across multiple technologies
Hands-on API labs (DNA Center, ACI, Webex Teams, Intro to DevNet, etc.)
Progress tracking so users can resume sessions later

front.text: What is the purpose of the DevNet Community page?

back.text: The Community page is where users connect with DevNet ambassadors and evangelists, read blogs, sign up for developer forums, follow DevNet on social media, and ask questions in a safe zone. It's the central starting point for engaging with the broader Cisco programmability community.

front.text: How are single-line comments indicated in Python, and why are they used?

back.text: Single-line comments begin with the # character. They typically describe the intent of an action and appear above the code they describe. Comments are not executed and are used to make scripts easier to read and maintain.

# This sends an HTTP POST to authenticate to DNA Center
response = requests.post(url, ...)

front.text: What is a Python 'condition' and how is one written?

back.text: A condition tests whether a logical statement is true and triggers an action depending on the result. The 'if' keyword is used, and equality is tested with == (double equals).

front.text: What are Python modules and why does the get_dnac_devices.py script import them?

back.text: A module is a collection of actions and instructions that extend Python's capabilities. Without importing the proper modules, Python may not understand how to perform certain tasks (like HTTP calls).

rows: Handles HTTP headers and form data

front.text: What is print() in Python and why doesn't it require an import?

back.text: print() is a built-in Python function used to output data to the terminal. Because it is included with the language itself, no import statement is required. In get_dnac_devices.py, print(dnac_devices) outputs the formatted PrettyTable of devices retrieved from DNA Center.

front.text: What does this Python statement do?
from env_lab import dnac

back.text: It imports the 'dnac' dictionary from the env_lab.py file, allowing the current script to reuse the connection variables (host, port, username, password) defined for the DNA Center sandbox without redefining them.

front.text: What does this line do, and why is it commonly used in DevNet sandbox scripts?
urllib3.disable_warnings(urllib3.exceptions.InsecureRequestWarning)

back.text: It silences the urllib3 InsecureRequestWarning that occurs when verify=False is used on HTTPS calls. DevNet sandboxes often use self-signed certificates that fail strict TLS validation, so warnings are suppressed for cleaner script output.
In production, always validate certificates rather than disabling warnings.

front.text: What workflow features does GitHub provide for collaborating on code?

items: Repositories store project code files in one place
Branches allow safe development separate from main code
Commits track changes with summary logs
Pull requests allow changes to be reviewed before merging
Built-in editor lets users modify files directly in the browser
Comments and peer review encourage community feedback

front.text: A Python script that worked yesterday now returns HTTP 401 from Cisco DNA Center, even though credentials haven't changed. What are the most likely causes?

items: Cached/expired token — the script reused an old token instead of calling the Token API again to obtain a new one
Token not properly placed in the X-Auth-Token header for subsequent calls
Sandbox credentials reset — DevNet always-on sandboxes are shared and credentials can rotate
Time skew or session expiration on the controller
Wrong sandbox host (using a reserved sandbox URL after the reservation expired)

back.text: Best practice: always re-authenticate at the start of each script run rather than caching tokens long-term.

front.text: What is Embedded Event Manager (EEM) and what is one of its main benefits?

back.text: EEM is a flexible Cisco IOS automation tool that lets engineers build software applets and Tcl scripts that automatically execute based on events on a device. Its main benefit is that it runs entirely on-box — no external scripting engine or monitoring server is needed.

front.text: What two primary building blocks make up an EEM applet, and what logic do they follow?

items: Events — what triggers the applet (e.g., syslog match, CLI pattern, timer)
Actions — what the applet does when triggered
Logic: if-then style — IF an event occurs, THEN actions are taken

front.text: Interpret the following EEM applet:

event manager applet LOOP0
 event syslog pattern "Interface Loopback0.* down" period 1
 action 1.0 cli command "enable"
 action 2.0 cli command "config terminal"
 action 3.0 cli command "interface loopback0"
 action 4.0 cli command "shutdown"
 action 5.0 cli command "no shutdown"
 action 6.0 syslog msg "I've fallen, and I can't get up!"

back.text: When a syslog message matches the regex 'Interface Loopback0.* down' within 1 second, the applet enters enable mode, goes into config mode, bounces Loopback0 (shutdown / no shutdown), and generates a custom syslog message. This is a self-healing applet that responds to a Loopback0 down event.

front.text: List key EEM applet best practices and gotchas.

items: Include 'enable' and 'configure terminal' at the start of the action list (applets begin in user EXEC mode)
If AAA command authorization is configured, use 'event manager session cli username <username>' or commands will fail
Use decimal labels (1.0, 2.0, 1.5) so new actions can be inserted later
Labels are parsed as strings — 10.0 sorts after 1.0, NOT after 9.0

front.text: What does this EEM applet do, and what kind of event triggers it?

event manager applet BACKUP-CONFIG
 event cli pattern "write mem.*" sync yes
 action 1.0 cli command "enable"
 action 5.0 cli command "copy start $tftpserver$filename"
 action 8.0 syslog priority informational msg "Configuration File Changed! TFTP backup successful."

back.text: It triggers on a CLI pattern event — whenever a user enters 'write mem' (or anything starting with it). The applet then automatically copies the startup-config to a TFTP server and logs an informational syslog message confirming the backup.

front.text: List the common EEM email environment variables.
How do you create and manually trigger an EEM applet that runs a Tcl script from flash?

back.text:
event manager applet Ping
 event none
 action 1.0 cli command "enable"
 action 1.1 cli command "tclsh flash:/ping.tcl"

! To run it manually:
Router# event manager run Ping
'event none' means there's no automatic trigger — the applet only runs when manually invoked with 'event manager run <name>'.

front.text: What is Tcl in the context of EEM, and how is a Tcl file viewed in flash?

back.text: Tcl (Tool Command Language) is a scripting language supported on Cisco IOS that EEM can call from applets to perform more complex automation. To view a Tcl script stored locally, use the 'more' command:
Router# more flash:ping.tcl

front.text: Categorize these tools as agent-based or agentless: Puppet, Chef, SaltStack, Ansible, Puppet Bolt, Salt SSH.
Describe the core components of a Puppet deployment.

items: Puppet server — central server that distributes configurations
Puppet agent — client installed on managed nodes
Puppet console — GUI where users build and apply changes
PuppetDB — database storing tasks/changes (can be on the server or separate)
Server replica (optional) — high-availability backup server

front.text: What is a Puppet module, and what is a Puppet manifest?

items: Module — a collection of manifests, templates, and files used to configure something
Manifest — the Puppet code (.pp file) pushed to nodes via SSL to make config changes
Manifests are written in Puppet's DSL (largely based on Ruby)
Manifests can run periodically to enforce desired state

front.text: What does this Puppet manifest do?

ntp_server { '1.2.3.4':
  ensure => 'present',
  key => 94,
  prefer => true,
  minpoll => 4,
  maxpoll => 14,
  source_interface => 'Vlan 42',
}

back.text: It ensures that an NTP server configuration for 1.2.3.4 is present on the device, using authentication key 94, marked as preferred, with poll intervals of 4–14, and sourced from VLAN 42. 'ensure => present' means Puppet will add this config if missing — and re-add it if removed when the manifest re-runs.

front.text: Describe Puppet's three installation modes and their scale.

rows: Monolithic with compile servers + standalone PE-PostgreSQL

front.text: What is Chef, what language is it written in, and what model does it use?

items: Open source configuration management tool
Written in Ruby and Erlang
Code is written in Ruby
Uses a pull model (clients pull updates from the server)
Operates as a client/server architecture

front.text: Compare Chef and Puppet terminology.
What are the four Chef server deployment types, and what are knife and OHAI?

items: Chef Solo — Chef server hosted locally on the workstation
Chef Client and Server — typical distributed deployment
Hosted Chef — Chef server hosted in the cloud
Private Chef — all components within the same enterprise network
knife — CLI tool used to upload cookbooks (e.g., 'knife upload <cookbook>')
OHAI — service on each node that gathers system state and sends it to the Chef server

front.text: What is the Chef 'kitchen' used for?

back.text: The kitchen is where all recipes and cookbooks are automatically executed and tested before reaching production nodes. It supports cloud providers, virtualization platforms, and Ruby testing frameworks like BATS, Minitest, RSpec, and Serverspec.

front.text: What is SaltStack, what language is it built on, and what messaging library does it use by default?

items: Configuration management tool (similar category to Puppet/Chef)
Built on Python — supports a Python interface for direct programming
Uses YAML or a DSL for state instructions (Salt formulas)
Default messaging: 0MQ (ZeroMQ) for fast, reliable distributed messaging
Architecture: master(s) and minions (parallel command execution)

front.text: Compare SaltStack pillars and grains.

rows: Gather system info (uptime, OS, etc.) and report back to master
Hold data minions can retrieve; can be scoped to specific minions for sensitive data

front.text: What are SaltStack reactors and beacons, and how do they interact?

items: Reactors live on the master and listen for changes that differ from the desired state
Beacons live on minions and notify the master when something changes (CLI config, disk/memory/CPU, service status)
Together they form the remote execution system — jobs are run to bring minions back to desired state
Executed jobs can be stored in an external database for review/reuse

front.text: What are the key characteristics of Ansible?

items: Open source automation tool acquired by Red Hat in 2015
Agentless — no software installed on managed devices
Communicates primarily via SSH (also supports WinRM)
Uses YAML for playbooks
Designed to be consistent, secure, highly reliable, and easy to learn
Runs from a control station (laptop or server)

front.text: Describe the structure of an Ansible playbook (playbook, play, task).

rows: Set of plays for remote systems
Set of tasks applied to a host or host group
A call to an Ansible module
Login to a device and run a 'show' command

front.text: What are the key syntax rules for YAML files in Ansible?

items: Often begins with three dashes (---) and ends with three periods (...)
Comments start with # (pound sign)
Lists use a leading dash and space (- item)
Dictionaries use key: value pairs (no quotes required)
Indentation defines structure and improves readability

back.text:
---
Employee1:
    Name: John Dough
    Title: Developer
...

front.text: Match each Ansible CLI command with its use case.

rows: Show CLI syntax and parameter docs
Switch from default push to pull model
Encrypt YAML files containing sensitive data

front.text: What does this Ansible inventory file define?

back.text: It defines three host groups — routers, switches, and primary-gateway. Hosts can belong to multiple groups; for example, 192.168.10.1 is in both 'routers' and 'primary-gateway'. Inventory entries can be IP addresses or DNS names.

front.text: Interpret this Ansible playbook task.

tasks:
 - name: Configure GigabitEthernet2 Interface
   ios_config:
     lines:
       - description Configured by ANSIBLE!!!
       - ip address 10.1.1.1 255.255.255.0
       - no shutdown
     parents: interface GigabitEthernet2

back.text: It uses the ios_config module to enter 'interface GigabitEthernet2' as the parent context, then applies a description, an IP address (10.1.1.1/24), and 'no shutdown'. The result is a fully configured Gi2 interface on the targeted IOS device.

front.text: An Ansible PLAY RECAP shows: ok=4 changed=3 unreachable=0 failed=0. What does this mean?

back.text: Four tasks completed successfully (ok=4), three of those actually modified the device (changed=3), no hosts were unreachable, and no tasks failed. The 1 'ok' task that wasn't 'changed' likely just confirmed state or saved configuration without altering it.

front.text: What is Puppet Bolt and how does it differ from traditional Puppet?

items: Open source agentless tool — no Puppet server or agents required
Connects to devices via SSH or WinRM
Based on Ruby; installs as a single package
Two modes: orchestrator-driven tasks (large-scale, integrates with Puppet) and standalone tasks (no Puppet infrastructure needed)
Useful for ad-hoc immediate changes (vs. Puppet's periodic state enforcement)
Copies scripts to a temp dir on the remote device, runs them, captures results, then cleans up

front.text: What is Salt SSH and what are its requirements and trade-offs?

items: Agentless option for SaltStack — no minion installed on the remote system
Requirements: SSH enabled on remote system + Python installed
Installs a lightweight version of SaltStack in a temp directory (can persist or be cleaned up)
Can coexist with master/minion deployments OR run fully agentless
Uses roster files to track hosts without minions
Trade-off: slower than 0MQ-based master/minion, but still faster than manual logins

front.text: Memorize this high-level comparison of automation tools (architecture, language, terminology, agentless option).

back.text: Exam tips: Ansible uses YAML (---) and is agentless. SaltStack and Ansible are Python-based. Chef uses Ruby. The 'ansible-playbook' command runs a playbook. PPDIOO = Prepare, Plan, Design, Implement, Operate, Optimize.

front.text: What is the role of EEM event detectors?

back.text: Event detectors are the components within EEM that monitor specific subsystems on the device (syslog, CLI, SNMP, counters, timers, etc.). When a monitored condition is met, the detector triggers the configured EEM applet's actions, enabling proactive on-box automation.

front.text: How does EEM shift network monitoring from reactive to proactive?

back.text: Instead of an external NMS continually polling devices to ask if anything is wrong, EEM allows devices to monitor themselves and report (or auto-remediate) when something happens. This reduces network load, improves monitoring efficiency, and enables faster response.

front.text: What does the $_cli_result keyword do in an EEM applet?

back.text: $_cli_result captures the output from CLI commands executed inside the applet. It can be embedded in actions like syslog messages or email bodies so that the applet's notifications include the actual command output (e.g., 'show interface loopback0').

front.text: Match each EEM debug command to its purpose.

rows: Show CLI actions executing inside an applet
Show full output for all configured actions
Filter to only SMTP/email-related output for troubleshooting mail actions

front.text: Why is 'file prompt quiet' often included inside an EEM applet that copies a config file?

back.text: It disables IOS's interactive confirmation prompts. Without it, commands like 'copy startup-config tftp:' would pause waiting for the user to confirm filenames or destinations — which would cause the applet to hang. After the copy completes, 'no file prompt quiet' restores normal behavior.

front.text: An EEM applet's CLI actions silently fail on a device that uses AAA command authorization. What's the most likely fix?

back.text: Add the 'event manager session cli username <username>' command so that EEM sessions run as an authorized user. Without this, AAA blocks the applet's CLI commands because they have no associated authorized user.
event manager session cli username admin

front.text: Why might EEM action 10.0 run BEFORE action 9.0 even though 10 > 9?

back.text: Because EEM action labels are sorted as STRINGS, not numbers. '10.0' sorts after '1.0' but before '9.0' in string order. Use consistent decimal naming (e.g., 01.0, 02.0... or 1.0, 1.5, 2.0) to keep ordering predictable.

front.text: How do you define and use a reusable EEM environment variable in an applet?

back.text:
event manager environment filename Router.cfg
event manager environment tftpserver tftp://10.1.200.29/

event manager applet BACKUP-CONFIG
 action 5.0 cli command "copy start $tftpserver$filename"
Use 'event manager environment <name> <value>' globally, then reference it inside any applet with $name. This avoids hardcoding values into individual actions.

front.text: What are common repetitive configuration areas where automation tools deliver value?

items: Device hostnames and IP addressing
Quality of Service (QoS) policies
Access list (ACL) entries
Usernames and passwords
SNMP settings
Compliance and configuration drift checking

front.text: What are the four key business benefits of automating daily configuration tasks?

items: Increased agility — faster change deployment
Reduced opex — less manual effort
Streamlined management — consistent processes across devices
Reduced human error — proven, tested automation replaces ad-hoc CLI

front.text: Compare push and pull configuration management models.

rows: Central server pushes config to managed devices
Clients/agents check in with the server and pull updates

front.text: How do puppet agents communicate with the puppet server?

items: Each puppet agent uses a unique TCP connection to the puppet server
Manifests are pushed using SSL with certificates installed for security
Puppet can periodically verify configuration on devices and re-enforce desired state
If config drifts, Puppet can alert and/or automatically restore the previous configuration

front.text: What is Puppet Forge and what value does it (and similar repos) provide?

items: Free community site for sharing Puppet modules, manifests, and code (forge.puppet.com)
Helps users get started with Puppet quickly
Many Puppet (and similar) artifacts are also on github.com
Benefits: version tracking, knowing who contributed, collaboration, and faster software project velocity

front.text: What does this Puppet manifest do?

back.text: It configures the message-of-the-day (MOTD) banner on the managed Cisco IOS device to read 'Violators will be prosecuted'. The 'banner' resource type is from the cisco_ios Puppet module.

front.text: Describe the Chef workflow from authoring code to executing it on a node.

items: Engineer creates a recipe (.rb) on the workstation
Recipe is bundled into a cookbook
Cookbook is uploaded to the Chef server using 'knife upload <cookbook>'
OHAI on the node collects current state and sends it to the Chef server via the Chef client
Chef server compares OHAI data to the cookbook/recipe
Chef client pulls down updated configuration if state differs
Recipe is applied on the node to enforce desired configuration

front.text: What does this Chef recipe snippet do?

cisco_command_config 'router_bgp_42' do
  action :update
  command '
    router bgp 42
      router-id 192.168.1.42
      address-family ipv4 unicast
        network 1.0.0.0/8
      neighbor 10.1.1.1
        remote-as 99
  '
end

back.text: It uses the cisco_command_config resource to update BGP configuration on the device — enabling AS 42 with router-id 192.168.1.42, advertising 1.0.0.0/8 in the IPv4 unicast address family, and forming an eBGP peering to 10.1.1.1 in AS 99.

front.text: How does SaltStack target minions when running commands?

items: By MinionID (specific minion)
By wildcard '*' to match all managed minions
By globbing patterns, e.g., 'Minion*' matches all MinionIDs starting with 'Minion'
Command structure: <target> <module.function> <arguments>

back.text: salt '*' cmd.run 'ls -l /etc'

front.text: What does the SaltStack command `salt '*' cmd.run 'ls -l /etc'` do?

back.text: It runs the ad-hoc Linux CLI command 'ls -l /etc' on every SaltStack-managed minion in parallel and returns each minion's output to the master. cmd.run is the Salt module function used for arbitrary shell commands.

front.text: An ops team needs the MAC, name, state, and IPv4/IPv6 of every interface on every Linux server. What SaltStack module function fits this best, and why?

back.text: network.interfaces is a purpose-built module for this. Unlike cmd.run, which would require parsing ad-hoc shell output, network.interfaces returns structured data (interface name, MAC, state, IPv4/IPv6 addresses) consistently across heterogeneous Linux hosts — far more reliable for automation.

front.text: What is SynDic in SaltStack?

back.text: SynDic is the SaltStack enterprise GUI feature that enables a 'master of masters' design. It allows large-scale deployments to be managed hierarchically — multiple Salt masters can be controlled from a higher-level master via SynDic.

front.text: How does Ansible handle privilege escalation, and why does it matter that it doesn't need an admin account?

back.text: Ansible can connect as an unprivileged user and use built-in escalation mechanisms like sudo when admin-level changes are needed. This means you don't have to give Ansible direct admin/root credentials — improving security while still allowing it to perform privileged tasks on demand.

front.text: What does PPDIOO stand for?

items: Prepare
Plan
Design
Implement
Operate
Optimize

back.text: Common methodology applied to automation projects — starts with the desired outcome, then plans how to achieve it without duplicating bad processes.

front.text: Which of the following is valid YAML?

A) { "user": "root", "user": "Jason" }
B) # HR Employee record
   Employee1:
       Name: John Dough
       Title: Developer
C) [users[root|Jason]]

back.text: B is valid YAML. It uses # for a comment, key: value pairs (no quotes required), and indentation to express hierarchy. A is JSON-like, and C is not a recognized structured data format.

front.text: What is YAML Lint and why is it useful for Ansible work?

back.text: YAML Lint (yamllint.com) is a free online tool that validates YAML syntax. Because YAML is whitespace-sensitive and small indentation mistakes can break Ansible playbooks, Lint helps catch errors before running automation against real devices.

front.text: When automating Cisco IOS, when do you use ios_config vs ios_command?

rows: Setting interface IP, EIGRP, OSPF, etc.
EXEC mode commands that don't change config

front.text: What is the proper command to run an Ansible playbook?

back.text: ansible-playbook ConfigureInterface.yaml
'ansible' runs ad-hoc modules; 'ansible-playbook' is specifically for executing playbook YAML files.

front.text: Compare Puppet Bolt commands, scripts, and tasks.

rows: Reusable, sharable, often paired with JSON metadata file

front.text: What is a Puppet Bolt task metadata file and how is it used?

items: JSON file describing how to run a task (parameters, comments, structure)
Typically named the same as the task script with a .json extension
Provides standardized documentation for sharing tasks on Puppet Forge
Viewable from the CLI with: bolt task show <module>::<taskfile>

front.text: What does this Salt SSH roster file define?

managed:
   host: 192.168.10.1
   user: admin

back.text: It defines a host entry named 'managed' with target IP 192.168.10.1, accessed as user 'admin'. Salt SSH uses roster files to track agentless hosts (no minion installed) and how to reach them.

front.text: Your team has strong Python skills, prefers a CLI-driven workflow, and needs fast parallel execution across many devices. Which automation tool aligns best, and why?

back.text: SaltStack. It's built on Python (so the team can extend it natively), it's CLI-friendly, and 0MQ enables fast parallel execution at scale. Ansible would also be reasonable (Python-based, agentless), but SaltStack is the strongest match for teams already comfortable at the command line who want maximum speed and tight Python integration.
Exam tip: Tool choice questions usually come down to language familiarity (Ruby → Chef/Puppet, Python → SaltStack/Ansible) and operational style (agentless preferred → Ansible, Bolt, Salt SSH).

front.text: List several practical use cases for EEM applets in a production network.

items: Auto-recover from interface flaps (shut/no shut)
Auto-backup config to TFTP/SCP after 'write mem'
Send custom syslog or email when a critical interface goes down
Run scheduled diagnostics via 'event timer cron'
Track unauthorized config changes via CLI pattern matching
Trigger remediation when SNMP thresholds are exceeded

front.text: Compare on-box automation (EEM) with off-box automation tools (Ansible, Puppet, Chef, SaltStack).

rows: Fast local response, no external dependency

front.text: Why are regular expressions important when matching syslog or CLI patterns in EEM?

back.text: Regex provides granular, flexible pattern matching. For example, 'Interface Loopback0.* down' matches any syslog message about Loopback0 going down, regardless of varying details. Without regex, applets would only fire on exact string matches, which would miss most real syslog variations.

front.text: What is the effect of 'sync yes' in 'event cli pattern "..." sync yes'?

back.text: 'sync yes' makes EEM run the applet synchronously with the CLI command — meaning the user's command waits for the applet to complete (or be allowed) before returning. This enables the applet to permit, deny, or augment the CLI behavior in real time.

front.text: An EEM applet fires successfully, but the email action never arrives. Syslog shows '%HA_EM-3-FMPD_SMTP: Error occurred when sending mail to SMTP server'. How do you troubleshoot?

items: Run 'debug event manager action mail' to filter to mail-related output
Verify reachability to the SMTP server (ping/traceroute)
Check that TCP/25 (or configured port) is open from the device
Confirm correct _email_server, _email_to, and _email_from values
Ensure the SMTP server allows the device's source IP to relay
Check DNS if a hostname (not IP) is used in _email_server

front.text: True or false: Automation always reduces risk.

back.text: False — with caveat. Automation reduces risk only when it duplicates a known-good, tested process. If a flawed configuration or process is automated, the tool will replicate that mistake at scale, very quickly. Always validate processes before automating them.

front.text: Across what stages of a device's lifecycle can Puppet be used?

items: Initial deployment (Day 0/Day 1 provisioning)
Ongoing configuration management (drift detection and enforcement)
Repurposing devices for new roles
Decommissioning/removing devices from the network

front.text: What three components make up a Puppet module?

items: Manifests — Puppet code (.pp) describing desired state
Templates — dynamic content templates
Files — static files distributed to nodes

front.text: What are Puppet 'compile servers' and why are they needed at scale?

back.text: Compile servers are load-balanced Puppet servers that compile catalogs for agents. They scale out the number of agents a single Puppet environment can support. In monolithic deployments above ~4,000 nodes, compile servers are added; above ~20,000 nodes, the PE-PostgreSQL DB is also separated onto a standalone host.

front.text: What is a Puppet 'server of servers' (SoS)?

back.text: An SoS is a higher-level management layer that manages multiple distributed puppet servers and their databases. It simplifies operations in very large environments where there are many puppet servers/compile servers spread across regions or sites.

front.text: What language do Chef users actually write code in, and what is Chef itself implemented in?

items: Code written by users (recipes/cookbooks): Ruby
Chef itself is implemented in Ruby and Erlang
Recipe files use the .rb extension

front.text: Compare Hosted Chef and Private Chef.

rows: All Chef components inside enterprise network

front.text: Which Ruby testing frameworks does the Chef kitchen support?

items: BATS (Bash Automated Testing System)
Minitest
RSpec
Serverspec

front.text: What are Salt formulas?

back.text: Salt formulas are pre-built sets of states (instructions) sent to minions, written in YAML or a DSL. They're designed to work out of the box but are also customizable. Formulas are conceptually similar to Puppet manifests or Chef recipes — they describe the desired configuration.

front.text: What is the salt-minion daemon, and what is it analogous to in Chef?

back.text: The salt-minion daemon runs on minions and gathers system information (grains) to send back to the master. It's analogous to Chef's OHAI service, which performs the same role in the Chef ecosystem.

front.text: What is a 'job' in SaltStack?

back.text: A job is the actual unit of work executed by SaltStack — typically the action triggered by a reactor in response to a change reported by a beacon. Executed jobs can be stored in an external database for review, auditing, or reuse.

front.text: What is the Ansible 'control station' and what runs on managed hosts?

items: Control station — a laptop or server where Ansible is installed and from which all changes/requests originate
Managed hosts — devices/servers configured by Ansible
Nothing extra runs on managed hosts (Ansible is agentless — just SSH/WinRM)

front.text: What three major automation areas does Ansible cover?

items: Cloud provisioning
Application deployment
Configuration management

front.text: Compare YAML lists and dictionaries.

back.text: Lists and dictionaries can be combined (a dictionary value can be a list, or vice versa).

front.text: What is ansible-vault used for and why is it important?

back.text: ansible-vault encrypts YAML files containing sensitive data such as passwords, API tokens, or private keys. It allows secrets to be safely stored alongside playbooks (e.g., in Git) without exposing plaintext credentials to anyone with read access to the repository.

front.text: What does each status field in an Ansible PLAY RECAP mean (ok, changed, unreachable, failed)?

rows: Tasks that completed successfully (whether or not they changed anything)
Tasks that actually modified the target
Hosts Ansible couldn't connect to (SSH/WinRM failure)
Tasks that errored out and stopped

front.text: After an Ansible playbook runs that includes a 'write memory' task, how do you verify the configuration persisted?

back.text: Router# show startup-config | section interface GigabitEthernet2|router eigrp 100

Run 'show startup-config' (filtered if needed) on the device. If the changed config appears in startup-config, you've confirmed the 'write memory' task saved it. Use the | section filter to show only the relevant blocks.

front.text: What kinds of problems is Puppet Bolt especially well-suited for?

items: Pushing immediate (one-off) configuration changes
Managing services — start/stop/restart
Deploying applications
Tasks that don't fit the periodic state-enforcement model of traditional Puppet
Environments without Puppet servers/agents installed

front.text: Describe the lifecycle of a Puppet Bolt script execution on a remote device.

items: Bolt copies the script into a temporary directory on the remote device
Bolt executes the script remotely
Bolt captures the script's results and returns them to the operator
Bolt removes the script from the remote system, leaving no residue

back.text: This 'leave no trace' behavior is one reason Bolt is appealing for ad-hoc remote operations.

front.text: Can Salt SSH and the master/minion model be used together?

back.text: Yes. Salt SSH can operate alongside a traditional master/minion environment — some hosts use minions for fast 0MQ communication, while others (where agents can't be installed) are managed agentless via SSH. This makes SaltStack flexible for heterogeneous environments.

front.text: Why is Salt SSH slower than the standard SaltStack master/minion model?

back.text: The master/minion model uses 0MQ — a high-performance, persistent distributed messaging library. Salt SSH instead opens a fresh SSH session per command and runs Python on the remote host on demand. SSH-based execution has higher per-command overhead, but it's still faster than humans logging in manually.

front.text: Your operations team has deep Ruby experience and wants strict periodic state enforcement on infrastructure. Which tool fits best, and why?

back.text: Chef. The team's Ruby fluency aligns directly with how Chef recipes are written, and Chef's pull-based model with periodic check-ins enforces desired state continuously. Puppet would be a close second (DSL is Ruby-based), but native Ruby code is more idiomatic in Chef.

front.text: Security policy forbids installing any agent software on managed devices. Which automation tools remain viable?

items: Ansible — agentless by design (SSH/WinRM)
Puppet Bolt — agentless variant of Puppet
Salt SSH — agentless variant of SaltStack

back.text: Traditional Puppet, Chef, and SaltStack master/minion deployments would NOT fit this constraint.

front.text: What's the recommended starting point when planning to automate a task?

back.text: Start with the desired outcome. Once the outcome is clearly defined, you can build a plan to achieve it — often using a methodology like PPDIOO. Automating without a clear goal risks codifying a flawed process or misconfiguration at scale.

front.text: Final Chapter 29 quick-recall exam tips.

items: EEM = on-box, if-then style applets, Tcl scripts; uses event detectors
Use 'event manager run <name>' to manually trigger an applet with 'event none'
PPDIOO: Prepare, Plan, Design, Implement, Operate, Optimize
Agent-based: Puppet, Chef, SaltStack (master/minion)
Agentless: Ansible, Puppet Bolt, Salt SSH
Languages: Puppet DSL/Ruby, Chef = Ruby, Ansible = YAML, SaltStack = YAML/Python
Terminology pairs — Puppet: modules/manifests; Chef: cookbooks/recipes; Ansible: playbooks/plays/tasks; Salt: pillars/grains, masters/minions
Ansible runs playbooks via 'ansible-playbook <file>.yaml'
YAML files often start with --- and end with ...
Chef uses 'knife upload <cookbook>' to publish to the Chef server
Puppet Bolt copies script → runs → cleans up; tasks use module::taskfile naming
Salt SSH requires SSH + Python on the remote host; uses roster files

front.text: EEM applet exam-day cheat sheet — what must you remember about applet structure?

items: Applets follow if-then logic: event = trigger, action = response
Applets start in user EXEC — always include 'enable' and 'configure terminal' before config actions
With AAA command authorization, add 'event manager session cli username <user>' or actions silently fail
Use decimal labels (1.0, 1.5, 2.0) — labels sort as STRINGS, so 10.0 sorts after 1.0 but before 9.0
$_cli_result captures output of CLI commands inside the applet for use in syslog/email

front.text: Quick-recall: EEM event types and how to manually trigger an applet.

items: event syslog pattern "..." — fires on matching syslog regex
event cli pattern "..." sync yes — fires on matching CLI input (sync = applet runs before CLI completes)
event none — applet only runs when manually triggered
Manual trigger command: 'event manager run <applet-name>'
View Tcl scripts in flash with: 'more flash:<file>.tcl'

front.text: Memorize: which tools are agent-based vs agentless?

back.text: Common exam trap: SaltStack and Puppet have BOTH agent-based and agentless variants. Don't blanket-classify them.

front.text: Match each automation tool to its primary language(s).

back.text: If asked 'which is built on Python?' the answer is Ansible AND SaltStack.

front.text: Memorize the parallel terminology used by Puppet, Chef, Ansible, and SaltStack.
Ansible/YAML quick-recall facts.

items: Ansible uses YAML — NOT 'TAML' (common exam trick wording)
YAML files commonly start with --- and end with ...
Comments begin with # (pound sign)
Lists: items begin with '- ' (dash + space)
Dictionaries: 'key: value' (no quotes required)
Run a playbook with: ansible-playbook <file>.yaml
ansible = ad-hoc modules; ansible-playbook = playbooks; ansible-vault = encrypt secrets; ansible-pull = pull mode

front.text: What does PPDIOO stand for, in the correct order?

items: Prepare
Plan
Design
Implement
Operate
Optimize

back.text: Watch for distractor choices that swap 'Operate' with 'Observe' or 'Implement' with 'Integrate' — those are wrong.

front.text: Puppet exam-day quick facts.

items: Modules contain manifests, templates, and files
Manifests = .pp files written in Puppet DSL (Ruby-based)
Manifests pushed via SSL with certificates
Scale: Monolithic (≤4k) → +Compile servers (4k–20k) → +Standalone PE-PostgreSQL (>20k)
Server-of-Servers (SoS) used to manage many puppet servers in very large environments
Puppet Forge = community module/manifest repo; many also on GitHub
Puppet Bolt = agentless variant — runs commands/scripts/tasks via SSH or WinRM

front.text: Chef and SaltStack rapid-fire exam recall.

items: Chef: cookbooks → recipes (.rb), uploaded with 'knife upload <cookbook>'
Chef nodes run OHAI to gather state and report to the Chef server
Chef deployment types: Solo, Client/Server, Hosted, Private
Chef 'kitchen' = pre-prod test environment; supports BATS, Minitest, RSpec, Serverspec
SaltStack: master/minions, default messaging = 0MQ (ZeroMQ)
Grains run on minions (gather data); Pillars stored on master (deliver scoped data)
Reactors live on master, beacons live on minions
SynDic = master-of-masters GUI for very large SaltStack deployments
Salt SSH = agentless — requires SSH + Python on remote host; uses roster files

front.text: Final Chapter 29 comparison cheat sheet — memorize this table.

back.text: If you only memorize one Chapter 29 artifact for the exam, make it this table.

front.text: What three configuration parameters MUST match for two switches to belong to the same MST region?

items: MST configuration name (region name)
MST configuration revision number
VLAN-to-instance mapping (every VLAN must map to the same MST instance on both switches)

back.text: If ANY of these three differ — even by a typo or one missing VLAN — the neighbor is treated as OUTSIDE the region, regardless of trunk status.

front.text: Compare the IST (MST0) and the CIST in MST.

rows: Internal to a single MST region
Spans the entire bridged network across regions and STP domains
Yes — every MST deployment has a CIST
Default instance; carries any VLAN not mapped elsewhere
Common spanning tree view used to interoperate with other regions and 802.1D/RSTP
Hidden — other regions see the whole MST region as a single virtual bridge via the CIST

back.text: Outside the MST region, the entire region looks like one big switch on the CIST. MST instances 1+ are completely hidden from outside the region.

front.text: True or false: In MST, root bridge placement is configured per VLAN.

back.text: False. Root placement in MST is PER INSTANCE, not per VLAN. You set a root for an MST instance (e.g., MST1, MST2), and every VLAN mapped to that instance inherits that root. To split traffic across two roots, you map VLANs into different instances and elect different roots per instance.

spanning-tree mst 1 root primary
spanning-tree mst 2 root primary

front.text: Which command proves a switch's MST region inputs (name, revision, VLAN-to-instance mapping)?

back.text:
Switch# show spanning-tree mst configuration

Name      [CAMPUS]
Revision  10     Instances configured 3

Instance  Vlans mapped
--------  ---------------------------------------------------------------------
0         1-9,20-99,300-4094
1         10-19
2         100-299
Compare this output side-by-side on neighbors. If Name, Revision, OR any mapped VLAN differs, the switches are in different MST regions and the link becomes a region boundary.

front.text: Which command shows MST roots, VLAN mappings, and per-instance port roles, and how do you read it?

back.text:
Switch# show spanning-tree mst

##### MST0    vlans mapped:   1-9,20-99,300-4094
Bridge        address aaaa.bbbb.0001  priority      32768 (32768 sysid 0)
Root          address aaaa.bbbb.0001  priority      32768 (this bridge is root)
Interface        Role     Sts  Cost      Prio.Nbr Type
----------------- ----- ---  --------- -------- --------------------------------
Gi1/0/1           Desg     FWD  20000     128.1    P2p

##### MST1    vlans mapped:   10-19
Bridge        address aaaa.bbbb.0001  priority      4097 (4096 sysid 1)
Root          this switch for MST1

##### MST2    vlans mapped:   100-299
Bridge        address aaaa.bbbb.0001  priority      32770 (32768 sysid 2)
Root          address cccc.dddd.0002  priority      4098 (4096 sysid 2) cost 20000

items: Confirms which switch is root for each MST instance
Shows which VLANs are mapped to each instance
Displays port roles (Root / Desg / Altn / Backup) PER INSTANCE
A port can be Root for MST1 but Designated/Alternate for MST2 — that's the whole point of per-instance load sharing

front.text: How do you configure DSW1 to be primary root for MST1 and DSW2 to be primary root for MST2 (with mutual backups)?

back.text:
! On DSW1
spanning-tree mst 1 root primary
spanning-tree mst 2 root secondary

! On DSW2
spanning-tree mst 2 root primary
spanning-tree mst 1 root secondary
This gives you per-instance load sharing: VLANs mapped to MST1 send traffic toward DSW1, and VLANs mapped to MST2 send traffic toward DSW2. If either DSW fails, the other takes over both instances.

front.text: Show the configuration to place a switch into MST region 'CAMPUS', revision 10, with VLANs 10–19 in MST1 and 100–299 in MST2.

back.text:
spanning-tree mode mst
!
spanning-tree mst configuration
 name CAMPUS
 revision 10
 instance 1 vlan 10-19
 instance 2 vlan 100-299
 exit
Any VLAN NOT explicitly mapped to instance 1 or 2 lands in MST0 (the IST) by default. The 'name' and 'revision' values must match exactly across all switches in the region.

cards.subtopic: Region Mismatch — Trunk Up but Boundary

front.text: Why can a trunk be 'up/up' and forwarding VLANs between two switches, yet MST treat the neighbor as being outside the region?

back.text: Because trunk operational state and MST region membership are independent. The trunk only requires Layer 2 negotiation (DTP/802.1Q, matching native VLAN, allowed VLAN list). MST region membership requires matching NAME + REVISION + VLAN-to-instance MAPPING. If even one of those three differs, MST treats the link as a region boundary — the port becomes a CIST boundary port and only MST0/CIST BPDUs cross the link, even though the trunk is fully operational.
Symptom: Trunk forwards user VLANs fine, but 'show spanning-tree mst configuration' differs between sides, and 'show spanning-tree mst' shows the link as a boundary port (Bound (RSTP)/Bound (PVST)).

front.text: What are the operational symptoms of an MST region mismatch between two switches connected by a working trunk?

items: Trunk is up/up and forwards data VLANs as expected
'show spanning-tree mst' marks the port as a boundary (Bound (RSTP) / Bound (PVST))
Per-instance (MST1+) information does NOT cross the link — only CIST/MST0 BPDUs are exchanged
Roots intended to be shared per instance don't appear correctly on the neighbor
Load sharing per instance breaks because the neighbor doesn't see MST1/MST2 BPDUs
'show spanning-tree mst configuration' reveals different name, revision, or VLAN mapping

front.text: Two distribution switches show as boundary ports to each other in MST. What is the troubleshooting workflow to fix the region mismatch?

items: Run 'show spanning-tree mst configuration' on BOTH switches
Compare the region name (case-sensitive) — must match exactly
Compare the revision number — must match exactly
Compare every instance-to-VLAN mapping line — even one missing VLAN breaks region membership
Correct the mismatch under 'spanning-tree mst configuration' and 'exit' to commit
Re-verify with 'show spanning-tree mst' that the port role is no longer 'Boundary'
Confirm per-instance roots and port roles look as designed

back.text: Don't forget: changes inside 'spanning-tree mst configuration' aren't applied until you 'exit' the sub-mode. Until exit, you're in a pending region edit.

front.text: An access switch (ASW) uplinks to DSW1 and DSW2. DSW1 is root for MST1 and DSW2 is root for MST2. Why might ASW's root port be different for MST1 vs MST2?

back.text: Because root port election is performed PER MST INSTANCE. For MST1, ASW elects the lowest-cost path toward DSW1 — usually the uplink directly to DSW1 — making the DSW1-facing port the Root Port for MST1 and the DSW2-facing port Alternate. For MST2, ASW elects the lowest-cost path toward DSW2 — making the DSW2-facing port the Root Port for MST2 and the DSW1-facing port Alternate. The result: VLANs mapped to MST1 leave ASW via DSW1, and VLANs mapped to MST2 leave via DSW2 — that's per-instance load sharing in action.
Verify with 'show spanning-tree mst' on the ASW — same physical port, different role per instance.

front.text: You configured DSW1 as primary root for MST1, but 'show spanning-tree mst 1' on the ASW shows a different switch as root. What are the most likely causes?

items: MST region mismatch — ASW or an intermediate switch isn't in the same MST region as DSW1, so the DSW1 BPDUs for MST1 never propagate
VLAN-to-instance mapping difference — the VLANs you expect on MST1 are actually mapped to a different instance on ASW
Another switch has a lower priority for MST1 (someone else also ran 'spanning-tree mst 1 root primary' or set a manually low priority)
MST1 simply doesn't exist on ASW because no VLANs are mapped to instance 1 there — ASW silently treats those VLANs as MST0
Boundary port between DSW1 and ASW — only CIST/MST0 information crosses, so MST1 root info never arrives

front.text: Compare 'trunk operational state' vs 'MST region membership'.

rows: Encapsulation (802.1Q), allowed VLAN list, native VLAN, DTP
Trunk down or VLAN not allowed
Port becomes MST boundary; per-instance BPDUs don't cross
Determines if frames pass at all
Frames still pass, but STP topology may not be what you expect
Yes — trunk can be perfectly fine
Yes — region can still be mismatched

back.text: Key takeaway: A green trunk does NOT prove MST region consistency. Always verify both.

front.text: What happens at an MST boundary port that connects to a PVST+ or RSTP neighbor (a different STP domain)?

items: The MST switch interoperates via the CIST
From the PVST+/RSTP side, the entire MST region appears as a single bridge
Only CIST BPDUs are exchanged across the boundary — internal MST instance information is NOT shared
PVST simulation handles per-VLAN BPDUs from the PVST+ neighbor on the boundary port
If PVST simulation detects an inconsistency (e.g., the PVST+ side claims a better root than the CIST), the port can be put into PVST_Peer_Inconsistent state, blocking the VLAN(s) to prevent loops

front.text: Why does MST0 always exist, and what VLANs live there?

back.text: MST0 is the IST (Internal Spanning Tree) and is mandatory — every MST deployment has it. It carries every VLAN that is NOT explicitly mapped to another MST instance. MST0 is also the instance that interacts with the CIST at the region boundary, providing the single 'virtual bridge' view of the region to the outside world.
Common pitfall: Forgetting to map a VLAN to MST1 or MST2 means it silently lives in MST0 — and won't follow your intended per-instance root design.

front.text: Interpret this output. What's the problem and how do you fix it?

DSW1# show spanning-tree mst configuration
Name      [CAMPUS]
Revision  10     Instances configured 3
Instance  Vlans mapped
--------  ---------------------------------------------------------------------
0         1-9,20-99,300-4094
1         10-19
2         100-299

DSW2# show spanning-tree mst configuration
Name      [Campus]
Revision  10     Instances configured 3
Instance  Vlans mapped
--------  ---------------------------------------------------------------------
0         1-9,20-99,300-4094
1         10-19
2         100-299

back.text: The region NAME is 'CAMPUS' on DSW1 but 'Campus' on DSW2. Region names are case-sensitive, so DSW1 and DSW2 are in DIFFERENT MST regions even though revision and VLAN mappings match. Their interconnect will be a boundary port and per-instance BPDUs won't cross.

DSW2(config)# spanning-tree mst configuration
DSW2(config-mst)# name CAMPUS
DSW2(config-mst)# exit
Always 'exit' the mst configuration sub-mode to commit changes — until then, the new region settings are pending and not active.

front.text: DSW1 maps VLAN 10-19 to MST1. DSW2 maps VLAN 10-18 to MST1 (forgot 19). All other MST settings match. What happens, and what do you observe?

items: DSW1 and DSW2 are in DIFFERENT MST regions because the VLAN-to-instance mapping is not identical
The trunk between them becomes an MST boundary port
Per-instance BPDUs (MST1, MST2) don't cross — only CIST/MST0 BPDUs do
Per-instance root design (DSW1 root for MST1, DSW2 root for MST2) silently breaks — neighbors don't see each other's per-instance bridge IDs
VLAN 19 traffic still flows because the trunk is up and 802.1Q tagging works fine — masking the STP problem

back.text: Fix: 'instance 1 vlan 10-19' on DSW2, then 'exit' to commit. Re-verify with 'show spanning-tree mst' that the port is no longer a boundary.

front.text: Besides 'root primary/secondary', how can you manually set MST instance priority, and what value rule must you follow?

back.text:
spanning-tree mst 1 priority 4096
spanning-tree mst 2 priority 8192

Priority must be a multiple of 4096 (valid: 0, 4096, 8192, 12288, ..., 61440). The lower the priority, the more likely the switch wins root election for that instance. 'spanning-tree mst <id> root primary' is a macro that automatically picks a low value (typically 24576 or lower).

front.text: Which targeted commands confirm that an ASW has different root ports for MST1 and MST2?

back.text:
ASW# show spanning-tree mst 1
ASW# show spanning-tree mst 2

! Or per-interface:
ASW# show spanning-tree mst interface GigabitEthernet1/0/1
ASW# show spanning-tree mst interface GigabitEthernet1/0/2

Look at the Role column (Root / Desg / Altn / Backup) per instance. The same physical port can be Root for MST1 and Alternate for MST2 (or vice versa) when DSW1 is root for MST1 and DSW2 is root for MST2 — this is the expected per-instance load-sharing pattern.

front.text: An ASW shows the following on its uplink. What does it tell you?

ASW# show spanning-tree mst 1

##### MST1    vlans mapped:   10-19
Bridge        address bbbb.cccc.0001  priority      32769 (32768 sysid 1)
Root          address aaaa.bbbb.0001  priority      24577 (24576 sysid 1)
              port    Gi1/0/1         cost          20000

Interface        Role     Sts  Cost      Prio.Nbr Type
----------------- ----- ---  --------- -------- --------------------------------
Gi1/0/1           Root     FWD  20000     128.1    P2p
Gi1/0/2           Desg     FWD  20000     128.2    P2p Bound(RSTP)

items: MST1 root is reachable via Gi1/0/1 (Root Port, cost 20000) — that side is in the same MST region
Gi1/0/2 is marked 'Bound(RSTP)' — it's a boundary port to a switch that is NOT in the same MST region (or is running RSTP/PVST+)
Per-instance MST BPDUs do NOT cross Gi1/0/2 — only CIST/MST0 information
Likely cause on Gi1/0/2 side: different MST name, revision, or VLAN-to-instance mapping
Fix: align region config on the neighbor of Gi1/0/2; re-check that the boundary marker disappears

front.text: From outside an MST region, how does the rest of the bridged network 'see' the region?

back.text: The entire MST region appears as a single virtual bridge on the CIST. Switches outside the region (running RSTP, PVST+, or in a different MST region) cannot see MST instances 1+ or the internal topology. They only see one bridge ID — the CIST regional root's bridge ID — and the cost to reach the region. This is why MST is so scalable: internal complexity is hidden behind a single CIST view, regardless of how many switches and instances exist inside.
Implication: Per-instance load sharing is purely an internal optimization. From outside, none of it is visible.
